topics.title: Traditional Economic Theory and Assumptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Traditional Economic Theory and Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Traditional Economic Theory**, often called **Neoclassical Economics** or the **Standard Economic Model (SEM)**, is the dominant analytical framework in mainstream economics that emerged in the late 19th century (the *Marginalist Revolution* led by Jevons, Menger, and Walras). It models human decision-making through the idealized agent known as **Homo Economicus** (Economic Man).

The framework rests on a single, sweeping claim:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> *All human decision-making under scarcity can be modelled as the outcome of rational, utility-maximizing agents who possess stable preferences, unlimited cognitive ability, and perfect access to information, operating within competitive markets that clear at equilibrium prices.*

The mathematical engine of this theory is **constrained optimization**, where a representative agent selects the bundle of goods, actions, or beliefs that maximizes a well-behaved **utility function** $U(\cdot)$ subject to a binding **budget constraint**, an **information constraint**, or a **feasibility set**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine every human being as a **silent, flawless pocket calculator** that:

1. Instantly knows every price, every risk, and every future consequence of every possible action.
2. Has a perfectly stable, internally consistent list of preferences (yesterday's ranking of coffee vs. tea is the same as today's).
3. Never makes arithmetic errors, never feels tired, never acts on impulse, and never lets emotions, social pressure, or procrastination interfere.
4. Will always pick the mathematically optimal option, even if it requires hours of complex mental computation.

In this worldview, markets work beautifully because millions of these hyper-rational calculators trade with one another, and their collective self-interest produces socially efficient outcomes (the famous *"Invisible Hand"* of Adam Smith). Behavioural Economics is born precisely because **real humans are not pocket calculators** — and the rest of this course is about documenting where, how, and why the calculator breaks down.

## 1.3 The Position in the KTU Syllabus

This topic is the **conceptual foundation** of Module 1. Every subsequent module (cognitive biases, heuristics, prospect theory, time inconsistency, social preferences) is, by definition, a *deviation* from the benchmark defined here. You cannot understand *where behavioural economics goes* until you understand *where neoclassical economics starts*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**
> In the University Exam, this topic is almost never asked in isolation. It is the *preamble* to questions on anomalies, biases, and Prospect Theory. Examiners expect you to *contrast* the traditional model with the behavioural critique, so memorize the assumptions tightly — they are your reference baseline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of Traditional Economic Theory

Traditional economics is built in **five logically stacked layers**. To answer any KTU question confidently, you must be able to name, define, and derive consequences from each layer.

### Layer 1 — The Axiom of Scarcity
Resources (time, money, attention) are finite, so **choice is necessary**. Every decision therefore involves **trade-offs** and **opportunity cost**.

### Layer 2 — The Agent: Homo Economicus
The decision-maker is a single, unified, consistent self with the following five properties:

| # | Property | Plain-English Meaning | KTU Keyword |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Perfect Rationality** | Chooses the action that mathematically maximizes expected utility. | *Instrumental Rationality* |
| 2 | **Stable Preferences** | The ranking of outcomes does not change across time, context, or mood. | *Preference Invariance* |
| 3 | **Self-Interest Motive** | The agent cares only about her own payoff, not others' welfare. | *Methodological Individualism* |
| 4 | **Unbounded Cognitive Capacity** | Can solve arbitrarily complex optimization problems instantly and costlessly. | *Computational Omniscience* |
| 5 | **Perfect / Costless Information** | Knows all prices, probabilities, and consequences relevant to the choice. | *Informational Completeness* |

### Layer 3 — Utility Maximization
Given preferences, the agent solves:

$$\max_{x} \; U(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad p \cdot x \le m$$

where $U(\cdot)$ is the utility function, $p$ is the price vector, $x$ is the choice vector, and $m$ is income.

### Layer 4 — Equilibrium
In a market of $N$ such agents, prices adjust so that **demand equals supply** (Walrasian equilibrium). No agent has an incentive to deviate unilaterally.

### Layer 5 — Welfare Theorems
Under perfect competition, no externalities, complete markets, and symmetric information, the competitive equilibrium is **Pareto-efficient** (First Welfare Theorem). Any Pareto-efficient allocation can be supported by some competitive equilibrium (Second Welfare Theorem).

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Sub-Theories

Traditional economics is not one monolith; it is a federation of three interlocking sub-theories. The KTU syllabus tests all three explicitly.

### (a) Rational Choice Theory (RCT)
The general theory of purposive action: an agent with stable preferences chooses the best feasible option. It supplies the **decision-making engine**.

### (b) Expected Utility Theory (EUT)
Bernoulli's (1738) and von Neumann–Morgenstern's (1944) theory of decision-making under risk. It supplies the **choice-under-uncertainty engine**.

### (c) Discounted Utility Theory (DUT)
Samuelson's (1937) model of inter-temporal choice. It supplies the **choice-across-time engine**.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every symbol and every equation below is high-yield for the KTU University Exam.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Symbols & Conditions | Real-World Use |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Utility Function | $U(x)$ | $U'(x) > 0$ (more is better), $U''(x) < 0$ (diminishing marginal utility) | Models consumer satisfaction from a basket of goods $x$. |
| 2 | Marginal Utility | $MU_x = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial x}$ | Positive but decreasing for normal goods | Measures the extra satisfaction from one more unit of $x$. |
| 3 | Budget Constraint | $p_x x + p_y y \le m$ | Prices $p_x, p_y$ are exogenous | Represents scarcity. |
| 4 | Lagrangian Setup | $\mathcal{L} = U(x,y) - \lambda(p_x x + p_y y - m)$ | $\lambda \ge 0$ is the shadow price of money | Tool for solving the constrained optimum. |
| 5 | First-Order Conditions | $\dfrac{MU_x}{MU_y} = \dfrac{p_x}{p_y}$ | Tangency between indifference curve and budget line | Defines the consumer's optimal basket (MRS = price ratio). |
| 6 | Indifference Curve | Set of $(x,y)$ such that $U(x,y) = \bar{U}$ | Convex to origin, downward-sloping | Graph of equally-preferred bundles. |
| 7 | Expected Utility | $EU(a) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \, U(x_i)$ | $\sum p_i = 1$, $p_i \ge 0$ | Predicts choice among risky lotteries $a$. |
| 8 | Certainty Equivalent | $U(CE) = EU(\text{lottery})$ | $CE \le E[X]$ for risk-averse agents | The guaranteed amount equally attractive to a lottery. |
| 9 | Risk Premium | $\pi = E[X] - CE$ | $\pi > 0$ for risk-aversion | What an agent is willing to pay to avoid risk. |
| 10 | Coefficient of Risk Aversion (CARA) | $U(x) = -e^{-\gamma x}$ | $\gamma > 0$ is the risk-aversion coefficient | Constant absolute risk aversion. |
| 11 | Coefficient of Risk Aversion (CRRA) | $U(x) = \dfrac{x^{1-\sigma}}{1-\sigma}$ | $\sigma > 0$ is relative risk aversion | Constant relative risk aversion. |
| 12 | Discounted Utility | $U_t = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{u(c_t)}{(1+\rho)^t}$ | $\rho > 0$ is the time-preference rate | Models savings, addiction, climate policy. |
| 13 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $r$ is the market discount rate | Capital-budgeting and project evaluation. |
| 14 | Engel Aggregation | $\displaystyle\sum_{i} p_i x_i = m$ | Walras' Law | Total spending equals income. |
| 15 | Slutsky Equation | $\dfrac{\partial x_i}{\partial p_j} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial p_j}}_{\text{substitution}} - \underbrace{x_j \dfrac{\partial x_i}{\partial m}}_{\text{income}}$ | Own-price effect on demand | Decomposes price effect into substitution and income. |
| 16 | Revealed Preference | If $p \cdot x^0 \le p \cdot x^1$ and $x^0 \ne x^1$, then $x^1$ is revealed preferred to $x^0$ | Consistency axiom | Derives preferences from observed choices (Samuelson, 1938). |
| 17 | Bayesian Updating | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \, P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Prior $\times$ Likelihood $\propto$ Posterior | Models learning and belief revision. |
| 18 | Nash Equilibrium | For every player $i$: $a_i^* \in \arg\max_{a_i} u_i(a_i, a_{-i}^*)$ | Best-response condition | Predicts outcomes in strategic games. |

## 2.4 The Role in Modern Engineering & Policy

Traditional economic theory is not abstract. It is the **operational engine** behind:

- **Public infrastructure pricing** (marginal-cost road pricing, congestion tolls).
- **Energy markets and carbon trading** (utility-maximizing firms and consumers).
- **Auctions for spectrum allocation** (5G, satellite bands — Vickrey second-price auctions).
- **Engineering project evaluation** (NPV-based capital budgeting).
- **Software economics** (mechanism design for ad auctions, cloud-resource allocation).

Behavioural economics then asks: *what happens when the agents in these systems are systematically irrational?* — which is the entire premise of the rest of this course.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for Engineers**
> As a B.Tech graduate, you will design systems, products, and policies. Traditional economics assumes the *user* of your system behaves like a calculator. Behavioural economics says the user is **predictably irrational**. Designing for the calculator works only when the calculator is real; designing for the human is the engineering challenge of the 21st century.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Working Requirement**
> Board examiners allocate full marks only when each logical step is *explicitly written*. The following derivations show exactly the working style expected. Memorize the *order* of steps (set up → first-order conditions → second-order conditions → comparative statics → economic interpretation).

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Consumer's Optimal Choice (Cobb–Douglas Example)

**Setup.** Suppose the consumer has utility function $U(x,y) = x^{\alpha} y^{1-\alpha}$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$, faces prices $(p_x, p_y)$, and has income $m$.

**Step 1.** Write the optimization problem in Lagrangian form.

$$\mathcal{L} = x^{\alpha} y^{1-\alpha} - \lambda(p_x x + p_y y - m)$$

**Step 2.** Take the partial derivative with respect to $x$ and set it to zero. The economic content of this step is: *at the optimum, the extra utility from one more rupee spent on $x$ must equal the extra utility lost by spending one less rupee on $y$.*

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \alpha x^{\alpha - 1} y^{1-\alpha} - \lambda p_x = 0$$

**Step 3.** Take the partial derivative with respect to $y$ and set it to zero.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = (1-\alpha) x^{\alpha} y^{-\alpha} - \lambda p_y = 0$$

**Step 4.** Divide equation (2) by equation (1) to eliminate $\lambda$. This ratio is the **Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRS)** equated to the price ratio.

$$\frac{(1-\alpha) x^{\alpha} y^{-\alpha}}{\alpha x^{\alpha - 1} y^{1-\alpha}} = \frac{p_y}{p_x}$$

**Step 5.** Simplify by cancelling common powers of $x$ and $y$.

$$\frac{(1-\alpha)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{x}{y} = \frac{p_y}{p_x}$$

**Step 6.** Solve for the ratio $x/y$ to get the *optimal consumption ratio*.

$$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \cdot \frac{p_y}{p_x}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute this ratio into the budget constraint $p_x x + p_y y = m$.

$$p_x \left( \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \cdot \frac{p_y}{p_x} \right) y + p_y y = m$$

**Step 8.** Factor out $y$.

$$\frac{\alpha \, p_y}{1-\alpha} \, y + p_y y = m$$

**Step 9.** Combine the terms over a common denominator.

$$y \left( \frac{\alpha \, p_y + (1-\alpha) p_y}{1-\alpha} \right) = m \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y \left( \frac{p_y}{1-\alpha} \right) = m$$

**Step 10.** Solve explicitly for $y^\star$.

$$y^\star = \frac{(1-\alpha) m}{p_y}$$

**Step 11.** By symmetry, substitute back to get $x^\star$.

$$x^\star = \frac{\alpha m}{p_x}$$

**Step 12.** Economic interpretation. The consumer spends a fixed *share* $\alpha$ of income on good $x$ and $(1-\alpha)$ on good $y$, **independent of prices**. This is the famous Cobb–Douglas homothetic preference result.

$$\text{Expenditure share on } x = \frac{p_x x^\star}{m} = \alpha$$

**Second-Order Check.** The bordered Hessian for a concave utility function and a linear budget set is negative definite, confirming a *maximum*, not a minimum or saddle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Utility Maximization under Risk

**Setup.** A risk-averse agent with $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ must choose between (a) a *certain* amount $C$ and (b) a lottery $L$ that pays $16$ with probability $0.5$ and $4$ with probability $0.5$.

**Step 1.** Compute the expected value of the lottery (the *mathematical* expectation).

$$E[L] = 0.5 \times 16 + 0.5 \times 4 = 8 + 2 = 10$$

**Step 2.** Compute the expected utility of the lottery.

$$EU(L) = 0.5 \, U(16) + 0.5 \, U(4) = 0.5 \times \sqrt{16} + 0.5 \times \sqrt{4} = 0.5 \times 4 + 0.5 \times 2 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Step 3.** Find the certainty equivalent $CE$ such that $U(CE) = EU(L)$.

$$U(CE) = 3 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sqrt{CE} = 3 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad CE = 9$$

**Step 4.** Compute the risk premium $\pi$.

$$\pi = E[L] - CE = 10 - 9 = 1$$

**Step 5.** Economic interpretation. The agent is willing to **give up ₹1** of expected value to convert the risky lottery into a sure thing. This single number summarizes her attitude toward risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Behavioural Hook (Preview of Module 2)**
> In *Prospect Theory* (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979), agents do *not* evaluate lotteries this way. They use a reference point, distort probabilities, and exhibit loss aversion. Keep this derivation in mind — it is the benchmark that Prospect Theory will overturn.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Intertemporal Choice (Exponential Discounting)

**Setup.** The agent must choose between ₹100 today and ₹110 in one year. She has utility $u(c) = \ln c$ and discount rate $\rho = 0.05$.

**Step 1.** Compute the present utility of receiving ₹100 today.

$$U_{\text{today}} = \ln(100) \approx 4.6052$$

**Step 2.** Compute the discounted utility of receiving ₹110 in one year.

$$U_{\text{delayed}} = \frac{\ln(110)}{1 + 0.05} = \frac{\ln(110)}{1.05} \approx \frac{4.7005}{1.05} \approx 4.4766$$

**Step 3.** Compare.

$$U_{\text{today}} = 4.6052 \;>\; U_{\text{delayed}} = 4.4766$$

**Step 4.** Decision. The rational agent chooses ₹100 today, even though the delayed option has a higher *nominal* return (10 % vs. 5 % discount rate).

**Step 5.** General formula. An agent prefers $X$ at $t=0$ over $Y$ at $t=1$ if and only if:

$$u(X) \ge \frac{u(Y)}{1+\rho}$$

**Step 6.** Behavioural preview. Real humans discount the future **hyperbolically**, not exponentially. They procrastinate, default on retirement savings, and over-consume credit. This single deviation from exponential discounting is the foundation of *Liberal Paternalism* (Thaler & Sunstein, 2003) and the entire *nudge* policy agenda.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Assumption vs. Empirical Reality

The most powerful KTU answer is one that lists each neoclassical assumption and the **empirical evidence** that breaks it. This is the analytical scaffolding you must memorize.

| # | Neoclassical Assumption | Formal Statement | Empirical Violation | Behavioural Counterpart |
|---|------------------------|------------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Perfect Rationality | Agent picks the utility-maximizer | Cognitive errors, logical fallacies | Bounded Rationality (Simon, 1957) |
| 2 | Stable Preferences | $U$ does not depend on context | Framing effects, context effects | Constructed Preferences (Slovic) |
| 3 | Self-Interest | Utility depends only on own payoff | Altruism, fairness, spite | Social Preferences (Fehr & Gächter) |
| 4 | Unlimited Cognition | $\epsilon$-optimal choice is free | Mental fatigue, limited attention | Cognitive Load Theory |
| 5 | Perfect Information | All prices & probabilities known | Inattention, misperception of risk | Heuristics & Biases |
| 6 | Exponential Discounting | Constant discount rate | Present bias, hyperbolic discounting | Time-Inconsistency (Laibson, 1997) |
| 7 | Expected Utility | $EU = \sum p_i U(x_i)$ | Allais, Ellsberg paradoxes | Prospect Theory (1979) |
| 8 | Revealed Preference | Choices reveal preferences | Choice inconsistency, status-quo bias | Preference Reversals (Lichtenstein & Slovic, 1971) |
| 9 | Methodological Individualism | Only individual choices matter | Herding, social norms, identity | Social Influence (Asch, 1951) |
| 10 | Equilibrium | Markets clear at $p^\star$ | Bubbles, crashes, persistent unemployment | Adaptive Markets Hypothesis (Lo, 2004) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is your most powerful KTU revision tool.** Memorize the *pairing* of each neoclassical assumption with its empirical violation and its behavioural counterpart. Any question that says *"Discuss the limitations of the traditional economic model"* is essentially asking you to recite this table with definitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Traditional Economic Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Layer Scarcity and Choice] --> B[Agent Layer Homo Economicus]
    B --> B1[Perfect Rationality]
    B --> B2[Stable Preferences]
    B --> B3[Self Interest Motive]
    B --> B4[Unlimited Cognition]
    B --> B5[Perfect Information]
    B --> C[Decision Engine Layer Optimization]
    C --> C1[Rational Choice Theory]
    C --> C2[Expected Utility Theory]
    C --> C3[Discounted Utility Theory]
    C --> D[Market Layer Walrasian Equilibrium]
    D --> E[Output Layer Welfare Theorems]
    E --> E1[First Welfare Theorem Pareto Efficiency]
    E --> E2[Second Welfare Theorem Implementation]
    F[Behavioural Critique Layer Module 2 Onwards] -. Contrasts .-> B
    F -. Contrasts .-> C
    F -. Contrasts .-> D
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#b03a2e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#424242,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

**How to read this diagram in a KTU answer:**
The blue **Input Layer** establishes scarcity. The green **Agent Layer** defines *who* makes the decision (Homo Economicus and its five properties). The orange **Decision Engine Layer** is the *how* — three sub-theories for the three fundamental decision contexts (discrete choice, risky choice, inter-temporal choice). The purple **Market Layer** aggregates across agents. The red **Output Layer** delivers welfare conclusions. The black **Behavioural Critique Layer** is *not* part of the traditional model — it is overlaid on top to flag the empirical failures that justify the rest of this course.

**Reading guide.** The blue nodes on the left are the *traditional* model's logical chain. The red node marks the **empirical breaking point** — the moment when data refused to fit the model. The orange nodes are the five foundational anomalies. The green node is the **birth of behavioural economics** as a unified discipline. The purple nodes are its policy descendants.

**Reading guide.** This diagram is your *defensive shield* in the exam. If a question begins *"Discuss the traditional model of choice under…"*, you can use this single diagram to identify which sub-theory is in play, state the equation, name the tool, and answer. Examiners reward structural clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test *recall* and *definition*. Answers must be precise, 3–4 sentences, and end with a *consequence* or *example*. Vague answers fetch 1 mark out of 3.

### Q1. Define the concept of Homo Economicus. Mention any three of its core properties. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks).**
Homo Economicus is the idealized decision-maker assumed by traditional (neoclassical) economics. It is a *theoretical construct* used to model how a perfectly informed, perfectly rational individual would allocate scarce resources to maximize personal welfare.
**Three core properties:**
1. **Rationality** — selects the action that maximizes expected utility.
2. **Self-interest** — utility depends only on the agent's own payoff.
3. **Perfect information** — knows all prices, probabilities, and consequences.
**Significance:** It supplies the *micro-foundation* of the entire neoclassical edifice, including consumer theory, firm theory, and welfare economics.

### Q2. State the Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility and write its mathematical condition. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks).**
The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility states that as the consumption of a good increases, the *additional* utility derived from each successive unit **decreases**, holding consumption of all other goods constant. This captures the empirical regularity that the first unit of any good (the first glass of water, the first slice of bread) yields the most satisfaction, with later units yielding progressively less.
**Mathematical condition:** For a utility function $U(x)$ that is twice-differentiable, the condition is:

$$U'(x) > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad U''(x) < 0$$

The first inequality says *more is better* (non-satiation); the second says *at a decreasing rate* (concavity). It is the mathematical foundation of the convex indifference curve and the *risk-aversion* property of Expected Utility Theory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an *internal choice*. The structure is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must attempt *one full question* (both sub-parts). Each sub-part requires definition, derivation, diagram, and *applied example*. The model answers below exceed the expected length to give you a complete reference; in the exam, you may compress the prose but **must keep all formulas, all steps, and the diagram description**.

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

#### (a) Explain the five core assumptions of the Traditional Economic Model. Discuss how each assumption simplifies the analysis of human decision-making. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer.**

The Traditional Economic Model (Neoclassical Economics) rests on five interlocking assumptions about the decision-maker. Each is a *deliberate simplification* designed to make the mathematics tractable.

**1. Perfect Rationality.** The agent always chooses the action that maximizes her expected utility. She never makes a logical error, never confuses cause and effect, and never overlooks a profitable opportunity. *Simplification:* This converts the messy psychology of real decision-making into a clean *constrained-optimization* problem that can be solved with calculus or convex analysis.

**2. Stable Preferences.** The agent's ranking of outcomes is invariant across time, mood, and context. The same person, asked the same question tomorrow, will give the same answer. *Simplification:* Stable preferences give the utility function a *well-defined* shape, allowing the use of indifference curves and the formal apparatus of revealed preference.

**3. Self-Interest Motive.** Utility depends only on the agent's own consumption or payoff. Altruism, fairness, spite, and social pressure are absent. *Simplification:* It eliminates the need to model interpersonal utility comparisons (the "utility monster" problem) and isolates the individual as the unit of analysis (Methodological Individualism).

**4. Unbounded Cognitive Capacity.** There is no cost — in time, attention, or effort — to thinking. The agent can solve arbitrarily complex optimization problems instantly. *Simplification:* All *transaction costs* of decision-making drop to zero, so the only constraint on choice is the *resource* constraint (budget, time, technology), not the *cognitive* constraint.

**5. Perfect / Costless Information.** The agent knows every price, every probability, and every consequence of every action. There is no uncertainty, ambiguity, or asymmetric information. *Simplification:* It removes *information asymmetries* and *search frictions* from the model, allowing the First Welfare Theorem to prove that markets are Pareto-efficient.

**Critique in one line:** Real humans violate *all five* assumptions, and this is the empirical opening for Behavioural Economics.

#### (b) Derive the consumer's optimal choice for a utility function $U(x,y) = x^{0.4} \, y^{0.6}$ given prices $p_x$, $p_y$ and income $m$. Hence, state and explain the Expenditure-Shares Result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer.**

**Step 1.** State the problem.

$$\max_{x,y} \; x^{0.4} y^{0.6} \quad \text{subject to} \quad p_x x + p_y y = m$$

**Step 2.** Form the Lagrangian.

$$\mathcal{L} = x^{0.4} y^{0.6} - \lambda(p_x x + p_y y - m)$$

**Step 3.** First-order condition w.r.t. $x$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 0.4 \, x^{-0.6} y^{0.6} - \lambda p_x = 0$$

**Step 4.** First-order condition w.r.t. $y$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 0.6 \, x^{0.4} y^{-0.4} - \lambda p_y = 0$$

**Step 5.** Divide (3) by (4) to eliminate $\lambda$.

$$\frac{0.4 \, x^{-0.6} y^{0.6}}{0.6 \, x^{0.4} y^{-0.4}} = \frac{p_x}{p_y}$$

**Step 6.** Simplify.

$$\frac{0.4}{0.6} \cdot \frac{y}{x} = \frac{p_x}{p_y} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{y}{x} = \frac{0.6}{0.4} \cdot \frac{p_x}{p_y} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{p_x}{p_y}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute into the budget constraint.

$$p_x x + p_y \left(1.5 \cdot \frac{p_x}{p_y} \cdot x\right) = m \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p_x x + 1.5 \, p_x x = m$$

**Step 8.** Solve for $x^\star$.

$$x^\star = \frac{m}{2.5 \, p_x} = \frac{0.4 \, m}{p_x}$$

**Step 9.** By symmetry, $y^\star = \dfrac{0.6 \, m}{p_y}$.

**Step 10. Expenditure Shares.**

$$s_x = \frac{p_x x^\star}{m} = 0.4, \qquad s_y = \frac{p_y y^\star}{m} = 0.6$$

**Interpretation.** The consumer spends a *constant* 40 % of income on $x$ and 60 % on $y$, regardless of the price level. This is the *homothetic* property of Cobb–Douglas preferences. It also says that the Cobb–Douglas exponents $(\alpha, 1-\alpha)$ are *budget shares*, not just parameters of the utility function — a remarkable economic fact.

**Why it matters.** The result is a *testable prediction* of the traditional model. Empirical studies (e.g., Engel's Law on food expenditure as income rises) show that budget shares are *not* constant — they fall for food and rise for luxuries as income grows. This violation is one of the empirical motivations for richer preference structures (e.g., Stone-Geary, CES).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

#### (a) Explain the Expected Utility Theory (EUT) of Bernoulli and von Neumann–Morgenstern. State the four axioms of EUT. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer.**

**Historical origin.** Daniel Bernoulli (1738) noted that most people would prefer a sure 10,000 ducats to a 50–50 lottery paying 20,000 or nothing, even though both have an expected value of 10,000. He proposed that people maximize *expected utility* (the *moral* expectation, in his words), not expected *monetary* value. This was formalized by John von Neumann and Oskar Morgenstern (1944) into a rigorous axiomatic theory.

**Definition.** EUT is the neoclassical model of decision-making under *risk* (known probabilities). Given a set of lotteries $\mathcal{L}$, the agent has a utility function $U: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ and chooses the lottery $L^\star$ that maximizes:

$$EU(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \, U(x_i)$$

**The Four Axioms of EUT (VNM Axioms).**

1. **Completeness.** For any two lotteries $A$ and $B$, the agent can rank them: $A \succ B$, $B \succ A$, or $A \sim B$. *Meaning:* No "indecision" — preferences are total.

2. **Transitivity.** If $A \succ B$ and $B \succ C$, then $A \succ C$. *Meaning:* Preferences are internally consistent — the agent is not subject to money-pump cycles.

3. **Independence (Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives).** If $A \succ B$, then for any third lottery $C$ and probability $p \in [0,1]$, the mixed lottery $pA + (1-p)C \succ pB + (1-p)C$. *Meaning:* The ranking of $A$ vs. $B$ does not change when both are "contaminated" with the same common lottery $C$.

4. **Continuity.** If $A \succ B \succ C$, then there exists a probability $p \in (0,1)$ such that $pA + (1-p)C \sim B$. *Meaning:* Smooth preference structure — preferences do not have "jumps".

If all four axioms hold, the **VNM Expected Utility Theorem** guarantees the existence of a utility function $U(\cdot)$ such that the agent's choices are exactly as if she were maximizing $EU(L)$. This is the *behavioural hypothesis* of EUT.

**Why it matters.** EUT is the foundation of insurance pricing, financial-risk management, and the entire modern theory of finance. Its violation (the *Allais Paradox*, the *Ellsberg Paradox*) is the empirical bridge to Prospect Theory (Module 2).

#### (b) A risk-averse agent with $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is offered a lottery that pays ₹25 with probability 0.6 and ₹4 with probability 0.4. Compute: (i) the expected monetary value, (ii) the expected utility, (iii) the certainty equivalent, and (iv) the risk premium. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer.**

**Step 1. Expected Monetary Value (EMV).**

$$E[L] = 0.6 \times 25 + 0.4 \times 4 = 15 + 1.6 = 16.6$$

So the *mathematical* average payoff is ₹16.60.

**Step 2. Expected Utility (EU).**

$$EU(L) = 0.6 \, U(25) + 0.4 \, U(4) = 0.6 \times \sqrt{25} + 0.4 \times \sqrt{4}$$

$$EU(L) = 0.6 \times 5 + 0.4 \times 2 = 3.0 + 0.8 = 3.8$$

**Step 3. Certainty Equivalent (CE).** Find the sure amount $C$ such that $U(C) = EU(L)$.

$$U(C) = 3.8 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sqrt{C} = 3.8 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C = 14.44$$

The agent is *indifferent* between the risky lottery and a sure ₹14.44.

**Step 4. Risk Premium (π).**

$$\pi = E[L] - CE = 16.6 - 14.44 = 2.16$$

**Interpretation.** The risk-averse agent would be willing to pay **up to ₹2.16** above the certainty equivalent (or up to **₹16.60 − ₹14.44 = ₹2.16** below the expected value) to convert the lottery into a sure thing. The risk premium measures the *cost* of the agent's risk aversion.

**Verification of Risk-Aversion.** Compute $U(E[L]) = \sqrt{16.6} \approx 4.074$. Since $U(E[L]) = 4.074 > EU(L) = 3.8$, the utility function is *strictly concave* and the agent is indeed *strictly risk-averse*. (Jensen's Inequality: for any concave $U$, $U(E[X]) \ge E[U(X)]$, with strict inequality unless $X$ is degenerate.)

**Connection to Insurance.** If a fair insurance policy costs $E[L] - CE = 2.16$ per unit coverage, the agent will buy it. This is the neoclassical foundation of *demand for insurance*. Empirically, however, observed insurance demand is often *too low* (Behavioural Explanation: *probability weighting* and *present bias* — covered in Module 2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps (read carefully before writing your exam)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Rationality" with "Selfishness".** In KTU answers, *rationality* means *consistency with one's own objectives*, **not** *being selfish*. A saint who maximizes charity is perfectly rational in the neoclassical sense. Mixing these two concepts costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint.** When asked to "derive the optimum", students write only the first-order condition and forget to substitute back into the budget line. KTU examiners specifically check the *interior solution*. Missing the substitution costs **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **No mention of second-order conditions.** For Part-B answers, write at least one sentence confirming that the bordered Hessian is negative definite or that the utility function is concave, ensuring a *maximum*. Examiners often allocate **1 mark** for this.
>
> 4. **Stopping at definitions.** A 14-mark question that merely *defines* five assumptions fetches at most 5–6 marks. You must (a) define, (b) explain the *simplification* it provides, (c) give an *example*, and (d) connect to the *behavioural critique*. The structure "Define → Example → Critique" is worth **3 marks** in itself.
>
> 5. **Mis-stating the VNM axioms.** The *Independence Axiom* is the *defining* feature of EUT. Many students confuse it with the *Sure-Thing Principle* (which is a *weaker* consequence). Write the full name: "Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives" or the modern name "Independence Axiom".
>
> 6. **Confusing $EU$ with $E[X]$.** In derivation questions, examiners expect a clear distinction: *expected utility* is a number in *utility space*, while *expected monetary value* is in *rupee space*. Mixing them up is a **1-mark deduction** guaranteed.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Risk-Premium interpretation.** A numerical answer that ends at $\pi = 2.16$ without a one-line *economic interpretation* ("the maximum the agent will pay to avoid the risk") forfeits **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Definition of Traditional Economic Theory:** the neoclassical framework modelling decision-making as utility maximization by Homo Economicus.
- **Homo Economicus:** an idealized agent with perfect rationality, stable preferences, self-interest, unbounded cognition, and perfect information.
- **The five assumptions in exam-ready order:** Rationality → Stable Preferences → Self-Interest → Unlimited Cognition → Perfect Information.
- **The three sub-theories:** Rational Choice Theory (static), Expected Utility Theory (risk), Discounted Utility Theory (time).
- **The master optimization problem:** $\max U(x)$ subject to $p \cdot x \le m$, solved by the Lagrangian and the MRS-equals-price-ratio condition $MU_x / MU_y = p_x / p_y$.
- **Cobb–Douglas property:** budget shares are constant and equal to the exponents.
- **Expected Utility formula:** $EU = \sum p_i U(x_i)$ — memorize this *symbol-for-symbol*.
- **Risk-Premium formula:** $\pi = E[X] - CE$, where $U(CE) = EU(\text{lottery})$.
- **Concavity condition:** $U'(x) > 0$ and $U''(x) < 0$ — non-satiation plus diminishing marginal utility.
- **Exponential Discounting:** $u(c_0) \gtrless u(c_t) / (1+\rho)^t$ is the criterion for intertemporal choice.
- **VNM Four Axioms:** Completeness, Transitivity, Independence, Continuity — name all four in order.
- **The First Welfare Theorem** requires: perfect competition, no externalities, complete markets, symmetric information, and convex preferences.
- **Why Behavioural Economics exists:** *all five* assumptions of the traditional model are violated empirically — Allais, Ellsberg, framing, hyperbolic discounting, social preferences.
- **Key founding authors to mention:** Adam Smith (1776), Bernoulli (1738), Jevons/Menger/Walras (1870s), Pareto (1906), Samuelson (1937, 1947), von Neumann–Morgenstern (1944), Friedman (1953), Simon (1957 bounded rationality), Kahneman–Tversky (1974, 1979).
- **Examiners reward structure:** write in *layers* — Definition → Assumption → Mathematical Form → Worked Example → Behavioural Critique. This single habit adds **2–3 marks** per Part-B answer.
- **The single best exam sentence:** *"The traditional model treats the human agent as a utility-maximizing calculator; behavioural economics treats the human agent as a predictably irrational decision-maker whose systematic deviations can themselves be modelled, predicted, and used to design better policies."* — Use this as a *thesis statement* in any 14-mark answer to immediately signal to the examiner that you understand the entire course in one breath.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Traditional Economic Theory and Assumptions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limits of Rationality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Limits of Rationality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Limits of Rationality** refers to the systematic deviations, constraints, and cognitive shortcomings that prevent human decision-makers from acting as the perfectly rational, fully-informed, utility-maximizing agents assumed in the **Neo-Classical Economic Model (Homo Economicus)**. The concept establishes that human rationality is **bounded** (limited), **procedural** (rule-based), and **ecologically adaptive** rather than absolute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor Definition (Herbert Simon, 1957):**
> *"Bounded Rationality is the property of an agent that behaves in a manner that is nearly optimal with respect to its actual goals, but is constrained by the cognitive resources available to it, the time available, and the information at its disposal."*
> — Herbert A. Simon, *A Behavioral Model of Rational Choice* (1955); formalized in *Administrative Behavior* (3rd Ed., 1976).

The **three primary limits** of rationality that the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes are:

1. **Cognitive Limits** – finite memory, attention span, and computational capacity of the human brain (~7 ± 2 chunks in working memory, per Miller, 1956).
2. **Informational Limits** – incomplete, asymmetric, or costly information in real markets.
3. **Temporal Limits** – decisions must often be made under time pressure, precluding exhaustive search.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Lunch Decision Paradox"**
>
> Imagine you walk into a food court with **40 stalls**. A *perfectly rational* agent (Homo Economicus) would:
> 1. Visit every stall, read every menu,
> 2. Compare nutrient content, price, hygiene rating, wait time,
> 3. Compute the **globally optimal** meal, and then order.
>
> What do *you* actually do? You pick something that is **"good enough"** — the stall nearest to you with a familiar cuisine and a short queue. This is **satisficing**, not maximizing. This is the **limit of rationality in action**: you have a *satisficing threshold* and stop searching once it is met.

### 1.3 The Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Anchors to Memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - **Miller's Number (1956):** 7 ± 2 — the capacity of short-term/working memory chunks.
> - **Optimal Search Rule (Stigler, 1961):** Stop searching when the marginal cost of acquiring the next piece of information equals the marginal benefit of improved decision quality.
> - **Cognitive Budget (Simon):** Decision cost = $C_{search} + C_{error}$ — the brain allocates a finite cognitive budget to minimize total decision cost.
> - **Discounting Rate (Hyperbolic):** $\beta = 0.6$ to $0.8$ (empirical average for human inter-temporal choice) — much lower than the exponential rate assumed in classical models.

### 1.4 Why "Limits" — The Neo-Classical Benchmark

The classical economic agent (Homo Economicus) is assumed to be:
- **Omniscient** (knows all options and outcomes),
- **Infinitely fast** (computes the best choice instantly),
- **Selfish-Utility-Maximizing** (single, stable preference ordering),
- **Emotionless** (no cognitive bias).

Behavioural Economics argues that **real humans violate all four of these assumptions systematically** — not randomly, but in *predictable* ways. The study of these violations is the study of the **Limits of Rationality**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Bounded Rationality

#### Pillar 1: Cognitive Constraints (the "Hardware" Limit)

- The human brain is a **serial processor** with severe capacity limits.
- **Working memory** holds only **7 ± 2** items (Miller, 1956).
- **Attention** is a **scarce resource** (Kahneman, 1973) — the "bottleneck" of cognition.
- **Cognitive load** (Sweller, 1988) — complex decisions deplete mental energy and degrade subsequent decisions (decision fatigue).

#### Pillar 2: Information Constraints (the "Software/Data" Limit)

- **Information Asymmetry (Akerlof, 1970)** — one party has more/better information (e.g., used-car market → "Market for Lemons").
- **Search Costs** — obtaining information is time-consuming and expensive.
- **Information Overload** — beyond ~4 simultaneous options, choice quality *decreases* (Iyengar & Lepper, 2000 — the "Paradox of Choice").

#### Pillar 3: Motivational / Emotional Constraints (the "Operating System" Limit)

- **Loss Aversion (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979):** losses loom ~2× larger than equivalent gains.
- **Risk Aversion in the gain domain**, **Risk Seeking in the loss domain** (Reflection Effect).
- **Status Quo Bias, Endowment Effect, Sunk Cost Fallacy.**
- **Herd Behavior & Social Proof** — humans imitate others rather than computing independent rational choices.

### 2.2 The Two Strategic Responses: Maximizing vs. Satisficing

| Strategic Mode | Definition | Decision Rule | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximizing** (Classical Rational) | Search exhaustively; pick the **global optimum**. | $x^* = \arg\max_{x \in X} \, U(x)$ | When search cost ≈ 0 and full info is available (almost never in real life) |
| **Satisficing** (Simon, 1956) | Search until a **satisfactory threshold** $\bar{U}$ is met; pick the first option that clears it. | $x_s = \{x \in X : U(x) \geq \bar{U}\}$ | When search cost > 0 and time is finite (the realistic case) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference for "Limits of Rationality" (KTU 2024)**
> 
> | Concept | Formula / Mathematical Form | Boundary / Limiting Case | Intuition |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Expected Utility (Rational Baseline) | $EU = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot U(x_i)$ | If $p_i = 1$ for some $i$, $EU = U(x_i)$ (certainty) | The benchmark that real humans deviate from |
> | Simon's Satisficing Rule | $U(x_s) \geq \bar{U}$ where $\bar{U}$ = aspiration level | $\bar{U} \to \infty \Rightarrow$ maximizing | "Good enough" beats "best possible" |
> | Stigler's Optimal Search Rule | Stop when $MC_{search} = MB_{info}$ | $MC_{search} = 0 \Rightarrow$ exhaustive search | Marginal cost = marginal benefit |
> | Probability Weighting (Tversky-Kahneman, 1992) | $w(p) = \dfrac{p^{\gamma}}{(p^{\gamma} + (1-p)^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma}}$ with $\gamma \approx 0.61$ | $p \to 0$: $w(p) > p$ (over-weight small probs); $p \to 1$: $w(p) < p$ (under-weight certainty) | People overweight rare events (lottery) |
> | Prospect Theory Value Function | $v(x) = x^{\alpha}$ for $x \geq 0$; $v(x) = -\lambda \vert x \vert^{\alpha}$ for $x < 0$ with $\alpha \approx 0.88$, $\lambda \approx 2.25$ | $\lambda > 1 \Rightarrow$ loss aversion | Concave in gains, convex in losses, kink at 0 |
> | Hyperbolic Discounting | $V_t = \dfrac{V}{1 + k \cdot t}$ | Exponential: $V_t = V \cdot e^{-rt}$ (rational) | Present bias — people prefer immediate rewards disproportionately |
> | Bayes' Theorem (Rational Updating) | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | — | The rational benchmark; humans systematically violate this (base-rate neglect) |
> | Cognitive Budget Equation | $C_{total} = C_{search} + C_{error}$ | Minimum at optimality: $\dfrac{dC_{search}}{d\,n} = -\dfrac{dC_{error}}{d\,n}$ | The brain minimizes total cost, not error alone |
> | Decision Quality vs. Number of Options | $Q(n) = Q_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{-\beta n})$ | For very large $n$, marginal $Q$ gain $\to 0$ | Diminishing returns to choice expansion |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the real world:**
> - **UI/UX Design:** Forms should not exceed 7 ± 2 visible fields (Miller's Law) — exceeding this violates cognitive limits.
> - **Public Policy:** Default options (e.g., organ donation, retirement savings) leverage status-quo bias to *nudge* behaviour toward socially optimal outcomes (**Nudge Theory — Thaler & Sunstein, 2008**).
> - **Algorithmic Trading:** Bots that mimic human heuristics (loss aversion) outperform purely rational expected-utility bots in volatile markets (Kahneman's *noise*).
> - **Project Management (PERT/CPM):** Bounded rationality explains why Gantt charts always have optimistic/pessimistic/most-likely estimates instead of a single deterministic value.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Expected Utility to Bounded Rationality (The Deviation Map)

We start with the **rational benchmark** and then mathematically *relax* its assumptions one at a time to reach the bounded-rationality model.

**Step 1 — The Rational Benchmark (Expected Utility Theory — von Neumann-Morgenstern, 1944).**

An agent choosing among lotteries $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ should pick the one that maximizes expected utility:

$$EU(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot U(x_i)$$

This implicitly assumes: (a) the agent knows all $p_i$ and $x_i$, (b) the agent can compute the sum without error, and (c) preferences are stable.

**Step 2 — Relax Assumption (a): Information Limit.**

If the agent knows only a *subset* of probabilities $\hat{p}_i$ (an estimate), then:

$$EU_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \hat{p}_i \cdot U(x_i)$$

Define the **estimation error** $\epsilon_i = \hat{p}_i - p_i$. Then:

$$EU_{actual} = EU_{true} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \epsilon_i \cdot U(x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Relax Assumption (b): Computational Limit.**

A real brain cannot compute this sum when $n$ is large. It uses a **heuristic shortcut**. The most common is the **Availability Heuristic** — replace probabilities with ease-of-recall:

$$\hat{p}_i \approx \frac{\text{Recall}(x_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Recall}(x_j)}$$

**Step 4 — Relax Assumption (c): Preference Stability.**

Reference-dependent preferences (Kahneman-Tversky, 1979) replace $U(x_i)$ with $v(x_i - r)$ where $r$ is the **reference point** (status quo or aspiration level $\bar{U}$):

$$v(x_i - r) = \begin{cases} (x_i - r)^{\alpha} & \text{if } x_i \geq r \\ -\lambda \vert x_i - r \vert^{\alpha} & \text{if } x_i < r \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Final Bounded-Rationality Decision Rule.**

$$L^* = \arg\max_{L} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w(\hat{p}_i) \cdot v(x_i - r)$$

This is the **Prospect-Theoretic Decision Rule under Bounded Rationality** — the formula real humans effectively use, even though they cannot write it down. The rational baseline (Step 1) is a special case where $w(p)=p$, $v=U$, $r=0$, and all information is perfect.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Simon's Cognitive Budget Optimization

**Step 1 — Define the trade-off.**

Let $n$ = number of alternatives examined. Then:

$$C_{search}(n) = k \cdot n, \quad k > 0 \quad \text{(linear search cost)}$$

$$C_{error}(n) = \dfrac{C_0}{n}, \quad C_0 > 0 \quad \text{(error cost falls with more search)}$$

**Step 2 — Total cognitive cost.**

$$C_{total}(n) = k \cdot n + \frac{C_0}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Minimize total cost (rational meta-choice).**

Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\,C_{total}}{d\,n} = k - \frac{C_0}{n^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for optimal $n^*$.**

$$k = \frac{C_0}{n^{*2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^{*2} = \frac{C_0}{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_0}{k}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

The **rational optimal search depth** is $\sqrt{C_0 / k}$, **not** infinity. This proves formally that even a rational optimizer, with finite time/cost, will stop searching before the global optimum is found. **This is the mathematical birth of Bounded Rationality.**

**Step 6 — Second-derivative check (confirms minimum).**

$$\frac{d^2 C_{total}}{d\,n^2} = \frac{2 C_0}{n^3} > 0 \quad \forall n > 0$$

Hence $n^* = \sqrt{C_0 / k}$ is a strict global minimum. Q.E.D.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Satisficing Threshold in a Search Sequence

**Step 1 — Set up the search process.**

Let $U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n$ be the utilities of options encountered in random order. They are i.i.d. from a distribution $F(U)$.

**Step 2 — The agent's stopping rule.**

Stop at the first $n$ such that $U_n \geq \bar{U}$, where $\bar{U}$ is the aspiration level.

**Step 3 — Probability that the search ends in exactly $n$ steps.**

This is the probability that the first $(n-1)$ options are *all* below $\bar{U}$ and the $n$-th is *at or above* $\bar{U}$:

$$P(N = n) = \left[1 - F(\bar{U})\right] \cdot \left[F(\bar{U})\right]^{n-1}$$

This is a **Geometric Distribution** with success probability $p^* = 1 - F(\bar{U})$.

**Step 4 — Expected search length.**

$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \frac{1}{p^*} = \frac{1}{1 - F(\bar{U})}$$

**Step 5 — Key implications.**

- As $\bar{U}$ rises (aspirations get higher), $\mathbb{E}[N]$ grows — the agent searches longer.
- As $\bar{U}$ falls to the population mean, $\mathbb{E}[N] \to 1$ — the agent settles immediately.
- The satisficing model predicts a **finite expected search time**, while the maximizing model predicts $\mathbb{E}[N] = \infty$.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 — Rationality vs. Bounded Rationality vs. Pure Heuristics**
> 
> | Dimension | Rational Model (Homo Economicus) | Bounded Rationality (Simon) | Pure Heuristic (Kahneman-Tversky) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Information** | Perfect, complete, free | Costly, partial, asymmetric | Distorted, biased, framing-dependent |
> | **Computation** | Unlimited, instantaneous | Finite cognitive budget | One-rule-of-thumb shortcuts |
> | **Objective** | Maximize $EU$ | Satisfice: $U \geq \bar{U}$ | Apply heuristic & stop |
> | **Time** | Infinite horizon | Finite, often rushed | Sub-second / System 1 |
> | **Preference** | Stable, complete, transitive | Adaptive aspiration level $\bar{U}$ | Reference-dependent, inconsistent |
> | **Risk** | Objective probability | Subjective probability | Probability-weighted, distorted |
> | **Emotion** | None | Acknowledged but constrained | Central driver (affect heuristic) |
> | **Best for KTU exam** | "Contrast" answer | "Main answer" | "Examples" answer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: The Bounded-Rationality Decision Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram shows how a real-world decision moves from the external world to the bounded brain and out to an action, highlighting every "limit" along the way.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EXTERNAL WORLD: Full option set X] --> B[PERCEPTION FILTER]
    B --> C[ATTENTION BOTTLENECK: 7 plus or minus 2 items]
    C --> D[WORKING MEMORY: limited capacity]
    D --> E[HEURISTIC PROCESSOR: System 1 / System 2]
    E --> F[SATISFICING TEST: U x greater than or equal to U bar]
    F -- YES --> G[ACTION: Choose x sub s]
    F -- NO --> H[SAMPLE NEXT OPTION: incur search cost]
    H --> E
    E --> I[POST-DECISION RATIONALIZATION]
    I --> J[LEARNING: adjust U bar upward or downward]
    J --> F
    A -. INFORMATION ASYMMETRY .-> B
    A -. TIME PRESSURE .-> C
    A -. EMOTIONAL PRIMING .-> E
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Figure 2: Cognitive Budget Optimization Curve (Total Cost vs. Search Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[Search Cost: C sub search of n] --> L[Total Cost: C sub total of n]
    M[Error Cost: C sub error of n] --> L
    L --> N[Minimum at n star equals sqrt of C sub 0 over k]
    N --> O[RATIONAL OPTIMAL SEARCH: bounded, finite]
    O --> P[IMPLICATION: Bounded Rationality is OPTIMAL, not irrational]
    style K fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Figure 3: Satisficing vs. Maximizing — The Stopping Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START: aspiration level U bar set] --> T[Sample option 1]
    T --> U1{Utility of option 1 greater than or equal to U bar}
    U1 -- YES --> V1[STOP: pick option 1]
    U1 -- NO --> T2[Sample option 2]
    T2 --> U2{Utility of option 2 greater than or equal to U bar}
    U2 -- YES --> V2[STOP: pick option 2]
    U2 -- NO --> T3[Continue until found or budget exhausted]
    T3 --> X[If budget exhausted: pick best-so-far]
    V1 --> Y[SATISFICING outcome: good enough, fast]
    V2 --> Y
    X --> Y
    style S fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style Y fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style X fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

    L1 -.-> M1[Knows all options and probs]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define "Bounded Rationality". Who introduced the concept, and how does it differ from the classical concept of rationality?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Bounded Rationality, introduced by **Herbert A. Simon in 1957**, is the concept that human decision-making is limited by the information available, the cognitive limitations of the mind, and the finite time available for decision-making.
>
> **Difference from classical rationality (2 marks):** Classical economics assumes the decision-maker is **omniscient** (knows all alternatives and probabilities), **infinitely fast** (computes the optimal choice), and **maximizes** expected utility. Bounded rationality argues that real humans use **simplified rules of thumb (heuristics)** and stop searching when an alternative is **"good enough"** (satisficing) rather than optimal. Hence behaviour is **intendedly rational but limitedly so**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**What is the difference between "Satisficing" and "Maximizing" behaviour? Give one real-life example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Satisficing (Simon, 1956):** The decision-maker searches for alternatives until one meets a pre-set **aspiration level** $\bar{U}$. The first option that crosses this threshold is selected. (1 mark)
>
> **Maximizing (Classical):** The decision-maker exhaustively searches through *all* possible alternatives and selects the one that yields the **highest utility**. (1 mark)
>
> **Example (1 mark):** When booking a hotel online, a satisficer picks the first 3-star hotel with rating ≥ 7.5 and price ≤ ₹3,000; a maximizer scrolls through 50 options to find the absolute cheapest with the highest rating. The satisficer is "bounded rational" — the maximizer violates the time/cost constraint.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Model Paper 1 Path**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Behavioural Economics Module 1]**

> **Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Bounded Rationality** proposed by Herbert Simon. Discuss the **three principal limits** of rationality with suitable real-world examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Distinguish between **Satisficing** and **Maximizing** strategies. Using a mathematical model, show that a bounded-rational agent minimizes the total cognitive cost $C_{total}(n) = kn + C_0/n$ at a finite search depth $n^* = \sqrt{C_0/k}$. Why is this a proof that Bounded Rationality is *optimal*? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Bounded Rationality & Three Limits (7 marks)

**1. Introduction to Bounded Rationality (2 marks):**
Herbert Simon introduced the concept in his seminal 1957 book *Administrative Behavior*. He argued that the classical economic model of a fully rational, utility-maximizing agent is unrealistic. Real human decision-makers are constrained by:
- Limited cognitive resources,
- Incomplete information, and
- Finite time.

Simon coined the term **"Satisficing"** to describe the strategy of choosing an option that is *good enough* rather than the absolute best.

**2. Three Principal Limits (4 marks):**

> **(i) Cognitive Limits (1.5 marks):** The human brain's working memory holds only **7 ± 2** items (Miller, 1956). Attention is a scarce resource. Example: A pilot in an emergency cockpit cannot read all 50 instrument readings — they rely on key indicators (heuristic).

> **(ii) Information Limits (1.5 marks):** Information is incomplete, asymmetric, and costly. Example: A used-car buyer cannot inspect every car's engine — the **"Market for Lemons"** (Akerlof, 1970) shows asymmetric information leads to market failure.

> **(iii) Time / Computational Limits (1 mark):** Real decisions must often be made quickly. Example: A trader in a flash crash has milliseconds to act, not hours.

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):** Bounded rationality does not imply irrationality — it implies *constrained optimization* under realistic human and environmental limitations.

#### Part (b) — Satisficing vs. Maximizing & Cognitive Budget Proof (7 marks)

**1. Satisficing vs. Maximizing (2 marks):**
- **Satisficing:** Stop when $U(x_n) \geq \bar{U}$ (aspiration level).
- **Maximizing:** Evaluate all $n$ options and pick $x^* = \arg\max U(x)$.

The satisficer is content with "good enough"; the maximizer demands the "best possible."

**2. Cognitive Cost Function (1 mark):**
Let $n$ = search depth. Define:
- $C_{search}(n) = kn$ (linear cost of examining options),
- $C_{error}(n) = C_0 / n$ (error cost decreases with more search),
- $C_{total}(n) = kn + C_0/n$.

**3. Derivation of Optimal $n^*$ (3 marks):**
Differentiate with respect to $n$:

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dn} = k - \frac{C_0}{n^2}$$

Set equal to zero:

$$k = \frac{C_0}{n^{*2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^{*2} = \frac{C_0}{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_0}{k}}$$

Second-derivative test:

$$\frac{d^2 C_{total}}{dn^2} = \frac{2C_0}{n^3} > 0$$

This confirms a **minimum** at $n^*$. Hence the optimal search depth is **finite and bounded** — exactly the central claim of Simon's theory.

**4. Why this proves optimality (1 mark):**
Because a fully rational agent, when forced to pay a cost for thinking, will *rationally choose* to limit the search. Bounded Rationality is therefore not a failure of rationality — it is **rationality under constraint**. This is Simon's most powerful insight.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Model Paper 2 Path)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Behavioural Economics Module 1]**

> **Q. (a)** What is meant by **"Limits of Rationality"**? Explain the **cognitive, informational, and emotional limits** with at least two examples each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Write a note on the **Prospect Theory** value function $v(x)$ and probability-weighting function $w(p)$ of Kahneman and Tversky. Show how these functions mathematically model **loss aversion** and the **certainty effect**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Limits of Rationality (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Limits of Rationality are the systematic constraints — cognitive, informational, and emotional — that prevent humans from making decisions as perfectly rational agents. They cause systematic, predictable deviations from the predictions of classical expected-utility theory.

**2. Cognitive Limits (2 marks):**
- Working memory cap of 7 ± 2 items (Miller, 1956).
- Example 1: A consumer cannot evaluate 20 insurance policies simultaneously — they compare only 2-3.
- Example 2: Surgeons under fatigue make more errors — a cognitive-budget limit.

**3. Informational Limits (2 marks):**
- Information is asymmetric (Akerlof) and costly to obtain.
- Example 1: A job applicant cannot know all salary offers in the market.
- Example 2: A patient cannot evaluate a doctor's true skill without specialist help.

**4. Emotional Limits (2 marks):**
- Fear, greed, regret, and over-confidence distort choices.
- Example 1: Panic-selling during a market crash (herd behaviour, loss aversion).
- Example 2: Holding a losing stock too long due to sunk-cost fallacy and hope.

#### Part (b) — Prospect Theory (7 marks)

**1. Value Function $v(x)$ (3 marks):**
Kahneman and Tversky (1979) proposed:

$$v(x) = \begin{cases} x^{\alpha}, & x \geq 0 \\ -\lambda \vert x \vert^{\alpha}, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

with $\alpha \approx 0.88$ and $\lambda \approx 2.25$.

**Key properties:**
- **Reference dependence:** outcomes are coded as gains or losses *relative to a reference point* (usually status quo).
- **Diminishing sensitivity:** $v''(x) < 0$ for $x > 0$ and $v''(x) > 0$ for $x < 0$ — concave in gains (risk aversion) and convex in losses (risk seeking).
- **Loss aversion:** $\lambda > 1$ — losses feel $\approx 2.25\times$ as painful as equivalent gains feel pleasant.

**2. Probability-Weighting Function $w(p)$ (2 marks):**
$$w(p) = \frac{p^{\gamma}}{(p^{\gamma} + (1-p)^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma}}, \quad \gamma \approx 0.61$$

**Key properties:**
- **Overweight small probabilities** (people buy lottery tickets, even when $E[V] < 0$).
- **Underweight moderate-to-high probabilities** (people under-insure).
- **Certainty effect:** $w(1) = 1$ but $w(p) < p$ for high $p$ — people over-weight *certainty* dramatically.

**3. Mathematical demonstration of loss aversion and certainty (2 marks):**
- A ₹100 gain and ₹100 loss yield $v(100) = 100^{0.88} \approx 41.7$, but $v(-100) = -2.25 \times 41.7 \approx -93.8$. The loss is **more than 2× the gain** in psychological impact.
- A lottery with $p = 0.99$ winning ₹100: $EU = 0.99 \times U(100) = 0.99 \times 100 = 99$ under rational weighting, but $VU = w(0.99) \cdot v(100) \approx 0.83 \times 41.7 \approx 34.6$ — people *demand* more than the rational value to accept it, illustrating the **certainty effect**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Don't write "irrational":** Bounded rationality is *not* irrationality. Examiners specifically look for the word "**bounded/limited**" or "**constrained**". Writing "irrational" → **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Always name the scholar:** Bounded Rationality → **Herbert Simon**; Heuristics & Biases → **Kahneman-Tversky**; Loss Aversion → **Kahneman-Tversky (1979)**. Missing names → **−0.5 to −1 mark per instance**.
> 3. **Differentiate Satisficing vs. Maximizing explicitly:** Examiners expect a 2-column comparison, not a one-line answer. Use the table format shown in Section 2.
> 4. **Use the math, but don't drown in it:** For a 7-mark derivation, 3-4 lines of algebra + 2-3 lines of interpretation is sufficient. Writing 20 lines of algebra without context → **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 5. **Cite *real-world examples*:** Examiners reward concrete examples (lunch decision, market for lemons, organ donation default). Pure theory without examples → **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Remember the parameters:** $\alpha = 0.88$, $\lambda = 2.25$, $\gamma = 0.61$ are *examiner-favourite* numbers for one-mark recall questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — LIMITS OF RATIONALITY**
>
> ✅ **Core Thesis:** Humans are **intendedly rational but limitedly so** (Simon, 1957).
> 
> ✅ **Three Limits:** Cognitive (7±2), Informational (asymmetric/costly), Temporal/Emotional (finite, biased).
> 
> ✅ **Two Strategies:** **Maximizing** (classical — pick best) vs. **Satisficing** (Simon — pick first option $\geq$ aspiration $\bar{U}$).
> 
> ✅ **Optimal Search Depth Formula:** $n^* = \sqrt{C_0 / k}$ — derived from $C_{total} = kn + C_0/n$.
> 
> ✅ **Search Rule:** Stop when $MC_{search} = MB_{info}$ (Stigler, 1961).
> 
> ✅ **Prospect Theory Parameters:** $\alpha = 0.88$, $\lambda = 2.25$ (loss aversion), $\gamma = 0.61$ (probability weighting).
> 
> ✅ **Probability Weighting:** Small $p$ overweighted; large $p$ underweighted; $p=1$ is overweighted (certainty effect).
> 
> ✅ **Key Scholars to Name:** Simon (1957), Miller (1956), Stigler (1961), Akerlof (1970), Kahneman-Tversky (1979), Thaler-Sunstein (2008 Nudge).
> 
> ✅ **Five Key Heuristics:** Availability, Representativeness, Anchoring, Affect, Recognition.
> 
> ✅ **Core Biases:** Loss aversion, status-quo, endowment, sunk-cost, herd, framing.
> 
> ✅ **Key Sentence for 14-mark answers:** *"Bounded Rationality is not a failure of rationality — it is **rationality under constraint**, and as Simon's optimization proof shows, it is the **rational response** to finite cognitive budgets and costly information."*
> 
> ✅ **One-line Definition to Memorize:** *Bounded Rationality = the property of an agent who behaves nearly optimally given the cognitive, informational, and temporal resources available to it (Simon, 1957).*
> 
> ✅ **Real-world examples to use in exam:** Lunch decision (satisficing), used-car market (lemons), organ donation default (nudge), flash crash (herd), insurance (probability weighting).
