topics.title: Bounded Rationality Heuristics and Bias: Representativeness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bounded Rationality Heuristics and Bias: Representativeness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bounded Rationality: The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bounded Rationality (Herbert A. Simon, 1957)**
> A model of human decision-making in which rationality is limited by the **information available**, the **cognitive limitations of the mind**, and the **finite time available** for deliberation. Decision-makers therefore act as **"satisficers"** rather than **"maximizers"** — they settle for a "good enough" choice rather than exhaustively computing the optimal one.

Simon was awarded the **Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 1978** specifically for this pioneering work that challenged the classical economic assumption of *homo economicus* (the perfectly rational economic agent).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Choice

Imagine you walk into an unfamiliar city and need to find dinner. A perfectly rational agent (homo economicus) would:
- Read every restaurant's full menu
- Cross-reference every dish with your dietary needs
- Calculate the precise cost-benefit of every option
- Read every online review

A **boundedly rational agent** does this instead:
- Glances at 2–3 nearby restaurants
- Picks the one that *looks* familiar and has a few people inside
- Orders a dish that *sounds* typical

This is not laziness — it is an **adaptive response to real-world constraints of time, information, and mental bandwidth**.

---

## 1.2 Heuristics: The Cognitive Shortcuts of Bounded Rationality

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic (Greek: heuriskein = "to discover")**
> A mental shortcut or rule-of-thumb that simplifies complex decisions by focusing on a subset of available cues. Heuristics are generally **useful** (fast, low-effort, often accurate) but are **systematically prone to predictable errors called biases**.

**Daniel Kahneman** and **Amos Tversky** (Nobel Prize 2002) identified three core heuristics in their landmark 1974 paper *"Judgment Under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases"*:

| # | Heuristic | Core Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Representativeness** | *"How typical / similar is this?"* |
| 2 | **Availability** | *"How easily can I recall an example?"* |
| 3 | **Anchoring & Adjustment** | *"What number was I first given?"* |

This note covers **Heuristic #1: Representativeness**.

---

## 1.3 Representativeness Heuristic — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Representativeness Heuristic (Kahneman & Tversky, 1972, 1973)**
> A cognitive shortcut in which people estimate the **probability** that an object *A* belongs to category *B*, or that event *A* originates from process *B*, by assessing the **degree to which *A* resembles (is "representative of") the prototype of *B***.

In simpler terms:
> *"If it looks like a duck, walks like a duck, and quacks like a duck — people will judge it overwhelmingly likely to be a duck, even when the base-rate statistics say ducks are vanishingly rare."*

The **representativeness heuristic** is essentially a **prototype-matching** algorithm run by System 1 (fast, automatic thinking).

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Overview — The "Looks-Like" Trap

### Real-World Example: The Investor and the Startup

A founder wearing a hoodie, speaking in Silicon Valley jargon, pitching from a garage, showing hockey-stick growth charts. Investors using **representativeness** will *immediately* classify this as a "high-potential tech startup" because it **resembles the prototype** they have seen in success stories like Apple, Google, Facebook.

They may completely ignore:
- The actual **base rate** of startup success (≈ 10%)
- The **sample size** (this is just one of 10,000 pitches this year)
- **Regression to the mean** (the next pitch will be statistically worse)

This is the **"looks-like" trap** — judging **likelihood by resemblance**, not by base-rate statistics.

---

## 1.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Economics

For KTU students, the relevance is enormous:

- **Managerial Decision-Making**: Project managers evaluating technology adoption
- **Consumer Behaviour**: Predicting how users will perceive a new product
- **Risk Assessment**: Engineers under-estimating rare-but-severe failure modes because "it has never happened before in our prototype"
- **Investment Decisions**: Venture capital, R&D portfolio choices

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations**
> Whenever a question asks "why do decision-makers deviate from rationality?" the **correct, mark-winning answer** is *bounded rationality leading to heuristic use, producing systematic biases* — not "people are irrational" or "people make mistakes."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Representativeness

The representativeness heuristic operates through **four sequential mental steps**:

### Step 1 — Prototype Activation
The brain retrieves an *internal prototype* (a mental schema) of the target category.
- Prototype of "doctor" → white coat, stethoscope, caring, scientifically trained.
- Prototype of "engineer" → calculator, technical drawings, problem-solving.

### Step 2 — Feature Extraction
Features of the object/event *A* are extracted and compared to the prototype.

### Step 3 — Similarity Scoring
A similarity score *S(A, B)* is computed (largely unconsciously).
- If Steve is shy, introverted, loves books → high *S(Steve, librarian)*

### Step 4 — Probability Estimation
The probability judgment *P(A ∈ B)* is set roughly proportional to *S(A, B)*:

$$P(A \in B) \approx f\bigl(S(A, B)\bigr)$$

Where *f* is a monotonically increasing function — **higher similarity → higher judged probability**.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Fundamental Flaw**
> This equation *omits* the base rate, the sample size, and the predictability of the event. The judge behaves as if:
> $$P(A \in B) \propto S(A, B) \quad \text{(WRONG — should also include prior } P(B) \text{ via Bayes' Theorem)}$$

---

## 2.2 The "Correct" Bayesian Benchmark

Bayes' Theorem (the normative standard) says:

$$P(B \mid A) \;=\; \frac{P(A \mid B)\cdot P(B)}{P(A)}$$

where the components are:

- $P(B \mid A)$ = posterior probability (what we want)
- $P(A \mid B)$ = likelihood (how representative *A* is of *B*)
- $P(B)$ = **base rate** (prior probability of *B*)
- $P(A)$ = evidence (normalizing constant)

Representativeness corresponds *only* to $P(A \mid B)$ — but humans treat it as if it were $P(B \mid A)$, **ignoring the base rate** $P(B)$.

---

## 2.3 The Five Major Biases Generated by Representativeness

| # | Bias Name | Definition | Canonical Experiment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base-Rate Neglect / Base-Rate Fallacy** | Ignoring statistical base rates when judging probability | Tversky & Kahneman (1980) cab problem |
| 2 | **Conjunction Fallacy** | Judging $P(A \cap B) > P(A)$ — impossible by set theory | Linda problem (Tversky & Kahneman, 1983) |
| 3 | **Insensitivity to Sample Size** | Treating small samples as representative of large ones | Tversky & Kahneman (1971) hospital problem |
| 4 | **Misconception of Regression** | Expecting extreme outcomes to persist rather than regress to the mean | Kahneman & Tversky (1973) flight training |
| 5 | **Gambler's Fallacy** | Believing deviations from the mean will be corrected in the short run | Coin toss sequences |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Ready — High-Yield Formulas for Representativeness & Bounded Rationality**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Engineering / Economic Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem (normative benchmark) | $P(B \vert A) = \dfrac{P(A \vert B)\cdot P(B)}{P(A)}$ | The correct way to update beliefs with evidence |
| 2 | Representativeness shortcut (heuristic) | $P_{heuristic}(B \vert A) \approx f\bigl(S(A, B)\bigr)$ | What humans actually do — ignore base rate |
| 3 | Law of Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \vert B_i)\cdot P(B_i)$ | Computing overall evidence probability |
| 4 | Conjunction Rule (always true) | $P(A \cap B) \le \min[P(A), P(B)]$ | Why judging $P(A\cap B) > P(A)$ is impossible |
| 5 | Satisficing rule (Simon) | Choose first alternative where $V(x) \geq V^*$ | Bounded rationality — accept "good enough" |
| 6 | Aspiration level dynamics | $V^*_{t+1} = V^*_t + \alpha (V_{achieved} - V^*_t)$ | Adaptive aspiration level adjustment |
| 7 | Regression to the mean | $E[X_{n+1}] = \bar{X} + \rho(X_n - \bar{X})$ | Extreme values are partially random; they fade |
| 8 | Expected Utility (normative) | $EU = \sum_i p_i \cdot U(x_i)$ | Rational economic choice baseline |
| 9 | Prospect Theory value (Kahneman-Tversky 1979) | $V(x) = \begin{cases} x^\alpha & x \ge 0 \\ -\lambda(-x)^\beta & x < 0 \end{cases}$ | Descriptive model: $\alpha, \beta \approx 0.88$; $\lambda \approx 2.25$ |
| 10 | Probability Weighting | $w(p) = \dfrac{p^\gamma}{(p^\gamma + (1-p)^\gamma)^{1/\gamma}}$ | Overweighting of small probabilities; $\gamma \approx 0.61$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize formulas (1), (2), (4), and (7). These appear almost every year in either Part A or Part B questions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Marketing** | Brand managers design products to *resemble* successful prototypes (e.g., iPhone-clone designs) |
| **Finance** | Algorithmic trading exploits representativeness biases in retail investors |
| **Public Policy** | Risk communication must combat representativeness bias (e.g., vaccine fears after vivid adverse-event news) |
| **Product Design** | UX designers ensure new software feels "familiar" — leveraging prototype recognition |
| **Project Management** | Avoiding the "this worked at Google, so it'll work for us" fallacy |
| **Cybersecurity** | Phishing emails exploit representativeness — they *look like* legitimate bank emails |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example A — The Linda Problem (Conjunction Fallacy)

> **Setup (Tversky & Kahneman, 1983):**
> *Linda is 31 years old, single, outspoken, and majored in philosophy. As a student, she was deeply concerned with issues of discrimination and social justice, and she also participated in anti-nuclear demonstrations.*
>
> Which is **more probable**?
> (1) Linda is a bank teller.
> (2) Linda is a bank teller **and** is active in the feminist movement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Translate to set notation**

Let:
- $T$ = event that Linda is a bank teller
- $F$ = event that Linda is active in the feminist movement

The two options are:
- Option 1: $P(T)$
- Option 2: $P(T \cap F)$

**Step 2 — Apply the conjunction rule from set theory**

For any two events $T$ and $F$:

$$P(T \cap F) \;\le\; \min\bigl[P(T),\; P(F)\bigr] \;\le\; P(T)$$

This is because the intersection of two sets is a **subset of each set**, so it cannot be larger than either parent set.

**Step 3 — Compute the strict ordering**

$$P(T \cap F) \;\le\; P(T)$$

Therefore **Option 1 is strictly more probable (or equal)**. Option 2 cannot be more probable.

**Step 4 — Observe the empirical result**

In Kahneman & Tversky's original experiment, **85% of respondents** chose Option 2 — directly violating the conjunction rule. This is the **Conjunction Fallacy**.

**Step 5 — Explain via representativeness**

The descriptive biography of Linda is **highly representative of the feminist activist prototype**. The mental process:

$$P_{representativeness}(Linda \in \text{"feminist bank teller"}) \approx S_{high}$$

$$P_{representativeness}(Linda \in \text{"bank teller"}) \approx S_{low}$$

$$S_{high} > S_{low} \;\Longrightarrow\; P_{representativeness}(T \cap F) > P_{representativeness}(T)$$

But **representativeness ≠ probability**. The conjunction fallacy is the direct consequence of this confusion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — The Cab Problem (Base-Rate Neglect)

> **Setup (Tversky & Kahneman, 1980):**
> A cab was involved in a hit-and-run accident at night. Two cab companies operate:
> - **Green Cab**: 85% of the cabs in the city
> - **Blue Cab**: 15% of the cabs in the city
>
> A witness identifies the cab as **blue**. The court tested the witness's reliability and found that the witness correctly identifies the color **80% of the time** (and is wrong 20%).
>
> **Question:** What is the probability that the cab was actually **blue**?

### Step-by-Step Bayesian Solution

**Step 1 — Define events and probabilities**

Let:
- $B$ = the cab is blue
- $G$ = the cab is green
- $W_B$ = witness says "blue"
- $W_G$ = witness says "green"

Given:
- $P(B) = 0.15$ (base rate of blue cabs)
- $P(G) = 0.85$ (base rate of green cabs)
- $P(W_B \vert B) = 0.80$ (witness correctly identifies blue)
- $P(W_B \vert G) = 0.20$ (witness mis-identifies green as blue)

**Step 2 — Compute $P(W_B)$ via total probability**

$$P(W_B) \;=\; P(W_B \vert B)\cdot P(B) \;+\; P(W_B \vert G)\cdot P(G)$$

$$P(W_B) \;=\; (0.80)(0.15) \;+\; (0.20)(0.85)$$

$$P(W_B) \;=\; 0.12 \;+\; 0.17 \;=\; 0.29$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem**

$$P(B \vert W_B) \;=\; \frac{P(W_B \vert B)\cdot P(B)}{P(W_B)}$$

$$P(B \vert W_B) \;=\; \frac{(0.80)(0.15)}{0.29} \;=\; \frac{0.12}{0.29}$$

$$P(B \vert W_B) \;\approx\; 0.4138 \;\approx\; \mathbf{41.4\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result**

Even though the witness says "blue" with 80% reliability, the probability that the cab is **actually blue is only 41.4%**. This is **lower than the witness's stated reliability** because the **base rate of blue cabs (15%) is very low**.

**Step 5 — The Representativeness Error**

The naïve (representativeness-based) answer that most people give is:

$$P(B \vert W_B) \approx P(W_B \vert B) = 0.80 \quad \text{(WRONG)}$$

This is because the witness's identification **looks representative of** "the cab being blue", so people substitute the **likelihood** $P(W_B \vert B)$ for the **posterior** $P(B \vert W_B)$, **completely ignoring the base rate**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — Insensitivity to Sample Size

> **Setup (Tversky & Kahneman, 1971):**
> A hospital has two wards: a small one with **15 births per day** and a large one with **45 births per day**. Over a year, in **each ward separately**, the percentage of days with **> 60% boys** is recorded.
>
> **Question:** In which ward is this percentage **higher**?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply statistical theory**

By the law of large numbers, the variance of the sample mean decreases as $n$ increases:

$$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{X}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

So a **smaller sample** (the small ward, $n=15$) will have **higher variance** and therefore a higher probability of producing extreme outcomes like "more than 60% boys".

**Step 2 — Expected proportion of "boy-heavy" days**

- Small ward ($n=15$): approximately **10%** of days exceed 60% boys
- Large ward ($n=45$): approximately **5%** of days exceed 60% boys

**Step 3 — The representativeness error**

People using representativeness assume both wards have the **same probability** of any given boy/girl split — because they imagine a "prototype day" where the proportion is 50/50. They **ignore sample size**.

**Step 4 — Key takeaway**

Insensitivity to sample size is a direct consequence of judging outcomes by how closely they match the prototype (50/50) rather than by considering the actual statistical distribution of sample proportions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Misconceptions of Regression

> **Setup (Kahneman, 2011 — Israeli flight instructors):**
> A flight instructor praises a student after an exceptionally good landing. On the next flight, the student performs worse. The instructor concludes that *"praise harms performance"*.
>
> He then criticizes a student after an exceptionally bad landing. On the next flight, the student performs better. The instructor concludes *"criticism improves performance"*.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify the underlying statistical reality**

Performance on any given flight is partly due to skill and partly due to random noise. An **extreme outcome** (very good or very bad) is more likely to be followed by a **less extreme outcome** — this is **regression to the mean**.

$$\text{Expected next score} \;=\; \bar{X} \;+\; \rho \cdot (X_{current} - \bar{X})$$

where $0 < \rho < 1$ is the correlation between successive attempts.

**Step 2 — Decompose the observed pattern**

- Praising after good performance → noise caused the good performance → next performance regresses down → looks like praise "caused" decline.
- Criticizing after bad performance → noise caused the bad performance → next performance regresses up → looks like criticism "caused" improvement.

**Step 3 — Identify the representativeness error**

The instructor judges the **cause** by **how representative** the post-feedback performance looks ("low after praise" feels representative of "praise failed"). In reality, the regression is a **statistical artifact**, not a causal effect.

**Step 4 — Engineering analogy**

In quality control, this is why a "corrective action" taken after an extreme defect rate often appears to "work" — the rate would have reverted to baseline regardless. Failure to recognize this leads to **illusory correlations** and bad engineering decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map: Bounded Rationality → Heuristics → Biases

This diagram traces the conceptual flow from Simon's bounded rationality framework through Kahneman & Tversky's heuristics to the specific biases produced by representativeness.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SIMON 1957\nBounded Rationality"] --> B["Limited Info\nLimited Time\nLimited Cognition"]
    B --> C["Decision-Maker as SATISFICER\n(accept good enough)"]
    C --> D["Use of HEURISTICS\n(mental shortcuts)"]
    D --> E1["Representativeness\nHow typical?"]
    D --> E2["Availability\nHow recallable?"]
    D --> E3["Anchoring\nAdjustment from start"]
    E1 --> F1["Base-Rate Neglect"]
    E1 --> F2["Conjunction Fallacy"]
    E1 --> F3["Insensitivity to Sample Size"]
    E1 --> F4["Misconception of Regression"]
    E1 --> F5["Gamblers Fallacy"]
    F1 --> G["SYSTEMATIC ERRORS\nin Judgment & Decision-Making"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Representativeness vs. Bayesian Reasoning

This block diagram shows the contrast between the **heuristic (descriptive) path** and the **Bayesian (normative) path** that a rational agent should follow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HEURISTIC["HEURISTIC PATH (What humans do)"]
        H1["Observe Event A"] --> H2["Compare A to Prototype of B"]
        H2 --> H3["Compute Similarity Score S(A,B)"]
        H3 --> H4["Output: P(B|A) approx f(S)"]
    end
    subgraph BAYESIAN["BAYESIAN PATH (What is correct)"]
        N1["Observe Event A"] --> N2["Retrieve Base Rate P(B)"]
        N2 --> N3["Compute Likelihood P(A|B)"]
        N3 --> N4["Compute Total Evidence P(A)"]
        N4 --> N5["Apply Bayes Theorem"]
        N5 --> N6["Output: P(B|A) = P(A|B)P(B) / P(A)"]
    end
    H4 -. IGNORES BASE RATE .-> X["BIASED JUDGMENT"]
    N6 --> Y["NORMATIVE JUDGMENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The heuristic path entirely skips the **base rate node** (N2), which is the structural source of representativeness bias. This is the most common diagram to draw in KTU Part B questions worth 7+ marks.

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Map: The Five Biases of Representativeness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["REPRESENTATIVENESS HEURISTIC"] --> B1["Base-Rate Neglect"]
    R --> B2["Conjunction Fallacy"]
    R --> B3["Insensitivity to Sample Size"]
    R --> B4["Misconception of Regression"]
    R --> B5["Gamblers Fallacy"]
    B1 --> EX1["Cab Problem 1980"]
    B2 --> EX2["Linda Problem 1983"]
    B3 --> EX3["Hospital Ward Problem 1971"]
    B4 --> EX4["Flight Instructor Study"]
    B5 --> EX5["Coin Toss Sequences"]
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flow: Representativeness in a Hiring Decision

A practical engineering application showing how representativeness operates in a real managerial decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Recruiter Sees Candidate CV"] --> P1["Activate Prototype:\n'Ideal Engineer'"]
    P1 --> P2["Extract Features:\nIIT degree, coding contest winner,\nspeaks about ML"]
    P2 --> P3["Compute Similarity:\nHIGH match to Ideal Engineer prototype"]
    P3 --> J1["Judgment:\n'Candidate is excellent'\nHIGH PROBABILITY"]
    J1 --> M["Mental Shortcut:\nSkip base-rate analysis,\nskip sample-of-one validation,\nskip regression awareness"]
    M --> D["DECISION: HIRE\n(possibly biased)"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Define bounded rationality. Explain how it leads to the use of heuristics in decision-making.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bounded rationality, proposed by **Herbert Simon (1957)**, is a model of decision-making that recognizes real-world limitations on the rationality of human agents: **limited information**, **limited cognitive capacity**, and **limited time**.

Because perfect optimization is impossible, decision-makers use **heuristics** — mental shortcuts that simplify complex judgments by focusing on a small number of salient cues. These heuristics generally produce fast, accurate-enough decisions but can also generate **systematic biases** such as representativeness, availability, and anchoring.

*(Word count ≈ 70. Marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for Simon reference, 1 for linking to heuristics.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**What is the representativeness heuristic? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **representativeness heuristic**, identified by **Kahneman and Tversky (1972)**, is a mental shortcut in which people judge the probability of an event or the category membership of an object by **how closely it resembles a typical prototype** of that category, rather than by computing actual probabilities or considering base rates.

**Example:** On seeing a quiet, introverted person who loves reading, observers typically judge them to be a librarian rather than a lawyer or farmer — because they resemble the **stereotype of a librarian**, even though lawyers outnumber librarians in most populations.

*(Word count ≈ 80. Marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for Kahneman-Tversky reference, 1 for example.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has **internal choice**. You must answer EITHER Option (a) OR Option (b) in full (7 + 7 marks). Choosing both wastes time.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **bounded rationality** as proposed by Herbert Simon. Discuss its **limitations on classical economic rationality** and the implications for managerial decision-making. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **representativeness heuristic** in detail. Explain **any three biases** that arise from the use of this heuristic, with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

### Part (a) — Bounded Rationality (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Bounded rationality is a model of human decision-making, introduced by **Herbert A. Simon (1957)**, which holds that decision-makers are **intentionally rational** but only within the **bounds imposed by:**
- (i) **Information availability** — they rarely possess complete information about all alternatives and outcomes.
- (ii) **Cognitive limitations** — the human mind has finite processing capacity and memory.
- (iii) **Time constraints** — most decisions must be made under deadlines.

**Contrast with Classical Rationality (2 marks):** Classical economics assumes **homo economicus** — a decision-maker who:
- Knows all alternatives
- Knows all consequences
- Has stable, well-ordered preferences
- Selects the utility-maximizing option

Simon showed that real humans cannot satisfy these conditions. Instead, they act as **satisficers**: they search until they find an alternative that meets an **aspiration level** $V^*$, then stop searching.

**Satisficing Formalism (1 mark):** Choose first alternative where $V(x) \geq V^*$, where $V^*$ is the aspiration level adapted over time:

$$V^*_{t+1} = V^* + \alpha(V_{achieved} - V^*)$$

**Implications for Managerial Decision-Making (3 marks):**

1. **Decentralization is necessary** — managers cannot centralize all information; bounded rationality justifies *bounded* organizational hierarchies.
2. **Procedures and routines emerge** — Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are organizational heuristics that compensate for individual cognitive limits.
3. **Incrementalism prevails** — Managers rarely make revolutionary decisions; they muddle through with **mixed scanning** and **successive limited comparisons** (Lindblom's "science of muddling through").
4. **Information systems design** — Good MIS design reduces cognitive load on managers by presenting relevant, filtered information.
5. **Satisficing vs. optimizing** — Managers must accept that "good enough" decisions, executed well, often outperform optimal decisions delayed indefinitely.

---

### Part (b) — Representativeness Heuristic & Three Biases (7 marks)

**Definition of Representativeness (1 mark):** As defined by **Kahneman and Tversky (1972)**, the representativeness heuristic is a mental shortcut whereby people judge the probability that *A* belongs to category *B* by the **degree to which *A* resembles the prototype of *B***. Formally, people behave as if:

$$P_{heuristic}(B \vert A) \approx f\bigl(S(A, B)\bigr)$$

ignoring the base rate $P(B)$.

**Bias 1 — Conjunction Fallacy (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Judging $P(A \cap B) > P(A)$, which violates the conjunction rule $P(A \cap B) \le P(A)$.
- **Example (Linda Problem, 1983):** Linda is described as a philosophy major concerned with social justice. Asked whether she is more likely to be (i) a bank teller or (ii) a bank teller **and** an active feminist — **85% of respondents** chose (ii), violating logic. Option (i) is strictly more probable.

**Bias 2 — Base-Rate Neglect (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Ignoring or under-weighting statistical base rates when judging probability.
- **Example (Cab Problem, 1980):** Witness says a cab is blue with 80% reliability; the base rate of blue cabs is only 15%. The correct Bayesian posterior is **41.4%**, but most people answer **80%**, treating the witness's reliability as the answer.

**Bias 3 — Insensitivity to Sample Size (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Treating small samples as equally informative as large samples.
- **Example (Hospital Problem, 1971):** People judge that a small ward and a large ward have an equal chance of having >60% boys on a given day. Statistically, the **smaller ward has more extreme outcomes** because $\mathrm{Var}(\bar X) = \sigma^2/n$ is larger.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **three major heuristics** identified by Kahneman and Tversky (1974): representativeness, availability, and anchoring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyse the **conjunction fallacy** and the **misconception of regression** as illustrations of how representativeness produces **systematic deviation from rational choice**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

### Part (a) — Three Heuristics Comparison (7 marks)

| Dimension | **Representativeness** | **Availability** | **Anchoring** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mental Question** | *"How typical is this?"* | *"How easily can I recall it?"* | *"What was my starting number?"* |
| **What is judged** | Probability / category membership | Frequency / probability | Numerical estimate |
| **Cognitive shortcut** | Prototype matching | Memory retrieval | Insufficient adjustment from an initial value |
| **Key bias produced** | Conjunction fallacy, base-rate neglect | Recency bias, vividness bias | Insufficient adjustment, conservatism |
| **Canonical experiment** | Linda problem (1983) | "Letters R as first vs. third" (1973) | Wheel-of-fortune estimation (1974) |
| **What is ignored** | Base rates, sample size | True underlying frequency | New information beyond the anchor |

**Similarities (1 mark):** All three are **System 1 cognitive shortcuts**, evolved to reduce cognitive load. All produce **fast, intuitive judgments** that are often **adaptive but systematically biased** in well-defined conditions.

**Differences (1 mark):** They differ in **what cognitive cue they exploit** — similarity to prototype (representativeness), ease of recall (availability), or starting point (anchoring). The corrective intervention is different for each: more base-rate awareness, debiasing via checklists, or considering the opposite direction of adjustment.

**Why They Matter in Engineering Economics (2 marks):** Engineers, managers, and consumers use all three unconsciously. For example:
- **Representativeness** biases technology-adoption decisions (new tech "looks like" previous winners)
- **Availability** biases risk perception (a recent accident inflates perceived risk of all similar technologies)
- **Anchoring** biases cost estimation (initial budget proposals stick despite new data)

**Synthesis:** Together, these three heuristics explain the bulk of **predictable irrationality** documented in behavioural economics research.

---

### Part (b) — Conjunction Fallacy & Misconception of Regression (7 marks)

**Conjunction Fallacy (3.5 marks):**

**Theoretical Foundation:** The conjunction rule, derived from the axioms of probability, states that for any two events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \cap B) \;\le\; \min\bigl[P(A),\, P(B)\bigr]$$

**The Fallacy:** When people judge $P(A \cap B) > P(A)$, they commit the conjunction fallacy.

**The Linda Problem (Tversky & Kahneman, 1983):** Linda is described with features **representative of a feminist activist** (philosophy major, anti-nuclear demonstrations). When asked whether she is more likely to be a bank teller or a **bank teller and feminist**, 85% chose the conjunction — which is logically less probable. The reason: the description **resembles the feminist prototype** more strongly than it resembles the bank-teller prototype.

**Representativeness as Cause:** The representativeness heuristic treats similarity to a prototype as a direct proxy for probability, **bypassing probability calculus entirely**.

**Consequences:** Erroneous risk assessment in engineering (judging two independent rare events as more probable together than alone), and misjudgments in financial forecasting.

---

**Misconception of Regression (3.5 marks):**

**Theoretical Foundation:** Any measurement is composed of signal + noise:

$$X_i = \mu + \text{signal}_i + \epsilon_i$$

When we observe an extreme value, it is likely that the **noise** was unusually large. On the next measurement, the noise is unlikely to be as extreme, so the value **regresses toward the mean** $\mu$.

**The Fallacy:** People fail to anticipate regression and therefore construct **spurious causal narratives** to explain it.

**Flight Instructor Example (Kahneman, 2011):** Praise after a good flight is "followed" by a worse flight; criticism after a bad flight is "followed" by a better flight. The instructor infers that **praise hurts, criticism helps** — but the pattern is just regression.

**Representativeness as Cause:** The observed pattern (low after praise) **looks representative of** "the praise failed" — so the mind assigns a causal story that matches the resemblance rather than the underlying statistical reality.

**Consequences:** Bad management practices (over-rewarding mediocre performance to "maintain" excellence), superstitious behaviours in sports ("lucky socks"), and misdiagnosis of quality-control issues in manufacturing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse bounded rationality with irrationality.** Bounded rationality is **intentionally rational within limits**, not random behaviour. Examiners deduct marks for saying "people are irrational".
>
> 2. **Always cite Kahneman & Tversky (1972, 1973, 1983)** and **Herbert Simon (1957)** when discussing heuristics. Vague references like "psychologists have shown..." lose a mark.
>
> 3. **In Bayesian problems**, show **every step of the calculation** — base rate, likelihood, total probability, posterior. Skipping the total-probability step is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks on the cab problem.
>
> 4. **For the conjunction fallacy**, do not forget the formal inequality $P(A \cap B) \le P(A)$. Examiners reward formal precision.
>
> 5. **Distinguish clearly between the five biases.** Students often mix up base-rate neglect with conjunction fallacy. Remember: conjunction fallacy is about **violating set-theory rules**; base-rate neglect is about **ignoring prior probabilities**.
>
> 6. **Always draw a diagram** (flowchart, hierarchy, or Bayes' tree) for any 7-mark question. Visual representation earns 1–2 marks even if the text is incomplete.
>
> 7. **Avoid colloquial language** like "people are stupid" or "humans are dumb". Use professional behavioural-economics terminology: "systematic bias," "predictable deviation," "heuristic substitution."

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [ ] **Bounded Rationality** = Simon (1957). Decision-makers are **satisficers**, not maximizers, due to limits on **information, cognition, and time**.
- [ ] **Heuristics** are mental shortcuts, evolved to be **fast and frugal**, but systematically biased in well-defined conditions.
- [ ] **Kahneman & Tversky (1974)** identified three core heuristics: **Representativeness, Availability, Anchoring**.
- [ ] **Representativeness Heuristic** = judging probability by **how much A resembles prototype of B**. Formal shortcut: $P(B \vert A) \approx f(S(A,B))$, **ignoring base rate**.
- [ ] **Bayes' Theorem (normative):** $P(B \vert A) = \dfrac{P(A \vert B)\cdot P(B)}{P(A)}$ — the correct way to update beliefs.
- [ ] **Five biases of representativeness:**
  - [ ] (i) **Base-rate neglect** — ignoring $P(B)$; cab problem answer is 41.4%, not 80%
  - [ ] (ii) **Conjunction fallacy** — judging $P(A \cap B) > P(A)$; Linda problem; 85% error rate
  - [ ] (iii) **Insensitivity to sample size** — small samples appear more extreme
  - [ ] (iv) **Misconception of regression** — extreme outcomes regress toward mean
  - [ ] (v) **Gambler's fallacy** — believing deviations must be corrected
- [ ] **Conjunction Rule (set theory):** $P(A \cap B) \le \min[P(A), P(B)]$ — **always true, never violated**.
- [ ] **Regression to the Mean:** Expected next value $= \bar{X} + \rho(X_{current} - \bar{X})$.
- [ ] **Representative bias arises because System 1 (fast thinking) substitutes similarity for probability**, bypassing System 2 (slow, deliberate) Bayesian reasoning.
- [ ] **Real-world relevance:** Hiring, investment, product adoption, risk perception, project management, consumer behaviour.
- [ ] **Exam golden rules:**
  - [ ] Always cite authors and years (Simon 1957; Kahneman-Tversky 1972, 1973, 1983)
  - [ ] Always show full Bayesian calculations
  - [ ] Always include at least one diagram in a 7-mark question
  - [ ] Never use the word "irrational" — use "boundedly rational" or "systematically biased"
- [ ] **Nobel Prizes to remember:** Simon (1978) — bounded rationality; Kahneman (2002) — heuristics and biases (Tversky died in 1996 and was ineligible; his contribution is widely recognized).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biases Resulting from Representativeness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biases Resulting from Representativeness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Representativeness Heuristic** is a cognitive mental shortcut (a "rule of thumb") originally formalised by **Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky (1972, 1973)** in their seminal work on heuristics and biases under the broader framework of **Prospect Theory** and the heuristics-and-biases programme of behavioural economics.

When asked to assess the probability that an object or event **A** belongs to a category **B**, or that event **A** originated from process **B**, individuals typically rely on the **representativeness heuristic** — they evaluate the probability by the degree to which **A** resembles (i.e., is *representative of*) the essential characteristics of **B**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT866 – Module 1):**
> *"Representativeness refers to the process by which people judge the probability of an event occurring based on how similar or representative it is of a broader category, often leading to systematic deviations from the normative laws of probability."*

In normative terms (under classical expected utility and Bayesian probability), the probability $P(A \in B)$ is governed by **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(A \in B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot \dfrac{P(A)}{P(B)}$$

The representativeness heuristic, however, ignores the **prior probability** $P(A)$ (the base rate) and often misjudges $P(B \mid A)$ by superficial similarity matching.

## 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Mango in a Basket"**
>
> Imagine you are shown a single yellow, sweet-smelling fruit and asked: *"Is this a mango or a pineapple?"* Even if mangoes are rare in your city (only 1 in 100 fruits imported) and pineapples are common (99 in 100), your brain automatically says **"Mango!"** because the fruit *looks* and *smells* like a mango. You just **ignored the base rate** (rarity) and used pure **resemblance** as a proxy for probability.
>
> This is exactly the representativeness heuristic at work: **resemblance replaces rational probability.**

## 1.3 The Six Core Biases Under Representativeness

Kahneman and Tversky identified **six systematic biases** that result from over-reliance on representativeness:

| # | Bias | One-line Definition |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Base Rate Neglect** | Ignoring prior probabilities when judging likelihood |
| 2 | **Insensitivity to Sample Size** | Treating small samples as if they were large |
| 3 | **Misconceptions of Chance** | Believing chance will self-correct (Gambler's fallacy) |
| 4 | **Insensitivity to Predictability** | Over-confidence in non-existent patterns |
| 5 | **Illusion of Validity** | Over-trusting consistent, coherent stereotypes |
| 6 | **Misconception of Regression** | Failing to recognise regression to the mean |

These six biases form the spine of **Module 1** in the KTU 2024 PECBT866 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Representativeness Heuristic

The heuristic operates in **four sequential cognitive steps**:

1. **Categorical Identification** — The brain identifies a *prototype* (a "typical" member) of category B by retrieving salient, schema-consistent features.
2. **Feature Matching** — The brain performs a similarity test: how many of A's features overlap with the prototype of B?
3. **Probability Substitution** — The matched-similarity score is *substituted* for the true probability. The more A "looks like" B, the higher $P(A \in B)$ is judged.
4. **Inhibition Failure** — Diagnostic, base-rate, and statistical evidence are *suppressed* because the resemblance answer feels intuitively correct.

> [!NOTE]
> The substitution of *resemblance* for *probability* is itself a manifestation of a deeper cognitive law, the **Attribute Substitution Principle** (Kahneman & Frederick, 2002): when a target attribute (true probability) is hard to compute, the brain swaps in a heuristic attribute (similarity).

## 2.2 The Six Biases — Detailed Logic & "Why"

### 2.2.1 Base Rate Fallacy (Neglect of Prior Probabilities)
- **Why it happens:** When a vivid, *descriptive* description of a person (e.g., "Linda — 31, single, outspoken, philosophy major") is highly representative of a *specific* category (e.g., "feminist bank teller"), people ignore the **base rate** of that category in the population.
- **Classic Experiment — "Linda the Bank Teller" (1983):** 85% of respondents violated the **conjunction rule** by judging $P(\text{feminist bank teller}) > P(\text{bank teller})$, which is impossible since the intersection cannot exceed either set.
- **Real-world utility:** Critical in medical diagnosis, security screening, and risk management — diagnostic tests' false positive rates depend heavily on disease prevalence (base rate).

### 2.2.2 Insensitivity to Sample Size
- **Why it happens:** The representativeness heuristic treats a sample of size $n = 10$ drawn from a population as *just as representative* as a sample of size $n = 1000$. The law of large numbers is cognitively suppressed.
- **Engineering example:** A quality engineer who inspects 5 chips and concludes the entire batch is defective behaves identically to one who inspects 5000 chips, under the spell of representativeness.
- **Standard deviation of sample mean** scales as $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ — small $n$ means **larger statistical uncertainty**, which the heuristic ignores.

### 2.2.3 Misconceptions of Chance (Gambler's Fallacy)
- **Why it happens:** Because a *fair* sequence of coin tosses is *representative* of randomness (i.e., it should show a 50-50 split in any short window), deviations from the expected proportion are judged as *unlikely*. Hence "red has come up 7 times in a row on the roulette wheel, so black is *due*."
- **Reality:** Each spin is **independent**. The probability of red on the next spin remains $\approx 0.486$ (accounting for the green zero), regardless of history.
- **Mirror error — Hot-Hand Fallacy:** The opposite error in basketball, where a player on a "streak" is judged *more* likely to score the next shot, again treating streaks as *more* representative of skill than chance.

### 2.2.4 Insensitivity to Predictability
- **Why it happens:** When people are given a description of a company and asked to predict its future earnings, they anchor on the *representativeness* of the description (e.g., a "high-tech, aggressive, adaptive" firm) and predict *very high* growth — even when the actual correlation between such descriptions and earnings is essentially **zero**.
- **Insight:** The more *vivid* and *coherent* a description, the more confident the prediction — yet the more confidently wrong it can be.

### 2.2.5 Illusion of Validity
- **Why it happens:** Predictions and judgements that feel *coherent* and *internally consistent* are perceived as more valid, even if the underlying data is noisy or the predictor variables are weak. A stockbroker with a "winning streak" feels his skill is real — but if the streak is short, it is statistically indistinguishable from chance.
- **Philosopher's stone:** Coherence is a property of the *narrative*, not of the *predictive accuracy*.

### 2.2.6 Misconception of Regression (Failure to Recognise Regression to the Mean)
- **Why it happens:** Because an extreme performance is *not representative* of the average, it should "regress" toward the mean on the next trial. People do not expect this — they expect extremes to persist (e.g., "the star pilot just had a bad day" or "the struggling student finally got lucky").
- **Sir Francis Galton's classic finding (1886):** Tall parents have, on average, slightly shorter children; very short parents have, on average, slightly taller children — this is **regression to mediocrity**, now termed *regression to the mean*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sir Francis Galton's Coefficient of Regression:** Galton quantified the relationship as
> $$\rho_{XY} = r$$
> where $r$ is the correlation between parent and offspring traits, and the offspring trait regresses toward the mean by a factor of $(1 - r^2)$ in variance.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Key Equations for Representativeness Biases**

| Concept | Formula | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---------|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Bayes' Theorem (Normative) | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Correct updating of probability | Contrast with heuristic |
| Conjunction Rule (Normative) | $P(A \cap B) \leq \min[P(A), P(B)]$ | Joint probability cannot exceed individual | Defied in Linda problem |
| Sample Mean | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Average of $n$ observations | Sample size effect |
| Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Variability of $\bar{X}$ shrinks as $\sqrt{n}$ | Ignored in small samples |
| Law of Large Numbers | $\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\vert \bar{X}_n - \mu \vert < \varepsilon) = 1$ | Sample mean converges to population mean | Cognitively underweighted |
| Regression to Mean | $E[Y \mid X = x] = \rho\,x + (1-\rho)\mu$ | Extreme $X$ values pull $Y$ toward $\mu$ | Often misjudged |
| Variance of Regression | $\text{Var}(Y \mid X) = \sigma_Y^2 (1 - \rho^2)$ | Uncertainty remains | Overlooked |
| Hot-Hand Index | $\Pr(\text{hit} \mid \text{prev. hit}) = \Pr(\text{hit})$ | Should equal base rate for independent trials | Violated in streak beliefs |

> **Note on Notation:** All conditional probability symbols use the standard vertical bar `|`. Inside table cells, the $\vert$ notation is used (as instructed) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Across Engineering & Economics

- **Finance:** Stock brokers' illusion of validity → market bubbles and crashes.
- **Project Management:** PMs overestimate project success based on "enthusiastic" team descriptions (insensitivity to predictability).
- **Quality Control:** Engineers misjudge process capability from tiny pilot samples (insensitivity to sample size).
- **Medical AI:** Diagnostic algorithms suffer from base rate neglect when screening for rare diseases.
- **Cybersecurity:** Threat analysts may ignore base rates of vulnerabilities, focusing only on dramatic attack patterns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conjunction Fallacy & Bayes' Boundary

We derive the **normative boundary** that the **Linda problem** violates.

**Step 1.** Define two events:
- $F$ = "Linda is a feminist"
- $B$ = "Linda is a bank teller"

**Step 2.** The set of feminist bank tellers is the **intersection** $F \cap B$, which is a *subset* of both $F$ and $B$. Mathematically:

$$F \cap B \subseteq F \quad \text{and} \quad F \cap B \subseteq B$$

**Step 3.** By the **monotonicity of probability measures** (a Kolmogorov axiom), if $X \subseteq Y$, then $P(X) \leq P(Y)$. Therefore:

$$P(F \cap B) \leq P(F) \quad \text{and} \quad P(F \cap B) \leq P(B)$$

**Step 4.** Combining the two inequalities:

$$P(F \cap B) \leq \min[P(F), P(B)]$$

**Step 5.** Since $F \cap B$ is a subset of $B$, we have the **strict** inequality (assuming some feminists are not bank tellers, which is empirically true):

$$P(F \cap B) < P(B)$$

**Step 6.** The Linda experiment showed that ~85% of respondents rated $P(F \cap B) > P(B)$, directly violating the inequality above.

> **Interpretation:** Representativeness makes the description of Linda *feel* more like a feminist bank teller than a plain bank teller, so the brain *adds probability mass* to the conjunction — defying the axioms of probability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Standard Error & Sample Size Sensitivity

The Standard Error (SE) of the sample mean is derived as follows.

**Step 1.** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be i.i.d. random variables with population mean $\mu$ and population standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 2.** The sample mean is defined as:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

**Step 3.** The variance of the sample mean is computed using the property that for i.i.d. variables, variances add:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i)$$

**Step 4.** Since $\text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \cdot \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 5.** The standard error of the mean is the square root:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{X})} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 6.** Numerical illustration: if $\sigma = 10$ and $n = 25$, then $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 2$. If $n = 100$, then $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 1$. A four-fold increase in sample size **halves** the standard error.

> **Interpretation:** Heuristic users treat $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ as essentially **constant** in $n$, which causes them to draw strong conclusions from small samples. The KTU-mark-winning answer always emphasises the $\sqrt{n}$ scaling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Regression to the Mean (Galton's Formula)

We derive the **expected value of a child's height conditional on the parent's height**.

**Step 1.** Let $X$ = parent's height (standardised: mean 0, variance 1) and $Y$ = child's height (also standardised). The bivariate normal correlation is $\rho$.

**Step 2.** In a bivariate normal distribution, the conditional expectation of $Y$ given $X = x$ is **linear**:

$$E[Y \mid X = x] = \rho \cdot x$$

**Step 3.** To de-standardise (re-introduce the means $\mu_X, \mu_Y$ and standard deviations $\sigma_X, \sigma_Y$):

$$E[Y \mid X = x] = \mu_Y + \rho \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}(x - \mu_X)$$

**Step 4.** Rearranging to highlight "regression":

$$E[Y \mid X = x] - \mu_Y = \rho \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X} \cdot (x - \mu_X)$$

**Step 5.** Since $\vert \rho \vert \leq 1$, the deviation of the expected child height from the population mean is **smaller in magnitude** than the parent's deviation. This is the **literal regression toward the mean**.

**Step 6.** The conditional variance — i.e., the remaining uncertainty — is:

$$\text{Var}(Y \mid X) = \sigma_Y^2 (1 - \rho^2)$$

> **Interpretation:** People under the representativeness heuristic expect a tall parent to have a tall child of *equal* extremity, predicting $E[Y \mid X = x] = x$ (no regression). The correct formula shows a **shrinkage factor** $\rho < 1$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Bias | Normative Rule (Should) | Heuristic Behaviour (Does) | KTU-Marker Cue |
|------|------------------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Base Rate Neglect | Weight prior $P(A)$ in Bayes' theorem | Ignore prior; over-rely on $P(B \mid A)$ | "Linda problem" |
| Sample Size | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | Treat all $n$ identically | "Hospital size" problem |
| Chance Misconception | Independent events | Streak-reversal or streak-continuation belief | "Gambler's fallacy" |
| Predictability | Use valid predictors only | Trust vivid narratives | "Company forecasting" task |
| Illusion of Validity | Calibration of confidence | Overconfidence in coherent stories | Stockbroker case |
| Regression | $E[Y \mid X] = \rho x + (1-\rho)\mu$ | Expect $E[Y \mid X] = x$ (no regression) | "Flight training" classic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Causal Flow of the Representativeness Heuristic

This diagram captures the cognitive pipeline from stimulus to biased judgement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus or Object A] --> B[Retrieve Prototype of Category B]
    B --> C[Compute Similarity Score SA-B]
    C --> D{Is SA-B High?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Judge P Sub A in B as High]
    D -- No --> F[Judge P Sub A in B as Low]
    E --> G[Suppress Base Rate P of A]
    E --> H[Suppress Sample Size Info n]
    E --> I[Suppress Regression to Mean]
    E --> J[Suppress Independence of Events]
    F --> G
    F --> H
    F --> I
    F --> J
    G --> K[Systematic Bias Output]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Biased Probability Judgement]
```

> **Read this diagram top-to-bottom:** A single stimulus flows through prototype retrieval, similarity scoring, and *suppression* of four statistical regularities, producing one of six canonical biases.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Six Biases and Their Interconnections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REP[Representativeness Heuristic] --> BR[Base Rate Neglect]
    REP --> IS[Insensitivity to Sample Size]
    REP --> MC[Misconceptions of Chance]
    REP --> IP[Insensitivity to Predictability]
    REP --> IV[Illusion of Validity]
    REP --> MR[Misconception of Regression]
    BR --> LK[Linda Problem]
    BR --> MED[Medical Diagnosis Errors]
    IS --> QC[Quality Control Errors]
    IS --> POL[Poll Over-interpretation]
    MC --> GF[Gamblers Fallacy]
    MC --> HH[Hot Hand Fallacy]
    IP --> FIN[Financial Forecasting Errors]
    IV --> SB[Stockbroker Overconfidence]
    MR --> RTM[Regression to Mean Ignored]
    MR --> FT[Flight Training Punishment Myth]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Normative vs Heuristic Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NORM[Normative Bayesian Path]
        N1[Prior P of A] --> N2[Compute P Sub B given A]
        N2 --> N3[Apply Bayes Theorem]
        N3 --> N4[Posterior P of A in B]
    end
    subgraph HEUR[Heuristic Representativeness Path]
        H1[Prototype of B] --> H2[Similarity Sub A to B]
        H2 --> H3[Resemblance Equals Probability]
        H3 --> H4[Posterior P of A in B]
    end
    N4 -.->|Should Equal| H4
    H4 -.->|Often Diverges From| N4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A`, `BR`, `NORM`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone node IDs.
- All node labels inside double-quotes are clean uppercase alphanumeric only — no bold/italics/HTML inside labels.
