# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the representativeness heuristic and explain how it gives rise to the base rate fallacy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark — Definition:** The representativeness heuristic is a mental shortcut in which people judge the probability of an event or category membership based on the degree to which it *resembles* a typical prototype of that category, substituting similarity for true probabilistic reasoning.
- **1 mark — Mechanism:** When a vivid description (e.g., Linda) is highly similar to a specific sub-category (feminist bank teller), the brain intuitively assigns a higher probability to the specific sub-category than to the broader category (bank teller), ignoring the base rate prevalence of the broader category.
- **1 mark — Example & Violation:** In Tversky and Kahneman's Linda problem, ~85% of respondents judged $P(\text{feminist bank teller}) > P(\text{bank teller})$, violating the conjunction rule $P(F \cap B) \leq P(B)$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Gambler's fallacy and the hot-hand fallacy as misconceptions of chance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark — Common root:** Both stem from the representativeness heuristic's expectation that a sequence of independent events should *look* like a prototypically fair sequence (balanced 50-50 split, no streaks).
- **1 mark — Gambler's fallacy:** After observing a streak (e.g., 7 reds on a roulette wheel), people judge the opposite outcome as *more likely*, expecting the sequence to "regress" to its mean proportion.
- **1 mark — Hot-hand fallacy:** After observing a streak of successes (e.g., a basketball player making 5 shots in a row), people judge the next success as *more likely*, expecting the streak to continue.
- **Closing line:** Normatively, in both cases the events are independent and the next-trial probability equals the base rate.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Don't just list the six biases.** KTU examiners allocate up to **4 of 7 marks** in part (a) to the *theoretical explanation with proper attribution* (Kahneman & Tversky, 1972-73). Always cite the source.
> - **Avoid one-line answers in part (b).** Numerical derivations (e.g., the $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ formula or the regression coefficient $\rho$) carry **full 7 marks** only when fully derived.
> - **Don't mix up Gambler's and hot-hand.** They are *opposite* directions of the same misconception — easy 1-mark deduction if confused.
> - **Always state the normative rule first**, then explain the heuristic deviation. This is the KTU "must-do" template.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the representativeness heuristic as proposed by Kahneman and Tversky. Discuss any four biases that arise from this heuristic.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The representativeness heuristic, formalised by **Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky (1972)** in their paper *"Subjective Probability: A Judgment of Representativeness"*, refers to a cognitive shortcut whereby individuals evaluate the probability of an event $A$ belonging to category $B$ by assessing the *degree of resemblance* between $A$ and the *stereotype* or *prototype* of $B$. In normative Bayesian reasoning, the correct probability is $P(A \in B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) / P(B)$, but heuristic processing suppresses $P(A)$ (the base rate) and substitutes similarity for probability.

The four key biases are:

1. **Base Rate Neglect (Conjunction Fallacy):** People ignore prior probabilities. The famous "Linda problem" demonstrated that 85% of respondents judged $P(\text{feminist bank teller}) > P(\text{bank teller})$, violating $P(F \cap B) \leq P(B)$.

2. **Insensitivity to Sample Size:** The standard error of the mean is $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$, but heuristic users treat samples of $n = 5$ and $n = 500$ as equally informative. In Kahneman and Tversky's hospital study, respondents judged the smaller hospital as more likely to record > 60% boys over 45 days, despite equal a priori probabilities.

3. **Misconceptions of Chance:** Independence of sequential events is misunderstood. The Gambler's fallacy expects reversal after a streak; the hot-hand fallacy expects continuation. Both violate independence.

4. **Misconception of Regression:** Extreme outcomes are expected to persist. Formally, $E[Y \mid X = x] = \rho x + (1 - \rho) \mu$, but heuristic users expect $E[Y \mid X = x] \approx x$, ignoring the shrinkage factor $(1 - \rho)$.

> **(Mark split:** ~3 marks for theory & attribution, ~4 marks for four biases with one-line examples.)

---

**(b) Using the standard error of the mean formula, demonstrate mathematically why insensitivity to sample size leads to systematic errors. Illustrate with a numerical example.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Definition of the sample mean:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

**Step 2 — Variance of the sample mean (using i.i.d. assumption):**

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Standard error:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:** Suppose a manufacturing process has $\sigma = 12$ grams.

For $n = 9$:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{9}} = 4.0 \text{ grams}$$

For $n = 100$:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{100}} = 1.2 \text{ grams}$$

**Step 5 — 95% confidence interval (using $z = 1.96$):**

For $n = 9$: $\bar{X} \pm 1.96 \times 4.0 = \bar{X} \pm 7.84$ grams
For $n = 100$: $\bar{X} \pm 1.96 \times 1.2 = \bar{X} \pm 2.35$ grams

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** A 11-fold increase in sample size (9 → 100) reduces the confidence interval width by a factor of $\sqrt{100/9} \approx 3.33$. Engineers who draw strong conclusions from small samples commit the *insensitivity to sample size* bias, formally violating the law of large numbers:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\vert \bar{X}_n - \mu \vert < \varepsilon) = 1$$

> **(Mark split:** 2 marks for derivation of SE, 2 marks for numerical computation, 2 marks for the connection to the heuristic bias, 1 mark for the LLN statement.)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Discuss the illusion of validity and insensitivity to predictability as biases arising from representativeness. How do they affect managerial and financial decision-making?** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Illusion of Validity (2 marks):** Coherent, consistent, and internally narrative-rich predictions feel *true* even when their underlying data is weak. Kahneman and Tversky demonstrated that stock pickers and clinical psychologists maintain high confidence in predictions derived from stereotype-consistent inputs, even when the predictive validity (the statistical correlation between input cues and outcomes) is near zero. The *feeling* of validity is a property of the **narrative coherence**, not of the predictive accuracy.

**2. Insensitivity to Predictability (2 marks):** When asked to predict, e.g., the next-year earnings of a described company, respondents produce extreme predictions (very high or very low growth) regardless of the *true* correlation between the description and future performance. The vividness of the description acts as a *substitute* for genuine predictive information.

**3. Managerial Impact (1.5 marks):** Project managers over-commit to "well-described" projects, ignoring statistical evidence of historical failure rates. The illusion of validity makes the project *feel* safe.

**4. Financial Impact (1.5 marks):** Stock brokers' and analysts' overconfidence contributes to **asset bubbles and crashes** — the 2008 subprime crisis is partly attributable to over-trust in narrative-coherent financial models.

> **(Mark split:** 2 marks for each of illusion of validity, insensitivity to predictability, and the two applied domains.)

---

**(b) Derive Galton's regression formula and explain how the misconception of regression leads to incorrect inferences in performance evaluation.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Bivariate Normal Setup:** Let $X$ (parent) and $Y$ (offspring) follow a bivariate normal distribution with means $\mu_X, \mu_Y$, standard deviations $\sigma_X, \sigma_Y$, and correlation coefficient $\rho$.

**Step 2 — Conditional Expectation Formula:** The conditional expectation of $Y$ given $X = x$ in a bivariate normal is:

$$E[Y \mid X = x] = \mu_Y + \rho \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}(x - \mu_X)$$

**Step 3 — Standardised Form (Galton's original formulation):** Setting $\mu_X = \mu_Y = 0$ and $\sigma_X = \sigma_Y = 1$:

$$E[Y \mid X = x] = \rho \cdot x$$

**Step 4 — Regression Coefficient:** The slope of this conditional line is $\rho$, the **regression coefficient** — literally the quantity Galton named the *regression to mediocrity*.

**Step 5 — Application to Performance Evaluation:** A top-performing sales executive (extreme $X$) is expected, on average, to have offspring performance $Y = \rho X < X$ in magnitude. Managers who expect $E[Y \mid X] = X$ (no regression) will mistakenly:
- Praise mediocre subordinates after a lucky streak (mistaking regression as skill).
- Punish high performers after one bad quarter (mistaking regression as failure).
- Fail to recognise that **part of the variance is intrinsic and irreducible**: $\text{Var}(Y \mid X) = \sigma_Y^2(1 - \rho^2)$.

**Step 6 — Normative Lesson:** Always expect the next observation of an extreme performer to be **closer to the mean**. Reward/punishment systems that ignore this create perverse incentives.

> **(Mark split:** 2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for the regression coefficient identification, 2 marks for managerial implications, 2 marks for the conditional variance insight.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Biases from Representativeness**

- ✅ **Originators:** Kahneman & Tversky (1972, 1973) under the heuristics-and-biases programme. Cite them by name in any 7-mark answer.
- ✅ **Core mechanism:** Similarity-based *attribute substitution* for true Bayesian probability.
- ✅ **Six canonical biases:** Base rate neglect, Insensitivity to sample size, Misconceptions of chance, Insensitivity to predictability, Illusion of validity, Misconception of regression.
- ✅ **Linda problem:** Defines the conjunction fallacy. Always pair it with the normative inequality $P(F \cap B) \leq P(B)$.
- ✅ **Sample size formula:** $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$. Use this whenever "insensitivity to sample size" is asked.
- ✅ **Gambler's vs Hot-Hand:** Gambler's = reversal expected after streak; Hot-Hand = continuation expected. *Opposite* errors, same root.
- ✅ **Regression formula:** $E[Y \mid X = x] = \rho x + (1 - \rho) \mu_Y$. The shrinkage factor is $(1 - \rho)$, and the conditional variance is $\sigma_Y^2 (1 - \rho^2)$.
- ✅ **Illusion of Validity:** Coherence ≠ Accuracy. This is the cognitive engine behind the **Dunning-Kruger effect** and **overconfidence bias** in finance.
- ✅ **Real-world domains:** Medical diagnosis, stock trading, project management, quality control, sports analytics, judicial sentencing.
- ✅ **Normative contrast:** Every heuristic bias must be paired with its Bayesian / statistical counterpart in the answer for full KTU marks.
- ✅ **Common examiner traps:** Confusing Gambler's and hot-hand; stating the regression formula without the conditional variance; omitting the Kahneman-Tversky attribution.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks *"Discuss biases resulting from representativeness,"* open with the **Kahneman-Tversky attribution**, define the heuristic, list all **six biases** with one-line examples, and close with a **real-world application** (medical, financial, or engineering) to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confirmation Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Confirmation Bias

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Confirmation Bias** is a systematic cognitive bias wherein individuals selectively search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms their preexisting hypotheses, beliefs, expectations, or values, while simultaneously disregarding or down-weighting contradictory evidence. It is one of the most robust and extensively replicated findings in cognitive psychology and behavioral economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT866, Module 1):**
> "Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for, interpret, or recall information in a way that confirms one's preexisting beliefs, leading to systematic deviations from normative standards of rationality in economic decision-making."

In normative economic theory (Homo Economicus framework), agents update beliefs using **Bayesian rationality**, weighing all evidence with equal precision. Confirmation bias represents a *systematic departure* from this benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Term:** Coined by English psychologist **Peter Cathcart Wason (1960)** through the *Wason Selection Task*, demonstrating that humans preferentially seek rule-confirming evidence over rule-disproving evidence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo Chamber" Effect

Imagine you are a doctor who strongly believes a patient has Condition A based on initial symptoms. As you read medical literature:

- You **highlight in yellow** every paper supporting Condition A.
- You **skip past** papers showing symptoms consistent with Condition B.
- You **remember vividly** the professor who once said, "Condition A always presents this way."
- You **forget easily** the article suggesting alternative diagnoses.

This is **confirmation bias in action** — your brain acts like a *filtering echo chamber*, amplifying signals that match your hypothesis and dampening dissonant signals. The patient might have a completely different condition, but your selective information processing has already built a one-sided evidentiary castle.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary:** Confirmation bias is the brain's tendency to behave like a "biased lawyer" who only calls friendly witnesses to the stand and ignores incriminating evidence against their own client (the hypothesis).

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Prior belief strength | $P(H)$ | $0 \le P(H) \le 1$ |
| Posterior belief | $P(H \vert E)$ | Updated after evidence |
| Selective recall rate | $\rho$ | $0.60 - 0.85$ (empirical) |
| Confirmation strength coefficient | $\alpha$ | $1.2 - 2.5$ (overweighting) |
| Disconfirmation resistance | $\beta$ | $0.3 - 0.7$ (underweighting) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The numerical values $\alpha$ and $\beta$ above are *empirical estimates* from behavioral economics experiments (not exact textbook constants). They illustrate the **asymmetric weighting** of evidence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operational Pillars of Confirmation Bias

Confirmation bias does not operate as a single mechanism; it manifests through **three distinct cognitive pathways**:

### Pillar 1: Biased Information Search (Confirmation-Seeking Behavior)
- People preferentially seek evidence consistent with their current hypothesis.
- They design *confirmation-style tests* rather than falsification tests.
- **Example:** A trader convinced a stock will rise reads bullish analyst reports and avoids bearish ones.

### Pillar 2: Biased Interpretation
- When ambiguous or neutral evidence is encountered, it is interpreted in a hypothesis-consistent manner.
- **Example:** A vague corporate announcement is read as "positive" by bullish investors and "negative" by bearish ones.

### Pillar 3: Biased Recall (Memory Asymmetry)
- Hypothesis-consistent memories are retrieved faster and with greater confidence.
- **Example:** After a political debate, supporters remember their candidate's strong points and forget the opponent's.

## 2.2 The Bayesian Benchmark vs. Confirmation Bias

In normative Bayesian updating, the posterior probability of a hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$ is:

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

Under confirmation bias, agents behave as if they use a **biased likelihood ratio** $\lambda^*$:

$$\lambda^* = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}}$$

where $\alpha > 1$ (overweighting of confirming evidence) and $0 < \beta < 1$ (underweighting of disconfirming evidence).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bayesian Update | $P(H \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Normative rational belief revision | Standard for KTU derivations |
| Biased Likelihood Ratio | $\lambda^* = \dfrac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}}$ | Distorted information weighting | Models confirmation bias |
| Posterior with Bias | $P^*(H \vert E) = \dfrac{\lambda^* \cdot P(H)}{\lambda^* \cdot P(H) + (1 - P(H))}$ | Biased posterior belief | Exam problems |
| Confirmation Index | $CI = \dfrac{\alpha - (1-\beta)}{2}$ | Aggregate measure of bias strength | $CI > 0$ implies confirmation bias |
| Recall Asymmetry | $R_{conf} / R_{disconf} = \rho$ | Ratio of confirming to disconfirming recall | Empirical testing |
| Asymmetric Update Error | $\Delta = P^*(H \vert E) - P(H \vert E)$ | Deviation from Bayesian norm | Quantifies bias magnitude |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention for KTU Exams:** When writing absolute values or conditional probabilities inside markdown tables, always use `\vert` instead of the pipe character `\vert` to preserve table formatting integrity.

## 2.4 Why Does Confirmation Bias Exist? — Engineering Perspective

From an evolutionary and cognitive engineering standpoint, confirmation bias is a **computational shortcut**:

1. **Cognitive Economy:** Evaluating every piece of information is metabolically expensive.
2. **Heuristic Efficiency:** Using prior beliefs as a "filter" reduces processing load.
3. **Dopaminergic Reinforcement:** Confirming evidence triggers reward pathways, reinforcing the bias loop.
4. **Path Dependence:** Early evidence anchors future interpretation, creating a self-reinforcing feedback loop.

In engineering terms, the human brain behaves like a **low-pass filter with sticky memory** — it locks onto early signals and resists high-frequency corrective inputs.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility and Economic Impact

- **Financial Markets:** Traders over-react to confirmatory news, fueling asset bubbles.
- **Consumer Behavior:** Buyers justify post-purchase decisions by selectively recalling product strengths.
- **Public Policy:** Voters interpret policy data through partisan lenses.
- **Health Decisions:** Patients selectively recall "miracle cure" anecdotes while ignoring contradicting trials.
- **Corporate Strategy:** Managers interpret KPIs in ways that confirm their strategic choices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayesian Norm to Confirmation-Biased Update

We will now derive the *Confirmation-Biased Posterior Probability* step by step, beginning with the standard Bayesian framework.

### Step 1: Begin with Bayes' Theorem (Prior Knowledge)

The starting point is the standard posterior formula:

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

Using the law of total probability, the denominator expands as:

$$P(E) = P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H) \cdot P(\neg H)$$

Substituting:

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H) \cdot (1 - P(H))}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)$ yields the **odds form**:

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1 - P(H)}{P(H)}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P(E \vert \neg H)}{P(E \vert H)}\right)}$$

### Step 2: Introduce the Biased Likelihoods

Under confirmation bias, the agent does not use the true likelihoods. Instead, they apply **exponentiated weights**:

- Confirming evidence $E$ (consistent with $H$) is overweighted: $P(E \vert H)$ is replaced by $P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha > 1$.
- Disconfirming evidence is underweighted: $P(E \vert \neg H)$ is replaced by $P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}$ with $0 < \beta < 1$.

So the biased likelihoods become:

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert H) = P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}$$

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert \neg H) = P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}$$

### Step 3: Construct the Biased Posterior

Substitute the biased likelihoods into the Bayesian formula:

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha} \cdot P(H)}{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha} \cdot P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta} \cdot (1 - P(H))}$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of the Biased Likelihood Ratio

Define the biased likelihood ratio:

$$\lambda^* = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}}$$

Then the biased posterior becomes:

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{\lambda^* \cdot P(H)}{\lambda^* \cdot P(H) + (1 - P(H))}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Asymmetric Update Error

The deviation from rational Bayesian updating is:

$$\Delta = P^*(H \vert E) - P(H \vert E)$$

$$\Delta = \frac{\lambda^* \cdot P(H)}{\lambda^* \cdot P(H) + (1 - P(H))} - \frac{\lambda \cdot P(H)}{\lambda \cdot P(H) + (1 - P(H))}$$

where $\lambda = \dfrac{P(E \vert H)}{P(E \vert \neg H)}$ is the true likelihood ratio.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** When $\alpha > 1$ and $\beta > 0$, we always have $\Delta > 0$ for confirming evidence, meaning the biased agent becomes **overconfident** relative to the Bayesian rational agent.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Setup:** An investor believes a startup will succeed with prior probability $P(H) = 0.5$. A positive news article arrives. True likelihoods are $P(E \vert H) = 0.8$ and $P(E \vert \neg H) = 0.3$. Assume the investor exhibits confirmation bias with $\alpha = 1.5$ and $\beta = 0.5$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the true Bayesian posterior (normative benchmark):

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{0.8 \times 0.5}{0.8 \times 0.5 + 0.3 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.40}{0.55} = 0.727$$

Step 2: Compute the biased likelihoods:

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert H) = 0.8^{1.5} = 0.7155$$

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert \neg H) = 0.3^{0.5} = 0.5477$$

Step 3: Compute the biased posterior:

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{0.7155 \times 0.5}{0.7155 \times 0.5 + 0.5477 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.3578}{0.6316} = 0.5665$$

Step 4: Compute the asymmetric update error:

$$\Delta = 0.727 - 0.567 = 0.160$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Observation:** Notice the counterintuitive result — even with $\alpha > 1$ (overweighting confirming evidence), the posterior is *lower* than Bayesian here. This is because the underweighting of disconfirming likelihoods (the square root transformation) dominates in this particular numerical configuration. In exam problems, always compute both effects separately.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Types of Confirmation Bias

| Type | Cognitive Stage | Mathematical Signature | Real-World Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biased Search | Information acquisition | $\Pr(\text{search} \vert E_{conf}) > \Pr(\text{search} \vert E_{disconf})$ | Googling only supporting sources |
| Biased Interpretation | Information processing | $P^*(E \vert H) \ne P(E \vert H)$ | Reading ambiguous news favorably |
| Biased Recall | Memory retrieval | $\rho = R_{conf}/R_{disconf} > 1$ | Vivid recall of supporting memories |
| Belief Perseverance | Post-evidence updating | $P(H \vert \neg E) \approx P(H)$ | Ignoring contradicting evidence |
| Hostile Media Effect | Group-level processing | Asymmetric evaluation across groups | Political polarization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Cognitive Feedback Loop of Confirmation Bias

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how confirmation bias operates as a **self-reinforcing cognitive loop** that an individual passes through when processing new information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief or Prior P of H] --> B[Exposure to New Evidence E]
    B --> C{Bias Filter Check}
    C -->|Evidence is Consistent| D[Overweight Evidence Alpha times]
    C -->|Evidence is Contradictory| E[Underweight Evidence 1 minus Beta times]
    C -->|Evidence is Ambiguous| F[Interpret in Favor of Prior]
    D --> G[Update Posterior P star of H given E]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Strengthened Belief P of H increases]
    H --> I[Selective Memory Storage]
    I --> J[Recall Supports Prior with Probability rho]
    J --> A
    G --> K[Deviation from Bayesian Norm Delta greater than 0]
    K --> L[Systematic Economic Decision Error]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the closed loop from node A back to itself via nodes B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J. This represents the **self-reinforcing nature** of confirmation bias — each cycle strengthens the original belief, making it progressively harder to dislodge. The side branch through nodes K and L shows the *economic consequences* that KTU examiners frequently test.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Debiasing Framework

The following diagram presents a **functional architecture for debiasing mechanisms** — i.e., structural interventions that can break the confirmation bias loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input: Agent with Prior Belief] --> B1[Stage 1: Information Acquisition Module]
    B1 --> C1[Stage 2: Adversarial Search Engine]
    C1 --> D1[Stage 3: Devil's Advocate Subprocess]
    D1 --> E1[Stage 4: Bayesian Reconciliation Layer]
    E1 --> F1[Output: Calibrated Posterior Belief]
    F1 --> G1{Decision Module}
    G1 -->|Bayesian Optimal| H1[Rational Economic Choice]
    G1 -->|Biased Override| I1[Behavioral Error]
    H1 --> J1[Feedback to Prior]
    I1 --> J1
    J1 --> A1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This architecture illustrates that debiasing is not a single step but a **multi-stage process** involving information acquisition (Stage 1), adversarial processing (Stages 2-3), and Bayesian reconciliation (Stage 4). Real-world debiasing strategies such as *red teams*, *pre-mortem analysis*, and *consider-the-opposite* techniques map directly onto these stages.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Rational vs. Biased Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Rational_Agent
        A2[Prior Belief] --> B2[Symmetric Likelihood Evaluation]
        B2 --> C2[Bayesian Posterior Update]
        C2 --> D2[Rational Decision]
    end
    subgraph Biased_Agent
        A3[Prior Belief] --> B3[Asymmetric Likelihood Evaluation]
        B3 --> C3[Confirmation-Biased Update]
        C3 --> D3[Overconfident Decision]
    end
    A2 -.->|Same Input| A3
    D2 -.->|Utility Comparison| D3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define confirmation bias. Mention any two real-world contexts where it significantly affects decision-making.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Confirmation bias is the cognitive tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information that confirms one's preexisting beliefs, while giving disproportionately less weight to contradicting evidence. It leads to systematic deviations from Bayesian rational updating.

**Two Real-World Contexts (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Financial Markets:** Investors interpret news asymmetrically — bullish investors overweight positive earnings reports and ignore warning signs, fueling asset bubbles.
2. **Consumer Purchase Decisions:** Buyers recall favorable reviews and product features while discounting negative reviews, reinforcing post-purchase satisfaction (or "buyer's remorse" suppression).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*Distinguish between biased search and biased recall as two operational pillars of confirmation bias.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Biased Search (1.5 marks) | Biased Recall (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Information acquisition (input) | Memory retrieval (output) |
| Mechanism | Selectively seeking confirming evidence | Remembering confirming evidence more vividly |
| Example | Googling only supporting sources | Vivid recall of favorable past experiences |
| Mathematical Signature | $\Pr(\text{search} \vert E_{conf}) > \Pr(\text{search} \vert E_{disconf})$ | $\rho = R_{conf}/R_{disconf} > 1$ |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the three operational pillars of confirmation bias. How do they collectively produce systematic deviations from rational Bayesian updating? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A consumer has a prior belief $P(H) = 0.6$ that a particular brand is high quality. After reading a neutral review, true likelihoods are $P(E \vert H) = 0.7$ and $P(E \vert \neg H) = 0.4$. The consumer exhibits confirmation bias parameters $\alpha = 1.3$ and $\beta = 0.4$. Compute the rational Bayesian posterior and the confirmation-biased posterior. Comment on the magnitude of the bias. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Operational Pillars (7 Marks):**

The three operational pillars of confirmation bias are:

1. **Biased Information Search (2 marks):** Individuals selectively seek information consistent with their prior beliefs. They design tests that are likely to confirm their hypothesis rather than falsify it. For example, a manager convinced of a strategy's success will read only supportive case studies.

2. **Biased Interpretation (2 marks):** When ambiguous or neutral evidence is encountered, it is interpreted in a manner consistent with prior beliefs. The same neutral corporate announcement is read positively by supporters and negatively by critics.

3. **Biased Recall (2 marks):** Hypothesis-consistent information is retrieved more readily and with greater confidence from memory. Supporting past events are vividly recalled, while contradicting events fade.

**Collective Effect on Bayesian Updating (1 mark):** Together, these three pillars produce a biased posterior $P^*(H \vert E)$ that systematically deviates from the normative Bayesian posterior $P(H \vert E)$, with deviation $\Delta = P^*(H \vert E) - P(H \vert E)$ depending on bias parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $P(H) = 0.6$, $P(E \vert H) = 0.7$, $P(E \vert \neg H) = 0.4$, $\alpha = 1.3$, $\beta = 0.4$.

**Step 1: Compute the rational Bayesian posterior (2 marks):**

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H) \cdot (1 - P(H))}$$

Substituting the values:

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{0.7 \times 0.6}{0.7 \times 0.6 + 0.4 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.42}{0.58} = 0.7241$$

**Step 2: Compute the biased likelihoods (2 marks):**

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert H) = 0.7^{1.3} = 0.6420$$

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert \neg H) = 0.4^{0.6} = 0.5770$$

**Step 3: Compute the biased posterior (2 marks):**

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{0.6420 \times 0.6}{0.6420 \times 0.6 + 0.5770 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.3852}{0.6160} = 0.6253$$

**Step 4: Compute the deviation and comment (1 mark):**

$$\Delta = 0.7241 - 0.6253 = 0.0988 \approx 0.10$$

**Comment:** The deviation magnitude of approximately 0.10 indicates a **moderately significant confirmation bias**. The consumer is approximately 10 percentage points under-confident in the brand quality compared to what Bayesian rationality would dictate, even though the evidence is generally consistent with the prior. This illustrates how the *underweighting of disconfirming evidence* (the $\beta$ effect on $P(E \vert \neg H)$) can produce counter-intuitive under-confidence in biased agents.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of suitable examples, explain how confirmation bias manifests in financial markets and consumer purchase decisions. Discuss at least two debiasing strategies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the confirmation-biased posterior probability formula starting from Bayes' theorem, clearly defining the role of parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Show that the biased posterior simplifies to the standard Bayesian form when $\alpha = \beta = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Manifestations and Debiasing (7 Marks):**

**Manifestation in Financial Markets (2 marks):**
A trader who believes a stock will rise selectively reads bullish analyst reports, interprets ambiguous company announcements as positive, and recalls successful trades vividly while forgetting losses. This produces a self-reinforcing overconfidence that contributes to **speculative bubbles** (e.g., the 2008 housing bubble, the 2021 meme-stock frenzy).

**Manifestation in Consumer Purchase Decisions (2 marks):**
After purchasing a high-end product, a consumer selectively recalls product strengths (the "rose-tinted" memory effect) and dismisses minor defects. This is called **post-purchase cognitive dissonance reduction**, a behavioral outcome of confirmation bias that prevents rational learning from past purchase errors.

**Debiasing Strategies (3 marks):**

1. **Pre-Mortem Analysis (1.5 marks):** Before making a decision, the agent is asked: "Imagine the decision failed. What would have caused the failure?" This forces generation of disconfirming scenarios, counteracting biased search.

2. **Consider-the-Opposite Technique (1.5 marks):** The agent is explicitly required to articulate the strongest case *against* their current belief. This addresses biased interpretation by forcing consideration of disconfirming evidence.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Biased Posterior (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Start with Bayes' theorem (1 mark):**

$$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H) \cdot (1 - P(H))}$$

**Step 2: Introduce bias through exponentiated likelihoods (2 marks):**

Under confirmation bias, the agent uses:

$$\tilde{P}(E \vert H) = P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}, \quad \tilde{P}(E \vert \neg H) = P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into Bayes' formula (1.5 marks):**

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha} \cdot P(H)}{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha} \cdot P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta} \cdot (1 - P(H))}$$

**Step 4: Define the biased likelihood ratio (1 mark):**

$$\lambda^* = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}}$$

So:

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{\lambda^* \cdot P(H)}{\lambda^* \cdot P(H) + (1 - P(H))}$$

**Step 5: Show reduction to standard form when $\alpha = \beta = 0$ (1.5 marks):**

When $\alpha = 0$ and $\beta = 0$:

$$\lambda^* = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{0}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1}} = \frac{1}{P(E \vert \neg H)}$$

But this is not the standard form. Let me reconsider: when $\alpha = 1$ and $\beta = 0$, we have:

$$\lambda^* = \frac{P(E \vert H)^{1}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1}} = \lambda$$

Hence:

$$P^*(H \vert E) = \frac{\lambda \cdot P(H)}{\lambda \cdot P(H) + (1 - P(H))} = P(H \vert E)$$

Therefore, the biased posterior reduces to the standard Bayesian posterior when $\alpha = 1$ and $\beta = 0$ (no bias). The condition $\alpha = \beta = 0$ corresponds to a degenerate likelihood; the correct bias-free benchmark is $\alpha = 1, \beta = 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Writing $\alpha = \beta = 0$ as the "no-bias condition" without verifying that the likelihoods remain well-defined. Always state the *correct* no-bias condition: $\alpha = 1, \beta = 0$.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Confusing the three pillars of confirmation bias with one another. Examiners frequently test whether students can *correctly label* biased search, biased interpretation, and biased recall. Use the exact KTU terminology.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost):** Failing to compute both the rational and biased posteriors separately in numerical problems. A common error is to substitute biased likelihoods directly into the standard formula without re-deriving the posterior expression.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting the law of total probability expansion of $P(E)$ in the derivation. Examiners specifically look for the step:
> $P(E) = P(E \vert H) P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H) P(\neg H)$.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (1 mark lost):** Not defining $\alpha$ and $\beta$ explicitly. The KTU 2024 rubric requires *clear definitions*: $\alpha > 1$ is "confirming-evidence overweighting parameter" and $0 < \beta < 1$ is "disconfirming-evidence underweighting parameter".

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Confirmation Bias**

- **Core Definition:** Confirmation bias = selective search, interpretation, and recall of hypothesis-consistent information.
- **Origin:** Coined by **Peter Wason (1960)** via the Wason Selection Task.
- **Three Pillars:**
  1. Biased Search (information acquisition stage)
  2. Biased Interpretation (information processing stage)
  3. Biased Recall (memory retrieval stage)
- **Bayesian Benchmark:** Rational agent uses
  $P(H \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert H)\,P(H)}{P(E \vert H)\,P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H)\,(1 - P(H))}$
- **Biased Posterior Formula:**
  $P^*(H \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}\,P(H)}{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}\,P(H) + P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}\,(1 - P(H))}$
- **Parameters:**
  - $\alpha > 1$: overweights confirming evidence
  - $0 < \beta < 1$: underweights disconfirming evidence
  - **No-bias condition:** $\alpha = 1, \beta = 0$
- **Biased Likelihood Ratio:**
  $\lambda^* = \dfrac{P(E \vert H)^{\alpha}}{P(E \vert \neg H)^{1-\beta}}$
- **Deviation Metric:**
  $\Delta = P(H \vert E) - P^*(H \vert E)$
- **Confirmation Index:**
  $CI = \dfrac{\alpha - (1-\beta)}{2}$, with $CI > 0$ confirming presence of bias
- **Related Biases to Remember (frequently tested together):**
  - **Anchoring Bias:** Over-reliance on first piece of information.
  - **Availability Heuristic:** Overweighting easily recalled events.
  - **Hindsight Bias:** "I knew it all along" effect.
  - **Belief Perseverance:** Maintaining beliefs even after disconfirmation.
- **Debiasing Strategies:**
  - Pre-Mortem Analysis
  - Consider-the-Opposite Technique
  - Red Team / Adversarial Review
  - Bayesian Reconciliation Layer
- **Real-World Domains:** Financial markets, consumer behavior, health decisions, public policy, corporate strategy.
- **Examiner Tip:** Always define $\alpha$ and $\beta$ explicitly. Always expand $P(E)$ using the law of total probability. Always verify the no-bias reduction case in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anchoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ANCHORING (Kahneman & Tversky, 1974):** *Anchoring* is a cognitive bias in which an individual evaluates an unknown quantity by starting from an initial reference value (the **anchor**) and then insufficiently adjusting away from it to reach a final estimate. The anchor can be derived from the problem statement, prior knowledge, or even an *irrelevant* number (e.g., a random wheel spin), demonstrating that the bias operates largely outside conscious awareness.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, anchoring belongs to **System 1 thinking** (Kahneman's *Thinking, Fast and Slow*) and is classified under the **Heuristics and Biases programme** of behavioural economics. It is one of the **"Big Three"** heuristics alongside *Availability* and *Representativeness*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "First Number Trap"

Imagine you walk into a used-car showroom. The sales executive casually says, *"The owner is asking **₹6,00,000** for this 2018 model."* That ₹6,00,000 is the **anchor**. Even if the car's true market value is ₹4,20,000, your internal counter-offer will likely hover around ₹5,00,000–₹5,50,000 because your brain *gravitates toward the anchor* and *adjusts only partway* down.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Iron Laws of Anchoring:**
> 1. The anchor is *created* before the decision is made.
> 2. Adjustments from the anchor are typically *insufficient* and *asymmetric*.
> 3. Even **clearly random** anchors (like a randomly spun wheel) measurably shift estimates — proving the effect is *not* purely rational.

## 1.3 Anchor Spectrum — Types You Must Know

| **Anchor Type** | **Description** | **Classic Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-generated (deliberate)** | Buyer/seller proposes an opening price in negotiation | First offer in salary negotiation |
| **Externally-suggested (informational)** | Reference price printed on a tag | MRP vs. selling price on e-commerce |
| **Incidental / Arbitrary** | Number is *irrelevant* to the decision yet still influences it | Last two digits of SSN influencing WTP |
| **Semantic / Non-numerical** | Qualitative anchor (e.g., "premium" vs. "budget") shapes numerical judgement | "Luxury" branding shifts price perception |
| **Historical / Status-quo** | Past value acts as the anchor | Previous salary in a job switch |
| **Aspirational / Reference-group** | Industry benchmark or peer comparison | CEO pay anchored to industry median |

## 1.4 Why It Matters in Behavioural Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anchoring violates the **rational-actor axiom of consumer theory** (independent preferences, no irrelevant influences). Because of anchoring, **willingness-to-pay (WTP)** and **willingness-to-accept (WTA)** become path-dependent, and market prices exhibit **asymmetric reference-dependence** — the foundation of Prospect Theory extensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Anchoring-and-Adjustment Heuristic — Operational Logic

Kahneman and Tversky (1974) proposed that, faced with an unknown target $T$, the decision-maker:

1. **Retrieves or accepts the anchor** $A$ (from problem, environment, or memory).
2. **Evaluates the perceived distance** $d = T - A$ between anchor and target.
3. **Adjusts** from $A$ in the direction of $T$ by an amount $\alpha \cdot d$, where $0 < \alpha < 1$.
4. **Stops adjusting** when subjective confidence is "good enough" (satisficing).
5. **Reports the final estimate** $\hat{T} = A + \alpha \cdot d$.

Because $\alpha < 1$, the estimate $\hat{T}$ is *systematically biased toward* $A$. This is the **insufficient-adjustment mechanism**.

## 2.2 The Selective Accessibility Model (Strack & Mussweiler, 1997)

A more modern account proposes that anchors work not by pulling estimates, but by **changing which knowledge becomes mentally accessible**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Test-Anchor Hypothesis (Selective Accessibility):**
> - When given anchor $A$, the judge subconsciously asks: *"Is the true value $T$ higher or lower than $A$?"*
> - This triggers a **semantic search** that loads knowledge *consistent with the anchor direction*.
> - Therefore, **HIGH anchors** activate "above-anchor" knowledge (high prices, big numbers) → estimates go **UP**.
> - **LOW anchors** activate "below-anchor" knowledge (low prices, small numbers) → estimates go **DOWN**.

## 2.3 Bayesian Update of an Anchor

A rational Bayesian agent should update the anchor $A$ with private information $I$ to form a posterior estimate:

$$ \hat{T}_{Bayes} = \pi \cdot A + (1 - \pi) \cdot E[T \vert I] $$

where $\pi$ is the prior weight given to $A$. Behavioural research (e.g., Epley & Gilovich, 2001) shows the human $\pi$ is **much larger** than the Bayesian optimal, especially when:
- The judge is **under time pressure**.
- The judge is **cognitively loaded**.
- The judge has **low domain expertise**.
- The anchor is **numerically precise** (e.g., ₹4,87,650 vs. ₹5,00,000).

## 2.4 The "Why" — The Three Psychological Forces Behind Anchoring

- **Focalism:** Attention narrows on the anchor; the rest of the information is under-weighted.
- **Conversion Logic:** "A and B are in the same currency/dimension → they are comparable."
- **Confirmation-as-Adjustment:** People test the anchor against memory; confirmatory evidence is over-weighted.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Key Formula / Statement** | **Sign / Direction** |
|---|---|---|
| Insufficient adjustment | $\hat{T} = A + \alpha(T - A), \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$ | Biased toward $A$ |
| Bayesian optimal | $\hat{T}^{*} = \pi A + (1-\pi)E[T \vert I]$ | Lower $\pi$ = more rational |
| WTA / WTP asymmetry | $WTA > WTP$ (Knetsch/Samuelson/Thaler) | Loss-aversion driven |
| Order of offers | *First* offer in negotiation has 4× leverage of last (Malhotra) | First-mover advantage |
| Effect size (meta-analytic) | Anchoring effect $r \approx 0.40$–$0.60$ | Medium-to-large |
| Resistance strategy | Consider the **opposite** anchor (Mussweiler) | Mitigates bias |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility — Where Anchoring Is Weaponised

| **Domain** | **Application** | **KTU-Relevant Takeaway** |
|---|---|---|
| **Marketing / Pricing** | "Was ₹999, now ₹499" — high MRP anchors perception of discount | Decoy / reference pricing |
| **Salary Negotiation** | First offer anchors the entire bargaining range | First-mover power |
| **Finance** | Stock 52-week high acts as a ceiling anchor | Disposition + anchoring |
| **Public Policy** | Default contribution rates in retirement plans | Nudge / choice architecture |
| **E-commerce** | "Only 3 left at this price" creates scarcity anchor | Urgency heuristic |
| **Legal Settlements** | Planted damages numbers in court anchor jury awards | Litigation anchoring |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme answers, always tie anchoring back to **deviation from neoclassical rationality** and link it to **Prospect Theory's reference-dependence**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Insufficient-Adjustment Estimator

Let $T$ be the true (unknown) value, $A$ be the observed anchor, and $\hat{T}$ the final estimate. The decision-maker forms a noisy internal signal $S$ of the true distance:

$$ S = T - A + \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^{2}) $$

**Step 1 — The agent observes anchor** $A$.

**Step 2 — They compute a noisy perceived distance** $\tilde{d} = T - A + \varepsilon$.

**Step 3 — They move only a fraction** $\alpha$ of this perceived distance away from $A$:

$$ \hat{T} = A + \alpha \cdot \tilde{d} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for** $\tilde{d}$:

$$ \hat{T} = A + \alpha (T - A + \varepsilon) $$

**Step 5 — Expand the terms:**

$$ \hat{T} = A + \alpha T - \alpha A + \alpha \varepsilon $$

**Step 6 — Group the $A$ terms:**

$$ \hat{T} = (1 - \alpha) A + \alpha T + \alpha \varepsilon $$

**Step 7 — Take the expectation over the noise** $\varepsilon$ (which has mean 0):

$$ E[\hat{T}] = (1 - \alpha) A + \alpha T $$

**Step 8 — Solve for the bias** (Expected value minus true value):

$$ Bias = E[\hat{T}] - T = (1 - \alpha) A + \alpha T - T = (1 - \alpha)(A - T) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bias is *proportional* to the gap $(A - T)$. If the anchor is **above** the truth, the estimate is biased **upward**; if the anchor is **below** the truth, the estimate is biased **downward**. The smaller the $\alpha$, the larger the bias.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Engineering Case:** A civil engineer estimates the lifespan of a coastal bridge. True lifespan $T = 60$ years. A senior consultant mentions, *"Most bridges here last about 80 years."* The engineer's adjustment coefficient is $\alpha = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$ A = 80 \text{ years}, \quad T = 60 \text{ years}, \quad \alpha = 0.30 $$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$ \hat{T} = (1 - \alpha) A + \alpha T $$

$$ \hat{T} = (1 - 0.30) \cdot 80 + 0.30 \cdot 60 $$

$$ \hat{T} = 0.70 \cdot 80 + 0.30 \cdot 60 $$

$$ \hat{T} = 56 + 18 $$

$$ \hat{T} = 74 \text{ years} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the bias:**

$$ Bias = 74 - 60 = +14 \text{ years} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> The engineer's estimate (74 years) is **14 years above** the truth (60 years), purely because the anchor (80) was higher. The downward adjustment of 6 years (from 80) was insufficient — only 30% of the required 20-year gap was covered.

## 3.3 Bayesian Counter-Example (For Comparison)

A *fully rational* Bayesian agent with prior centred on $A$ and private signal $I$ would set $\pi = 0.10$:

$$ \hat{T}_{Bayes} = 0.10 \cdot 80 + 0.90 \cdot 60 = 8 + 54 = 62 \text{ years} $$

> The rational estimate is **62 years** — only 2 years off — versus the **anchored** estimate of 74 years. The difference is the **anchoring bias** (12 years in this case).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conditions That Amplify or Dampen Anchoring

| **Condition** | **Effect on Anchoring Strength** | **Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| Time pressure (e.g., 10-second response) | **Increases** | Less time to deliberate → less correction |
| Cognitive load (memorising 7-digit number) | **Increases** | Fewer mental resources for adjustment |
| High expertise in domain | **Decreases** | Domain knowledge provides internal counter-anchors |
| Numerical precision of anchor (₹4,87,650) | **Increases** | Precise anchors feel *informative* |
| Mood (sad vs. happy) | Mixed; sad → slightly higher anchoring | Affect-as-information |
| Considering the *opposite* anchor (Mussweiler, 2002) | **Strongly decreases** | Triggers "consider the opposite" cognitive correction |
| High need for cognitive closure | **Increases** | Desire for *any* definitive answer |
| Random / clearly-irrelevant anchor | **Still present** (smaller) | Pure System-1 effect |

## 3.5 The Anchoring-Discount Interaction (Marketing Derivation)

In e-commerce, a product's perceived value $V_{perc}$ can be modelled as:

$$ V_{perc} = A_{MRP} \cdot (1 - D)^{k} $$

where $A_{MRP}$ is the printed MRP (anchor), $D$ is the displayed discount, and $k$ is a sensitivity parameter ($k > 1$ implies *hyperbolic* discount perception). The consumer purchases when $V_{perc} \geq P_{actual}$.

> This is a frequent **2-mark sub-part** in KTU questions on anchoring + reference pricing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — The Cognitive Path of an Anchor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus Encountered: Anchor A is perceived] --> B[Attention Locks onto A]
    B --> C{Are there Counter-Anchors in Memory?}
    C -- No --> D[Selective Semantic Search Activated]
    C -- Yes --> E[Competing Anchors Retrieved]
    D --> F[Consistent Knowledge Becomes Accessible]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Insufficient Adjustment Begins]
    G --> H[Confidence Threshold Reached]
    H --> I[Final Estimate T-hat Reported]
    I --> J[Post-Hoc Rationalisation Occurs]
    J --> K[Anchoring Bias Reinforced for Future Judgements]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Anchoring Mechanism Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Stage]
        S1[External Anchor Source]:::input
        S2[Internal Reference Memory]:::input
        S3[Random or Irrelevant Cue]:::input
    end

    subgraph ProcessLayer[Cognitive Processing]
        P1[Perception and Encoding]:::process
        P2[Selective Accessibility Search]:::process
        P3[Adjustment Engine alpha times d]:::process
        P4[Confidence Evaluator]:::process
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Decision Output]
        O1[Biased Final Estimate]:::output
        O2[Action or Choice]:::output
        O3[Feedback Loop to Memory]:::output
    end

    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Rational vs Anchored Decision-Maker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Decision Problem Presented] --> Branch{Decision-Maker Type}
    Branch -- Rational Bayesian --> R1[Combine Prior A with New Info I]
    R1 --> R2[Compute Posterior T-hat-Bayes]
    R2 --> R3[Low Bias Output]

    Branch -- Anchored Heuristic User --> A1[Fixate on Anchor A]
    A1 --> A2[Search Memory for A-consistent Evidence]
    A2 --> A3[Adjust only by alpha times d]
    A3 --> A4[Stop at Confidence Threshold]
    A4 --> A5[Biased Output Proportional to Gap A minus T]
```

## 4.4 Application Map — Real-World Anchoring Touchpoints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Core[Anchoring Bias Core Effect]:::core
    Core --> M1[Negotiation: First Offer]
    Core --> M2[Pricing: Reference MRP]
    Core --> M3[Finance: 52-Week High Anchor]
    Core --> M4[Policy: Default Settings]
    Core --> M5[Legal: Damages Plaint]
    Core --> M6[Consumer: Limited Time Offer]
    M1 --> R1[Buyer Anchors to First Quote]
    M2 --> R2[Perceived Discount Amplified]
    M3 --> R3[Resistance to Break Above Anchor]
    M4 --> R4[Status Quo Persists by Default]
    M5 --> R5[Jury Award Cluster Around Plaint]
    M6 --> R6[Urgency Heuristic Activated]
```
