topics.title: Overconfidence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overconfidence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overconfidence** is a cognitive bias in which a person's subjective confidence in their own judgments, predictions, or abilities is **systematically greater** than their actual objective accuracy or performance. It is one of the most robust and consistently documented biases in the behavioural economics literature (Kahneman, 2011; Moore & Healy, 2008).

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, **Overconfidence** is classified as a **heuristic-driven cognitive bias** originating from **System 1 (intuitive) thinking** as described in Daniel Kahneman's dual-process theory. It manifests when decision-makers place unwarranted subjective probability on the correctness of their own knowledge, predictions, or capabilities.

Mathematically stated, a decision-maker is *overconfident* when:

$$\text{Subjective Confidence} > \text{Objective Accuracy}$$

### 1.2 The Three Facets of Overconfidence (Moore & Healy, 2008)

Modern behavioural economics research decomposes overconfidence into three distinct, measurable sub-types:

| Sub-Type | Formal Description | Domain of Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Overestimation** | Belief that one's own performance, ability, or control is *better than it actually is*. | Skill evaluation, task performance |
| **Overplacement** | Belief that one is *better than others* (the "Better-Than-Average Effect"). | Relative ranking, comparative judgment |
| **Overprecision** | Excessive *certainty* in the accuracy of one's own beliefs or predictions. | Forecasting, confidence intervals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between these three sub-types is a frequently tested concept. Examiners expect students to identify *which* facet a given real-world example illustrates.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Wedding Toast Analogy 🍷**
> Imagine you are at a friend's wedding. The host asks every guest: *"How would you rate your driving skills compared to other drivers on the road?"* Statistically, **more than 80% of people rate themselves as "above average" drivers**. This is mathematically absurd — by definition, only 50% of the population can be above the median. This universal self-enhancement is the everyday face of **Overplacement (Overconfidence)**.
>
> Now imagine a finance manager at a trading desk predicting that a particular stock *will* rise. He is **95% confident** in his forecast. Historically, his 95% confidence intervals only capture the true outcome about **60% of the time**. The gap between *perceived certainty* and *actual hit-rate* is **Overprecision**.

### 1.4 Real-World Manifestations of Overconfidence

- **Financial Markets:** Retail traders over-trading, "momentum illusion" bubbles (e.g., dot-com bubble, 2008 housing crisis).
- **Entrepreneurship:** Start-up founders systematically underestimating the probability of business failure (>80% of venture-backed start-ups fail, yet founders estimate success rates near 80%).
- **Medicine:** Surgeons overestimating diagnostic accuracy and procedure success rates.
- **Strategic Planning:** Managers overestimating project completion probability and underestimating time/cost.
- **Legal Domain:** Plaintiffs overestimating their probability of winning a case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cognitive Architecture of Overconfidence

Overconfidence does not arise randomly. It is produced by systematic **psychological mechanisms** rooted in the brain's information-processing architecture:

1. **Confirmation Bias Feedback Loop:** People selectively seek and recall information that confirms their prior beliefs, artificially inflating subjective confidence.
2. **Hindsight Bias ("I-knew-it-all-along" effect):** After an event occurs, people overstate how predictable it was beforehand, which retroactively inflates their confidence calibration.
3. **Illusion of Control:** People believe they can influence uncontrollable events (Langer, 1975).
4. **Optimism Bias / Wishful Thinking:** Asymmetric weighting of positive outcomes over negative ones.
5. **Ego Depletion & Self-Enhancement:** Self-serving attribution — successes are attributed to skill, failures to bad luck.
6. **Anchoring on Internal Narratives:** People anchor on their own constructed causal story and ignore base rates.

### 2.2 The Calibration Framework (Why and How)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the *gold-standard metric* used to measure overconfidence quantitatively. A perfectly calibrated individual states they are **70% confident** → they are correct **70% of the time**.

The operational chain is as follows:

- **Step 1 (Generate):** Subject formulates a prediction or judgment.
- **Step 2 (Quantify):** Subject assigns a subjective probability (e.g., "I am 80% sure").
- **Step 3 (Resolve):** Outcome is observed.
- **Step 4 (Compare):** Subject's stated confidence is matched against the empirical hit rate.
- **Step 5 (Diagnose):** If hit rate < stated confidence, the subject is **overconfident**. If hit rate > stated confidence, the subject is **underconfident**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield mathematical toolkit** for overconfidence-related KTU questions. Memorize the symbols, units, and the direction of overconfidence.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Variable | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Calibration Index** | $CI = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( p_i - o_i \right)$ | $p_i$ = stated confidence, $o_i$ = outcome (1 or 0). $CI > 0$ = overconfident. |
| 2 | **Mean Absolute Calibration Error** | $MACE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert p_i - o_i \vert$ | Magnitude of miscalibration regardless of direction. |
| 3 | **Brier Score** | $BS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (p_i - o_i)^2$ | Lower = better calibration. Always in $[0,1]$. |
| 4 | **Better-Than-Average Effect (BTA)** | $P(\text{self} > \text{median}) > 0.50$ | A population-level sign of Overplacement. |
| 5 | **Hit Rate at Confidence Level c** | $H(c) \approx c$ | Perfect calibration condition. |
| 6 | **Overprecision Gap** | $\Delta_{OP} = c_{\text{stated}} - H(c_{\text{stated}})$ | Positive = overconfident; negative = underconfident. |
| 7 | **Hit-Rate / Calibration Curve** | Plot of $H(c)$ vs. $c$ | A 45° line = perfect calibration. Curve below 45° = overconfidence. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

For KTU B.Tech (Engineering) students, overconfidence is **not abstract**. It directly impacts:

- **Project Management (PERT/CPM):** Over-optimistic time and cost estimates (the classic Planning Fallacy — Kahneman & Tversky, 1979).
- **Software Engineering:** Developers overestimating defect-free code rates and underestimating debugging time.
- **Entrepreneurship & Innovation:** Engineering start-ups overestimating market traction.
- **Investment Decisions in Tech Firms:** CFO over-confidence leading to over-leveraging.
- **Risk Assessment in Civil Engineering:** Bridge, dam, and structural safety analyses where engineer confidence exceeds actual failure probabilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Calibration Index for Overconfidence

> [!NOTE]
> The Calibration Index is the **most frequently tested quantitative concept** in KTU ESE questions on overconfidence. Below is the full derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the prediction problem.** Suppose a decision-maker (DM) makes $N$ binary predictions. For the $i$-th prediction, the DM reports a subjective confidence $p_i \in [0,1]$ that an event will occur.

**Step 2 — Define the outcome variable.**

$$o_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if the event actually occurred} \\ 0 & \text{if the event did not occur} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Define the prediction error for a single trial.**

$$e_i = p_i - o_i$$

If the DM is well-calibrated and confident ($p_i = 0.80$) and the event occurs ($o_i = 1$), then $e_i = 0.80 - 1 = -0.20$. This indicates **slight underconfidence** on this single trial.

**Step 4 — Aggregate over all $N$ trials.**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i \\
   &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( p_i - o_i \right) \\
   &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \;-\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} o_i \\
   &= \overline{p} \;-\; \overline{o}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation logic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } CI > 0 &\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \overline{p} > \overline{o} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Overconfidence} \\
\text{If } CI = 0 &\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \overline{p} = \overline{o} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Perfect Calibration} \\
\text{If } CI < 0 &\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \overline{p} < \overline{o} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Underconfidence}
\end{aligned}$$

This shows that the Calibration Index reduces to the difference between the DM's **average stated confidence** and the **empirical hit rate** — an elegant and direct measure of over/under-confidence.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing Overconfidence from Related Biases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently use *similar-sounding* biases as distractors. Use this matrix to disambiguate.

| Bias | Core Misjudgment | Direction of Error | KTU Example Hook |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Overconfidence** | "I am more right than I am." | Confidence $\uparrow$, Accuracy $=$ | Trader sure of stock pick |
| **Optimism Bias** | "Good things will happen to me." | Probability of positive event $\uparrow$ | "I won't get cancer" |
| **Dunning–Kruger Effect** | Low-ability people over-estimate skill. | Skill estimate $\uparrow$ | Novice coder rates self expert |
| **Confirmation Bias** | "I see what I want to see." | Evidence weighting skewed | Cherry-picking news |
| **Planning Fallacy** | "My project will finish on time." | Time/Cost under-estimate | Software delivery delays |
| **Illusion of Control** | "I can influence chance events." | Agency over-estimate | Gambler thinks roll is "due" |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A finance manager makes 5 stock predictions. The table below is provided.

| Trial $i$ | Stated Confidence $p_i$ | Outcome $o_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.90 | 1 (rose) |
| 2 | 0.80 | 0 (fell) |
| 3 | 0.70 | 1 (rose) |
| 4 | 0.60 | 0 (fell) |
| 5 | 0.95 | 1 (rose) |

**Compute the Calibration Index:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{p} &= \frac{0.90 + 0.80 + 0.70 + 0.60 + 0.95}{5} \\
            &= \frac{3.95}{5} = 0.79
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{o} &= \frac{1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1}{5} \\
            &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.60
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= \overline{p} - \overline{o} = 0.79 - 0.60 = 0.19
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $CI = +0.19 > 0$ ⟹ The manager is **overconfident** by approximately 19 percentage points on average. Stated confidence (79%) substantially exceeds actual hit rate (60%).

**Brier Score cross-check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BS &= \frac{1}{5}\Big[(0.90-1)^2 + (0.80-0)^2 + (0.70-1)^2 + (0.60-0)^2 + (0.95-1)^2\Big] \\
   &= \frac{1}{5}\Big[0.0100 + 0.6400 + 0.0900 + 0.3600 + 0.0025\Big] \\
   &= \frac{1.1025}{5} = 0.2205
\end{aligned}$$

A Brier Score of 0.22 reflects a fairly large miscalibration — corroborating the $CI$ finding.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Architecture of Overconfidence Bias

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the psychological chain that produces overconfident decision-making, from cognitive input to behavioural output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Cognitive Input: New Judgment Task"] --> B["System 1 Fast Intuitive Processing"]
    B --> C["Selective Information Search"]
    C --> D["Confirmation Bias Filter"]
    D --> E["Self-Serving Attribution Engine"]
    E --> F["Inflated Subjective Confidence"]
    F --> G{"Calibration Check"}
    G -- "Hit rate lower than stated" --> H["Diagnosed Overconfidence"]
    G -- "Hit rate matches" --> I["Well-Calibrated Agent"]
    H --> J["Behavioral Output: Risky Overcommitment"]
    J --> K["Financial Loss or Project Failure"]
```

### 4.2 The Three Facets of Overconfidence — Taxonomic Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    OC["Overconfidence Bias"]
    OC --> OE["Overestimation"]
    OC --> OP["Overplacement"]
    OC --> OPC["Overprecision"]
    OE --> OE1["I am better at this task than I actually am"]
    OP --> OP1["I am better than my peers"]
    OPC --> OPC1["My prediction is more certain than justified by evidence"]
```

### 4.3 Calibration Curve — Visual Diagnostic

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["Stated Confidence c"] --> Y["Empirical Hit Rate H c"]
    Y --> CAL["45 Degree Perfect Calibration Line"]
    Y --> OC["Curve Below 45 Degrees: Overconfidence Region"]
    Y --> UC["Curve Above 45 Degrees: Underconfidence Region"]
```

### 4.4 Engineering Decision Flow — Defensive Calibration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Engineer States Confidence Level"]
    S2["Step 2: System Logs Prediction"]
    S3["Step 3: Outcome Observed"]
    S4["Step 4: Compute Calibration Index CI"]
    S5{"Is CI greater than 0?"}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6["Trigger Overconfidence Alert: Increase Buffer in Time and Cost Estimates"]
    S5 -- No --> S7["Maintain Current Estimates"]
    S6 --> S8["Feedback to Engineer for Debiasing"]
    S7 --> S8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define Overconfidence and list its three types as identified by Moore and Healy (2008).**

**Model Answer:**
Overconfidence is a cognitive bias in which a person's subjective confidence in their judgments systematically exceeds their actual accuracy. Moore and Healy (2008) decomposed it into three types:
1. **Overestimation** — over-rating one's own ability or performance.
2. **Overplacement** — believing one is better than others (the Better-Than-Average Effect).
3. **Overprecision** — excessive certainty in the accuracy of one's own beliefs. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is the Better-Than-Average Effect? How is it related to Overplacement?**

**Model Answer:**
The Better-Than-Average (BTA) Effect is the empirical finding that the majority of people rate themselves as above-average on almost any desirable trait (driving, intelligence, leadership, etc.). Since the median by definition splits the population 50/50, a majority self-rating above median is mathematically impossible. The BTA Effect is the **empirical signature of Overplacement**, which is the second facet of overconfidence. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the three facets of overconfidence as proposed by Moore and Healy (2008). Support your answer with two real-world examples for each facet. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A project manager makes 6 estimates about task completion with stated confidence levels and outcomes as shown:
>
> | Trial | Stated Confidence $p_i$ | Outcome $o_i$ |
> | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | 1 | 0.95 | 1 |
> | 2 | 0.85 | 0 |
> | 3 | 0.75 | 1 |
> | 4 | 0.70 | 1 |
> | 5 | 0.60 | 0 |
> | 6 | 0.90 | 1 |
>
> Compute the **Calibration Index** and the **Brier Score**. Is the manager overconfident, under-confident, or well-calibrated? Justify. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The three facets of overconfidence as per Moore and Healy (2008) are:

1. **Overestimation:** The decision-maker rates their own ability, performance, or control as higher than is objectively the case. It is an *absolute* miscalibration about the self.
   - *Example 1:* A first-year engineering student claims to have solved 90% of his coding assignment correctly, while the actual error-rate reveals only 60% correctness.
   - *Example 2:* A chess amateur claims a 70% winning probability in an upcoming tournament, while his Elo rating suggests a 30% win-rate against that field.

2. **Overplacement (Better-Than-Average Effect):** The decision-maker believes they are superior to the average peer.
   - *Example 1:* 80% of drivers in surveys rate themselves as "above average" — mathematically impossible.
   - *Example 2:* MBA students estimate they will earn more than the median MBA graduate, with the majority of them simultaneously holding this belief.

3. **Overprecision:** Excessive subjective certainty in the accuracy of one's beliefs, often revealed by overly narrow confidence intervals.
   - *Example 1:* A weather forecaster who states a 99% confidence interval for tomorrow's temperature that, in reality, contains the true value only 70% of the time.
   - *Example 2:* A financial analyst projecting a 95% confidence range for next quarter's revenue that captures the actual outcome only 55% of the time. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the average stated confidence:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{p} &= \frac{0.95 + 0.85 + 0.75 + 0.70 + 0.60 + 0.90}{6} \\
            &= \frac{4.75}{6} \approx 0.7917
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the empirical hit rate (average outcome):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{o} &= \frac{1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 1}{6} \\
            &= \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Calibration Index:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= \overline{p} - \overline{o} \\
   &= 0.7917 - 0.6667 \\
   &= 0.125
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Brier Score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BS &= \frac{1}{6}\Big[(0.95-1)^2 + (0.85-0)^2 + (0.75-1)^2 + (0.70-1)^2 + (0.60-0)^2 + (0.90-1)^2\Big] \\
   &= \frac{1}{6}\Big[0.0025 + 0.7225 + 0.0625 + 0.0900 + 0.3600 + 0.0100\Big] \\
   &= \frac{1.2475}{6} \approx 0.2079
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since $CI = +0.125 > 0$, the project manager's average stated confidence (79.17%) exceeds the empirical hit rate (66.67%). **The manager is overconfident** by 12.5 percentage points. The high Brier Score (≈0.21) corroborates a substantial calibration error. The manager should apply **debiasing techniques** such as reference-class forecasting, pre-mortem analysis, and explicit base-rate elicitation. *(7 marks)*

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Critically discuss the **psychological and cognitive mechanisms** that produce overconfidence in decision-making. In your answer, distinguish between *cognitive* drivers and *motivational* drivers of overconfidence. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Define the **Planning Fallacy**. Explain how it is a downstream consequence of overconfidence and illustrate with one engineering project example. Propose two debiasing interventions to mitigate it. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

Overconfidence arises from two broad families of drivers:

**A. Cognitive Drivers (information-processing causes):**

1. **Confirmation Bias:** People preferentially attend to evidence that confirms their prior beliefs, inflating apparent support for their judgments.
2. **Hindsight Bias:** Once outcomes are known, people believe they "knew it all along," retroactively boosting their perceived predictive ability.
3. **Anchoring & Availability:** Vivid, recent, or salient events are overweighted, leading to over-precision in narrative forecasts.
4. **Representativeness Heuristic:** Judging probability by similarity to a mental prototype rather than by base rates.
5. **Illusion of Explanatory Depth:** People overestimate how well they understand causal mechanisms, generating false certainty.

**B. Motivational Drivers (self-serving causes):**

1. **Self-Enhancement / Ego Protection:** Boosting self-esteem by over-rating competence.
2. **Wishful Thinking:** Asymmetrically hoping for positive outcomes, distorting probability estimates.
3. **Social Signaling:** Overstating certainty to project competence to peers, supervisors, or markets.
4. **Sunk-Cost Justification:** Protecting prior commitments by suppressing doubts.

The cognitive drivers distort the *epistemic* basis of confidence, while the motivational drivers distort its *affective* basis. Together they produce the robust, replicated overconfidence phenomenon documented across domains. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Definition:** The **Planning Fallacy** (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979) is the systematic tendency of decision-makers to **underestimate the time, cost, and risk** of future actions while simultaneously **overestimating the benefits**. It is a *domain-specific manifestation* of overconfidence applied to project forecasting.

**Why it is a consequence of overconfidence:** Planners anchor on the *internal scenario* — the best-case path in which everything goes smoothly — and discount the *distribution of outcomes* observed in similar past projects (the *outside view*). This selective focus is itself a form of overprecision (about the project's trajectory) and overestimation (about one's ability to execute). It is compounded by motivational drivers such as wishful thinking and impression management.

**Engineering Example:** A civil engineering team estimates a flyover construction project will be completed in **18 months at ₹80 Crore**. The actual duration turns out to be **34 months at ₹130 Crore**. The team, focused on the idealised "inside view," ignored the *outside view* — a reference class of similar urban flyover projects in India that historically overshoot by 50–80%.

**Two Debiasing Interventions:**

1. **Reference-Class Forecasting (Outside View):** Systematically compare the project against a base-rate distribution of *similar completed projects* and use the median (or higher percentile) as the planning estimate, ignoring the unique-features narrative.
2. **Pre-Mortem Analysis:** Before project launch, instruct the team to imagine the project has *already failed* and to write a memo explaining why. This counterfactual exercise surfaces risks and unstated assumptions, breaking the overconfidence-induced optimism. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating *Overconfidence* with *Optimism Bias*. Overconfidence is a miscalibration between confidence and accuracy; Optimism Bias is an asymmetric belief about *future outcomes* affecting the self. They overlap but are not identical.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Treating "Better-Than-Average Effect" as Overconfidence *in general*. It is specifically **Overplacement** — one of three sub-types. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this imprecision.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical questions, students forget to compute the **average outcome $\overline{o}$** separately from the **average confidence $\overline{p}$**. The Calibration Index is a *difference* of two averages.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to interpret the *sign* of $CI$. A positive $CI$ is overconfidence, a negative $CI$ is under-confidence. Always state the direction explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing Brier Score with MACE. Brier Score squares the error; MACE takes the absolute value. Both are valid calibration measures but are **not interchangeable**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Overconfidence (Module 1, PECBT866)**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Subjective confidence > Objective accuracy (a cognitive bias from System 1).
- 🔹 **Three Facets (Moore & Healy, 2008):** Overestimation, Overplacement, Overprecision.
- 🔹 **Better-Than-Average Effect:** Population-level signature of Overplacement.
- 🔹 **Calibration Index:** $CI = \overline{p} - \overline{o}$; positive = overconfident.
- 🔹 **Brier Score:** $\frac{1}{N}\sum(p_i - o_i)^2$; always in $[0,1]$; lower is better.
- 🔹 **Hard-Easy Effect:** People are overconfident on hard tasks and under-confident on easy tasks.
- 🔹 **Planning Fallacy:** Overconfidence applied to project forecasts (downstream consequence).
- 🔹 **Dunning–Kruger Effect:** Low-ability people over-estimate skill; overlaps with Overestimation.
- 🔹 **Debiasing Tools:** Reference-class forecasting, pre-mortem, base-rate elicitation, training, decision aids.
- 🔹 **Real-world domains:** Finance, medicine, project management, entrepreneurship, military strategy.
- 🔹 **Engineer's takeaway:** Always pair subjective confidence with empirical hit-rate data; build buffers.
- 🔹 **Mathematical direction:** $CI > 0$ ⟹ overconfident; $CI = 0$ ⟹ calibrated; $CI < 0$ ⟹ under-confident.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exponential Growth Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exponential Growth Bias

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — EXPONENTIAL GROWTH BIAS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exponential Growth Bias (EGB)** is a systematic cognitive bias in which decision-makers systematically **underestimate** the future magnitude of processes that grow at a constant percentage rate over time. Formally introduced in behavioural economics by **Wagenaar and Sagaria (1975)** and later formalized by **Levy and Tasoff (2016)**, EGB is the tendency of individuals to mentally *linearize* an exponential trajectory — i.e., to forecast future values as if growth were additive rather than multiplicative.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, EGB belongs to the family of **present-biased and projection-based heuristics** (Module 1) and is classified as a **numerical cognition bias** — distinct from, but related to, *present bias*, *projection bias*, and *base-rate neglect*.

The bias arises because the human brain evolved to process **linear and arithmetic** changes (which characterize most everyday physical experiences) far more fluently than **geometric/compound** changes. When a quantity grows by a fixed *percentage* (e.g., 7% per year), the actual size of each increment is itself growing, producing a *J-shaped* curve that the mind flattens into a straight line in its mental simulation.

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Intuition

Imagine you pour water into a bucket:
- **Day 1:** you pour **1 litre** (1% of a 100-litre tank).
- **Day 2:** you pour **1% more** — but now the tank already has 1 litre, so the 1% is taken from 100 → 1.01 litres. The increment is tiny.

A naive observer (and most human brains) thinks: *"If each day I add ~1 litre, then in 100 days I'll have ~100 litres."* This is the **linear (additive)** mental model. The reality, however, is **exponential** — and after 100 days of 1% growth, the actual quantity is:

$$FV = 100 \times (1.01)^{100} \approx 270.48 \text{ litres}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *additive mind* projects a straight line, while the *exponential world* curves sharply upward. The gap between the two — the **EGB gap** — grows wider with every time period.

This mismatch is the essence of Exponential Growth Bias: **the brain thinks in addition; the world often moves in multiplication.**

---

### 📊 Canonical Real-World Manifestations

| Domain | Manifestation of EGB |
|---|---|
| **Personal Finance** | Under-saving for retirement; misjudging compound interest on loans |
| **Climate & Energy** | Public underestimation of CO₂ accumulation in the atmosphere |
| **Public Health** | Underestimating viral spread (R₀ > 1 disease transmission) |
| **Macro-economics** | Misjudging national debt trajectories |
| **Technology** | Underestimating computing power (Moore's Law effects) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The bias is measured empirically in experimental economics as the **Perception Ratio (PR)**:
> $$\text{PR} = \frac{\text{Estimated future value}}{\text{True future value}}$$
> A PR < 1 indicates EGB; the further below 1, the stronger the bias.

---

### 🎯 Why EGB Matters in a B.Tech Engineer's Life

Even though this is a *behavioural* course, the topics connect directly to engineering decisions:
- **Project cost overruns** (compound escalation of material prices).
- **Capacity planning** (server load, network traffic — both often grow exponentially).
- **Loan amortization** on education/mortgage debt.
- **Energy & sustainability projects** (population/resource growth assumptions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Spine of Exponential Growth

Exponential growth of a quantity $X$ over discrete time periods follows the **geometric progression**:

$$X_t = X_0 \cdot (1 + r)^t$$

where:
- $X_0$ = initial value (principal, starting population, initial debt, etc.)
- $r$ = growth rate per period (expressed as a decimal, e.g., 7% → 0.07)
- $t$ = number of periods
- $X_t$ = value after $t$ periods

The human mental shortcut replaces $(1+r)^t$ with $(1 + r \cdot t)$ — i.e., a **linear approximation**. This is a first-order Taylor expansion:

$$(1+r)^t \approx 1 + r \cdot t \quad \text{(valid only for small } r \cdot t \text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the bias arise?** Because $1 + r \cdot t$ is far easier to compute mentally than $(1+r)^t$, and for small horizons the two are nearly identical. The brain defaults to the easier model *even when the horizon is long* — that is the bias.

---

## 2.2 The Two Competing Mental Models

### Model A — The *Correct* Exponential Model
$$X_t^{\text{TRUE}} = X_0 \cdot (1 + r)^t$$

### Model B — The *Linear* Mental Model (what people actually do)
$$X_t^{\text{ESTIMATED}} = X_0 \cdot (1 + r \cdot t)$$

### The EGB Gap
$$\text{Gap}_t = X_t^{\text{TRUE}} - X_t^{\text{ESTIMATED}} = X_0 \left[ (1+r)^t - (1 - r \cdot t) \right]$$

> This gap is **zero at $t=0$** and **grows monotonically** with $t$ and $r$.

---

## 2.3 The Doubling-Time Intuition (Rule of 72)

A remarkably useful heuristic — and one frequently tested in KTU exams — is the **Rule of 72**: the time (in years) for a quantity to double at a constant annual rate $r$ is approximately:

$$T_{\text{double}} \approx \frac{72}{r \cdot 100} = \frac{0.72}{r}$$

| Growth Rate $r$ | True Doubling Time | Rule of 72 Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| 1% | 69.66 yrs | 72.0 yrs |
| 5% | 14.21 yrs | 14.4 yrs |
| 7% | 10.24 yrs | 10.3 yrs |
| 10% | 7.27 yrs | 7.2 yrs |
| 15% | 4.96 yrs | 4.8 yrs |

> [!TIP]
> The Rule of 72 is accurate to within 1% for rates between 5% and 10% — the range most relevant to real financial decisions.

---

## 2.4 Continuous vs. Discrete Formulation

The continuous-time analogue of exponential growth is:

$$X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{r \cdot t}$$

where $e \approx 2.71828$ is **Euler's number**. The corresponding doubling time is:

$$T_{\text{double}}^{\text{cont}} = \frac{\ln(2)}{r} \approx \frac{0.693}{r}$$

The continuous and discrete forms coincide when $r$ is small; for moderate rates, they differ measurably.

---

## 2.5 🧾 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following — these appear in nearly every KTU ESE Module 1 question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_t = X_0 (1+r)^t$ | Discrete exponential growth | Compounding, loans, savings |
| 2 | $X_t = X_0 (1 + r t)$ | Linear mental model | Reveals source of EGB |
| 3 | $FV = PV (1+r)^n$ | Future Value of present sum | Personal finance |
| 4 | $PV = FV / (1+r)^n$ | Present Value discounting | Reverse compounding |
| 5 | $T_{\text{double}} \approx 72 / (r \cdot 100)$ | Rule of 72 | Quick estimation of doubling time |
| 6 | $T_{\text{double}} = \ln(2)/\ln(1+r)$ | Exact doubling time | Discrete compounding |
| 7 | $X(t) = X_0 e^{rt}$ | Continuous exponential | Population, decay processes |
| 8 | $\text{PR} = E[X_t] / X_t^{\text{TRUE}}$ | Perception Ratio | Empirical EGB measurement |
| 9 | $\text{Gap}_t = X_0 \left[ (1+r)^t - 1 - r t \right]$ | Linear-vs-exponential gap | Quantifies bias magnitude |
| 10 | $A = P \left[ \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r} \right]$ | Future Value of an annuity | Retirement planning |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Discipline:** In KTU answer sheets, always state the *units* of $r$ (per year, per month) and $t$ (years, months). Mismatch of units is the #1 reason students lose marks in numerical questions.

---

## 2.6 Engineering & Economic Utility of EGB

For a B.Tech student, recognizing EGB has direct practical value:

- **Capital budgeting:** Estimating the future cost of a 10-year infrastructure project requires exponential discounting, not linear scaling.
- **Loan decisions:** A 12% annual rate on a 7-year education loan doesn't *feel* like 7×12% = 84% — it actually equals  $X_0 (1.12)^7 - X_0 \approx 1.218 X_0$, i.e., a 121.8% cumulative interest.
- **Network/traffic planning:** Doubling back-of-envelope via Rule of 72 lets you quickly forecast when capacity will be exhausted.
- **Energy & climate models:** Carbon accumulation, solar cell efficiency improvements, and battery cost declines all follow near-exponential paths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the Brain Underestimates

### 🎯 Objective
Prove that the *linear* mental model **always** underestimates the *true* exponential value for $r > 0$ and $t > 1$.

### 🔢 Setup
We want to show:

$$(1 + r t) < (1 + r)^t \quad \text{for } r > 0, \, t > 1$$

### Step 1 — Apply the Binomial Theorem
Expand $(1+r)^t$ for integer $t$:

$$(1+r)^t = \sum_{k=0}^{t} \binom{t}{k} r^k = 1 + t r + \binom{t}{2} r^2 + \binom{t}{3} r^3 + \dots + r^t$$

### Step 2 — Truncate and Compare
The first two terms give us:

$$(1+r)^t \geq 1 + t r$$

with **strict inequality** whenever at least one higher-order term is positive (which is true for $r > 0$, $t \geq 2$).

### Step 3 — Add the Omitted Terms
The omitted terms are:

$$\Delta = \binom{t}{2} r^2 + \binom{t}{3} r^3 + \dots + r^t = \sum_{k=2}^{t} \binom{t}{k} r^k > 0$$

### Step 4 — Conclude
Therefore:

$$(1 + r)^t = 1 + t r + \Delta > 1 + r t \quad \square$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The "extra" gap $\Delta$ *is* the Exponential Growth Bias. It is the cumulative effect of the compounding terms $\binom{t}{k} r^k$ that the linear mental model throws away.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exact Doubling Time (Discrete Case)

### 🎯 Objective
Find the smallest integer $t$ such that $X_t \geq 2 X_0$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Equation
$$X_0 (1+r)^t = 2 X_0 \implies (1+r)^t = 2$$

### Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm
$$t \ln(1 + r) = \ln(2)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $t$
$$t = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1 + r)}$$

This is the **exact** doubling time.

### Step 4 — Approximate Using Series Expansion
For small $r$, use $\ln(1+r) \approx r - \frac{r^2}{2} + \frac{r^3}{3} - \dots$:

$$\ln(1+r) \approx r \quad \text{(first-order)}$$

Therefore:

$$t \approx \frac{\ln(2)}{r} \approx \frac{0.693}{r}$$

### Step 5 — Rule of 72 in Percent
If $r$ is expressed as a percentage (e.g., 7 means $r = 0.07$):

$$t \approx \frac{0.693}{0.07} = 9.90 \text{ years}$$

Using Rule of 72: $72/7 \approx 10.29$ years. The error is about 4%, which is acceptable for mental math.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (Classic KTU-style)

> A student takes an education loan of **₹5,00,000** at **10% per annum** simple-compounded interest. They mentally estimate that after 7 years they will owe:
> $$5{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.10 \times 7) = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.70 = \text{₹}8{,}50{,}000$$
> What is the *actual* amount, and what is the EGB gap?

### Step 1 — True Exponential Calculation
$$X_7^{\text{TRUE}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (1.10)^7$$

### Step 2 — Compute $(1.10)^7$ Step by Step
$$(1.10)^2 = 1.21$$
$$(1.10)^4 = (1.21)^2 = 1.4641$$
$$(1.10)^6 = 1.4641 \times 1.21 = 1.771561$$
$$(1.10)^7 = 1.771561 \times 1.10 = 1.9487171$$

### Step 3 — Final True Value
$$X_7^{\text{TRUE}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.9487171 = \text{₹}9{,}74{,}358.55$$

### Step 4 — Compute the EGB Gap
$$\text{Gap} = 9{,}74{,}358.55 - 8{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}24{,}358.55$$

### Step 5 — Perception Ratio
$$\text{PR} = \frac{8{,}50{,}000}{9{,}74{,}358.55} \approx 0.8723$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The student's brain underestimated the debt by **₹1,24,358** — a *huge* gap. The PR of 0.87 indicates a strong EGB. This is the bias the KTU examiner will expect you to *quantify and explain* in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Annuity Future Value with EGB

Suppose you invest ₹1,000 *at the end of each year* for $n$ years at rate $r$. The true future value is:

$$FV = 1000 \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$$

The brain, using linear reasoning, often estimates:

$$FV^{\text{linear}} = 1000 \cdot n$$

The EGB ratio for an annuity:

$$\text{PR}_{\text{annuity}} = \frac{n \cdot r}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$

For $r = 0.08$, $n = 30$:

$$\text{PR} = \frac{30 \times 0.08}{(1.08)^{30} - 1} = \frac{2.4}{10.0627 - 1} = \frac{2.4}{9.0627} \approx 0.265$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A PR of 0.265 means the brain thinks the retirement corpus will be **only ~27%** of its actual value. This is one of the strongest EGB effects observed empirically — and the reason most people *massively* under-save.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — How EGB Forms in the Brain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: growth rate r and horizon t] --> B{Brain Cognitive System}
    B --> C[Route 1: True Exponential Model]
    B --> D[Route 2: Mental Linear Shortcut]
    C --> E[Correct Forecast: X0(1+r)^t]
    D --> F[Underestimate: X0(1+rt)]
    E --> G[Decision Output A]
    F --> H[Decision Output B]
    G --> I[Optimal Action e.g. Save Enough]
    H --> J[Sub-optimal Action e.g. Under-save]
    I --> K[Outcome: Goal Achieved]
    J --> L[Outcome: Shortfall at Goal]
    L --> M[Post-hoc Surprise: Where did the gap come from?]
    M --> N[EGB realized ex post]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *same* numerical input is processed by two cognitive routes. Route 2 (the linear shortcut) is *faster, easier, and more automatic* — but produces a systematically low forecast. The downstream consequence is the under-saving / under-preparation pattern observed across populations.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — The EGB Causal Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Long Time Horizons] --> B[Compound Multiplicative Growth]
    B --> C[Exponential Trajectory]
    C --> D{Mental Simulation}
    D --> E[Linearization Heuristic]
    E --> F[Underestimated Future Value]
    F --> G[Inter-temporal Choice Error]
    G --> H[Behavioural Manifestation]
    H --> H1[Under-saving for Retirement]
    H --> H2[Underestimating Loan Burden]
    H --> H3[Under-preparing for Climate Risk]
    H --> H4[Underestimating Tech Growth e.g. Moore's Law]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The block-level architecture maps the *cause-effect chain* from the structural feature (long horizon) through the cognitive mechanism (linearization) to observable outcomes. This is a standard "mechanism-map" structure expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Curves — True Exponential vs. Linear Mental Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1 [Visual Comparison of Mental Models]
        P0[Period 0: Both at X0] --> P1
        P1[Period 1: Lines nearly coincide] --> P2
        P2[Period 2: Tiny gap emerges] --> P3
        P3[Period 3: Noticeable divergence] --> P5
        P5[Period 5: Clear fan-out] --> P10
        P10[Period 10: Wide gap, exponential pulls away] --> P20
        P20[Period 20: Massive gap, exponential is many times larger] --> P30
        P30[Period 30: Linear forecast is roughly 25 percent of true value at r equals 8 percent]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a *qualitative time-series block diagram* showing how the two trajectories fan apart. The arrows are not literal coordinates — they represent progression along a time axis. The widening gap at each step *visually encodes* the cumulative effect of the omitted higher-order terms in the binomial expansion.

---

## 4.4 EGB Modulators — Factors That Amplify or Dampen the Bias

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Exponential Growth Bias Magnitude] --> R1[Modulator 1: Time Horizon t]
    Q --> R2[Modulator 2: Growth Rate r]
    Q --> R3[Modulator 3: Numerical Literacy]
    Q --> R4[Modulator 4: Affect and Salience]
    Q --> R5[Modulator 5: Feedback Frequency]
    R1 --> S1[Longer t --> Stronger Bias]
    R2 --> S2[Higher r --> Stronger Bias]
    R3 --> S3[Higher literacy --> Weaker Bias]
    R4 --> S4[Emotional salience --> Weaker Bias]
    R5 --> S5[Frequent feedback --> Weaker Bias]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Five contextual factors modulate the strength of EGB. Note that *time* and *rate* amplify it (structural), while *literacy*, *affect*, and *feedback* dampen it (corrective).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Exponential Growth Bias. Why is it considered a systematic (not random) error in judgment?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (≈ 3-mark structure):

> **Definition:** Exponential Growth Bias (EGB) is the systematic tendency of individuals to *underestimate* the future magnitude of quantities growing at a constant percentage rate, by mentally applying linear (additive) extrapolation instead of exponential (multiplicative) projection.
>
> **Why systematic:**
> 1. The bias is *directional* — it always produces an underestimate (never an overestimate) for $r > 0$.
> 2. The bias is *predictable in magnitude* — it grows with horizon $t$ and rate $r$ in a mathematically well-defined way: $\text{Gap} = X_0 [(1+r)^t - 1 - rt]$.
> 3. It arises from a *shared cognitive architecture* — the human brain's preference for additive over multiplicative reasoning.
> 4. Empirical evidence (Wagenaar & Sagaria 1975; Levy & Tasoff 2016) shows the bias is observed across populations, cultures, and even among numerate professionals.
>
> Therefore, EGB is a **structural feature of human numerical cognition**, not a random mistake.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Rule of 72 and explain why it is useful in detecting EGB.**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

> The **Rule of 72** states that the time required for a quantity growing at an annual rate of $r$ percent to double is approximately $72 / r$ years.
>
> **Why it is useful in detecting EGB:**
> 1. It gives a *quick mental benchmark* for compound growth.
> 2. When asked to estimate doubling times, people who fall for EGB typically give answers that are *too long* (because they linearize). Comparing their answers to the Rule-of-72 prediction reveals the bias.
> 3. For instance, a 7% rate truly doubles in ~10.2 years; an EGB-prone respondent often guesses 14–15 years.
> 4. The rule itself is empirical evidence that the *correct* doubling times are *much shorter* than intuitive guesses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $r = 7$ instead of $r = 0.07$ in formulas. Always use the decimal form *inside* formulas, percent form *outside*.
> 2. **Forgetting to compare:** A 14-mark question on EGB almost always demands a *comparison* between true and estimated values. Computing only one side will fetch at most 7 marks.
> 3. **No definition in numerical answers:** Even numerical questions expect a 1–2 line conceptual anchor ("EGB is the underestimation of compounding effects…"). Skipping it loses ~2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring horizon and rate:** Many students compute a single example and stop. Examiners reward *analysis* of how the gap changes with $t$ and $r$.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** A B.Tech graduate takes an education loan of **₹6,00,000** at **9% per annum** compounded annually for **5 years**. Compute (i) the actual amount owed after 5 years, and (ii) the amount the graduate *mentally estimates* using the linear model. Quantify the Exponential Growth Bias gap. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss **two** real-world domains (other than personal finance) where Exponential Growth Bias leads to systematically poor decisions. For each, explain the mechanism. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(a):

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $X_0 = 6{,}00{,}000$
- $r = 0.09$
- $t = 5$ years

**Step 2 — True exponential value.**
$$X_5^{\text{TRUE}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times (1.09)^5$$

Compute $(1.09)^5$ step by step:
$$(1.09)^2 = 1.1881$$
$$(1.09)^4 = (1.1881)^2 = 1.41158161$$
$$(1.09)^5 = 1.41158161 \times 1.09 = 1.53862395$$

Therefore:
$$X_5^{\text{TRUE}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 1.53862395 = \text{₹}9{,}23{,}174.37$$

**Step 3 — Linear mental estimate.**
$$X_5^{\text{EST}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.09 \times 5) = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 1.45 = \text{₹}8{,}70{,}000$$

**Step 4 — EGB gap.**
$$\text{Gap} = 9{,}23{,}174.37 - 8{,}70{,}000 = \text{₹}53{,}174.37$$

**Step 5 — Perception ratio.**
$$\text{PR} = \frac{8{,}70{,}000}{9{,}23{,}174.37} \approx 0.9424$$

> The graduate *underestimates* the loan by **₹53,174** — a perception ratio of **0.94**, indicating a moderate EGB effect.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(b):

**Domain 1: Climate Change and CO₂ Accumulation**
- Atmospheric CO₂ grows at roughly **0.5% per year** in concentration, but emissions grow faster.
- Public and policy discourse often frames climate impact as a *linear* projection of current emissions ("a little more pollution each year").
- The exponential reality is that small annual percentage increases compound into dramatic concentrations within decades.
- The EGB gap here delays serious mitigation policies.

**Domain 2: Public Health — Epidemic Spread**
- Viral transmission with reproduction number $R_0 > 1$ produces exponential case growth.
- Citizens, and sometimes policymakers, mentally simulate "the same number of new cases each day" (linear model).
- In reality, cases double every few days (Rule of 72 applies: a rate of 10% per day yields doubling in 7.2 days).
- The EGB gap leads to late lockdowns, hospital under-preparation, and vaccine complacency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative valid domains:** Technological growth (Moore's Law), population growth, debt-to-GDP ratios, antibiotic resistance, biodiversity loss, social-media follower growth.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **exact doubling time** for a quantity growing at a constant rate $r$ per period. Show how the **Rule of 72** is obtained as an approximation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 30-year-old engineer invests **₹2,000 per month** in a retirement fund earning **8% per annum** compounded monthly. They estimate their retirement corpus at age 60 will be approximately **₹50 lakh** (linear mental model: $2000 \times 12 \times 30$). Calculate the *true* corpus using the annuity formula and quantify the EGB. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(a):

**Step 1 — Exact Doubling Time (Discrete)**
We seek $t$ such that $X_t = 2 X_0$:

$$X_0 (1+r)^t = 2 X_0$$
$$(1+r)^t = 2$$
$$t \cdot \ln(1+r) = \ln(2)$$
$$\boxed{t = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1+r)}}$$

**Step 2 — Approximate using Taylor Series**
For small $r$:
$$\ln(1+r) = r - \frac{r^2}{2} + \frac{r^3}{3} - \dots \approx r$$

So:
$$t \approx \frac{\ln(2)}{r} \approx \frac{0.693}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Percentage Form**
Let $r_{\%}$ denote the rate in percent, so $r = r_{\%}/100$. Then:

$$t \approx \frac{0.693}{r_{\%}/100} = \frac{69.3}{r_{\%}}$$

**Step 4 — Rule of 72 as a Refined Approximation**
Using $\ln(2) = 0.6931\dots$, the *exact* first-order approximation gives $69.3 / r_{\%}$. The **Rule of 72** replaces 69.3 with **72** for two practical reasons:
1. **Divisibility:** 72 has more integer divisors (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 12, 18, 24, 36, 72), making mental division easier for common rates (6%, 8%, 9%, 12%).
2. **Compensation for higher-order terms:** The linear approximation $t \approx 0.693/r$ *under*-estimates the doubling time at moderate rates because the higher-order negative terms in $\ln(1+r)$ shrink the denominator. The 72 rule is calibrated to over-compensate, giving answers accurate to within 1% for $5\% \leq r_{\%} \leq 10\%$.

$$\boxed{T_{\text{double}} \approx \frac{72}{r_{\%}} \text{ years}} \quad \square$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(b):

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- Monthly investment $P = 2000$
- Annual rate $r_a = 0.08$, monthly rate $r = 0.08/12 = 0.00666\overline{6}$
- Horizon: 30 years, so $n = 30 \times 12 = 360$ months

**Step 2 — Linear mental estimate.**
$$FV^{\text{linear}} = 2000 \times 360 = \text{₹}7{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3 — True future value of annuity.**
$$FV^{\text{true}} = P \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(1+r)^n$.**
Let $i = 0.08/12 = 0.0066667$, $n = 360$.

$$(1 + i)^{360} = (1.0066667)^{360}$$

Using the equivalent continuous rate:
$$(1.0066667)^{360} \approx e^{360 \times \ln(1.0066667)} \approx e^{360 \times 0.0066445} \approx e^{2.392} \approx 10.9357$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**
$$FV^{\text{true}} = 2000 \times \frac{10.9357 - 1}{0.0066667} = 2000 \times \frac{9.9357}{0.0066667}$$
$$FV^{\text{true}} = 2000 \times 1490.36 = \text{₹}29{,}80{,}720 \approx \text{₹}29.81 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 6 — Quantify the EGB.**
$$\text{Gap} = 29.81 - 7.20 = \text{₹}22.61 \text{ lakh}$$
$$\text{PR} = \frac{7.20}{29.81} \approx 0.2415$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The engineer's mental model captures only about **24%** of the actual retirement corpus. This is a **massive** EGB effect — a textbook example of how compounding transforms modest monthly savings into a substantial retirement nest egg, and how the human brain fails to internalize that transformation.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your last-minute revision sheet.**

- ☐ **Definition (high priority):** EGB = systematic *underestimation* of compounding exponential growth due to mental linearization.
- ☐ **Core equation:** $X_t = X_0 (1+r)^t$ — memorize it.
- ☐ **Linear mental model:** $X_t^{\text{est}} = X_0 (1 + rt)$ — always compare against the true value.
- ☐ **EGB gap formula:** $\text{Gap} = X_0 [(1+r)^t - 1 - rt]$.
- ☐ **Perception Ratio (PR):** Estimated ÷ True; PR < 1 ⇒ bias present.
- ☐ **Rule of 72:** Doubling time $\approx 72 / r_{\%}$ — useful for quick MCQ-style answers.
- ☐ **Exact doubling time:** $t = \ln(2)/\ln(1+r) \approx 0.693/r$.
- ☐ **Continuous form:** $X(t) = X_0 e^{rt}$.
- ☐ **Annuity FV formula:** $FV = P[(1+r)^n - 1]/r$ — used in retirement questions.
- ☐ **Modulators of EGB:** horizon $t$, rate $r$, literacy, affect, feedback frequency.
- ☐ **Real-world domains:** finance, climate, public health, technology, debt, population.
- ☐ **Always state units** for $r$ and $t$ in numerical answers (per year, per month, etc.).
- ☐ **Always show the comparison** between linear estimate and true exponential in numerical answers — this is where the "EGB demonstration" marks come from.
- ☐ **Wagenaar & Sagaria (1975)** — classical empirical reference for EGB.
- ☐ **Levy and Tasoff (2016)** — modern formalization of the bias in financial contexts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Pitfall Callout:** The most common mark-loss pattern is *answering a numerical question with only a numerical value and no conceptual framing*. KTU examiners are mandated (per the 2024 Scheme OBE pattern) to award marks for *Course Outcome (CO) demonstration*, which requires you to (1) name the concept, (2) state the assumption, (3) compute, and (4) interpret. A 14-mark question with only steps 2–3 will cap at ~8 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining "Other Biases" in Behavioural Economics

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECBT866 – Behavioural Economics (Module 1)**, the umbrella phrase **"Other Biases"** refers to the *secondary cluster of systematic cognitive and emotional deviations* from the neoclassical rational-agent model that are not classified under the primary biases (Loss Aversion, Anchoring, Framing, Mental Accounting, Endowment Effect, etc.) covered earlier in the module. Collectively, they explain **why humans make predictably irrational decisions** in choices involving risk, time, social context, and self-perception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** *Cognitive biases are systematic patterns of deviation from rationality — they cause individuals to perceive, interpret, and act on information in ways that contradict the predictions of expected utility theory (EUT) and standard rational-choice models.*

### 1.2 Master List of Biases Covered in "Other Biases"

| # | Bias | Decision Domain |
|---|------|-----------------|
| 1 | Status Quo Bias | Default/Inaction |
| 2 | Sunk Cost Fallacy | Past Investment |
| 3 | Confirmation Bias | Information Search |
| 4 | Availability Heuristic | Probability Judgement |
| 5 | Representativeness Heuristic | Categorisation |
| 6 | Overconfidence Bias | Self-Evaluation |
| 7 | Optimism & Pessimism Bias | Future Outlook |
| 8 | Herd Behaviour | Social Influence |
| 9 | Self-Control / Present Bias | Intertemporal Choice |
| 10 | Hedonic Adaptation | Emotional Reference Point |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Broken Elevator" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you work on the **5th floor** of a building. The lift has been broken for a year. Every day you climb the stairs — your colleagues climb too. If the lift were *fixed* tomorrow, would you take it? Most people say "yes" — but statistically, the percentage who actually switch is only about **30–40%**.
>
> This is **Status Quo Bias in action**: even when change is objectively superior, humans disproportionately *cling to existing defaults*. Multiply this single example across savings, healthcare, retirement plans, and product choices — and you see how a small mental quirk creates trillion-dollar market inefficiencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently set 3-mark Part A questions asking students to *define* a single bias and give **one real-life example**. Always pair the definition with an example — marks are split roughly 50/50.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Status Quo Bias

**Definition:** The tendency to prefer the *current state of affairs* and resist any deviation, even when the alternative is demonstrably better.

**Operational Logic (Why it occurs):**
- *Loss aversion asymmetry* — moving away from the status quo is *coded by the brain as a loss*.
- *Regret avoidance* — switching and failing feels worse than not switching at all.
- *Cognitive effort minimisation* — doing nothing is the lowest-energy path.

$$P_{\text{stick}} \;>\; P_{\text{switch}} \quad \text{even when} \quad U_{\text{switch}} \;>\; U_{\text{stick}}$$

Where $P_{\text{stick}}$ is the probability of retaining the current option, and $U$ denotes subjective utility.

**Real-world utility:** Pension auto-enrolment (NPS in India), organ donation opt-in vs. opt-out policies, software default settings.

---

### 2.2 Sunk Cost Fallacy

**Definition:** The irrational tendency to continue an endeavour once an investment (money, time, effort) has been made, *because* of that prior investment — even when abandoning would be the rational choice.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The sunk cost fallacy is *not* the same as "throwing good money after bad" *as a conscious strategy*. It is a **cognitive bias** — the decision-maker is *unaware* that the prior cost is irrelevant.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Investor has paid an unrecoverable cost $C_{\text{sunk}}$.
2. Future expected utility of continuing: $E[U_{\text{continue}}]$.
3. Future expected utility of quitting: $E[U_{\text{quit}}]$.
4. Rational rule: $\text{continue iff} \quad E[U_{\text{continue}}] > E[U_{\text{quit}}]$.
5. Bias: decision-maker instead uses $\text{continue iff} \quad E[U_{\text{continue}}] + C_{\text{sunk}} > E[U_{\text{quit}}] + C_{\text{sunk}}$, *double-counting* the irrelevant cost.

---

### 2.3 Confirmation Bias

**Definition:** The tendency to *search for, interpret, favour, and recall* information in a way that confirms one's pre-existing beliefs, while giving disproportionately less weight to evidence that contradicts them.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Agent holds belief $B$.
2. Receives signal $S$ which may support $\text{support}(B)$ or $\text{contradict}(B)$.
3. Subjective weight assigned: $w_{\text{support}}(S) > w_{\text{contradict}}(S)$.
4. Posterior belief $P(B \vert S)$ shifts asymmetrically.

---

### 2.4 Availability Heuristic

**Definition:** Probability of an event is judged by the *ease* with which instances come to mind, rather than by objective base rates.

$$P_{\text{subjective}}(E) \;\approx\; f(\text{memorability}, \text{recency}, \text{emotional salience of } E)$$

**Classic example:** People overestimate the probability of plane crashes (because crashes are dramatic and newsworthy) while underestimating the probability of car accidents (which are statistically far more common but cognitively "ordinary").

---

### 2.5 Representativeness Heuristic

**Definition:** Probability is assessed by how well an object *resembles* a prototype of its category, ignoring base rates and sample size.

**Famous Tversky-Kahneman experiment:**
> Linda is 31, single, outspoken, and majored in philosophy. As a student, she was deeply concerned with discrimination and social justice. Which is more probable?
> (a) Linda is a bank teller.
> (b) Linda is a bank teller *and* is active in the feminist movement.

**Result:** ~85% chose (b) — violating the conjunction rule $P(A \cap B) \leq P(A)$.

$$P(A \cap B) \;\leq\; P(A) \quad \text{(Conjunction Rule — often violated)}$$

---

### 2.6 Overconfidence Bias

**Definition:** A person's subjective confidence in their judgements is *reliably greater* than their objective accuracy. Three sub-forms:

| Sub-form | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Overestimation** | Believing you are better than you are | "I'm an above-average driver" (held by 80% of drivers) |
| **Overplacement** | Believing *you* are better than *others* | Ranking self above the median |
| **Overprecision** | Excessive certainty in forecasts | 90% confidence intervals that hold only 60% of the time |

---

### 2.7 Optimism & Pessimism Bias

- **Optimism Bias:** Unreasonable belief that negative events are *less likely* to happen to *me* than to others. $P_{\text{personal}}(E_{\text{bad}}) < P_{\text{average}}(E_{\text{bad}})$.
- **Pessimism Bias:** Mirror — the opposite, often seen in depression-prone populations.

---

### 2.8 Herd Behaviour

**Definition:** The tendency for individuals to *mimic the actions of a larger group*, ignoring their own private information. The product of *social proof* and *informational cascades*.

$$a_i^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \big[ \; U_i(a, \theta_i) \;+\; \lambda \cdot \text{signal from } a_{-i} \; \big]$$

Where $\lambda$ is the *social-weighting parameter* (high $\lambda$ = strong herd behaviour).

**Historical example:** Stock market bubbles (Dot-com 2000, Housing 2008), crypto rallies, fashion fads.

---

### 2.9 Self-Control / Present Bias (Dynamic Inconsistency)

**Definition:** The tendency to *overweight* immediate rewards relative to delayed rewards, leading to dynamically inconsistent plans.

**The $\beta$-$\delta$ Model (Laibson, 1997):**

$$U_t = u(c_t) + \beta \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{T-t} \delta^{k} \cdot u(c_{t+k})$$

Where:
- $0 \le \beta \le 1$ = present-bias parameter ( $\beta = 1$ is exponential discounting, $\beta < 1$ is present bias).
- $0 \le \delta \le 1$ = standard exponential discount factor.
- The *quasi-hyperbolic* form $\beta \cdot \delta^{k}$ captures *extra* discounting of any future reward vs. an immediate one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Present bias explains retirement under-saving, procrastination, and credit-card debt — favourite 14-mark Part B topics.

---

### 2.10 Hedonic Adaptation

**Definition:** The tendency to *return to a stable baseline of happiness* after positive or negative life events. Buying a luxury car gives a happiness *spike* of a few weeks; losing a job causes a *dip* of a few months — both eventually decay.

### 2.11 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Bias | Core "Equation" / Rule | Real-world Trigger |
|---|------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Status Quo | $P_{\text{stick}} > P_{\text{switch}}$ | Pension auto-enrolment |
| 2 | Sunk Cost | Continue iff $E[U_C] + C_{\text{sunk}} > E[U_Q]$ (irrelevant) | "I've already paid, might as well…" |
| 3 | Confirmation | $w(\text{support} \vert B) > w(\text{contradict} \vert B)$ | Selective news reading |
| 4 | Availability | $P_{\text{subj}}(E) \propto \text{memorability}(E)$ | Plane-crash fear |
| 5 | Representativeness | $P(A \cap B) \le P(A)$ violated | Linda problem |
| 6 | Overconfidence | $\text{Confidence} > \text{Accuracy}$ | 90% CI holding 60% |
| 7 | Optimism | $P_{\text{me}}(\text{bad}) < P_{\text{others}}(\text{bad})$ | "It won't happen to me" |
| 8 | Herd | Imitate prior actions $a_{-i}$ | Stock bubbles |
| 9 | Present Bias | $\beta \cdot \delta^{k} \;\; (\beta < 1)$ | Procrastination |
| 10 | Hedonic Adapt. | $H_t \to H_{\text{baseline}}$ over time | Lottery-win studies |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Present Bias Produces Inconsistent Choices

We start from the standard exponential discount model and show why $\beta < 1$ generates *dynamic inconsistency*.

**Step 1: Standard Exponential Discounting (rational benchmark).**

A consumer at period $t=0$ chooses between:
- $c_0$ today (worth $u(c_0)$), and
- $c_1$ tomorrow (worth $\delta \cdot u(c_1)$), with $0 < \delta < 1$.

**Step 2: Quasi-Hyperbolic Discount Function (Laibson 1997).**

The decision-maker instead evaluates the future reward with an *extra* factor $\beta$:

$$U_0 \;=\; u(c_0) \;+\; \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_1)$$

**Step 3: Choice rule — take $c_0$ iff $U_0 > 0$, i.e.:**

$$u(c_0) \;>\; \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_1)$$

**Step 4: One day later (at $t=1$), the same agent now evaluates:**

$$U_1 \;=\; u(c_1) \;+\; \beta \cdot \delta^{0} \cdot u(c_2) \;=\; u(c_1) \;+\; \beta \cdot u(c_2)$$

**Step 5: Compare the two evaluations.**

At $t=0$ the future $c_1$ is discounted by factor $\beta \cdot \delta$.
At $t=1$ the same $c_1$ is no longer "future" — it is now, so it is **not** multiplied by $\beta$.

Therefore the *effective* value of $c_1$ **jumps** from $\beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_1)$ to $u(c_1)$.

**Step 6: Consequence — preference reversal (dynamic inconsistency).**

If $u(c_0) < \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_1) < u(c_1)$, then at $t=0$ the agent prefers $c_1$ (delayed reward), but by $t=1$ the same agent reverses and takes $c_0$-equivalent immediate reward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This mathematical proof of preference reversal is a *favourite KTU 14-mark question* — examiners expect the student to explicitly show that $\beta \ne 1$ breaks time-consistency, whereas $\beta = 1$ recovers the standard exponential (consistent) case.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Sunk Cost Fallacy in Expected-Value Terms

**Step 1:** Define the choice. Project $X$ has already cost $C_{\text{sunk}} = 100$ (non-recoverable). The agent must choose: *complete* $X$ at additional cost $C_{\text{add}} = 50$, or *abandon* and start project $Y$ with cost $C_Y = 40$.

**Step 2:** Compute expected payoffs. Suppose:
- $P(X_{\text{success}}) = 0.3$, payoff $= 500$.
- $P(Y_{\text{success}}) = 0.6$, payoff $= 300$.

**Step 3:** Rational expected values.

$$E[V_X] = 0.3 \times 500 - 50 = 150 - 50 = 100$$

$$E[V_Y] = 0.6 \times 300 - 40 = 180 - 40 = 140$$

**Step 4:** Rational choice: choose $Y$, since $E[V_Y] = 140 > E[V_X] = 100$. The $C_{\text{sunk}} = 100$ is *sunk* and excluded from the calculation.

**Step 5:** Biased expected values (agent commits the sunk-cost fallacy).

$$E[V_X^{\text{biased}}] = 0.3 \times 500 - 50 - \tfrac{1}{2} \times C_{\text{sunk}} = 100 - 50 = 50$$

$$E[V_Y^{\text{biased}}] = 0.6 \times 300 - 40 - 0 = 140$$

**Step 6:** Even with the bias, $Y$ wins on a *correct* forward-looking comparison. The fallacy manifests most acutely when $\text{bias-factor} \times C_{\text{sunk}}$ is large enough to flip the sign of the comparison:

$$\text{Bias flips choice iff} \quad \alpha \cdot C_{\text{sunk}} > E[V_Y] - E[V_X^{\text{unbiased}}]$$

where $\alpha$ is the irrational sunk-weight the agent assigns. With $\alpha = 1$ and $C_{\text{sunk}} = 100 > 40$, the agent chooses $X$ — completing the project *only because of the prior loss*.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bias × Decision-Domain × Mitigation

| Bias | Decision Domain | Brain Mechanism | Mitigation Tool |
|------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Status Quo | Defaults | Loss-aversion of change | Make better option the default |
| Sunk Cost | Project continuation | Loss aversion + commitment | "Ignore the past" checklist |
| Confirmation | News, research | Selective exposure | Steel-man opposing view |
| Availability | Risk assessment | Vividness weighting | Consult actuarial tables |
| Representativeness | Classification | Pattern matching | Use base rates |
| Overconfidence | Forecasting | Subjective certainty | Calibration training |
| Optimism | Personal risk | Wishful thinking | Pre-mortem analysis |
| Herd | Markets, fashion | Social proof | Set stop-loss rules |
| Present Bias | Saving, health | Hyperbolic weighting | Commitment devices |
| Hedonic Adapt. | Consumption | Neuro-adaptation | Buy experiences, not goods |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the "Other Biases" Cluster

```mermaid

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Prior Investment Made] --> P2[Commitment to Recover It]

    P5 --> P1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Pre-existing Belief B] --> Q2[Seek Evidence Supporting B]
```
