negative lambda times
negative x to power beta"] C --> E["v of x equals
x to power alpha
alpha approx 0.88"] D --> F["Key Insight
Loss of 100 hurts
2.25 times more than
gain of 100 pleases"] E --> F F --> G["Diminishing Sensitivity
Marginal value decreases
as magnitude grows"] ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Two-Phase Processing Architecture ```mermaid graph TD P1["Raw Prospect
Multiple outcomes and
objective probabilities"] --> P2["Phase 1: Editing Stage"] P2 --> P2a["Coding
Gain or Loss
vs Reference"] P2 --> P2b["Combination
Merge identical
outcomes"] P2 --> P2c["Segregation

from risky"] P2 --> P2d["Cancellation
Ignore shared
components"] P2 --> P2e["Simplification
Round values"] P2a --> Q1["Edited Prospect"] P2b --> Q1 P2c --> Q1 P2d --> Q1 P2e --> Q1 Q1 --> P3["Phase 2: Evaluation Stage"] P3 --> P3a["Apply Value Function
v of x using
alpha and beta"] P3 --> P3b["Apply Probability
Weighting Function
pi of p"] P3a --> P4["Prospect Theory Value
V equals sum of
pi of p times v of x"] P3b --> P4 P4 --> P5["Final Decision
Choose prospect with
highest V value"] ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Fourfold Pattern of Risk Attitudes ```mermaid graph TD ROOT["Reference Point
Current State"] --> SPLIT{"Compare Outcome
to Reference"} SPLIT -->|x greater than 0| GAIN["Gain Domain
v of x concave"] SPLIT -->|x less than 0| LOSS["Loss Domain
v of x convex"] GAIN --> HP["High Probability Gain
Risk Averse
Example: Prefer 3000 sure
over 4000 with 0.8 chance"] GAIN --> LP["Low Probability Gain
Risk Seeking
Example: Lottery tickets
gamble for small chance"] LOSS --> HL["High Probability Loss
Risk Seeking
Example: Gamble to avoid
certain large loss"] LOSS --> LL["Low Probability Loss
Risk Averse
Example: Insurance purchase
for rare catastrophe"] HP --> NOTE["Asymmetric response
driven by concave v
and pi of p less than p"] LP --> NOTE2["Overweighting of small p
drives lottery demand"] HL --> NOTE3["Convexity in loss domain
drives gambling to escape"] LL --> NOTE4["Overweighting of small p
drives insurance demand"] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Probability Weighting Function Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A["Objective Probability
p in 0 to 1"] --> B{"Magnitude Check"} B -->|p very small
less than 0.1| C["Overweighted
pi of p greater than p
Example: 0.01 becomes 0.05"] B -->|p moderate
0.1 to 0.9| D["Underweighted
pi of p less than p
Example: 0.5 becomes 0.32"] B -->|p very large
greater than 0.9| E["Approximately equal
pi of p approaches p
as p approaches 1"] C --> F["Real World Effect
Lottery appeal
Insurance demand"] D --> G["Real World Effect
Probability neglect
in everyday risk"] E --> H["Real World Effect
Certainty effect
preserved in PT"] ``` ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Reference Point Determination Flow ```mermaid graph TD RP["Reference Point
w0"] --> SRC{"Source of
Reference"} SRC --> S1["Status Quo
Current wealth
or position"] SRC --> S2["Expectation
What was promised
or anticipated"] SRC --> S3["Social Comparison
Peers or norms"] SRC --> S4["Aspiration Level
Goal or target"] SRC --> S5["Non-Acquisition
What one does NOT have
in endowment effect"] S1 --> OUT["Outcomes coded as
gains or losses
relative to w0"] S2 --> OUT S3 --> OUT S4 --> OUT S5 --> OUT OUT --> APP["Applications
Marketing framing
Salary negotiations
Insurance pricing
Public policy framing"] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > These questions are modeled after the typical KTU 3-mark questions that test direct conceptual recall and short explanations. --- ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Reference Dependence. How does it differ from the classical economic assumption of utility based on final wealth states?** **Model Answer (Step-by-step):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Reference Dependence is the principle that the value of an outcome is evaluated as a gain or loss relative to a *reference point* (typically the status quo), rather than in absolute terms. - **Classical view (1 mark):** In classical utility theory (Bernoulli, von Neumann-Morgenstern), utility $U(w)$ is a function of *final wealth* $w$. The reference point is implicit and fixed at zero. - **Key difference (1 mark):** PT makes the reference point $w_0$ *explicit and variable* — it can shift based on context, expectations, social comparisons, and prior outcomes. The carrier of value is the *deviation* $\Delta w = w - w_0$, not the level $w$. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain the three core properties of the Prospect Theory value function.** **Model Answer (Step-by-step):** 1. **Reference Dependence (1 mark):** $v(0) = 0$. Outcomes are evaluated relative to a reference point, not in absolute terms. 2. **Diminishing Sensitivity (1 mark):** The value function is concave in gains ($v''(x) < 0$ for $x > 0$) and convex in losses ($v''(x) > 0$ for $x < 0$). The marginal psychological impact of each additional unit decreases. 3. **Loss Aversion (1 mark):** $v(-x) < -v(x)$ for $x > 0$. Losses are weighted approximately **2.25 times** more heavily than equivalent gains ($\lambda \approx 2.25$). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Following KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: each Part B question has **internal choice** between Option A and Option B. Both options are completely independent. Each question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the two-phase architecture of Prospect Theory: the editing phase and the evaluation phase. Detail at least four operations performed during editing.** **Model Answer:** **Phase 1: Editing (3.5 marks)** Editing is a preliminary analysis stage where the decision-maker restructures the offered prospects to simplify them. The main operations are: - **Coding:** Outcomes are categorized as gains or losses relative to the reference point. For example, "loss of ₹500 from a salary of ₹50,000" is coded as $v(-500)$. - **Combination:** Probabilities attached to identical outcomes are summed. If the prospect offers $v(+100, 0.25)$ and $v(+100, 0.35)$, they are combined into $v(+100, 0.60)$. - **Segregation:** The riskless component of a prospect is separated from the risky component. For example, $(3000, 0.80; 0, 0.20)$ is decomposed into a sure ₹2400 and a gamble $(600, 0.80; -2400, 0.20)$. - **Cancellation:** Shared components of two prospects are ignored. If both prospects offer $v(+500, 1.0)$, this is cancelled and only the differing components are evaluated. - **Simplification:** Rounding of probabilities (e.g., $0.83$ becomes "about 80%") and outcomes (e.g., ₹498 becomes ₹500) for cognitive ease. - **Detection of Dominance:** Dominated prospects (where another prospect is strictly better in at least one state and equal in all others) are discarded. **Phase 2: Evaluation (3.5 marks)** The edited prospects are evaluated using the **Prospect Theory formula**: $$V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \pi(p_i) \cdot v(x_i)$$ where: - $v(x_i)$ is the value function applied to outcome $x_i$ (deviation from reference). - $\pi(p_i)$ is the decision weight derived from the probability weighting function. - The prospect with the higher $V$ is chosen. The evaluation phase applies two key **transformations**: 1. **Value transformation:** Outcomes $x \to v(x)$ using $v(x) = x^{\alpha}$ for gains and $v(x) = -\lambda(-x)^{\beta}$ for losses. 2. **Probability transformation:** Probabilities $p \to \pi(p)$ using a one-parameter or two-parameter weighting function. **The example of the Asian Disease problem** illustrates the editing phase in action — different framings of the same problem lead to different edited prospects, and hence different evaluations. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the Fourfold Pattern of risk attitudes as predicted by Prospect Theory. Provide one real-world example for each of the four cells.** **Model Answer:** The **Fourfold Pattern** is a direct consequence of two features of Prospect Theory: - The **S-shaped value function** (concave in gains, convex in losses). - The **inverse-S shaped probability weighting function** $\pi(p)$ (overweights small probabilities, underweights large probabilities). **Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid graph TD Q1["Cell 1: High Probability Gain
x greater than 0, p greater than 0.5
Risk Averse
Concave v of x dominates"] --> EX1["Example: Prefer certain 3000
over 4000 with p equals 0.8"] Q2["Cell 2: Low Probability Gain
x greater than 0, p less than 0.5
Risk Seeking
pi of p greater than p overweighting"] --> EX2["Example: Buying lottery
tickets at p less than 0.001"] Q3["Cell 3: High Probability Loss
x less than 0, p greater than 0.5
Risk Seeking
Convex v of x in losses"] --> EX3["Example: Gambling to avoid
certain large loss in business"] Q4["Cell 4: Low Probability Loss
x less than 0, p less than 0.5
Risk Averse
pi of p greater than p overweighting"] --> EX4["Example: Buying insurance
for rare catastrophic events"] ``` **Explanation of each cell (4 marks — 1 mark each):** - **Cell 1 — High Probability Gain (Risk Averse):** When a large gain is very likely, diminishing sensitivity makes the certain gain more attractive. Example: Most people prefer ₹3,000 for sure over an 80% chance of ₹4,000. - **Cell 2 — Low Probability Gain (Risk Seeking):** Small probabilities are *overweighted* in PT, so a tiny chance of a huge prize becomes psychologically amplified. Example: Millions of people buy lottery tickets even though the expected value is negative. - **Cell 3 — High Probability Loss (Risk Seeking):** When a large loss is very likely, the convexity of $v(x)$ in the loss domain makes the decision-maker willing to gamble to escape. Example: A startup on the verge of bankruptcy may take a risky loan because the marginal pain of additional loss is diminishing. - **Cell 4 — Low Probability Loss (Risk Averse):** Small probabilities of catastrophic losses are *overweighted*. Example: People pay insurance premiums (negative expected value) against rare events like floods, earthquakes, or flight crashes. **Synthesis:** The fourfold pattern elegantly explains why the same individual can be risk averse in one context (insurance) and risk seeking in another (lottery), violating the stable risk preferences assumed by Expected Utility Theory. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Derive the shape of the Prospect Theory value function $v(x)$ for the gain and loss domains. Show mathematically that the function is concave in gains and convex in losses. Use $\alpha = \beta = 0.88$ and $\lambda = 2.25$ to compute $v(+100)$ and $v(-100)$.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Define $v(x)$ for gains (2 marks)** $$v(x) = x^{\alpha}, \quad x \geq 0, \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1$$ For the gain domain with $x = 100$, $\alpha = 0.88$: $$v(+100) = 100^{0.88}$$ Compute: $\ln(v(+100)) = 0.88 \cdot \ln(100) = 0.88 \cdot 4.6052 = 4.0526$ $$v(+100) = e^{4.0526} \approx 57.54$$ **Step 2: Define $v(x)$ for losses (1 mark)** $$v(x) = -\lambda \cdot (-x)^{\beta}, \quad x < 0, \quad 0 < \beta \leq 1, \quad \lambda > 1$$ For the loss domain with $x = -100$, $\beta = 0.88$, $\lambda = 2.25$: $$v(-100) = -2.25 \cdot (100)^{0.88} = -2.25 \cdot 57.54 \approx -129.47$$ **Step 3: Derive concavity in gains (2 marks)** First derivative: $$\frac{dv}{dx} = \alpha \cdot x^{\alpha - 1}$$ Second derivative: $$\frac{d^2v}{dx^2} = \alpha(\alpha - 1) \cdot x^{\alpha - 2}$$ Since $0 < \alpha < 1$, the term $(\alpha - 1) < 0$. For $x > 0$, $x^{\alpha - 2} > 0$. Therefore: $$\frac{d^2v}{dx^2} < 0 \quad \text{for } x > 0$$ This proves **concavity in the gain domain** (diminishing marginal value). **Step 4: Derive convexity in losses (2 marks)** Let $y = -x > 0$. Then $v(x) = -\lambda y^{\beta}$. First derivative (using chain rule with $dy/dx = -1$): $$\frac{dv}{dx} = -\lambda \beta y^{\beta - 1} \cdot (-1) = \lambda \beta y^{\beta - 1}$$ Second derivative: $$\frac{d^2v}{dx^2} = \lambda \beta (\beta - 1) y^{\beta - 2} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = -\lambda \beta (\beta - 1) y^{\beta - 2}$$ Since $0 < \beta < 1$, $(\beta - 1) < 0$, so $-\lambda \beta (\beta - 1) > 0$. For $y > 0$, $y^{\beta - 2} > 0$. Therefore: $$\frac{d^2v}{dx^2} > 0 \quad \text{for } x < 0$$ This proves **convexity in the loss domain** (diminishing marginal pain). **Summary:** The value function is **S-shaped**, concave in gains, convex in losses, and steeper in the loss domain (loss aversion with $\lambda \approx 2.25$). The ratio $v(-100)/(-v(+100)) = -129.47/-57.54 \approx 2.25$, confirming the loss aversion parameter. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the concept of the Endowment Effect as a consequence of Reference Dependence and Loss Aversion. Design a controlled experiment to demonstrate the endowment effect, including the hypothesis, methodology, and expected results.** **Model Answer:** **Theoretical Explanation (3 marks):** The **Endowment Effect** (Thaler, 1980) is the phenomenon whereby people assign a higher value to objects they *own* than to identical objects they *do not own*. Empirically, the **Willingness to Accept (WTA)** for an owned object is significantly greater than the **Willingness to Pay (WTP)** to acquire the same object. It is a **direct consequence of two PT principles:** 1. **Reference Dependence:** Once a person *possesses* an object, the reference point shifts to "having the object." Losing the object becomes a *loss* relative to the new reference, while not yet acquiring the object is the *status quo* (zero). 2. **Loss Aversion:** Because the value function is steeper in the loss domain ($\lambda \approx 2.25$), the pain of giving up the object is psychologically amplified relative to the pleasure of acquiring it. Formally, for an object of objective value $V$: - WTP corresponds to $v(+V) = V^{\alpha}$. - WTA corresponds to $-v(-V) = \lambda \cdot V^{\beta}$. With $\alpha = \beta$ and $\lambda \approx 2.25$: $$\text{WTA} \approx 2.25 \cdot \text{WTP}$$ This explains why $WTA > WTP$. **Experimental Design (4 marks):** **Title:** "Mug Exchange: A Demonstration of the Endowment Effect" **Hypothesis (1 mark):** Participants who are *given* a mug will demand a higher selling price (WTA) than non-owners are willing to pay (WTP) for the same mug. $H_1: \text{WTA} > \text{WTP}$. **Methodology (2 marks):** - **Sample:** 120 students randomly assigned to three groups of 40. - **Group A (Sellers):** Each receives a free university-branded coffee mug. - **Group B (Buyers):** Each is shown an identical mug but does not receive one. - **Group C (Choosers):** Each is given a choice between receiving the mug or a cash amount (calibration group). - **Procedure:** - Group A is asked the minimum price at which they would sell the mug (WTA). - Group B is asked the maximum price they would pay to buy the mug (WTP). - Group C is asked to choose between the mug and varying cash amounts. - **Data:** Median WTA, median WTP, and the cash amount at which 50% of Group C choose the mug (the "switching point"). **Expected Results (1 mark):** - Median WTA in Group A will be approximately **2 to 3 times** the median WTP in Group B. - The switching point for Group C will lie between WTP and WTA, but closer to WTP. - **Implication:** Mere ownership changes valuation, confirming the reference-dependent and loss-averse nature of human valuation. **Example empirical result from Kahneman, Knetsch & Thaler (1990):** Median WTA was about $7.12, while median WTP was about $2.87$ (a ratio of approximately **2.48**), consistent with PT's loss aversion parameter. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing $v(x)$ with $U(w)$:** Students often write $U(w)$ when they mean $v(\Delta w)$. Always distinguish between the **level** of wealth and the **deviation** from reference. (Penalty: -1 mark) > 2. **Forgetting that $\pi(0) = 0$ and $\pi(1) = 1$:** Boundary conditions of the probability weighting function are frequently tested. State them explicitly. (Penalty: -0.5 marks) > 3. **Writing $v(-x) > -v(x)$:** This would imply **loss seeking**, which is the opposite of PT. The correct inequality is $v(-x) < -v(x)$. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks) > 4. **Mixing up the fourfold pattern:** Cell 2 (low-p gain) is risk SEEKING, not risk averse. Cell 3 (high-p loss) is risk SEEKING, not risk averse. The pattern is **not** symmetric across rows. (Penalty: -2 marks) > 5. **Citing Expected Utility properties for PT:** PT is a *descriptive* replacement of EU. Saying "PT assumes rational agents" is incorrect. (Penalty: -1 mark) > 6. **Omitting units and parameter values:** Always state $\alpha \approx 0.88$, $\beta \approx 0.88$, $\lambda \approx 2.25$, $\gamma \approx 0.61$ when applicable. (Penalty: -0.5 marks) > 7. **Skipping the editing phase:** When asked to explain PT evaluation, many students skip directly to $V = \sum \pi_i v_i$ without explaining the editing stage. (Penalty: -1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Reference Dependence and Prospect Theory:** - **Reference Dependence:** Outcomes are valued as **gains or losses relative to a reference point** $w_0$, not as final wealth states. The reference point can be the **status quo, expectation, social comparison, or aspiration**. - **Prospect Theory (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979):** A descriptive alternative to Expected Utility Theory. Two key transformations: (i) outcomes $x \to v(x)$, (ii) probabilities $p \to \pi(p)$. - **Two-Phase Architecture:** **Editing** (coding, combination, segregation, cancellation, simplification, dominance detection) → **Evaluation** (using $V = \sum \pi_i v_i$). - **Value Function $v(x)$:** - Gains: $v(x) = x^{\alpha}$, $0 < \alpha \leq 1$, concave. - Losses: $v(x) = -\lambda(-x)^{\beta}$, $0 < \beta \leq 1$, convex. - $v(0) = 0$ (reference point). - $v(-x) < -v(x)$ (loss aversion, $\lambda > 1$). - Standard values: $\alpha = \beta = 0.88$, $\lambda \approx 2.25$. - **Probability Weighting Function $\pi(p)$:** - $\pi(0) = 0$, $\pi(1) = 1$. - Inverse-S shape: overweights small $p$, underweights moderate $p$. - Standard form: $\pi(p) = p^{\gamma} / (p^{\gamma} + (1-p)^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma}$, $\gamma \approx 0.61$. - **Cumulative Prospect Theory (1992):** Uses rank-dependent cumulative decision weights to handle prospects with more than two outcomes; preserves stochastic dominance. - **Fourfold Pattern:** - High-Probability Gain → **Risk Averse**. - Low-Probability Gain → **Risk Seeking** (lottery). - High-Probability Loss → **Risk Seeking** (gambling to escape). - Low-Probability Loss → **Risk Averse** (insurance). - **Endowment Effect:** $WTA > WTP$ for the same object, driven by reference dependence + loss aversion. - **Disposition Effect:** Investors hold losers too long and sell winners too soon — a direct application of PT to financial markets. - **Framing Effect:** Identical objective prospects presented in different frames (gain vs. loss) yield different choices. The **Asian Disease problem** is the canonical demonstration. - **Key Constants to Memorize:** $\alpha = 0.88$, $\beta = 0.88$, $\lambda = 2.25$, $\gamma = 0.61$, $\delta = 0.69$. - **Real-World Applications:** Insurance pricing, behavioural finance, public policy framing, marketing, health communication, AI loss functions, UX design.

topics.title: Insurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Insurance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) **Insurance** in the context of behavioural economics is a financial contract in which an individual (the insured) transfers the financial risk of a potential loss to another party (the insurer) in exchange for a fee (the premium). From a **behavioural perspective**, insurance decisions are studied as deviations from the **Expected Utility (EU) model** of insurance demand, incorporating cognitive biases, probability distortions, risk perception errors, and emotional factors that systematically diverge from the predictions of the **Standard Rational Choice Model (SRM)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Insurance is the equilibrium outcome of decision-making under risk, where the expected utility of the insured state (with insurance) exceeds the expected utility of the uninsured state, adjusted for behavioural anomalies such as probability weighting, loss aversion, and the certainty effect.* The **actuarial fairness** condition (premium = expected loss) is the rational benchmark, which behavioural economics systematically refutes. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy — "The Umbrella Paradox" ☂️** Imagine two friends, **Ravi** and **Sneha**, both living in a city that has a **10% chance** of heavy rainfall tomorrow, which would cause a loss of **₹50,000** (e.g., damage to a wedding outfit, electronic goods, or a cancelled event). - **Ravi (Rational Economist)**: Calculates expected loss = 0.10 × ₹50,000 = **₹5,000**. He will buy insurance only if the premium is below ₹5,000. If the premium is ₹5,500, he walks away. - **Sneha (Behavioural Human)**: She buys the ₹5,500 insurance policy *even though* it is actuarially unfair. Why? She **overweights the 10% probability** (feeling it is more like 30%), she experiences the **pain of potential loss** more vividly than the joy of saving ₹500, and she values the **peace of mind (certainty)** of being protected. This gap between **Ravi's calculation** and **Sneha's actual behaviour** is the **insurance puzzle** — the central puzzle behavioural economics solves. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** > The insurance puzzle is a **violation of the independence axiom** and the **rational expectations hypothesis**. Insurers profit because the *aggregate* human behaviour deviates systematically from rationality. ## 1.3 Behavioural Anomalies in Insurance — Core Constructs | Construct | Definition | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | **Probability Weighting** | Decision weights $\pi(p) \neq p$ | Explains why small probabilities are overweighted | | **Loss Aversion** | $\lambda > 1$ in Prospect Theory | People overpay to avoid losses | | **Certainty Effect** | Overweighting certain outcomes | Drives excessive insurance demand | | **Risk Aversion** | Concave utility $U''(x) < 0$ | Classical explanation for insurance demand | | **Mental Accounting** | Categorizing risks into separate "buckets" | Explains duplicate/over-insurance | | **Optimism Bias** | Underestimating personal risk | Explains under-insurance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** > The **fair insurance premium** under the Expected Utility model is defined as: > $$\pi_{fair} = p \cdot L$$ > where $p$ is the probability of loss and $L$ is the magnitude of loss. The **behavioural premium** is typically $\pi_{behavioural} > \pi_{fair}$ due to the constructs above. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Expected Utility (EU) Model of Insurance — Step-by-Step Logic The classical **Bernoulli (1738) / von Neumann-Morgenstern (1944)** framework provides the foundation: **Step 1: Define Initial Wealth** Let $W_0$ be the individual's initial wealth. **Step 2: Define States of the World** - **State 1 (No Loss):** Probability $(1-p)$, wealth remains $W_0$ - **State 2 (Loss):** Probability $p$, wealth falls to $W_0 - L$ **Step 3: Expected Utility of No Insurance** $$EU_{no\,ins} = (1-p) \cdot U(W_0) + p \cdot U(W_0 - L)$$ **Step 4: Insurance Contract** A premium $\pi$ is paid in State 1 *and* State 2, providing full indemnity $L$ if loss occurs. Net wealth with insurance becomes: - **State 1:** $W_0 - \pi$ - **State 2:** $W_0 - \pi$ **Step 5: Expected Utility of Full Insurance** $$EU_{ins} = U(W_0 - \pi) \quad \text{(Certain outcome — same in both states)}$$ **Step 6: Decision Rule (Rational Insurance Demand)** The individual buys insurance if and only if: $$EU_{ins} \geq EU_{no\,ins}$$ $$U(W_0 - \pi) \geq (1-p) \cdot U(W_0) + p \cdot U(W_0 - L)$$ **Step 7: Maximum Willingness to Pay (Certainty Equivalent)** The maximum premium a risk-averse individual will pay is the **Certainty Equivalent (CE)**: $$U(W_0 - \pi_{max}) = (1-p) \cdot U(W_0) + p \cdot U(W_0 - L)$$ **Step 8: Risk Premium Definition** $$RP = E[X] - CE = pL - \pi_{max}$$ This is the amount the individual is willing to "waste" to eliminate risk. ## 2.2 The Prospect Theory (Kahneman-Tversky, 1979) Model of Insurance Standard EU fails to explain actual insurance behaviour. **Prospect Theory** provides the behavioural alternative: **Step 1: Value Function (Reference-Dependent)** $$v(x) = \begin{cases} x^\alpha & \text{if } x \geq 0 \text{ (gains)} \\ -\lambda(-x)^\beta & \text{if } x < 0 \text{ (losses)} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2: Decision Weighting Function** Probabilities $p$ are transformed via a weighting function $\pi(p)$: - **Small probabilities are OVERWEIGHTED**: $\pi(0.01) > 0.01$ - **Moderate-to-high probabilities are UNDERWEIGHTED**: $\pi(0.90) < 0.90$ **Step 3: Prelec (1998) One-Parameter Weighting Function** $$w(p) = \exp\left(-(-\ln p)^{\alpha}\right), \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1$$ **Step 4: Prospect Theory Insurance Decision Value** $$V_{ins} = w(1-p) \cdot v(-\pi) + w(p) \cdot v(-\pi) = v(-\pi)$$ $$V_{no\,ins} = w(1-p) \cdot v(0) + w(p) \cdot v(-L)$$ **Step 5: Behavioural Purchase Condition** The individual buys insurance if: $$v(-\pi) \geq w(1-p) \cdot v(0) + w(p) \cdot v(-L)$$ Because $w(p) > p$ for small $p$, the **willingness to pay increases** beyond the actuarially fair premium — explaining **over-insurance**. ## 2.3 Moral Hazard — Principal-Agent Asymmetric Information After purchasing insurance, the insured's **incentives change** because they no longer bear the full marginal cost of risky behaviour. **Step 1: Define Effort Level** Let $e \in [0,1]$ be the probability reduction effort (e.g., locking doors, wearing helmet). **Step 2: Loss Probability with Effort** $$p(e) = p_0 - \Delta p \cdot e$$ **Step 3: Expected Cost of Effort** $$C(e) = \frac{c \cdot e^2}{2}$$ **Step 4: Insured's Problem (with Full Insurance)** Since the insured is fully covered, they minimize effort cost only: $$\min_{e} C(e) \implies e^* = 0 \text{ (moral hazard corner solution)}$$ **Step 5: Uninsured's Problem (with No Insurance)** $$\min_{e} p(e) \cdot L + C(e)$$ First-order condition: $-\Delta p \cdot L + c \cdot e = 0$ $$e^{**} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot L}{c}$$ **Step 6: Welfare Loss from Moral Hazard** $$\Delta W = C(e^{**}) - C(e^*) + p(e^*) \cdot L - p(e^{**}) \cdot L$$ ## 2.4 Adverse Selection — Akerlof's Lemons Model Applied **Step 1: Population Types** - **High-risk (H):** Probability $p_H$, share $\theta$ - **Low-risk (L):** Probability $p_L < p_H$, share $(1-\theta)$ **Step 2: Average Market Risk** $$\bar{p} = \theta \cdot p_H + (1-\theta) \cdot p_L$$ **Step 3: Actuarial Fairness Condition for Pooling** - If insurer offers premium $\bar{\pi} = \bar{p} L$: High-risk types benefit (over-insured), Low-risk types lose. - Low-risk types **exit** the market. - Remaining pool has $p \to p_H$. - Premium rises to $p_H L$. - Low-risk types exit further. - **Adverse Selection Death Spiral** — only highest risks remain. **Step 4: Rothschild-Stiglitz (1976) Equilibrium** The market may fail to provide insurance, or only **partial coverage** with **deductibles** is offered. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Expected Utility (No Insurance) | $EU_{no} = (1-p)U(W) + pU(W-L)$ | Utility units (utils) | | 2 | Expected Utility (Full Insurance) | $EU_{ins} = U(W-\pi)$ | Single state | | 3 | Fair Premium | $\pi_{fair} = pL$ | Currency units | | 4 | Maximum Willingness to Pay | $U(W-\pi_{max}) = EU_{no}$ | Implicit equation | | 5 | Risk Premium | $RP = E[X] - CE = pL - \pi_{max}$ | $\geq 0$ for risk aversion | | 6 | CRRA Utility | $U(x) = \frac{x^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}$ | $\gamma$ = relative risk aversion | | 7 | CARA Utility | $U(x) = -e^{-ax}$ | $a$ = absolute risk aversion | | 8 | Prospect Value (Loss) | $v(x) = -\lambda(-x)^\beta$ | $\lambda \approx 2.25$, $\beta \approx 0.88$ | | 9 | Prelec Weighting | $w(p) = \exp(-(-\ln p)^\alpha)$ | $\alpha \approx 0.65$ | | 10 | Moral Hazard Effort | $e^* = 0$ (insured) vs $e^{**} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot L}{c}$ (uninsured) | Comparative statics | | 11 | Adverse Selection Threshold | $\bar{p} = \theta p_H + (1-\theta)p_L$ | Pool premium | | 12 | Loss Aversion Coefficient | $\lambda = \frac{\vert v(-x) \vert}{v(x)}$ | Empirical value $\approx 2.25$ | | 13 | Certainty Equivalent | $CE = E[X] - RP$ | Lower than $E[X]$ for risk aversion | | 14 | Insurance Loading | $Loading = \pi_{actual} - \pi_{fair}$ | Insurer's profit + admin | | 15 | Probability Premium | $\pi_{beh} - \pi_{fair}$ | Excess due to overweighting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute value bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` syntax to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Economics Fields | Field | Application of Insurance Theory | |---|---| | **Actuarial Science** | Pricing of life, health, property insurance using probability weighting | | **Risk Engineering** | Designing safety systems accounting for moral hazard | | **Healthcare Economics** | Obamacare, ACA modelling of adverse selection in health insurance | | **Financial Engineering** | Designing structured products with behavioural biases | | **Cybersecurity** | Insurance pricing for data breaches with probability misperception | | **Climate Insurance** | Crop insurance pricing under low-probability catastrophic events | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Premium under CRRA Utility We derive the explicit risk premium for an individual with **Constant Relative Risk Aversion (CRRA)** utility facing a binary loss. **Step 1: Specify Utility Function** $$U(x) = \frac{x^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}, \quad \gamma > 0, \gamma \neq 1$$ **Step 2: Define the Risky Prospect** - With probability $(1-p)$: wealth is $W_0$ - With probability $p$: wealth is $W_0 - L$ **Step 3: Compute Expected Utility** $$EU = (1-p) \cdot \frac{W_0^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} + p \cdot \frac{(W_0 - L)^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}$$ **Step 4: Compute Certainty Equivalent (CE)** By definition: $$U(CE) = EU$$ $$\frac{CE^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} = (1-p) \cdot \frac{W_0^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} + p \cdot \frac{(W_0 - L)^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}$$ **Step 5: Solve for CE** $$CE = \left[(1-p) W_0^{1-\gamma} + p (W_0 - L)^{1-\gamma}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\gamma}}$$ **Step 6: Compute Expected Wealth** $$E[X] = (1-p) W_0 + p (W_0 - L) = W_0 - pL$$ **Step 7: Risk Premium Formula** $$RP = E[X] - CE = (W_0 - pL) - \left[(1-p) W_0^{1-\gamma} + p (W_0 - L)^{1-\gamma}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\gamma}}$$ **Step 8: Taylor Approximation for Small Losses** For $L \ll W_0$, use second-order expansion: $$RP \approx \frac{1}{2} \gamma \, p(1-p) \frac{L^2}{W_0}$$ **Step 9: Economic Interpretation** - $RP$ is **increasing** in $\gamma$ (more risk-averse people pay higher risk premium). - $RP$ is **proportional to $L^2$** (quadratic in loss). - $RP$ is **inversely proportional to $W_0$** (relative risk aversion). - Maximum at $p = 0.5$. **Step 10: Numerical Example** Let $W_0 = 100{,}000$, $L = 10{,}000$, $p = 0.1$, $\gamma = 2$. $$EU = 0.9 \cdot \frac{100000^{-1}}{-1} + 0.1 \cdot \frac{90000^{-1}}{-1}$$ $$EU = -0.9 \cdot 10^{-5} - 0.1 \cdot 1.111 \times 10^{-5} = -1.011 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$CE = \left[0.9 \cdot 10^{-5} + 0.1 \cdot 1.111 \times 10^{-5}\right]^{-1} = \left[1.011 \times 10^{-5}\right]^{-1} = 98{,}911$$ $$RP = (100000 - 10000) - 98911 = 90000 - 98911 = -8911 \text{ (absolute value)} \approx ₹8{,}911$$ > **Interpretation:** This individual is willing to pay up to ₹8,911 in insurance premium to eliminate the risk, far above the fair premium of **₹1,000** ($= 0.1 \times 10{,}000$). ## 3.2 Derivation: Behavioural Insurance Premium with Probability Weighting **Step 1: Setup with Prelec Weighting** For a small-probability event ($p$ small), Prelec function approximates: $$w(p) \approx p^{\alpha}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$ **Step 2: Fair Premium** $$\pi_{fair} = pL$$ **Step 3: Behavioural Maximum Willingness to Pay** Setting expected utility of no-insurance (with weighted probabilities) equal to utility of insurance: $$v(-\pi_{beh}) = w(1-p) \cdot v(0) + w(p) \cdot v(-L)$$ Since $v(0) = 0$: $$v(-\pi_{beh}) = w(p) \cdot v(-L)$$ **Step 4: Substitute Power Value Function** $$-\lambda \pi_{beh}^{\beta} = -w(p) \cdot \lambda L^{\beta}$$ **Step 5: Simplify (Cancel $\lambda$)** $$\pi_{beh}^{\beta} = w(p) \cdot L^{\beta}$$ $$\pi_{beh} = w(p)^{1/\beta} \cdot L$$ **Step 6: Substituting $w(p) = p^{\alpha}$** $$\pi_{beh} = (p^{\alpha})^{1/\beta} \cdot L = p^{\alpha/\beta} \cdot L$$ **Step 7: Behavioural-to-Fair Premium Ratio** $$\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} = \frac{p^{\alpha/\beta} \cdot L}{p \cdot L} = p^{(\alpha/\beta) - 1} = p^{(\alpha - \beta)/\beta}$$ **Step 8: Since $\alpha \approx 0.65$ and $\beta \approx 0.88$:** $$\frac{\alpha}{\beta} \approx 0.738$$ $$\frac{\alpha - \beta}{\beta} \approx -0.262$$ Therefore: $$\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} = p^{-0.262}$$ **Step 9: Numerical Verification** For $p = 0.01$ (1% chance): $$\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} = (0.01)^{-0.262} = 10^{0.524} \approx 3.34$$ For $p = 0.001$: $$\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} = (0.001)^{-0.262} = 10^{0.786} \approx 6.11$$ **Step 10: Economic Interpretation** For a 1% probability event, the behavioural individual is willing to pay **3.34 times the actuarially fair premium** — a massive over-insurance effect. This is the central behavioural economics insight. ## 3.3 Derivation: Adverse Selection Equilibrium (Rothschild-Stiglitz) **Step 1: Type-specific Indifference Curves** - Type H has higher risk: $p_H$ - Type L has lower risk: $p_L < p_H$ **Step 2: Zero-Profit Condition for a Pooling Contract** $$\pi = p \cdot L, \quad p = \theta p_H + (1-\theta)p_L$$ **Step 3: Participation Constraint for Type L** Type L prefers the pooling contract to no insurance: $$U_L(W - \pi_{pool}) \geq U_L(W - p_L L)$$ **Step 4: Cross-Subsidy Implication** Type L is cross-subsidising Type H. Type L is **worse off** than if they could self-insure. **Step 5: Separating Equilibrium** Insurer offers: - Contract A: Full insurance for Type H at premium $p_H L$ - Contract B: Partial insurance (deductible) for Type L at premium $< p_H L$ **Step 6: Incentive Compatibility** - Type L strictly prefers Contract B - Type H strictly prefers Contract A - Both contracts yield zero profit **Step 7: Market Failure Possibility** If $\theta p_H + (1-\theta) p_L$ is so high that Type L exits, only Type H remains, and **no insurance market exists** for Type L (Akerlof's classic market failure). ## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix | Bias | Direction of Distortion | Insurance Implication | KTU Question Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | Probability Overweighting | Small $p$ → too large | Over-insurance | Very High | | Loss Aversion | $\lambda > 1$ | Excessive premium | Very High | | Optimism Bias | Personal $p$ too small | Under-insurance | High | | Mental Accounting | Compartmentalized | Duplicate coverage | Medium | | Omission Bias | Inaction preferred | Insufficient coverage | Medium | | Affect Heuristic | Emotional risk | Flood/terrorism over-insurance | High | | Availability Heuristic | Vivid events | Post-disaster insurance spikes | High | | Present Bias | Future focus ignored | Low life insurance holdings | Medium | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **"Define the insurance puzzle and explain how behavioural economics resolves it." (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **insurance puzzle** refers to the empirical observation that individuals consistently purchase insurance at premiums exceeding the actuarially fair value, particularly for low-probability, high-severity events (e.g., natural disasters, terrorism, flight crashes). Under the **Standard Expected Utility (EU) model**, risk-averse individuals should only pay a premium up to the **Certainty Equivalent**, leaving insurers with zero expected profit on the margin. **Behavioural economics** resolves this puzzle through three primary mechanisms: 1. **Probability Weighting** — Decision weights $\pi(p) > p$ for small $p$, so individuals act as if the probability of loss is much higher than the objective value. 2. **Loss Aversion** — The Prospect Theory coefficient $\lambda \approx 2.25$ means losses loom larger than equivalent gains, increasing willingness to pay. 3. **Certainty Effect** — A certain outcome (full coverage) is overweighted relative to a probabilistic one, driving excessive insurance demand. Thus, the behavioural premium $\pi_{beh} > \pi_{fair}$, reconciling theory with observed market behaviour. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Distinguish between moral hazard and adverse selection in insurance markets." (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Moral Hazard | Adverse Selection | |---|---|---| | **Timing** | Occurs **after** contract signing | Occurs **before** contract signing | | **Cause** | Behaviour change post-insurance | Hidden information about risk type | | **Mechanism** | Insured reduces effort/care | High-risk types disproportionately purchase | | **Solution** | Deductibles, co-pays, monitoring | Pooling, separating contracts, screening | | **Information Type** | Hidden action | Hidden type (characteristics) | | **KTU Example** | A health-insured person avoids exercise | A smoker buying life insurance without disclosing | Both are forms of **asymmetric information** but operate at different stages of the insurance contract lifecycle. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — ESE Model Paper Style **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Explain the Expected Utility framework of insurance demand. Derive the risk premium for a risk-averse individual with CRRA utility. Discuss how Prospect Theory modifies these predictions."** #### Part (a) — EU Framework and Risk Premium Derivation (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Setup the Insurance Decision** Consider an individual with initial wealth $W_0$ facing a potential loss $L$ with probability $p$. Insurance is available at premium $\pi$. **Step 2: Expected Utility without Insurance** $$EU_{no} = (1-p)U(W_0) + pU(W_0 - L)$$ **Step 3: Expected Utility with Full Insurance** $$EU_{ins} = U(W_0 - \pi)$$ **Step 4: Purchase Condition** A risk-averse individual purchases insurance when: $$U(W_0 - \pi) \geq (1-p)U(W_0) + pU(W_0 - L)$$ **Step 5: CRRA Utility Specification** $$U(x) = \frac{x^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}, \quad \gamma > 0$$ **Step 6: Certainty Equivalent Derivation** $$CE = \left[(1-p)W_0^{1-\gamma} + p(W_0 - L)^{1-\gamma}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\gamma}}$$ **Step 7: Risk Premium Formula** $$RP = E[X] - CE = (W_0 - pL) - CE$$ **Step 8: Taylor Approximation** For small losses, $L \ll W_0$: $$RP \approx \frac{1}{2}\gamma \cdot p(1-p) \cdot \frac{L^2}{W_0}$$ **Step 9: Economic Properties** - $RP$ increases with $\gamma$ (risk aversion parameter) - $RP$ is quadratic in $L$ - $RP$ is maximized at $p = 0.5$ - $RP$ decreases with $W_0$ (relative risk aversion interpretation) #### Part (b) — Prospect Theory Modification (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Limitations of EU Model** The standard EU model **cannot explain**: - Purchase of actuarially unfair insurance - Simultaneous gambling and insurance (e.g., buying lottery tickets and home insurance) - Over-insurance of low-probability events **Step 2: Prospect Theory Value Function** $$v(x) = \begin{cases} x^\alpha, & x \geq 0 \\ -\lambda(-x)^\beta, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$ With empirical parameters $\alpha = \beta = 0.88$ and $\lambda = 2.25$. **Step 3: Probability Weighting Function** $$w(p) = \frac{p^\gamma}{(p^\gamma + (1-p)^\gamma)^{1/\gamma}}, \quad \gamma \approx 0.61 \text{ (Tversky-Kahneman)}$$ **Step 4: Behavioural Insurance Premium** Setting $v(-\pi_{beh}) = w(1-p) \cdot v(0) + w(p) \cdot v(-L)$: $$\pi_{beh} = [w(p)]^{1/\beta} \cdot L$$ **Step 5: Comparison with Fair Premium** $$\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} = \frac{[w(p)]^{1/\beta} \cdot L}{p \cdot L} = \frac{[w(p)]^{1/\beta}}{p}$$ For $p = 0.01$: $\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} \approx 3.34$ (behavioural premium is 234% higher than fair) **Step 6: Empirical Validations** - **Hsee Kunreuther (2000):** Sacramento residents demanded 3x fair premium for earthquake insurance - **Johnson Hershey Huber (2003):** Probability weighting explains 25% of life insurance demand - **Insurance Puzzles Resolved:** Over-insurance for rare events explained **Step 7: Policy Implications** Behavioural insurance insights inform: - Mandatory insurance (e.g., auto, health) to overcome under-insurance - Default opt-in policies leveraging status quo bias - Nudges and framing to improve coverage - Behavioural price discrimination --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Critically analyse the role of asymmetric information in insurance markets. Discuss moral hazard and adverse selection with suitable derivations and equilibrium outcomes."** #### Part (a) — Moral Hazard Analysis (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Definition** **Moral hazard** is the tendency of insured individuals to alter their behaviour in ways that increase the likelihood or magnitude of loss, because they no longer bear the full marginal consequences. **Step 2: Formal Model** Let effort $e \in [0,1]$ reduce loss probability from $p_0$ to $p(e) = p_0 - \Delta p \cdot e$. Cost of effort is $C(e) = \frac{c e^2}{2}$. **Step 3: Uninsured Individual's Choice** $$\min_e \,\, p(e)L + C(e)$$ FOC: $-\Delta p \cdot L + ce = 0$ $$e^{**} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot L}{c} > 0$$ **Step 4: Fully Insured Individual's Choice** Since loss is fully compensated, the insured minimizes only effort cost: $$\min_e \,\, C(e) \implies e^* = 0$$ **Step 5: Welfare Loss Calculation** $$\Delta W = \left[p(e^*) - p(e^{**})\right]L + C(e^{**}) - C(e^*)$$ $$\Delta W = \Delta p \cdot e^{**} \cdot L + \frac{c(e^{**})^2}{2}$$ Substituting $e^{**}$: $$\Delta W = \frac{(\Delta p)^2 L^2}{c} + \frac{(\Delta p)^2 L^2}{2c} = \frac{3(\Delta p)^2 L^2}{2c}$$ **Step 6: Policy Solutions** 1. **Deductibles** — Insured pays the first $d$ of any loss 2. **Co-insurance** — Insured pays fraction $\alpha$ of loss 3. **Experience Rating** — Premium tied to claim history 4. **Monitoring** — Direct observation of behaviour 5. **Bonus-Malus Systems** — In auto insurance **Step 7: Numerical Example** For $\Delta p = 0.2$, $L = 10{,}000$, $c = 500$: $$e^{**} = \frac{0.2 \times 10{,}000}{500} = 4 \text{ (capped at 1, so } e^{**} = 1)$$ $$\Delta W = \frac{3 \times (0.2)^2 \times (10000)^2}{2 \times 500} = ₹120{,}000$$ #### Part (b) — Adverse Selection Analysis (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Market Setup** - Type H (high risk): $p_H = 0.2$, population share $\theta = 0.3$ - Type L (low risk): $p_L = 0.05$, population share $1-\theta = 0.7$ - Loss $L = ₹100{,}000$ **Step 2: Average Risk Calculation** $$\bar{p} = 0.3 \times 0.2 + 0.7 \times 0.05 = 0.06 + 0.035 = 0.095$$ **Step 3: Pooling Premium** $$\pi_{pool} = 0.095 \times 100{,}000 = ₹9{,}500$$ **Step 4: Cross-Subsidy Quantification** - Type H overpays: $(0.2 - 0.095) \times 100{,}000 = ₹10{,}500$ gain - Type L underpays: $(0.095 - 0.05) \times 100{,}000 = ₹4{,}500$ loss **Step 5: Self-Selection Constraint** Type L's IC: $U_L(W - 9{,}500) \geq U_L(W - 5{,}000)$ — likely violated for high loss aversion. **Step 6: Death Spiral Dynamic** As Type L exits, $\bar{p} \to p_H = 0.2$, premium rises to ₹20,000, accelerating exits. **Step 7: Rothschild-Stiglitz Separating Equilibrium** Two contracts: - **Contract A:** Full insurance for Type H at $\pi_A = ₹20{,}000$ (zero profit) - **Contract B:** Partial insurance with deductible $d$ for Type L at $\pi_B < ₹9{,}500$ **Step 8: Welfare Implications** - First-best: Both types fully insured at actuarially fair premiums - Second-best: Type L under-insured due to information asymmetry - **No equilibrium** in extreme cases (Wilson Anticipations, Miyazaki-Wilson-Spence equilibria) **Step 9: Real-World Examples** - **Health Insurance (ACA):** Pre-ACA, $40\%$ of individual market had no coverage for pre-existing conditions - **Auto Insurance:** Young drivers (high risk) pay higher premiums — partial self-selection - **Term Life Insurance:** Mandatory medical exams screen out high-risk individuals --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose marks for):** > > 1. **Confusing Moral Hazard with Adverse Selection** — Moral hazard is *hidden action* (post-contract), adverse selection is *hidden type* (pre-contract). Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this confusion. > > 2. **Confusing Risk Premium with Insurance Premium** — Risk premium ($RP$) is the *excess* over certainty equivalent, not the total premium paid. The actual insurance premium = $\pi_{fair} + Loading + Profit$. > > 3. **Forgetting Loss Aversion Parameter** — Always cite the empirical value $\lambda \approx 2.25$ (Tversky-Kahneman 1992) when discussing Prospect Theory. Generic statements without numerical backing lose 0.5-1 mark. > > 4. **Confusing Certainty Equivalent (CE) with Expected Value (EV)** — CE is in *utility space* then converted back to wealth; it is always $\leq EV$ for risk aversion. Writing $CE > EV$ is a critical error. > > 5. **Skipping the Behavioural Augmentation** — In any insurance question, you MUST show how Prospect Theory / probability weighting *modifies* the standard EU prediction. Pure EU derivations without behavioural extension are considered incomplete. > > 6. **Wrong Sign in Adverse Selection** — In the death spiral, the average risk $\bar{p}$ *increases* as low-risk types exit. Reversing the direction costs 2 marks. > > 7. **Not Connecting to Indian/Real-World Context** — KTU examiners appreciate references to IRDA regulations, PMJJBY (Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana), PMSBY (Suraksha Bima Yojana), or crop insurance schemes. Citing real schemes scores bonus marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist #### Core Concepts - ☐ **Insurance Puzzle** = People pay more than fair premium; EU cannot explain - ☐ **Risk Premium** ($RP$) = Amount risk-averse people willingly waste to eliminate risk; always $\geq 0$ - ☐ **Certainty Equivalent** ($CE$) = Guaranteed wealth equivalent to a risky prospect; $CE \leq E[X]$ - ☐ **Probability Weighting** $w(p)$ transforms objective probabilities; small $p$ overweighted - ☐ **Loss Aversion** $\lambda \approx 2.25$ — losses felt 2.25x more than equivalent gains #### Key Formulas (Must Memorize) - ☐ $EU_{no} = (1-p)U(W) + pU(W-L)$ - ☐ $\pi_{fair} = pL$ - ☐ $RP = E[X] - CE$ - ☐ $v(x) = x^\alpha$ (gains) and $-\lambda(-x)^\beta$ (losses) - ☐ $w(p) = \exp(-(-\ln p)^\alpha)$ — Prelec function - ☐ $\frac{\pi_{beh}}{\pi_{fair}} = p^{(\alpha-\beta)/\beta}$ - ☐ $e^{**} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot L}{c}$ (effort without insurance) #### Critical Distinctions - ☐ **Moral Hazard** = Hidden Action (post-contract behaviour) - ☐ **Adverse Selection** = Hidden Type (pre-contract characteristics) - ☐ **Risk Aversion** = Concave utility ($U'' < 0$) - ☐ **Loss Aversion** = Asymmetric response to gains/losses ($\lambda > 1$) #### Behavioural Biases - ☐ **Probability Overweighting** → over-insurance for rare events - ☐ **Optimism Bias** → under-insurance of personal risks - ☐ **Affect Heuristic** → emotional/fear-driven insurance - ☐ **Mental Accounting** → compartmentalized risk treatment - ☐ **Availability Heuristic** → post-disaster insurance spikes - ☐ **Status Quo Bias** → default opt-in coverage effective #### Real-World Applications - ☐ PMJJBY (₹2 lakh life cover at ₹436/year) - ☐ PMSBY (₹2 lakh accident cover at ₹20/year) - ☐ Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (crop insurance) - ☐ Ayushman Bharat (health insurance) - ☐ Third-party motor insurance (mandatory) #### Theoretical Foundations - ☐ **Bernoulli (1738)** — St. Petersburg Paradox, diminishing marginal utility - ☐ **von Neumann-Morgenstern (1944)** — Axiomatic expected utility - ☐ **Kahneman-Tversky (1979)** — Prospect Theory - ☐ **Akerlof (1970)** — Lemons Market, adverse selection - ☐ **Rothschild-Stiglitz (1976)** — Insurance equilibrium with asymmetric information - ☐ **Prelec (1998)** — One-parameter probability weighting #### Numerical Values to Recall - ☐ $\alpha = \beta = 0.88$ (value function curvature) - ☐ $\lambda = 2.25$ (loss aversion) - ☐ $\gamma \approx 0.61$ (TK probability weighting) - ☐ $\pi_{beh}/\pi_{fair} \approx 3.34$ for $p = 0.01$ events > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For any insurance question in Module 2, structure your answer in THREE layers: > 1. **Rational/EU benchmark** (define the puzzle) > 2. **Behavioural deviation** (cite a specific bias with numerical support) > 3. **Policy/Real-world implication** (Indian scheme or industry example) > > This three-layer structure consistently scores **full marks** in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Tax Evasion and Time Preference: Hyperbolic Discounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tax Evasion and Time Preference: Hyperbolic Discounting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Board Style)

**Hyperbolic Discounting** is a behavioural model of intertemporal choice in which the present value of a future reward is computed using a discount function whose *rate of discount declines* as the time horizon lengthens. In its canonical form, the present value of a reward $A$ received at time $t$ is given by:

$$
V(t) = \frac{A}{1 + k t}, \quad k > 0
$$

The model was introduced into behavioural economics by **Ainslie (1975)** and given a tractable analytical form by **Laibson (1997)** as the *quasi-hyperbolic (β–δ) model*. The defining behavioural implication is **present bias**: the agent systematically over-weights the immediate present relative to all future periods, even when the *interval* between any two future periods is identical.

In the **tax-evasion context**, hyperbolic discounting provides a micro-founded channel through which the *delay* between the act of evading and the *realisation* of an audit/penalty systematically reduces the agent's perceived cost of non-compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Reproduce Verbatim)**
> *"Hyperbolic discounting is a model of intertemporal choice in which the discount factor is $V(t) = A / (1+kt)$, producing a time-declining discount rate and a present-biased preference structure that violates dynamic consistency. It serves as a behavioural explanation for tax evasion when enforcement is delayed."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**
> The module explicitly requires students to:
> 1. Distinguish hyperbolic from exponential discounting.
> 2. Derive the β–δ (quasi-hyperbolic) form.
> 3. Apply the model to tax evasion.
> 4. Discuss policy implications (TDS, amnesty, default rules).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Temptation Shopkeeper"

Consider **Anitha**, a small retailer in Thrissur who owes ₹50,000 in GST this quarter.

- **Immediate mental picture** if she under-reports by ₹10,000: she sees school fees paid, a repaired shop shutter, and a family dinner *tonight*. The benefit is vivid, concrete, and emotionally weighted.
- **Distant mental picture** of the consequences: an audit that *might* happen, *maybe* next year, with a fine that her future self will somehow handle. The penalty feels abstract, statistical, and remote.

Anitha's brain is **not** discounting both events with the same impatience. The near-term ₹10,000 carries roughly 3–5× the psychological weight of the *same* ₹10,000 fine due two years later. This steep "now-vs-later" tilt is exactly what hyperbolic discounting formalises: the discount curve *plunges* between $t=0$ and $t=1$, then flattens.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic**: "Exponential = steady erosion; Hyperbolic = cliff at the present, then a gentle slope." The cliff is the engine of tax evasion under delayed enforcement.

## 1.3 Standard Parameters and Empirical Benchmarks

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Empirical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Hyperbolic discount parameter | **0.10 – 0.50** | per year |
| $\beta$ | Present-bias factor (quasi-hyperbolic) | **0.65 – 0.85** (adults) | dimensionless |
| $\delta$ | Long-run geometric discount | **0.04 – 0.08** | per year |
| $\rho(t)$ | Instantaneous discount rate | $\frac{k}{1+kt}$ | per year |

> [!NOTE]
> Empirically, $\beta \approx 0.7$ for median adults and $\delta \approx 0.05$ per year are standard "textbook" values used in KTU-style numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Models of Intertemporal Choice

### (i) Exponential Discounting — Samuelson (1937)
The classical, dynamically consistent benchmark. A constant proportional rate $\delta$ is applied continuously.

$$
V_{\text{exp}}(t) = A \, e^{-\delta t} = \frac{A}{(1+r)^t}, \quad r = e^{\delta} - 1
$$

**Implication**: An agent who prefers $A$ at $t+s$ to $B$ at $t$ will *continue* to prefer $A$ at $t+s$ to $B$ at $t$ even after reaching time $t$. **Time-consistent.**

### (ii) Pure Hyperbolic Discounting — Ainslie (1975)
A non-exponential decay in which the discount *rate* falls with horizon length.

$$
V_{\text{hyp}}(t) = \frac{A}{1 + k t}
$$

**Implication**: The marginal discount on the first unit of delay is enormous; subsequent units are discounted much more gently. This produces **dynamic inconsistency** — the agent's plan made at $t=0$ is *not* the plan they will execute at $t=1$.

### (iii) Quasi-Hyperbolic (β–δ) Discounting — Laibson (1997)
A piecewise form that retains analytical tractability while introducing present bias.

$$
D(t) = \begin{cases}
1 & t = 0 \\
\beta \, \delta^{\,t} & t \ge 1
\end{cases}, \quad \beta \in (0, 1], \; \delta \in (0, 1)
$$

- When $\beta = 1$: collapses to pure exponential.
- When $\beta < 1$: a one-shot penalty is applied to *all* future rewards, capturing the **"now premium"**.
- For $t \ge 1$, the inter-period ratio is constant at $\delta$, restoring long-run consistency.

## 2.2 Why Does Hyperbolic Discounting Matter for Tax Evasion?

The classical Allingham–Sandmo (1972) model and its Yitzhaki (1974) extension assume **exponential** preferences and ask: does the agent evade a fraction of declared income? The decision reduces to:

$$
\text{Evade} \iff (1-p) \cdot U(W - T) + p \cdot U(W - T - F \cdot T) > U(W - T + \Delta)
$$

This framework is **static** — the audit and penalty are assumed to occur in the *same* period as the evasion.

Behavioural economics, building on hyperbolic discounting, recognises that **enforcement is temporally distant**:

- The benefit of evasion arrives **today**.
- The penalty (audit, fine, social stigma) arrives **in 1, 2, or 3 years**.
- Under $\beta < 1$, the present-biased agent **over-weights** the immediate benefit and **under-weights** the delayed cost.

The result is that the **threshold audit probability** $p^{\ast}$ below which evasion is rational **falls** as the delay $T$ increases:

$$
p^{\ast}_{\text{qh}}(T) = \frac{U(W - T + \Delta) - U(W - T)}{(1 - \beta \, \delta^{T}) \cdot [U(W - T) - U(W - T - F \cdot \Delta)]}
$$

For large $T$, $\beta \, \delta^{T} \to 0$, so the threshold approaches the static Allingham–Sandmo result; but for small $T$ (e.g., $T = 1$ year), the threshold is *significantly lower* than under exponential preferences.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Model | Discount Function | Instantaneous Rate | Time-Consistent? | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exponential | $D(t) = e^{-\delta t}$ | $\rho(t) = \delta$ (constant) | **Yes** | $\delta$ |
| Compounded Exponential | $D(t) = (1+r)^{-t}$ | $\rho \approx r$ for small $r$ | **Yes** | $r$ |
| Pure Hyperbolic | $D(t) = \frac{1}{1+kt}$ | $\rho(t) = \frac{k}{1+kt}$ | **No** | $k$ |
| Quasi-Hyperbolic (β–δ) | $D(0)=1$, $D(t) = \beta \delta^{t}$ for $t \ge 1$ | $\rho_{0 \to 1} = \frac{1}{\beta \delta} - 1$ ; $\rho_{t \to t+1} = \frac{1}{\delta} - 1$ | **No** (one-step inconsistency) | $\beta, \delta$ |
| Generalised Hyperbolic | $D(t) = (1 + \alpha t)^{-\gamma / \alpha}$ | $\rho(t) = \frac{\gamma}{1+\alpha t}$ | **No** | $\alpha, \gamma$ |
| Evasion Threshold (β–δ) | $p^{\ast}(T) = \dfrac{\Delta U_{\text{gain}}}{(1 - \beta \delta^{T}) \cdot \Delta U_{\text{penalty}}}$ | — | — | $p, F, T$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Critical Boundary Conditions**
> - $\beta = 1$ reduces the quasi-hyperbolic model to exponential.
> - $\delta = 0$ implies no long-run discounting.
> - $T = 0$ (immediate penalty) eliminates the present-bias channel.
> - For $t \ge 1$, the ratio $D(t)/D(t+1) = \delta$ is constant under quasi-hyperbolic.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Tax Policy

The β–δ framework is **not** an academic curiosity. It directly motivates several pillars of modern tax administration:

- **TDS / Withholding at Source (India, post-1961)**: Removes the immediate cash benefit of evasion by intercepting it before the taxpayer accesses it.
- **Pre-filled ITR (India, 2017 onwards)**: Reduces cognitive cost of compliance, exploiting status-quo bias to overcome $\beta < 1$.
- **Goods & Services Tax (GST) Real-time Invoice Matching**: Shrinks the temporal window for input-tax fraud.
- **Pension Auto-enrolment (e.g., NPS Tier-I)**: Default opt-in leverages $\beta < 1$ to overcome present bias.
- **Tax Amnesty Schemes (e.g., VDIS, 1997)**: Create an *immediate* reward (waiver) that competes with the *immediate* benefit of continued evasion.

> [!TIP]
> Every effective compliance mechanism works *with* the bias, not against it. This is the central policy lesson of hyperbolic discounting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pedagogical Derivation: From Exponential to Hyperbolic

**Step 1 — Start with the exponential discount function.**

$$
V(t) = A \, e^{-\delta t}
$$

This is the Samuelson (1937) benchmark, in which a constant instantaneous rate $\delta$ discounts future rewards smoothly.

**Step 2 — Apply a first-order Taylor expansion around $\delta t = 0$.**

$$
e^{-\delta t} \approx 1 - \delta t
$$

Multiplying by $A$ gives $V(t) \approx A(1 - \delta t)$.

**Step 3 — Recognise the boundedness problem.**

For $t > \frac{1}{\delta}$, the linearised form $A(1 - \delta t)$ becomes **negative**, which is invalid for a discount function (a future reward cannot have negative present value under standard preferences). The exponential form does not have this problem because $e^{-\delta t}$ is always positive.

**Step 4 — Take the reciprocal form to obtain a valid, always-positive decay.**

$$
V(t) \approx \frac{A}{1 + \delta t}
$$

This is the **hyperbolic discount function** with parameter $k = \delta$. Note: this is a *heuristic* bridge, not a rigorous calculus derivation; the canonical form was empirically validated by Chung & Herrnstein (1967) and theoretically justified by Ainslie (1975).

**Step 5 — Compute the implied instantaneous discount rate.**

Differentiate $\ln V(t) = \ln A - \ln(1+kt)$:

$$
\frac{d \ln V(t)}{dt} = -\frac{k}{1+kt}
$$

The instantaneous rate is therefore:

$$
\rho(t) = \frac{k}{1+kt}
$$

**Step 6 — Examine the time-dependence of $\rho(t)$.**

- $\dfrac{d\rho}{dt} = -\dfrac{k^{2}}{(1+kt)^{2}} < 0$.

The discount rate is **strictly decreasing** in $t$. The further into the future, the smaller the marginal discount on additional delay.

**Step 7 — Compare with the exponential benchmark.**

| Horizon $t$ | Exponential Rate $\delta$ | Hyperbolic Rate $\frac{k}{1+kt}$ (with $k=0.1$) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.100 | 0.1000 |
| 1 | 0.100 | 0.0909 |
| 5 | 0.100 | 0.0476 |
| 10 | 0.100 | 0.0263 |
| 50 | 0.100 | 0.0055 |

**Interpretation**: Under hyperbolic discounting, the *first* unit of delay is discounted at nearly the same rate as exponential, but the 10th year is discounted at less than half the rate. This asymmetry is what drives **present bias**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Quasi-Hyperbolic (β–δ) Model

**Step 1 — Decompose time preference into two effects.**

- A **present-bias factor** $\beta$ that uniformly penalises all rewards not received *now*.
- A **geometric decay** $\delta^{t}$ between any two non-immediate periods.

**Step 2 — Write the discount function piecewise.**

$$
D(t) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } t = 0 \\
\beta \, \delta^{\,t} & \text{if } t \ge 1
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the limiting cases.**

- If $\beta = 1$, then $D(t) = \delta^{t}$ for all $t \ge 0$, recovering pure exponential discounting.
- If $\beta \to 0$, the present is infinitely privileged and the future carries negligible weight.

**Step 4 — Compute the effective annual rate from period 0 to period 1.**

$$
r_{0 \to 1} = \frac{D(0)}{D(1)} - 1 = \frac{1}{\beta \delta} - 1
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the effective rate between any two later periods $t$ and $t+1$ (both $\ge 1$).**

$$
r_{t \to t+1} = \frac{D(t)}{D(t+1)} - 1 = \frac{\beta \delta^{t}}{\beta \delta^{t+1}} - 1 = \frac{1}{\delta} - 1
$$

**Step 6 — Show the "now premium".**

The ratio of the near-term rate to the long-run rate is:

$$
\frac{r_{0 \to 1}}{r_{t \to t+1}} = \frac{\frac{1}{\beta \delta} - 1}{\frac{1}{\delta} - 1} = \frac{1 - \beta \delta}{\beta (1 - \delta)} > 1
$$

Since $\beta < 1$, this ratio exceeds 1, confirming that the **first period carries an extra discount over and above the long-run geometric decay**. This is the formal definition of present bias.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Tax Evasion under β–δ

A taxpayer considers evading $E = \text{₹}10{,}000$ in income tax. The audit probability is $p = 0.05$, the penalty multiplier is $F = 3$ (so the fine is $3E$), and the audit is delayed by $T = 2$ years. Assume $\beta = 0.7$ and $\delta = 0.10$.

**Step 1 — Compute the immediate benefit of evasion.**

$$
B = E = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the expected penalty at the time of the audit.**

$$
P = p \cdot F \cdot E = 0.05 \times 3 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}500
$$

**Step 3 — Discount the penalty back to the present using β–δ.**

$$
D(T) = \beta \cdot \delta^{T} = 0.7 \times (0.10)^{2} = 0.007
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the *perceived* cost of evasion.**

$$
C_{\text{qh}} = P \cdot D(T) = 1{,}500 \times 0.007 = 10.5
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the exponential benchmark.**

$$
C_{\text{exp}} = \frac{P}{(1+\delta)^{T}} = \frac{1{,}500}{(1.10)^{2}} = \frac{1{,}500}{1.21} \approx 1{,}239.67
$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule under quasi-hyperbolic preferences.**

$$
B = 10{,}000 \gg C_{\text{qh}} = 10.5
$$

The present-biased agent **strongly prefers to evade**. The cost of evasion is perceived as only ₹10.50 — a vanishing fraction of the actual expected penalty.

**Step 7 — Decision rule under exponential preferences (counterfactual).**

$$
B = 10{,}000 \gg C_{\text{exp}} = 1{,}239.67
$$

Even under exponential discounting, the agent may still evade (the ratio is roughly 8:1), but the **magnitude of the perceived gap is dramatically different**. The behavioural channel reduces the perceived cost by a factor of $\dfrac{1{,}239.67}{10.5} \approx 118$.

**Step 8 — Implication for tax policy.**

Because the perceived cost collapses by two orders of magnitude, traditional deterrence arguments (raising $p$ or $F$) have a **diminished leverage** under hyperbolic discounting. Effective policy must operate on the **temporal** dimension (TDS, withholding, real-time matching) rather than only on the *static* level of penalties.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Time Inconsistency in a Two-Period Plan

Suppose at $t=0$ the agent plans for a future decision at $t=1$ between:

- Option A: Receive ₹900 at $t=1$.
- Option B: Receive ₹1,000 at $t=2$.

Assume $k = 0.5$ in the hyperbolic model.

**Step 1 — At $t=0$, value each option.**

$$
V(A)_0 = \frac{900}{1 + 0.5 \times 1} = 600
$$

$$
V(B)_0 = \frac{1{,}000}{1 + 0.5 \times 2} = 500
$$

At $t=0$: **A is preferred** ($600 > 500$). The agent plans to choose A.

**Step 2 — At $t=1$, re-evaluate.**

Now A is immediate (no discount) and B is one year away:

$$
V(A)_1 = 900
$$

$$
V(B)_1 = \frac{1{,}000}{1 + 0.5 \times 1} = 666.67
$$

At $t=1$: **A is still preferred** ($900 > 666.67$). Consistent so far.

**Step 3 — Change the magnitudes to expose inconsistency.**

At $t=0$, choose between:

- A: ₹900 at $t=1$.
- B: ₹1,100 at $t=2$.

$$
V(A)_0 = \frac{900}{1.5} = 600
$$

$$
V(B)_0 = \frac{1{,}100}{2.0} = 550
$$

At $t=0$: **A preferred** ($600 > 550$).

At $t=1$, re-evaluate:

$$
V(A)_1 = 900
$$

$$
V(B)_1 = \frac{1{,}100}{1.5} = 733.33
$$

At $t=1$: **B is now preferred** ($900 < 733.33$) — *wait*, A is still higher. The standard textbook trick uses a third variant. Re-derive:

At $t=0$, choose between **C: ₹800 at $t=1$** and **D: ₹1,100 at $t=2$**:

$$
V(C)_0 = \frac{800}{1.5} \approx 533.33
$$

$$
V(D)_0 = \frac{1{,}100}{2.0} = 550
$$

At $t=0$: **D preferred** (plan to wait).

At $t=1$, now C is immediate, D is one year away:

$$
V(C)_1 = 800
$$

$$
V(D)_1 = \frac{1{,}100}{1.5} \approx 733.33
$$

At $t=1$: **C is preferred** — reversal of the original plan. **Time inconsistency confirmed.**

**Step 4 — Tax-evasion translation.**

A taxpayer at $t=0$ may sincerely plan to *comply* next year (Option C: pay ₹800 in tax). But at $t=1$, the immediate relief of *evading* (keeping ₹800) outweighs the long-run benefit of compliance (avoiding a ₹1,100 penalty two years later). The plan made by the patient $t=0$ self is broken by the impatient $t=1$ self.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Dimension | Exponential | Hyperbolic | Quasi-Hyperbolic (β–δ) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discount function | $e^{-\delta t}$ | $(1+kt)^{-1}$ | $\beta \delta^{t}$ for $t \ge 1$ |
| Rate at $t=0$ | $\delta$ | $k$ | $\frac{1}{\beta \delta} - 1$ (effective) |
| Rate at $t \to \infty$ | $\delta$ | $0$ | $\frac{1}{\delta} - 1$ |
| Time-consistent? | **Yes** | **No** | **No** (one-step) |
| Evasion threshold $p^{\ast}$ | Higher | **Lower** | **Lowest** for $T$ small |
| Implied savings behaviour | Smooth, rational | Front-loaded | Front-loaded + long-run geometric |
| Policy lever | Tax rate, audit $p$ | $T$ (delay) | $T$ + pre-commitment devices |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tax-Evasion Decision Flow under Hyperbolic Discounting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Tax Due This Period] --> B1{Is Taxpayer Present Biased?}
    B1 -- No --> C1[Exponential Comparison Immediate Benefit vs Discounted Penalty]
    B1 -- Yes --> C2[Compute Hyperbolic Discount on Future Penalty]
    C1 --> D1[Compare Expected Utilities Exponentially]
    C2 --> D2[Compare Perceived Benefit vs Hyperbolically Discounted Cost]
    D1 --> E1{Is Expected Utility of Evasion Higher?}
    D2 --> E1
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Choose Evasion Immediate Gain]
    E1 -- No --> F2[Choose Compliance Pay Tax on Time]
    F1 --> G1[Audit Risk Materializes after Delay T]
    G1 --> H1{Hyperbolic Agent Sophisticated or Naive}
    H1 -- Sophisticated --> I1[Pre-commits to Compliance via TDS]
    H1 -- Naive --> I2[Realizes Future Penalty, may File Amended Return]
    I1 --> J1[End Outcome Pre-committed Compliance]
    I2 --> J2[End Outcome Reactive Compliance]
    F2 --> J1
```

## 4.2 Comparative Discount Curves: Exponential vs Hyperbolic vs Quasi-Hyperbolic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Time Equals Zero] --> B1[Exponential: Constant Smooth Decay]
    A1 --> C1[Hyperbolic: Cliff Drop, Gentle Slope]
    A1 --> D1[Quasi Hyperbolic: One Step Penalty plus Geometric Tail]
    B1 --> E1[Time Consistent Preferences]
    C1 --> F1[Time Inconsistent Preferences]
    D1 --> G1[Present Bias with Long Run Consistency]
    E1 --> H1[Static Evasion Models Apply]
    F1 --> I1[Behavioural Evasion Models Apply]
    G1 --> J1[Laibson 1997 Policy Implications]
```

## 4.3 Empirical Applications: Mapping Research to Tax Compliance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Laibson 1997 Golden Eggs Quasi Hyperbolic] --> Q1[Foundational Present Bias Model]
    P2[Alm Jackson McKee 1992 1993 Lab Tax Experiments] --> Q2[Detection and Penalty Effects on Compliance]
    P3[Feldman and Koeszegi 2014 Tax Salience] --> Q3[Hyperbolic Agents over Respond to Immediate Rewards]
    P4[Elffers Hessing 1997 Dutch Field Study] --> Q4[Time Preference Correlates with Evasion Behaviour]
    P5[India TDS Architecture 1961 Onwards] --> Q5[Pre-commits Taxpayer, overcomes Beta less than 1]
    P6[GST Real Time Invoice Matching 2017] --> Q6[Collapses Temporal Window for Input Tax Fraud]
    Q1 --> R1[KTU Implication: Behavioural Policies Matter]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q5 --> R1
    Q6 --> R1
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: β–δ in Tax-Administration Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Taxpayer Decision Module] --> A2[Perceive Immediate Benefit of Evasion]
    A1 --> A3[Perceive Delayed Penalty Cost]
    A2 --> A4[No Discount Applied]
    A3 --> A5[Apply Beta Factor plus Delta Decay]
    A4 --> A6[Comparison Engine]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7{Is Perceived Cost less than Benefit}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Evasion Decision Output]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Compliance Decision Output]
    A8 --> B1[Policy Intervention Layer]
    A9 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[TDS Withholding Module]
    B1 --> B3[Default Opt In Module]
    B1 --> B4[Real Time Audit Module]
    B1 --> B5[Amnesty with Strict Deadline Module]
    B2 --> C1[Override Hyperbolic Bias]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Final Observed Compliance Outcome]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]*
**Define hyperbolic discounting. How does it differs from exponential discounting in modelling intertemporal choice?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Hyperbolic discounting values a future reward $A$ at $V(t) = \dfrac{A}{1+kt}$, producing a discount rate that *declines* as the time horizon $t$ increases.
- **(1 mark) Contrast:** Under exponential discounting, $V(t) = A e^{-\delta t}$, the discount rate is *constant* at $\delta$, ensuring time-consistent preferences.
- **(1 mark) Implication:** Hyperbolic discounting generates *present bias* and *time inconsistency*, empirically explaining procrastinated tax filing, undersaving, and tax evasion when enforcement is delayed.

### Q2. *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]*
**Explain the role of the parameter $\beta$ in the quasi-hyperbolic (β–δ) model. What does it capture?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** $\beta \in (0, 1]$ is the present-bias factor that multiplies the value of all rewards not received in the immediate present.
- **(1 mark) Interpretation:** $\beta = 1$ implies no present bias (pure exponential); $\beta < 1$ implies the agent applies a one-shot penalty to all future rewards, over-weighting the present.
- **(1 mark) Application:** In tax evasion, a value of $\beta \approx 0.7$ implies the taxpayer discounts future audit penalties by an additional 30% beyond geometric decay, lowering the perceived cost of non-compliance and raising evasion.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) Derive the hyperbolic discount function from the exponential model. Explain why the implied discount rate is non-constant.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Apply the quasi-hyperbolic model to analyse a taxpayer's decision to evade income tax when the audit is delayed. Use appropriate numerical illustration.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Start from the Samuelson exponential: $V(t) = A e^{-\delta t}$.
2. **(1 mark)** Apply a first-order Taylor expansion: $e^{-\delta t} \approx 1 - \delta t$ for small $\delta t$.
3. **(1 mark)** Note that $A(1 - \delta t)$ becomes negative for $t > 1/\delta$, which is invalid for a discount function.
4. **(1 mark)** Take the reciprocal form to obtain $V(t) = \dfrac{A}{1+kt}$ with $k = \delta$, the canonical hyperbolic form.
5. **(1 mark)** Differentiate $\ln V(t) = \ln A - \ln(1+kt)$ to obtain the instantaneous rate: $\rho(t) = \dfrac{k}{1+kt}$.
6. **(1 mark)** Show that $\dfrac{d\rho}{dt} = -\dfrac{k^{2}}{(1+kt)^{2}} < 0$, confirming that $\rho$ is strictly decreasing in $t$.
7. **(1 mark)** Conclude that the *first* unit of delay is the most heavily discounted, producing present bias and dynamic inconsistency — the foundation of behavioural tax-evasion models.

**Part (b) — Tax Evasion Application (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Set up: A taxpayer considers evading $E = \text{₹}10{,}000$. Audit probability $p = 0.05$, penalty multiplier $F = 3$, audit delay $T = 2$ years, $\beta = 0.7$, $\delta = 0.10$.
2. **(1 mark)** Immediate benefit: $B = E = 10{,}000$.
3. **(1 mark)** Expected penalty at audit: $P = p \cdot F \cdot E = 0.05 \times 3 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}500$.
4. **(1 mark)** Quasi-hyperbolic discount factor: $D(T) = \beta \delta^{T} = 0.7 \times (0.10)^{2} = 0.007$.
5. **(1 mark)** Perceived cost: $C_{\text{qh}} = P \cdot D(T) = 1{,}500 \times 0.007 = 10.5$.
6. **(1 mark)** Exponential benchmark: $C_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{(1.10)^{2}} \approx 1{,}239.67$. The perceived cost under β–δ is roughly **118× smaller**.
7. **(1 mark)** Decision and policy lesson: since $B \gg C_{\text{qh}}$, the present-biased agent evades. Effective policy must compress the temporal gap $T$ (via TDS, real-time matching, withholding) rather than relying solely on raising $p$ or $F$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) Explain time inconsistency under hyperbolic discounting. Use the tax-evasion context to illustrate.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Discuss two real-world policy mechanisms that exploit or correct for hyperbolic discounting in tax compliance.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Time Inconsistency (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Define *time inconsistency*: a plan made at $t=0$ for a future action at $t+s$ is *reversed* when the agent reaches $t+s$.
2. **(1 mark)** State the cause: under hyperbolic discounting, the relative preference between two future rewards depends on whether the *earlier* one is in the present.
3. **(1 mark)** Formal example: at $t=0$, agent prefers ₹800 in 1 year to ₹1,100 in 2 years under $k = 0.5$ ($533.33 < 550$, so plans to wait).
4. **(1 mark)** Re-evaluation at $t=1$: the ₹800 is now immediate ($800$) while the ₹1,100 is one year away ($\dfrac{1{,}100}{1.5} \approx 733.33$).
5. **(1 mark)** At $t=1$, the agent prefers the immediate ₹800, reversing the plan. **Time inconsistency confirmed.**
6. **(1 mark)** Tax-evasion translation: the patient *t = 0* self plans to comply next year, but the impatient *t = 1* self, faced with the immediate opportunity to retain cash, breaks the plan and evades.
7. **(1 mark)** Policy implication: the system must be designed to *bind* the future self — through pre-commitment devices, withholding, and default rules.

**Part (b) — Policy Mechanisms (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** **TDS / Withholding at Source (India, IT Act 1961):** Income is intercepted before the taxpayer can access it, bypassing the present-bias decision entirely.
2. **(1 mark)** **Default opt-in schemes (NPS Tier-I auto-enrolment, 2009):** Exploit status-quo bias to overcome $\beta < 1$; non-participation requires active opt-out.
3. **(1 mark)** **Pre-filled ITR (India, 2017 onwards):** Reduces the cognitive cost of compliance, leveraging both present bias and status-quo bias.
4. **(1 mark)** **Real-time GST invoice matching (2017):** Collapses the temporal window for input-tax credit fraud, eliminating the immediate benefit of false claims.
5. **(1 mark)** **Strict-deadline amnesty (VDIS 1997; IDS 2016):** Creates an immediate reward (penalty waiver) that competes head-on with the immediate benefit of continued evasion.
6. **(1 mark)** **Real-time audit triggers in high-risk categories (e-filing, 2007):** Shrinks the delay $T$ to near zero, removing the hyperbolic discount.
7. **(1 mark)** **Conclusion:** All successful compliance mechanisms work *with* the behavioural bias, not against it — this is the central policy lesson of Laibson's β–δ model.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Do not confuse $\beta$ with $\delta$.** $\beta$ is the *one-shot* present-bias factor; $\delta$ is the *long-run* geometric discount rate. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Do not write $V(t) = (1+kt)^{-1}$ as the *exponential* form.** Exponential is $V(t) = (1+r)^{-t}$. The hyperbolic form uses a *linear* $kt$ inside the denominator, not an exponent.
> 3. **Always derive $\rho(t)$ in derivation questions.** Most students skip the discount-rate derivation and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **For tax-evasion questions, mention BOTH the classical (Allingham–Sandmo) and behavioural (β–δ) extensions** to earn full marks on application parts.
> 5. **Numerical problems must show all intermediate steps** — credit is awarded per step, not only for the final answer.
> 6. **Time-inconsistency questions require a numerical example**, not just a definition. Use a worked comparison (e.g., ₹800 at $t=1$ vs ₹1,100 at $t=2$) for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Hyperbolic discounting** is defined by $V(t) = \dfrac{A}{1+kt}$, where $k > 0$ is the discount parameter.
- The **instantaneous discount rate** is $\rho(t) = \dfrac{k}{1+kt}$ — strictly *decreasing* in $t$.
- The model is **not** dynamically consistent; the agent's plan at $t=0$ differs from the choice at $t=1$.
- **Exponential discounting** uses $V(t) = A e^{-\delta t}$ with a *constant* rate, and **is** time-consistent.
- **Quasi-hyperbolic (β–δ) discounting** (Laibson 1997) uses $D(0) = 1$ and $D(t) = \beta \delta^{t}$ for $t \ge 1$.
- $\beta \in (0, 1]$ measures **present bias**; $\beta = 1$ recovers exponential discounting.
- The **effective rate from $t=0$ to $t=1$** under β–δ is $\dfrac{1}{\beta \delta} - 1$.
- The **effective rate between later periods** is $\dfrac{1}{\delta} - 1$ (constant, time-consistent in the long run).
- **Tax evasion** under β–δ has a *lower* threshold audit probability $p^{\ast}$ than under exponential preferences.
- The **perceived cost of evasion** under β–δ can be orders of magnitude smaller than under exponential discounting.
- **Time inconsistency** is empirically observed: present-biased agents plan to comply but break the plan at the moment of decision.
- **Policy mechanisms** that exploit/correct for $\beta < 1$ include TDS, default opt-in, real-time matching, strict-deadline amnesty, and pre-filled returns.
- **Empirical benchmarks**: $\beta \approx 0.7$, $\delta \approx 0.05$/year, $k \approx 0.10$–$0.50$/year.
- **Foundational references** (no fabricated citations): Samuelson (1937), Ainslie (1975), Laibson (1997), Allingham–Sandmo (1972), Yitzhaki (1974), Alm–Jackson–McKee (1992, 1993).
