# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
*Define the "Tragedy of the Commons" with an example. Why did Garrett Hardin conclude that the tragedy is inevitable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Tragedy of the Commons, as articulated by **Garrett Hardin in 1968**, describes a socio-economic dilemma in which multiple individuals, each acting rationally in their own self-interest, ultimately deplete a shared, finite resource — even though none of them individually desires the resource's collapse.

**Key structural features (mention 2 for full marks):**
- The resource is **non-excludable** (no one can be prevented from using it).
- The resource is **rivalrous** (one user's consumption reduces what remains for others).
- Users face a dominant incentive to **over-use**, leading to **Pareto-inferior equilibrium**.

**Example:** A common grazing pasture open to all villagers. Each herder has an incentive to add one more animal, since the marginal cost of the additional animal is borne by the entire group (degraded grass), while the marginal benefit (extra milk/meat) accrues entirely to the herder. Result: **overgrazing → pasture collapse**.

**Hardin's conclusion:** Hardin argued that the tragedy is *inevitable* because users are trapped in a **collective-action problem** with no natural mechanism for self-restraint. He proposed only two possible escape routes: **(a) privatisation** (assign property rights) or **(b) state regulation** (mutual coercion, mutually agreed upon by the majority). [3 marks for definition + features + example + Hardin's conclusion.]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Distinguish between a Common Pool Resource (CPR) and a Public Good. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Common Pool Resource (CPR)** | **Pure Public Good** |
|---|---|---|
| **Excludability** | Non-excludable (cannot prevent access) | Non-excludable |
| **Rivalry** | **Rivalrous** (subtractable) | **Non-rivalrous** (one person's use doesn't reduce another's) |
| **Market failure risk** | **Tragedy of the Commons** (over-use) | **Free-rider problem** (under-provision) |
| **Example** | **Marine fishery, groundwater, grazing land, atmosphere** | **National defence, street lighting, lighthouse, public radio** |
| **Solution** | Ostrom's institutional design / privatisation / state quotas | Government provision / Pigouvian taxation |

The critical distinction is **rivalry**: in a CPR, my consumption *deprives* yours (the fish I catch is one you cannot); in a public good, my consumption does not reduce what is available to you (one person enjoying lighthouses does not reduce light for another). [3 marks for table + correct examples + rivalry emphasis.]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, the typical mark split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students commonly *lose marks* by: (i) writing essay-style answers without structured headings, (ii) omitting real-world examples, (iii) confusing Hardin's conclusion with Ostrom's rebuttal, and (iv) failing to connect theory to *Indian* context (KTU is a Kerala university — localising with Kerala marine fisheries, Vembanad Lake, Attappadi forests earns bonus marks). Always **define the term first**, then provide a **diagram or matrix**, then **analyse**, then **conclude with a solution**.

---

### **📝 Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Tragedy of the Commons using Garrett Hardin's classic grazing-land parable. Discuss the three institutional mechanisms that can resolve the tragedy. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Critically analyse Elinor Ostrom's 8 design principles for successful common-pool resource management. How does her work challenge Hardin's conclusion? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A, Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

**1. Hardin's Classic Parable (2 marks):**
Garrett Hardin (1968) presented a hypothetical village commons — open pasture land shared by all herders. Each herder seeks to maximise personal gain by adding one more animal to the common. The **individual** marginal benefit of an additional animal (+1) is captured entirely by the herder, while the **individual** marginal cost of overgrazing (−0.1 or so, due to dilution across all herders) is borne by the *entire group*. As every herder faces the identical incentive, **all** rationally add animals, leading to **cumulative overgrazing → stock collapse → ruin of all**. This is the *tragedy* — the pursuit of individual gain culminates in collective loss.

**2. Conditions Enabling the Tragedy (1 mark):**
- **Non-excludability** of the resource
- **Rivalry** in consumption
- **Open-access** regime (no assigned property rights)
- **Externalised costs** (the cost of one's own grazing falls on others)

**3. Three Institutional Resolutions (4 marks):**

| **Mechanism** | **Description** | **Critique** |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Privatisation (Coase)** | Assign individual property rights → owner internalises externality → sustainable use | Distributional inequity; impossible for non-excludable goods (atmosphere); high transaction costs |
| **B. State Regulation (Hardin)** | "Mutual coercion mutually agreed upon" → central authority imposes quotas, taxes, or bans | Bureaucratic failure; corruption; information asymmetry; enforcement costly |
| **C. Community Self-Governance (Ostrom)** | Local users design and enforce their own rules; nested polycentric institutions | Requires pre-existing social capital; slow to scale; risk of elite capture; vulnerable to outside interference |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** No single mechanism is universally optimal; the choice depends on **ecological context, social capital, governance capacity, and resource characteristics**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A, Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

**1. Ostrom's 8 Design Principles (4 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

Elinor Ostrom's research on 800+ years of successful commons worldwide identified eight institutional features that distinguish **durable** commons from **failed** ones:

1. **Clearly Defined Boundaries** — Both the resource boundary and the list of authorised users are unambiguous.
2. **Congruence with Local Conditions** — Rules match the specific ecological and social context.
3. **Collective-Choice Arrangements** — Affected users can participate in modifying operational rules.
4. **Monitoring** — Monitors are accountable to, and often drawn from, the user community.
5. **Graduated Sanctions** — Penalties escalate with severity and frequency of violation.
6. **Conflict-Resolution Mechanisms** — Low-cost, accessible arbitration is available.
7. **Minimal Recognition of Self-Organisation** — Higher authorities respect the community's right to self-govern.
8. **Nested Enterprises** — Governance is organised in multiple layered tiers for large-scale CPRs.

**2. Empirical Foundations (1 mark):**
Ostrom documented successful commons in:
- **Maine (USA)** — lobster fisheries self-regulating via territorial lotteries.
- **Switzerland** — Alpine pastures managed for centuries via *Allmend* cooperatives.
- **Japan** — forest commons (*iriai*) sustainably managed for 500+ years.
- **Nepal** — farmer-managed irrigation systems (e.g., Chhera Thana).
- **India** — Vembanad Lake, Attappadi tribal forests.

**3. Challenge to Hardin (1.5 marks):**
- Hardin asserted the tragedy is **inevitable** unless privatised or state-controlled. Ostrom **empirically refuted this** by showing that communities *do* self-organise to escape the tragedy.
- She demonstrated that **humanity is not doomed** to a binary of state or market — there exists a **third way**: collective action under **polycentric governance**.
- However, Ostrom's principles are **not a universal panacea**; they work only under specific social, ecological, and political conditions. They are **contingent**, not deterministic.
- Her work shifts the analytical lens: from *"is the resource doomed?"* to *"which institutional design fits this resource and community?"*

**4. Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Ostrom's framework is the **modern, behaviourally realistic** answer to Hardin's pessimism — institutions can be **crafted** to align individual incentives with collective welfare.

---

### **📝 Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Model the Tragedy of the Commons as an N-player Prisoner's Dilemma. Using a 2 × 2 payoff matrix, demonstrate why mutual defection is the dominant-strategy equilibrium and why this is socially suboptimal. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **repeated interaction**, **discount factors**, and **graduated sanctions** in sustaining cooperation in common-pool resource dilemmas. Include Axelrod's evolutionary game-theory findings in your answer. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

**1. Game-Theoretic Setup (2 marks):**
Consider **two herders, A and B**, sharing a common grazing ground. Each independently chooses between:
- **C (Cooperate / Restrain grazing)** — keep herd at sustainable size.
- **D (Defect / Overgraze)** — add an extra animal to capture personal gain.

**Payoffs (with the standard PD condition T > R > P > S):**

|  | **B: Cooperate** | **B: Defect** |
|---|---|---|
| **A: Cooperate** | (R, R) = **(5, 5)** | (S, T) = **(1, 10)** |
| **A: Defect** | (T, S) = **(10, 1)** | (P, P) = **(2, 2)** |

> T = 10 (Temptation); R = 5 (Reward for mutual cooperation); P = 2 (Punishment for mutual defection); S = 1 (Sucker's payoff).

**2. Dominant Strategy Analysis (2.5 marks):**
For Player A:
- If B chooses **C**: A's payoff from D (10) > payoff from C (5) → D is better.
- If B chooses **D**: A's payoff from D (2) > payoff from C (1) → D is better.

∴ **D is a strictly dominant strategy for A.** By symmetry, the same holds for B. The **unique Nash Equilibrium is (D, D) with payoff (2, 2)**.

**3. The Social Suboptimality (1.5 marks):**
The cooperative outcome (C, C) yields (5, 5), which is **Pareto-superior** to (D, D) = (2, 2). Both players are *strictly worse off* at the Nash Equilibrium than they would be if they could commit to mutual cooperation. This is the **paradox of individual rationality** — a classic Prisoner's Dilemma. In the N-player commons extension, the same logic drives *every* herder to overgraze, producing the **tragedy** (a collective bad emerging from individually rational choices).

**4. The N-Player Generalisation (1 mark):**
With $N$ herders, each herder $i$ chooses $g_i$ (number of animals). Total stock $G = \sum g_i$ damages the commons according to damage function $D(G)$. Individual net payoff:

$$\pi_i = g_i \cdot v - g_i \cdot D\left(\sum_{j} g_j\right) - c \cdot g_i$$

FOC for individual optimum: $v = D'(G) \cdot g_i$ (each ignores the externality $D'(G) \cdot (g_i - 1)$ they impose on others). Equilibrium $G^*$ exceeds socially optimal $G^{**}$. **The tragedy grows with group size when monitoring is absent.**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

**1. The Cooperation Condition (2 marks):**
In a *one-shot* game, defection is the only equilibrium. However, if the game is **infinitely repeated** with discount factor $\delta$ (where $\delta$ measures patience, with $0 \le \delta < 1$), mutual cooperation is sustainable as a **subgame-perfect equilibrium** under *Grim Trigger* or *Tit-for-Tat* strategies provided:

$$\delta \ge \frac{T - R}{T - P}$$

- $T - R$ = gain from defection when opponent cooperates.
- $T - P$ = maximum loss from retaliation.

**Intuition:** If players care enough about the future (low $\delta$ in the sense of *low discount rate*, i.e., $\delta$ is *close to 1*), the **shadow of the future** sustains cooperation.

**2. Axelrod's Tournaments (2 marks):**
Robert Axelrod (1980) ran computer tournaments pitting 14 strategies against each other in iterated PD. The winner was the simplest — **Tit-for-Tat (TFT)**: cooperate on move 1, then copy the opponent's previous move. Key findings:
- **Nice** strategies (never defect first) outperform nasty ones.
- **Retaliatory** (not forgiving) strategies deter exploitation.
- **Forgiving** (returning to cooperation) strategies recover from mistakes.
- **Clear and simple** strategies are robust.

In a commons context, *TFT* corresponds to a herder who normally restrains, but retaliates against overgrazers — a behavioural pattern consistent with **conditional cooperation** documented by Fehr & Gächter.

**3. Graduated Sanctions (1.5 marks):**
Ostrom's empirical work shows that **graduated sanctions** (escalating penalties: warning → fine → temporary exclusion → permanent ban) outperform binary punishments (harsh or no punishment). Reasons:
- **Proportionality** preserves community bonds and reciprocity.
- **Calibrated** penalties give violators a chance to learn and comply.
- They convert *defectors* into *conditional cooperators* over time.

**4. Implications for Commons Design (1 mark):**
The combination of **repeated interaction** (long time horizons, stable community membership), **patient discount factors** (cultural, generational, or legally enforced), and **graduated sanctions** (credible, proportionate) creates a behavioural infrastructure that can **replace** the need for heavy state intervention. This is the *behavioural core* of Ostrom's success stories.

**5. Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The tragedy is not destiny. It can be reversed by **institutional designs that internalise future consequences, lower the cost of monitoring, and embed cooperative norms**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Tragedy of the Commons = individual rational self-interest → collective ruin of a shared, rivalrous, non-excludable resource. (Hardin, 1968).
- ✅ **CPR vs Public Good:** CPR is **rivalrous + non-excludable**; Public Good is **non-rivalrous + non-excludable**. Both non-excludable, but rivalry is the decisive difference.
- ✅ **Three Pillars of the Tragedy:** *Non-excludability* + *Rivalry* + *Externalised marginal cost*.
- ✅ **Hardin's Two Solutions:** Privatisation (Coase) **OR** State regulation.
- ✅ **Ostrom's Third Way:** Community self-governance via **8 Design Principles** (Boundaries, Local Congruence, Collective Choice, Monitoring, Graduated Sanctions, Conflict Resolution, Recognition of Self-Org, Nested Enterprises).
- ✅ **Payoff Inequality:** $T > R > P > S$ defines a Prisoner's Dilemma → defection is the dominant strategy → Nash Equilibrium is Pareto-suboptimal.
- ✅ **Cooperation Condition (Axelrod):** $\delta \ge \dfrac{T - R}{T - P}$ → low discount rate + repeated play + retaliation = cooperation is sustainable.
- ✅ **Gordon-Schaefer:** Open-access effort is **double** the social-optimum effort (under standard assumptions); rent is completely dissipated.
- ✅ **Elinor Ostrom** won the 2009 Nobel Prize in Economics — *the first woman ever* to receive it.
- ✅ **Behavioural twists:** Bounded rationality, hyperbolic discounting, conditional cooperation, social preferences, framing effects, time-inconsistent preferences.
- ✅ **Indian Context (KTU Special!):** Vembanad Lake, Attappadi tribal forests, Kerala marine fisheries, Western Ghats water-sharing — all are real commons dilemmas with active institutional reform debates.
- ✅ **Polycentric Governance = Nested Enterprises:** Ostrom's Principle 8 — multi-layered, scale-matched institutional architecture.
- ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse Ostrom's framework with **Coasian bargaining** — Ostrom is about *community-designed* rules, not market-based private negotiation.
- ✅ **One-line mantra for viva:** *"The tragedy of the commons is a failure of institutional design, not of human nature — and good institutions are themselves a public good that communities can produce."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching Markets
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Matching Markets

# 🟢 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MATCHING MARKET (Two-Sided Matching Market):**
> A market structure in which **heterogeneous agents on two distinct sides** must be paired (or grouped) with agents on the opposite side, and where the allocation is determined not primarily by a single uniform price, but by an explicit **matching mechanism** that respects the **ordinal preferences** of every participant.

A matching market is formally described by the tuple:

$$
\mathcal{M} = (A, B, \succ_{A}, \succ_{B}, \mu)
$$

where:
- $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m\}$ — the set of agents on **side A** (e.g., men / hospitals / students)
- $B = \{b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n\}$ — the set of agents on **side B** (e.g., women / medical colleges / schools)
- $\succ_{a_i}$ — the strict preference ordering of agent $a_i$ over $B$
- $\succ_{b_j}$ — the strict preference ordering of agent $b_j$ over $A$
- $\mu : A \cup B \rightarrow A \cup B$ — the **matching function** that produces the final assignment

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Behavioural Economics:** Classical Walrasian price theory assumes a single numeraire clears markets. Matching markets violate that assumption because **(i)** prices may be fixed (e.g., wages, tuition) and **(ii)** preferences are **ordinal**, not cardinal — so the field requires new design tools (Gale-Shapley, Top Trading Cycles, Boston Mechanism).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Wedding Reception Seating" Analogy**
> Imagine a grand Kerala wedding reception with **200 guests on side A** (bride's relatives) and **200 guests on side B** (groom's relatives). The bride and groom must be paired into 200 couples for the family photo. There is **no price that will help** — what matters is the *qualitative* order of who each person wants to stand next to. The family hires a **matchmaker (the mechanism)** whose job is to assign pairs so that **no two people who secretly prefer each other end up with someone else** (otherwise: chaos at the buffet line).
> That matchmaker is solving a **stable matching problem**, and the design of that mechanism is the heart of matching market theory.

### Canonical Real-World Examples
| Real Market | Side A | Side B | Mechanism Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| National Resident Matching Program (NRMP, USA) | Medical Students | Hospitals | Gale–Shapley (Deferred Acceptance) |
| School Choice (NYC, Boston) | Students | Public Schools | Deferred Acceptance / Boston |
| Cadaveric Kidney Exchange | Patient–Donor Pairs | Patient–Donor Pairs | Top Trading Cycles |
| College Admissions (Hungary, Spain) | Students | Universities | Gale–Shapley variants |
| Online Dating Platforms | Users | Users | Proprietary matching algorithms |
| Labour Market Apprenticeships | Workers | Firms | Centralized clearing houses |

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Terms (Highlighted)

- **Stability** — a matching where no agent pair would *mutually* prefer to leave it.
- **Strategy-proofness** — a mechanism is *strategy-proof* (or *truthful*) if no agent gains by **misreporting** preferences.
- **Pareto Efficiency** — no agent can be made better off without making some other agent worse off.
- **Core** of the matching market — the set of matchings not *blocked* by any coalition.
- **Blocking Pair** — a pair $(a, b)$ that is **not matched** to each other but each strictly prefers the other over their current partner.
- **Hospital-optimal / Student-optimal** — the Gale–Shapley outcome that is *best for* the proposing side.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Matching Market

A matching market is built on **three structural pillars** (KTU 2024 emphasis):

### Pillar 1 — **Bilateral Preferences** (Two-Sided Heterogeneity)
Each agent holds a *complete, strict, transitive* ranking of all agents on the opposite side.
Formally, for every $a_i \in A$, the preference profile is a permutation:
$$
\mathcal{P}_i : B \to \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \quad \text{such that } b_x \succ_{a_i} b_y \iff \mathcal{P}_i(b_x) < \mathcal{P}_i(b_y)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In behavioural settings, real agents often have *incomplete* or *inconsistent* preferences — this is precisely where **behavioural economics** enriches classical matching theory (bounded rationality, reference-dependent preferences, status-quo bias).

### Pillar 2 — **The Matching Mechanism $\mu$**
A deterministic or randomized *function* that takes the preference profile as input and outputs a matching:
$$
\mu : \mathcal{P}_A \times \mathcal{P}_B \rightarrow \mathcal{M}_{\text{set}}
$$

Common mechanisms studied in KTU Module 3:
- **Gale–Shapley Deferred Acceptance (DA)** — Gale & Shapley, 1962
- **Boston Mechanism (Immediate Acceptance)** — Abdulkadiroğlu & Sönmez, 2003
- **Top Trading Cycles (TTC)** — Shapley & Scarf, 1974
- **Serial Dictatorship** — simple, non-strategy-proof
- **Random Serial Dictatorship** — probabilistic, ex-post fair

### Pillar 3 — **Stability & Welfare Criteria**
The mechanism is evaluated on five KTU-relevant properties:

| Property | Definition | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | No blocking pair exists | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Pareto Efficiency** | No reallocation makes all agents better off | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Strategy-Proofness** | Truth-telling is a dominant strategy | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Individual Rationality** | No agent is matched to an unacceptable partner | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Fairness / Equity** | Egalitarian welfare, ex-post fair | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.2 The Core Theorem of Matching Markets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THEOREM (Roth–Sotomayor, 1990):** *For any two-sided matching market with strict preferences, a **stable matching always exists** and can be computed by the Gale–Shapley algorithm in $O(mn)$ time. Furthermore, the set of all stable matchings forms a **distributive lattice** under the dominance partial order.*

This is one of the most celebrated positive-existence theorems in market design and the cornerstone of Module 3.

---

## 2.3 The Gale–Shapley Deferred Acceptance (DA) Algorithm — Logic Flow

The DA algorithm proceeds in **rounds**. It can be **student-proposing** (side A proposes) or **hospital-proposing** (side B proposes).

### Step-by-Step Logic (Student-Proposing Version)

1. **Initialization:** All agents on side $A$ are *unengaged*; all agents on side $B$ are *unengaged*; maintain a "next choice" pointer $c(a_i) = 1$ for every $a_i$.
2. **Round $t$:** Every *unengaged* agent $a_i$ on side $A$ **proposes** to the **highest-ranked** agent on side $B$ not yet proposed to.
3. **Tentative acceptance:** Each $b_j$ receiving one or more proposals **tentatively accepts** the most-preferred proposer, **rejects** all others, and those rejected become *unengaged* again.
4. **Deletion of rejected proposals:** A rejected agent $a_i$ advances its pointer $c(a_i) \gets c(a_i)+1$ and proceeds to the next choice in the next round.
5. **Termination:** Stop when **no unengaged agent on $A$ has any remaining acceptable partner to propose to**. The set of tentative holds is the **stable matching**.

### Why does DA produce a STABLE matching?
- **No agent $a_i$ is rejected by a partner they prefer to their final match** → no incentive to deviate by proposing elsewhere.
- **No agent $b_j$ holds a partner worse than anyone who has proposed and been rejected** → no incentive to deviate by accepting a previously rejected suitor.
- Hence, **no blocking pair can exist** at termination (proof by contradiction: if a blocking pair $(a_i, b_j)$ existed, then $a_i$ would have proposed to $b_j$ at some point, and $b_j$ would not have rejected $a_i$, contradiction).

### Key Properties of DA

> [!TIP]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD TRINITY OF DA:**
> 1. **DA yields a STABLE matching** ✔️
> 2. **DA is OPTIMAL for the proposing side** (no other stable matching makes the proposers strictly better off) ✔️
> 3. **DA is STRATEGY-PROOF for the proposing side** (truth-telling is a dominant strategy) ✔️
> BUT — DA is **NOT** strategy-proof for the receiving side, and the outcome is **PESSIMAL** (worst stable) for the receiving side.

---

## 2.4 Top Trading Cycles (TTC) Algorithm

Used primarily in **housing allocation** and **kidney exchange** where agents have *endowment* rights that can be traded.

### Logic Steps
1. Each agent $a_i$ points to the agent whose object they most prefer.
2. Form a directed graph; a **cycle** in this graph yields a set of agents who can trade among themselves.
3. Execute the trade in the cycle, **remove** these agents from the market, and **reassign** their objects.
4. Repeat until all agents are allocated.

> [!NOTE]
> **TTC produces the unique allocation in the CORE** of the housing market. It is **strategy-proof** for the endowment-owners.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Definition / Equation | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu(a_i)$ | Partner of $a_i$ in matching $\mu$ | All mechanisms |
| $\vert A \vert = m$, $\vert B \vert = n$ | Cardinalities of the two sides | Cardinality analysis |
| $b_j \succ_{a_i} \mu(a_i)$ | "Agent $a_i$ prefers $b_j$ to current partner" | Blocking pair test |
| $W(\mu) = \sum_{i} u_i(\mu(a_i))$ | Aggregate utilitarian welfare | Efficiency comparison |
| $\Delta_i = \text{rank}_{\mu}(a_i)$ | Position of assigned partner in $a_i$'s list | Strategy analysis |
| $\text{DA}_{\text{prop}}$ | Proposer-optimal stable matching | Welfare bounds |
| $\text{DA}_{\text{rec}}$ | Receiver-optimal stable matching | Welfare bounds |
| $C(\mu)$ | Core — set of unblocked matchings | TTC equivalence |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table syntax rule:** All set delimiters use $\vert$ (LaTeX) instead of literal `|` characters, to keep the markdown table intact.

---

## 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Utility

Matching theory is not pure abstraction — it **runs real systems**:

- **NRMP (USA):** Places ~40,000 doctors per year using DA.
- **NYC School Choice:** DA serves ~80,000 K–12 students annually.
- **DAV kidneys:** Kidney exchange programmes worldwide save thousands of lives via TTC.
- **Engineering Workforce:** Software engineering hiring, gig-economy platform design (Uber-driver matching, Upwork-freelancer pairing) all draw on matching theory.
- **Spectrum Auctions:** Combinatorial spectrum allocation uses a generalized DA (the *generalized deferred acceptance* of Hatfield & Milgrom, 2005).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Example: Gale–Shapley on a 3 × 3 Market

We construct a **canonical textbook example** that examiners frequently use.

### Preference Profiles

| Men (Side A) | Preferences | | Women (Side B) | Preferences |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ | $W_1 \succ W_2 \succ W_3$ | | $W_1$ | $M_2 \succ M_1 \succ M_3$ |
| $M_2$ | $W_1 \succ W_3 \succ W_2$ | | $W_2$ | $M_3 \succ M_2 \succ M_1$ |
| $M_3$ | $W_2 \succ W_1 \succ W_3$ | | $W_3$ | $M_1 \succ M_2 \succ M_3$ |

### Step 1 — Round 1 (All men propose to their top choice)

| Man | Proposes to | Status |
|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ | $W_1$ | Tentative (W1 → {M1}) |
| $M_2$ | $W_1$ | Conflict at $W_1$ |
| $M_3$ | $W_2$ | Tentative (W2 → {M3}) |

$W_1$ compares $M_1$ vs. $M_2$. From $W_1$'s list, $M_2 \succ M_1$, so $W_1$ **tentatively accepts $M_2$** and **rejects $M_1$**.

### Step 2 — Round 2 (Rejected man re-proposes)

$M_1$ is now free. $M_1$'s next choice is $W_2$. $M_1$ proposes to $W_2$.

$W_2$ now holds $\{M_3, M_1\}$. From $W_2$'s list, $M_3 \succ M_1$, so $W_2$ **rejects $M_1$**.

### Step 3 — Round 3

$M_1$ proposes to his last choice $W_3$. $W_3$ is free → **tentatively accepts $M_1$**.

### Step 4 — Termination

No more rejections. Final matching:

$$
\mu^* = \{(M_1, W_3),\; (M_2, W_1),\; (M_3, W_2)\}
$$

### Step 5 — Stability Check

We verify **no blocking pair** exists. Each man is matched to a partner at least as good as anyone who ever proposed to them, and each woman holds her most-preferred among proposers. So no $(M_i, W_j)$ is mutually strictly preferred to $\mu^*(M_i)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Outcome characterization:** $\mu^*$ is the **man-optimal / woman-pessimal** stable matching. If women proposed instead (i.e., woman-proposing DA), the result would be the **woman-optimal / man-pessimal** matching:
> $\mu^{\text{W-opt}} = \{(M_1, W_2),\; (M_2, W_1),\; (M_3, W_3)\}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Proposer-Optimality Holds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLAIM:** In the Gale–Shapley (proposer-proposing) algorithm, the resulting matching $\mu^{A\text{-opt}}$ is the **unique** stable matching that is *best* for every proposer simultaneously, among all stable matchings.

**Proof (by induction, sketch):**

Define $S_t$ = set of proposers tentatively engaged at the end of round $t$.

**Induction hypothesis (Inv. $I_t$):** After round $t$, every engaged proposer $a_i \in S_t$ holds their best possible partner across *all* stable matchings.

- **Base case ($t=0$):** Trivially true (no one is engaged).
- **Inductive step:** Suppose $I_t$ holds. In round $t+1$, the algorithm lets $b_j$ keep her most-preferred among current proposers and rejects the rest. If any stable matching $\nu$ matched a rejected $a_k$ to $b_j$, then $b_j$ would strictly prefer her DA partner (by construction, since $b_j$ kept the top proposer). This contradicts the fact that $\nu$ is stable and $a_k$ is rejected. Therefore, the rejected $a_k$ cannot be matched to $b_j$ in any stable matching — so $a_k$ cannot be made better off by being matched to $b_j$ under any stable outcome. Hence $a_k$ can advance without losing his best-stable-partner status, preserving the invariant.
- **Termination:** When no proposer is rejected, the matching is stable. By $I_t$, each proposer holds the best possible partner across all stable matchings, i.e., the result is **proposer-optimal**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Welfare Bounds — The "Duality" of DA

Define the two extreme stable matchings:

$$
\mu^{A\text{-opt}} = \text{DA with A proposing} \qquad \mu^{B\text{-opt}} = \text{DA with B proposing}
$$

Then for **every other stable matching** $\nu$:

$$
W(\mu^{A\text{-opt}}) \geq W(\nu) \geq W(\mu^{B\text{-opt}})
$$

and the receiver-pessimal matching is a *lower bound* on the welfare of any stable outcome.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** — be ready to write the inequalities and explain why the lattice structure forces this sandwich.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Boston Mechanism vs. DA (Efficiency Loss)

Consider three students $S_1, S_2, S_3$ and three schools $A, B, C$ with capacities $1, 1, 1$ and the following **true** preferences:

| Student | 1st | 2nd | 3rd |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | A | B | C |
| $S_2$ | A | B | C |
| $S_3$ | B | A | C |

- **DA outcome (student-proposing):** $S_1 \to A$, $S_2 \to B$, $S_3 \to C$ → **Stable**, Pareto efficient.
- **Boston mechanism:** Students list preferences. $S_1$ is processed first → $S_1$ gets A. $S_2$ now lists; only B, C remain, $S_2$ picks B. $S_3$ gets C. **Same outcome** here — but if processing order changes, Boston can yield unstable, inefficient matchings.

> [!WARNING]
> The Boston mechanism is **not strategy-proof** (students can game the order of application) and is **not stable** in general. KTU 2024 emphasises this contrast.

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Notation Quick-Reference

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stability: } & \nexists (a_i, b_j) \text{ s.t. } b_j \succ_{a_i} \mu(a_i) \text{ and } a_i \succ_{b_j} \mu(b_j) \\
\text{Individual Rationality: } & \mu(a_i) \succeq_{a_i} \emptyset \;\; \forall a_i \\
\text{Strategy-Proofness: } & \mu(P_i, P_{-i}) \succeq_{a_i} \mu(P_i', P_{-i}) \;\; \forall P_i' \\
\text{DA complexity: } & O(mn) \text{ time, } O(m+n) \text{ space} \\
\text{Lattice property: } & \mu^{A\text{-opt}} \text{ and } \mu^{B\text{-opt}} \text{ bound all stable matchings}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow — Gale–Shapley Deferred Acceptance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: All agents unengaged, pointers reset])
    proposeA[Each unengaged A-agent proposes to next preferred B-agent]
    receiveA[B-agent collects incoming proposals]
    decideA{B-agent ranks all proposers}
    acceptA[Tentatively accept top proposer]
    rejectA[Reject all lower-ranked proposers]
    advanceA[Rejected A-agents advance pointer to next choice]
    checkA{All A-agents engaged or no more choices?}
    outputA[Output stable matching mu star]
    stopA([Stop])

    startA --> proposeA
    proposeA --> receiveA
    receiveA --> decideA
    decideA -->|Top proposer chosen| acceptA
    decideA -->|Others| rejectA
    acceptA --> checkA
    rejectA --> advanceA
    advanceA --> proposeA
    checkA -->|No| proposeA
    checkA -->|Yes| outputA
    outputA --> stopA
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Matching Market Design Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    prefB[Preference Profile B]
    cap[Capacity / Endowment constraints]
    market[(Centralized Clearing House)]

    da1[Gale-Shapley DA]
    boston1[Boston Mechanism]
    ttc1[Top Trading Cycles]
    serial1[Serial Dictatorship]

    matchOut[Stable Matching mu]
    eval1[Stability Check]
    eval2[Efficiency Check]
    eval3[Strategy Proofness Check]
    report[Market Designer Report]

    prefA --> market
    prefB --> market
    cap --> market
    market --> da1
    market --> boston1
    market --> ttc1
    market --> serial1
    da1 --> matchOut
    boston1 --> matchOut
    ttc1 --> matchOut
    serial1 --> matchOut
    matchOut --> eval1
    matchOut --> eval2
    matchOut --> eval3

    eval2 --> report
    eval3 --> report
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Solution Concepts in Matching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sol[Solution Concepts in Matching Markets]

    sol --> stable[Stable Matching]
    sol --> efficient[Pareto Efficient Matching]
    sol --> opt[Optimal Matching]
    sol --> core[Core Allocation]
    sol --> fair[Fair / Egalitarian Matching]

    stable --> def1[No blocking pair exists]
    efficient --> def2[No agent can improve without harming another]
    opt --> def3[Best for proposing side in DA]
    core --> def4[Unblocked by any coalition]
    fair --> def5[Minimizes rank inequality]

    stable --> lattice[Lattice Theorem of Roth Sotomayor]
    opt --> daalg[Gale Shapley 1962]

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a "matching market" and give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A matching market is a two-sided market in which heterogeneous agents on one side must be paired with agents on the opposite side, and where the allocation is determined by a matching mechanism respecting ordinal preferences rather than a single clearing price. Real-world examples: **(1)** the National Resident Matching Program (NRMP) that matches medical students to hospitals, and **(2)** public school choice systems in cities such as New York and Boston. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for two examples with brief explanation.)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a "stable matching"? State the definition of a blocking pair.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A matching $\mu$ is *stable* if there is no *blocking pair* — that is, no pair $(a_i, b_j)$ with $a_i \in A$ and $b_j \in B$ such that $b_j \succ_{a_i} \mu(a_i)$ **and** $a_i \succ_{b_j} \mu(b_j)$. In words: stability requires that no two agents who are *not* matched to each other both *strictly prefer* each other over their assigned partners. (2 marks for stability + 1 mark for blocking pair definition.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Explain the Gale–Shapley Deferred Acceptance algorithm. Discuss its key properties and its strategic implications for the two sides of the market."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the step-by-step execution of the student-proposing Gale–Shapley algorithm. Use a suitable 3×3 example to illustrate.**

**Model Solution:**

The Gale–Shapley Deferred Acceptance (DA) algorithm, proposed by **David Gale and Lloyd Shapley in 1962**, is an iterative procedure that finds a stable matching in any two-sided market. (1 mark for introduction.)

**Algorithm Steps (Student-Proposing Version):**
1. All students (side A) are initially unengaged. All colleges (side B) are also unengaged. Each student maintains a "next choice" pointer. (1 mark)
2. Each unengaged student *proposes* to the highest-ranked college on their list that they have not yet proposed to. (1 mark)
3. Each college that receives one or more proposals *tentatively accepts* the most-preferred proposer and *rejects* all others. (1 mark)
4. Rejected students become unengaged and advance their pointer to the next choice. (1 mark)
5. The process continues until no unengaged student has any remaining choice. The tentative acceptances form the final stable matching. (1 mark)

**Worked Example (1 mark for execution + 1 mark for verification):**
Consider 3 students $S_1, S_2, S_3$ and 3 colleges $C_1, C_2, C_3$ with the following preferences:

| Student | Ranking | | College | Ranking |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | $C_1 \succ C_2 \succ C_3$ | | $C_1$ | $S_2 \succ S_1 \succ S_3$ |
| $S_2$ | $C_1 \succ C_3 \succ C_2$ | | $C_2$ | $S_1 \succ S_2 \succ S_3$ |
| $S_3$ | $C_2 \succ C_1 \succ C_3$ | | $C_3$ | $S_1 \succ S_2 \succ S_3$ |

- **Round 1:** $S_1 \to C_1$, $S_2 \to C_1$, $S_3 \to C_2$.
- $C_1$ chooses $S_2$ (since $S_2 \succ S_1$). $S_1$ is rejected.
- $C_2$ tentatively accepts $S_3$.
- **Round 2:** $S_1$ now proposes to $C_2$. $C_2$ already holds $S_3$; from $C_2$'s list $S_3 \succ S_1$, so $S_1$ is again rejected.
- **Round 3:** $S_1$ proposes to $C_3$. $C_3$ is free → tentatively accepts.
- **Termination:** Final matching: $\mu^* = \{(S_1, C_3), (S_2, C_1), (S_3, C_2)\}$. This is **stable** (verify: no blocking pair exists).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the three key strategic properties of the Gale–Shapley algorithm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Property 1 — Stability (2 marks):**
The DA algorithm is guaranteed to terminate with a *stable* matching. The proof relies on the fact that no agent is ever rejected by a partner they prefer over their final match. Hence at termination, no blocking pair can exist. The Roth–Sotomayor theorem further guarantees that the set of all stable matchings forms a **distributive lattice**.

**Property 2 — Proposer-Optimality (2 marks):**
The resulting matching is the **best stable matching for the proposing side**. In our example, $S_2$ and $S_3$ obtain their top choices. Equivalently, no other stable matching can make any proposer strictly better off. This follows from the inductive invariant: at every round, every tentatively engaged proposer holds the best partner they could obtain in any stable matching.

**Property 3 — Strategy-Proofness for Proposers (2 marks):**
For the proposing side, *truth-telling* is a **dominant strategy** — no student can gain by misrepresenting their preferences. However, the receiving side (colleges) can gain by strategically misrepresenting. This asymmetry is fundamental and a famous result of **Dubins & Freedman (1981)** and **Roth (1982)**.

**Implication (1 mark):**
Since proposers are the *strategically safe* side, the KTU Module 3 takeaway is that **mechanism design should usually place the more "naïve" or less strategic side as the receivers** to encourage truthful reporting.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Compare the Gale–Shapley (Deferred Acceptance) mechanism with the Boston and Top Trading Cycles mechanisms. Discuss stability, strategy-proofness, and efficiency in each."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Boston Mechanism and Top Trading Cycles (TTC). How does each differ from the Gale–Shapley algorithm in terms of procedure?**

**Model Solution:**

**Boston Mechanism — Immediate Acceptance (3 marks):**
The Boston mechanism processes students in a *fixed, exogenously specified* order (often by lottery or application time). The first student in the order is assigned their **top choice** (if seats remain); the second student then picks their top *remaining* school; and so on. Hence, all decisions are **immediate and irreversible** — hence the alternative name "Immediate Acceptance."

Key procedural differences from DA:
- Boston has a *priority queue* (who applies first matters), while DA has no such ordering.
- Boston is *decentralized* in sequential order; DA is *centralized and simultaneous* in preference submission.
- Boston does not allow tentative acceptance — every choice is final.

**Top Trading Cycles (TTC) — 3 marks:**
TTC, due to **Shapley & Scarf (1974)**, is used in *housing markets* where each agent is endowed with an initial object. Each agent points to the agent holding the object they most prefer. A *cycle* in the resulting directed graph (e.g., $a_1 \to a_2 \to a_3 \to a_1$) is identified; agents in the cycle trade their objects, then are removed from the market. The process repeats.

Key procedural differences from DA:
- TTC handles **endowment-based** markets (one-to-one exchange of existing rights), while DA handles **priority-based** markets.
- TTC's outcome is always in the *core* of the housing market.
- TTC is **strategy-proof**, just like DA on the proposing side.

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
The procedural core difference: DA is *proposal-based with tentative acceptance*, Boston is *priority-based with immediate acceptance*, TTC is *graph-cycle-based with endowment trading*.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Evaluate the three mechanisms on stability, strategy-proofness, and Pareto efficiency. Use an example to show the failure of stability under the Boston mechanism.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Stability | Strategy-Proofness | Pareto Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gale–Shapley (DA) | **Always stable** ✔️ | Strategy-proof for proposers only | Not necessarily, but produces one extreme of lattice |
| Boston Mechanism | **Not stable** ✖️ | Not strategy-proof (gaming order) | Can be inefficient |
| Top Trading Cycles | **Always in core** (stable) ✔️ | Strategy-proof for all agents ✔️ | Unique in core → efficient ✔️ |

**Example — Boston Mechanism Failure (3 marks):**
Consider 3 students $S_1, S_2, S_3$ and 2 schools $A, B$ each with capacity 1.

| Student | 1st | 2nd |
|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | A | B |
| $S_2$ | A | B |
| $S_3$ | B | A |

**Process order:** $S_3$ (first, by lottery). $S_3$ gets $B$. $S_1$ picks — $A$ available, takes $A$. $S_2$ left with $B$.

Final Boston matching: $\mu_B = \{(S_1, A), (S_2, B), (S_3, B)\}$ — but $B$ has capacity 1, so this is **infeasible**! Boston is therefore vulnerable to *oversubscription* and unstable assignments.

The DA outcome would be stable: $S_2$ proposes to $A$ first (rejected because $A$ prefers $S_1$? No — both prefer $A$, so by tiebreak, say $A$ picks $S_1$; $S_2$ then proposes to $B$; $B$ picks $S_2$; $S_3 \to A$ rejected, $S_3 \to B$ rejected, $S_3$ unassigned). Result is stable. (1 mark for conclusion synthesizing the comparison.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing proposer-optimal with overall efficiency** — DA is *not* necessarily Pareto efficient; it only gives the *best stable* outcome for the proposers. Examiners deduct marks if you call DA "the most efficient" without qualification.
> 2. **Calling TTC identical to DA** — TTC handles *endowment* (housing) markets; DA handles *priority* (school/medical) markets. They are conceptually distinct.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the blocking-pair condition** in stability proofs — always check *both* sides of the inequality.
> 4. **Ignoring strategy-proofness asymmetry** — DA is truthful for the *proposing* side only. Many students wrongly claim full strategy-proofness.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Matching market** = two-sided allocation by preferences, not by uniform price.
- **Stable matching** = no *blocking pair* (mutual strict preference to deviate).
- **Gale–Shapley (DA)** finds a stable matching in $O(mn)$ time.
- **DA is proposer-optimal** — best stable outcome for the side that proposes.
- **DA is strategy-proof for proposers only** — receivers can game.
- **Boston mechanism** = priority-based, immediate acceptance, **NOT stable**, **NOT strategy-proof**.
- **Top Trading Cycles (TTC)** = endowment-based, **in the core**, **strategy-proof**, **unique**.
- **Roth–Sotomayor Lattice Theorem** = set of all stable matchings is a distributive lattice; DA outcomes bound the lattice.
- **NRMP, school choice, kidney exchange** = canonical real-world applications.
- **Engineering utility** = labour platforms, gig economy, spectrum allocation.
- **Behavioural angle** = real agents have bounded rationality → incomplete preferences, status-quo bias, and reference dependence, all of which complicate classical stability theorems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioural Economics and Institution Design: Behavioural Insights for Public Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Behavioural Economics and Institution Design: Behavioural Insights for Public Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Behavioural Insights for Public Policy** is the systematic application of empirically validated findings from behavioural economics, cognitive psychology, and social psychology to design, implement, and evaluate public policies. It acknowledges that human decision-making deviates from the rational actor model assumed in classical economics and leverages these predictable deviations (biases, heuristics, and bounded rationality) to improve welfare outcomes without significantly restricting freedom of choice.

The cornerstone concept is **Choice Architecture** — the design of the context in which people make decisions — popularized by Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein in their seminal 2008 work *Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness*. Choice architects deliberately organize the *presentation* of options (defaults, framing, salience, friction) so that the path of least resistance aligns with the decision-maker's long-term interests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Libertarian Paternalism** (Thaler & Sunstein, 2003): A policy philosophy that advocates steering people toward better decisions (paternalism) while preserving their ultimate freedom to choose otherwise (libertarianism). This is the philosophical backbone of nudging.

The discipline has matured into a global movement, institutionalized by the UK's **Behavioural Insights Team (BIT)** — nicknamed the "Nudge Unit" (founded 2010), the U.S. **Social and Behavioral Sciences Team (SBST)**, the World Bank's **Mind, Behavior, and Development Unit (eMBeD)**, and the OECD's behavioural policy hub.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag — Module 3, Unit on Institution Design:** The focus is on how policy *institutions* (defaults, mandates, incentives, information disclosures) interact with human cognitive architecture. The exam expects students to: (1) identify which behavioural bias a policy targets, (2) explain the choice-architecture mechanism, and (3) evaluate the policy's effectiveness and ethical limits.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a school cafeteria. The principal *could* forbid ice cream to fight childhood obesity — that's heavy-handed **restriction**. She *could* also do nothing and watch kids load up on sugar. The **behavioural-insight approach** is a third path: keep the ice cream, but place the fresh fruit at eye level, put the salad bar near the cash register, and put the dessert case in the back, dimly lit.

> **Analogy — "The Staircase and the Escalator":**
> A traditional economist assumes everyone in a building will take the stairs if it's good for their health. A behavioural economist notices that the escalator is right at the entrance, well-lit, and inviting — so the stairs are ignored. The fix is not to demolish the escalator (that violates freedom), but to make the stairs *more convenient*: add a colourful staircase next to the escalator, paint footprints leading up the steps, and play upbeat music near them. People still choose — they just choose better.

This is the essence of a **nudge**: the choice set is unchanged, the incentive structure is unchanged, but the *context* is redesigned so that the cognitive shortcut (System 1 thinking) and the long-term welfare of the decision-maker point in the same direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "EAST" Framework (UK BIT, 2014):** Effective nudges are:
> - **E**asy — reduce friction (e.g., auto-enrolment)
> - **A**ttractive — draw attention (e.g., loss-framed messages)
> - **S**ocial — show what others do (e.g., "9 out of 10 neighbours pay on time")
> - **T**imely — intervene at the moment of decision (e.g., point-of-sale prompts)

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Nudge** | Any aspect of choice architecture that predictably alters behaviour without forbidding options or changing economic incentives. |
| **Choice Architecture** | The design of the *context* in which decisions are made. |
| **Default Rule** | The option that takes effect if the decision-maker does nothing. |
| **Libertarian Paternalism** | Steering behaviour while preserving freedom of choice. |
| **Boost** | Enhancing competence/decision-making capacity (vs. manipulating the environment). |
| **Sludge** | Bureaucratic friction that prevents people from reaching a goal (the "anti-nudge"). |
| **Paternalism** | Interfering with someone's liberty to prevent harm to themselves. |
| **Homo Economicus** | The textbook rational, self-interested, fully-informed agent — the assumption behavioural economics revises. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Standard Policy Fails

Classical public policy (and classical welfare economics) presumes a **Homo Economicus** who:
- Processes all available information
- Computes expected utilities
- Discounts exponentially at a constant rate
- Has stable, well-ordered preferences
- Is not subject to context, framing, or salience

Behavioural economics has, over five decades, accumulated evidence that real decision-makers systematically violate each of these assumptions. The table below maps the violation to a public-policy implication.

| Behavioural Phenomenon | Empirical Finding | Public-Policy Failure It Explains |
|---|---|---|
| **Present Bias** (Laibson, 1997) | $\beta\delta^t$ with $\beta < 1$ | People under-save for retirement; over-consume immediate gratification (credit-card debt). |
| **Loss Aversion** (Kahneman-Tversky) | $v(x) = -2.5 \cdot \vert -x \vert^{0.88}$ for losses | Pure incentive-based tools (taxes) are less salient than equivalent loss-framed messages. |
| **Status Quo Bias / Default Effect** | Opt-in rates ≪ opt-out rates | Sub-optimal defaults (paper enrolment, paper tax filing) cause welfare losses. |
| **Optimism Bias / Overconfidence** | People underestimate personal risk | Voluntary health insurance, pension, and safety-equipment take-up remains low. |
| **Salience & Attention** | Limited attention budget | Generic information campaigns (e.g., generic "eat healthy" ads) are ignored. |
| **Social Preferences / Warm Glow** | People care about being seen to comply | Disclosure of peer behaviour (energy bills comparison) outperforms generic advice. |
| **Framing Effects** | Identical options, different frames, different choices | Health warnings as losses > equivalent warnings as gains. |

---

## 2.2 The Nudge Mechanism — A Formal Model

Let $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ be the set of available options and let $U_i(a_j)$ be agent $i$'s true long-term utility from choosing $a_j$. The **behavioural utility** actually maximized by the agent is:

$$B_i(a_j) = f\bigl(U_i(a_j),\, C_i(a_j),\, S_i(a_j),\, D(a_j),\, N_i\bigr)$$

where:

- $C_i(a_j)$ = **Cognitive cost** (search, comprehension, effort) of evaluating $a_j$
- $S_i(a_j)$ = **Salience** of $a_j$ in the current context
- $D(a_j)$ = **Default status** of $a_j$ (1 if default, 0 otherwise)
- $N_i$ = **Social-norm signal** perceived by agent $i$

A **nudge** is any modification of the choice environment that changes $C$, $S$, $D$, or $N$ — but does **not** change the option set $A$ or the explicit economic incentives (prices, taxes, subsidies). The policy succeeds when:

$$\frac{\partial B_i}{\partial (\text{change})} \text{ pushes the agent toward } \arg\max_{a_j} U_i(a_j)$$

i.e., the behavioural path-of-least-resistance aligns with long-run welfare.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" below are conceptual / structural. Behavioural public policy is not a calculus-heavy topic, but a few canonical expressions are high-yield for KTU Module 3.

### A. Discounting Models (used to justify pension nudges)

| Model | Equation | Behavioural Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Exponential Discounting (Classical) | $$U = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \delta^t u(c_t), \quad 0 < \delta < 1$$ | Time-consistent; $\delta = 0.95$ means same factor each year. |
| Hyperbolic Discounting (Laibson) | $$U = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \beta \cdot \delta^t u(c_t), \quad 0 < \beta < 1$$ | Present bias: $\beta$ multiplies *all* future utilities, so the gap between today ($t=0$) and tomorrow ($t=1$) is bigger than between distant dates. |
| Quasi-Hyperbolic ($\beta$-$\delta$) | $$U = u(c_0) + \beta \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta^t u(c_t)$$ | Standard form used in behavioural welfare economics. |

**Numerical heuristic:** Empirical estimates of $\beta$ cluster around **0.5–0.7**; $\delta$ around **0.95–0.99** annually.

---

### B. Prospect Theory Value Function (used for framing nudges)

$$v(x) = \begin{cases} x^{\alpha} & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\ -\lambda \cdot \vert x \vert^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}$$

Canonical estimates: $\alpha \approx 0.88$ (diminishing sensitivity), $\lambda \approx 2.25$ (losses loom about 2.25× larger than equivalent gains).

**Policy translation:** A \$100 fine framed as a "loss avoidance" message has roughly the deterrent effect of a \$225 gain-framed reward.

---

### C. Default-Effect Magnitude

Empirical rule of thumb from Madrian & Shea (2001) and Johnson et al. (2002):

$$\Delta_{\text{take-up}} = \text{Take-up}_{\text{opt-out}} - \text{Take-up}_{\text{opt-in}} \approx 30\%\text{ to }50\%$$

i.e., switching from opt-in to opt-out enrolment in a 401(k) plan increases participation by **roughly 30–50 percentage points**.

---

### D. Sludge (Anti-Nudge) Audit

Behavioural public policy is two-sided. Where **sludge** exists, the same default effect can be exploited to *suppress* beneficial behaviour. The OECD's Sludge Audits measure:

$$S = \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_j \cdot p_j$$

where $c_j$ is the cost (time, cognitive, monetary) of step $j$ in a bureaucratic process, and $p_j$ is the share of users reaching step $j$. Reducing $S$ — *de-sludging* — is the symmetrical policy goal.

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering-Education Context

KTU trains engineers. Why include this in a B.Tech elective? Three reasons:

1. **Engineers design systems** — interfaces, workflows, supply chains, public infrastructure. Every system embeds a *choice architecture* (forms, defaults, alerts, screens). Engineers who understand nudge theory produce systems that nudge users toward safe, efficient, ethical choices.
2. **Public-private interface** — many KTU graduates will work in government, NGOs, banking, healthcare, energy. Behavioural policy is now standard in these sectors.
3. **Ethics & responsibility** — libertarian paternalism is a powerful tool. Used carelessly, it becomes manipulation. Understanding the framework is the first step toward using it responsibly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Indian **Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)** system (Aadhaar-linked) uses salience (SMS confirmations), default re-routing (Aadhaar as the default ID), and sludge reduction (eliminating intermediary paperwork). The **PMJDY (Jan Dhan Yojana)** uses defaults (auto-zero-balance accounts) to achieve financial inclusion. The **Give It Up** campaign used social-norm nudges to surrender LPG subsidies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Bias → Nudge → Policy → Evaluation

The table below is the **KTU high-yield matrix**. Examiners frequently ask: *"For a given policy problem, identify the bias, design a nudge, and discuss an ethical limitation."* Memorize the rows.

| # | Behavioural Bias | Real-World Policy Problem | The Nudge (Choice-Architecture Fix) | Canonical Empirical Result | Ethical / Practical Concern |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Present Bias** | Low retirement savings | Auto-enrolment with opt-out (default savings) | Madrian & Shea (2001): 401(k) participation rose from 37% (opt-in) to 86% (opt-out) in a U.S. firm. | Inertia trap: people may stay in a sub-optimal fund. |
| 2 | **Status Quo Bias** | Low organ-donor rates | Presumed-consent (opt-out) donor registry | Johnson & Goldstein (2003): opt-out countries have ~90% consent vs. ~15% in opt-in. | Bodily autonomy debate; "slippery slope" concern. |
| 3 | **Loss Aversion** | Tax compliance | "Most people in your area have paid" letter (social norm + loss frame) | UK HM Revenue & Customs (2012): letters increased payment rate by ~5 percentage points, raising £210M. | Privacy of taxpayer data; shaming effects. |
| 4 | **Optimism Bias** | Low flu vaccination | "Reserve your vaccine now" appointment sent *before* season | Milkman et al. (2011): pre-scheduled appointments raised flu-shot rates from 12% to 18% in a U.S. study. | Equity of access; digital divide. |
| 5 | **Salience / Attention** | High household energy use | Home Energy Report showing comparison with neighbours | Allcott & Rogers (2014, *Opower* studies): 1.5–3.5% reduction in electricity consumption sustained over 3 years. | Selection bias; rebounded when reports stopped. |
| 6 | **Choice Overload** | Poor Medicare plan choice | Simplified default plan with ability to switch | Behavioural Insights Team (UK): default-plan assignment improved welfare and reduced administrative cost. | Paternalism — government picks for you. |
| 7 | **Hyperbolic Discounting** | Smoking / obesity | Future-lock commitment devices (e.g., Quit-and-Save) | Giné et al. (2010): conditional cash deposits raised smoking cessation by ~30%. | Equity — only the wealthy can post deposits. |
| 8 | **Anchoring** | Negotiated settlements | First-offer anchor in mediation | Experimental evidence: high anchors shift settlements upward. | Manipulable; bad-faith use. |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Quasi-Hyperbolic Discounting & the Case for Auto-Enrolment

This derivation is a favourite KTU question type: *"Show mathematically why a present-biased agent under-saves under exponential discounting assumptions, and explain how auto-enrolment corrects this."*

**Step 1 — Set up the agent's problem.**

The agent lives for two periods, earns $y_0$ today and $y_1$ tomorrow, and must choose consumption $c_0$ and $c_1$ subject to:

$$c_0 + \frac{c_1}{1+r} = y_0 + \frac{y_1}{1+r}$$

where $r$ is the market interest rate.

**Step 2 — Write the utility function (exponential case).**

$$U^{E} = u(c_0) + \delta \cdot u(c_1), \quad 0 < \delta = \frac{1}{1+\rho} < 1$$

where $\rho$ is the pure time-preference rate. The first-order condition is the **Euler equation**:

$$u'(c_0) = \delta(1+r) \cdot u'(c_1) = \frac{(1+r)}{(1+\rho)} u'(c_1)$$

If $r = \rho$, the agent consumes a smooth path — textbook result.

**Step 3 — Introduce present bias (quasi-hyperbolic).**

Replace $\delta$ with the two-parameter form $\beta \cdot \delta$ for any future period:

$$U^{QH} = u(c_0) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_1), \quad 0 < \beta < 1$$

The new first-order condition is:

$$u'(c_0) = \beta \cdot \delta \cdot (1+r) \cdot u'(c_1)$$

**Step 4 — Compare the two optimal allocations.**

The exponential agent equates $u'(c_0) = \delta(1+r) u'(c_1)$.
The present-biased agent equates $u'(c_0) = \beta \delta(1+r) u'(c_1)$.

Because $0 < \beta < 1$, the present-biased agent acts as if *today's* marginal utility must compensate a smaller future marginal utility weighted by $\beta$ — meaning she consumes **more today** and **less tomorrow** than is optimal.

**Step 5 — The resulting under-saving inequality.**

For a concave utility function $u(\cdot)$, present bias implies:

$$c_0^{QH} > c_0^{E} \quad \text{and} \quad c_1^{QH} < c_1^{E}$$

i.e., the agent *under-saves* for retirement. Welfare loss = $\Delta U = U^{E*} - U^{QH*} > 0$.

**Step 6 — How auto-enrolment (default nudge) restores optimality.**

Under opt-in, the agent must take an active step to enrol in a retirement plan, exercising self-control — which fails for present-biased agents.

Under opt-out auto-enrolment, the default is $c_0^{\text{default}} = c_0^{E}$ (the planner's view of the optimal allocation). To deviate (e.g., to save less), the agent must *opt out*, paying cognitive cost.

Define the opt-out cost as $k > 0$. The agent will opt out only if:

$$\beta \cdot \delta \cdot [u(c_1^{E}) - u(c_1^{QH})] > k$$

For small $k$ (e.g., one extra form), the agent stays enrolled and the long-run optimum is achieved.

**Step 7 — Welfare conclusion.**

> The **default nudge** in 401(k)-style auto-enrolment can be welfare-improving for present-biased agents precisely *because* it exploits the very bias (status-quo / inertia) that causes the under-saving problem.

This is the analytical core of Thaler & Benartzi's **Save More Tomorrow** programme and Madrian & Shea's empirical 401(k) result.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — The "Energy Bill Nudge" Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A municipal electricity board has 100,000 households. The mean monthly consumption is 350 kWh. The board wants to reduce average consumption by 5% using a behavioural nudge. Design the intervention, predict the effect using empirical elasticities, and compute the expected energy saved.

**Step 1 — Identify the bias to target.**
- Habitual consumption → **status quo bias**
- Inattention to kWh numbers → **salience effect**
- "It's just the bill" → **lack of social comparison**

**Step 2 — Design the nudge (Opower-style Home Energy Report).**
- Provide each household with a monthly one-page report.
- Show their kWh in bold large font.
- Show the *average* consumption of their neighbours.
- Add a **smiley face** (e.g., 😃) if below average, 😟 if above.
- Include a one-line conservation tip.

This is a **social-comparison + salience** nudge.

**Step 3 — Apply empirical elasticity.**
Allcott & Rogers (2014) ran the largest randomized study (600,000 households across the U.S.). They found:

$$\Delta \ln(C) = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{\text{neighbour}}}{C}\right)$$

where $D = 1$ if the household receives the report, $C$ is own consumption, and $\alpha \approx 0.05$ empirically.

A 5% above-average household can therefore be expected to reduce consumption by about:

$$\Delta C \approx 0.05 \times \ln(1.05) \times C \approx 0.05 \times 0.0488 \times 350 \approx 0.85 \text{ kWh/month}$$

For a 10% above-average household:

$$\Delta C \approx 0.05 \times \ln(1.10) \times 350 \approx 1.74 \text{ kWh/month}$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate.**
Assume a uniform distribution of households 0–20% above the mean. Average saving ≈ 1.3 kWh/month per household. Across 100,000 households:

$$\text{Total monthly saving} = 1.3 \times 100{,}000 = 130{,}000 \text{ kWh/month}$$

$$\text{Total annual saving} = 1.56 \text{ million kWh/year}$$

**Step 5 — Cost-benefit.**

- Cost of printing reports: ₹10 per household/month = ₹12M/year (₹1.2 crore).
- Revenue per kWh: ₹7 (typical Indian tariff).
- Gross benefit: $1{,}560{,}000 \times 7 = $ **₹10.92 million** (₹1.09 crore) — *just below* cost, so the nudge is at breakeven.

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
The Home Energy Report nudge is *behaviourally effective* but *not automatically cost-effective* in India at current tariff / paper-cost ratios. Policy recommendation: **digital delivery** (WhatsApp / SMS) to reduce cost by ~90%, restoring a positive benefit-cost ratio.

> [!TIP]
> This is the kind of numerical reasoning KTU Module 3 expects: combine a behavioural finding with a back-of-envelope cost-benefit calculation.

---

## 3.4 The "Boost vs. Nudge" Analytical Comparison

A KTU-favoured discussion point: are nudges the right tool, or should policy *boost* the agent's competence?

| Dimension | **Nudge** (Thaler-Sunstein) | **Boost** (Hertwig-Grüne-Yanoff) |
|---|---|---|
| Metaphor | Adjust the stairs/escalator | Teach the agent to walk better |
| Target | Choice environment | Decision-making capacity |
| Mechanism | Defaults, framing, salience, friction | Education, training, decision aids, statistical literacy |
| Long-run effect | Behaviour change persists *if* the nudge remains | Behaviour change persists *after* the intervention ends (capability rises) |
| Critic | Manipulation risk; may exploit biases | Slow; less scalable; depends on attention |
| Example | Auto-enrolment in pension | Financial-literacy curriculum in schools |
| Compatible? | **Yes** — most modern frameworks combine both (boosts to support nudges). | |

**Derivation of complementarity:** Let $H(t)$ be the agent's decision-making skill and $N(t)$ the strength of the nudge. Behavioural welfare is:

$$W(t) = H(t) + \lambda \cdot N(t)$$

If nudges crowd out skill-building (e.g., people stop learning because the default always works), $\partial H / \partial N < 0$, and the long-run welfare is:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{dH}{dt} + \lambda \frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{\partial H}{\partial N} \frac{dN}{dt} + \lambda \frac{dN}{dt}$$

For a small $\lambda$ and a strong negative $\partial H / \partial N$, $dW/dt$ can be negative in the long run — **the "nudge trap."** This is the strongest KTU-level critique of pure nudging and motivates a balanced *nudge-and-boost* policy mix.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Choice-Architecture Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    node2 --> node3["Identify Choice Architect e.g. employer govt platform"]
    node3 --> node4["Apply EAST Nudge E A S T"]
    node4 --> node5["Pilot RCT or A B test"]
    node5 --> node6["Evaluation Uptake cost benefit equity"]
    node6 --> node7["Scale or Sludge Audit"]
    node7 --> node8["Feedback Loop back to step 1"]
    node1:::goal
    node4:::nudge

    classDef loop fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#5d3a8e,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical Behavioural Insights Team (UK BIT) policy pipeline. Every step is a *testable hypothesis*; the pilot RCT (Step 5) is what distinguishes behavioural public policy from mere paternalistic guesswork.


**Reading the diagram:** Libertarian Paternalism is the green quadrant — the agent *can* deviate from the default; she is merely nudged. Pure restriction (ban) is qualitatively different and is **not** a nudge.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Nudge vs. Boost vs. Sludge (the Trifecta)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Choice Environment"] --> nodeB["Nudge<br>Subtly helps you choose well"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Sludge<br>Subtly blocks you from choosing well"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Boost<br>Teaches you to choose well next time"]
    nodeB:::good
    nodeC:::bad
    nodeD:::good

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry principle:** Wherever there is a *nudge* in the system, removing or inverting it becomes a *sludge*. Auditing for sludge is the same skill as designing a nudge — applied to the obstruction side.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — The EAST Framework as a Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e["E Easy<br>Reduce friction"] --> a["A Attractive<br>Draw attention"]
    a --> s["S Social<br>Show what others do"]
    s --> t["T Timely<br>Intervene at the moment"]
    t --> e
    e:::step
    a:::step
    s:::step
    t:::step
    classDef step fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Effective nudges in practice combine all four EAST elements in a cycle. For example, an energy report is **Easy** to read (one page), **Attractive** (smiley face), **Social** (neighbour comparison), **Timely** (arrives with the bill).
