# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Distinguish between Adverse Selection and Moral Hazard with one real-world example each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

| Component | Marks | Content |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Definition of Adverse Selection | 1 | A pre-transactional market failure where the *hidden characteristics* of one party drive out good counterparties (e.g., used car "lemons" market, Akerlof 1970). |
| Definition of Moral Hazard | 1 | A post-transactional market failure where the *hidden actions* of an agent shift risk to the principal after the contract is signed (e.g., a fire-insured homeowner neglecting fire-prevention). |
| Differentiating characteristic | 1 | Timing — Adverse Selection is **ex-ante** (before deal); Moral Hazard is **ex-post** (after deal). |

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"What is meant by 'Signaling' in Behavioural Economics? Illustrate with Michael Spence's education model."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Signaling is a remedial action taken by the *better-informed* party to credibly convey private information to the uninformed party. The signal must be **costly enough** that low-type agents cannot profitably mimic it.
2. **Spence's Education Model (2 marks):** In Spence (1973), workers signal their *innate ability* to employers by acquiring education. High-ability workers face a *lower marginal cost* of education, so a separating equilibrium emerges where only high-ability workers acquire degrees above a threshold $e^{*}$. The model assumes education has no *intrinsic productivity* — it is a *pure signal* whose only function is information transmission.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

---

### 🔹 Question B-A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain George Akerlof's *Market for Lemons* model. Derive the condition for market unraveling in a market with quality uniformly distributed on $[0, \bar{q}]$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate **three** real-world institutional remedies that have been used to mitigate adverse selection in markets. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Model Setup (1 mark):**
A market with $N$ sellers; each seller privately knows the quality $q_i$ of his good, with $q \sim U[0, \bar{q}]$. Buyers value quality at $\beta q$ where $\beta > 1$ (so there are *potential* gains from trade), but buyers cannot observe $q$ and must offer a single price $P$ based on the *expected* quality in the market.

**Step 2 — Buyer Pricing (1 mark):**
Buyers offer the expected quality times their valuation:
$$P = \beta \cdot \mathbb{E}[q \mid q \leq P] = \beta \cdot \frac{P}{2}$$
because under uniformity, the conditional mean of $q \mid q \leq P$ equals $P/2$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition (2 marks):**
For an interior Bayesian-Nash equilibrium, the price must satisfy the fixed-point equation:
$$P = \beta \cdot \frac{P}{2} \Rightarrow P \left(1 - \frac{\beta}{2}\right) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solving the Unraveling Condition (2 marks):**
- If $\beta < 2$: the only solution is $P^{*} = 0$ → **complete market collapse**.
- If $\beta = 2$: any $P \in [0, \bar{q}]$ is an equilibrium (knife-edge case).
- If $\beta > 2$: the partial-pool equilibrium $P^{*} = \frac{\beta \bar{q}}{\beta - 2}$ is positive → market *survives* but at reduced volume.

**Step 5 — Economic Intuition (1 mark):**
Akerlof's genius was to show that even with *enormous potential gains from trade*, the *fear of being cheated* can drive markets to extinction. This is the central paradox of asymmetric information.

#### Model Solution to B-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Remedy 1: Warranties and Guarantees (2 marks):**
Sellers offer binding warranties that transfer risk back to themselves. A lemon-prone seller cannot afford to offer a generous warranty; thus warranties act as a *credible signal of quality*. Akerlof (1970) himself proposed warranties as a Pareto-improving institutional response.

**Remedy 2: Reputation Systems and Brand Equity (2 marks):**
In repeat-purchase settings (e.g., restaurants, e-commerce), sellers invest in *reputation capital* to assure future buyers of quality. Platform-mediated reputation (e.g., Amazon, Zomato ratings) dramatically reduces adverse selection by aggregating dispersed buyer experiences.

**Remedy 3: Third-Party Certification (2 marks):**
Independent certifiers (e.g., ISI mark, ISO 9001, BIS certification, JEE scores) provide a *neutral* verification of hidden characteristics. This transforms a *credence good* into a *search good*, restoring informational symmetry.

**Critical Evaluation (1 mark):**
All three remedies are imperfect — warranties may be dishonored, reputation can be gamed (fake reviews), and certifiers can be *captured* (regulatory failure, e.g., 2008 credit-rating agencies). Thus, complete elimination of information asymmetry is impossible — only *mitigation* is feasible.

---

### 🔹 Question B-B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**(a)** Explain the *Principal–Agent Problem* in detail. Derive the *Incentive Compatibility Constraint* and the *Participation Constraint* under a state-contingent wage scheme. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** "Moral hazard cannot be eliminated, only reallocated." Critically discuss this statement with reference to the financial crisis of 2008 and the Indian banking sector. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Framework Setup (1 mark):**
A *principal* (employer, shareholder, insurer) delegates a task to an *agent* (employee, manager, insured party). The agent chooses an action $a \in \{a_H, a_L\}$ (high or low effort) at personal cost $C(a)$, where $C(a_H) > C(a_L) \geq 0$. Output $y = a + \theta$, with $\theta$ being an i.i.d. random shock.

**Step 2 — Define the Two Constraints (3 marks):**

- **Participation Constraint (PC) / Individual Rationality (IR):** The agent must prefer to participate in the contract over his outside option $\bar{u}$:
$$\mathbb{E}[u(w(y)) \mid a] - C(a) \geq \bar{u}$$

- **Incentive Compatibility (IC) Constraint:** The agent must prefer to exert high effort:
$$\mathbb{E}[u(w(y)) \mid a_H] - C(a_H) \geq \mathbb{E}[u(w(y)) \mid a_L] - C(a_L)$$

**Step 3 — Optimal Contract (2 marks):**
The principal chooses $w(y)$ to maximize expected profit:
$$\max_{w(\cdot)} \mathbb{E}[y - w(y)]$$
subject to both PC and IC, with $w(y) \geq 0$ (limited liability). The *first-best* solution (full information) is unattainable under asymmetric information because of the risk-shifting / incentive trade-off.

**Step 4 — Key Result (1 mark):**
The optimal contract under asymmetric information involves **state-contingent bonuses** (high pay in good states, low pay in bad states), creating a *linear* wage structure $w = \alpha + \beta y$ where $\beta < 1$ is the *incentive intensity* and $(1-\beta)$ is the *insurance* component.

#### Model Solution to B-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Argumentative Thesis (1 mark):**
Moral hazard arises whenever a party insulated from consequences can shift risk to a counterparty. Complete elimination requires *perfect monitoring*, which is prohibitively costly — therefore moral hazard can only be *redistributed* through contracts, not destroyed.

**Case 1: 2008 Global Financial Crisis (3 marks):**
- US bankers originated subprime mortgages, packaged them into Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS), sold them to investors, and earned upfront fees.
- After sale, originators bore *no risk* of default → incentive to lower underwriting standards.
- Rating agencies (Moody's, S&P) issued inflated AAA ratings due to issuer-pays model (a textbook third-party *certifier capture*).
- Result: Risk was *redistributed* from Wall Street to global investors and taxpayers via TARP bailouts; the *moral hazard itself persisted* in derivative markets.

**Case 2: Indian Banking Sector — PSU Bank NPAs (3 marks):**
- Indian Public Sector Banks (PSBs) lent aggressively to large corporates in the 2010–2014 period, often under political pressure.
- Borrowers diverted funds → NPAs ballooned to ₹ 10+ lakh crore by 2018.
- The 2019 *PSBs Recapitalization* (₹ 70,000 crore) effectively socialized losses, *redistributing* moral hazard from PSB managers to the Indian taxpayer.
- RBI's 2018 *Prompt Corrective Action (PCA)* framework and *Insolvency & Bankruptcy Code (IBC) 2016* attempt to *re-internalize* moral hazard by hardening budget constraints.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deductions**
> - ❌ Confusing *signaling* with *screening* (signaling = informed party acts; screening = uninformed party designs a menu).
> - ❌ Treating *adverse selection* and *moral hazard* as synonyms (they differ in *timing* — before vs. after the contract).
> - ❌ Writing long essays without the *mathematical derivation* — KTU 14-mark questions *always* require at least 4–5 lines of formal derivation.
> - ❌ Omitting *real-world examples* — pure theory without application is penalized.
> - ❌ Writing "lemons are bad cars" without explaining the *Bayesian belief-formation mechanism*.
> - ❌ Forgetting the *Rothschild–Stiglitz* zero-profit condition when discussing insurance screening.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU Module 4**

- ✅ **Adverse Selection = Hidden Characteristics = Ex-Ante = Akerlof (1970)**
- ✅ **Moral Hazard = Hidden Actions = Ex-Post = Arrow (1963) / Pauly (1968)**
- ✅ **Lemons Probability** = $P(\text{lemon}) = \dfrac{\alpha \lambda}{\alpha \lambda + (1-\alpha)}$
- ✅ **Signaling** is initiated by the **informed** party (Spence education model).
- ✅ **Screening** is initiated by the **uninformed** party (Rothschild–Stiglitz insurance menus).
- ✅ **Akerlof Unraveling Condition**: market collapses if buyer's valuation coefficient $\beta < 2 \cdot$ mean quality.
- ✅ **Spence Paradox**: separating equilibrium may involve *socially wasteful* signaling.
- ✅ **Principal–Agent Trade-off**: Risk-sharing ↔ Incentive Provision (Holmström, 1979).
- ✅ **Three Remedies**: *Warranties* + *Reputation* + *Third-Party Certification*.
- ✅ **Nobel Prize 2001**: Akerlof, Spence, Stiglitz jointly for asymmetric information analysis.
- ✅ **Limitation**: All remedies are *second-best* — they reduce but never eliminate the welfare loss.
- ✅ **Bounded Rationality Link**: Heuristics like *availability* and *anchoring* interact with asymmetric info to amplify market failure (Behavioural twist on classical Akerlof).
- ✅ **KTU 14-Marker Template**: (i) Define → (ii) Diagram → (iii) Derive → (iv) Apply → (v) Critique.
- ✅ **Real-world Indian examples**: IRDA, SEBI disclosure norms, BIS certification, *Jan Dhan Yojana* (financial inclusion reduces information asymmetry for the poor).
- ✅ **Real-world Global examples**: 2008 GFC, Lemon Laws, Carfax vehicle history, eBay money-back guarantee.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INFORMATION ASYMMETRY (PECBT866 | KTU 2024 SCHEME)**

topics.title: Behavioural Interventions for Promoting Healthy Behaviours
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Behavioural Interventions for Promoting Healthy Behaviours # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Behavioural Interventions for Promoting Healthy Behaviours** are structured, evidence-based applications of behavioural economics principles (heuristics, biases, choice architecture, and cognitive constraints) designed to systematically influence individual and collective health-related decisions without removing freedom of choice or significantly altering economic incentives. These interventions operate on the foundational premise of **Behavioural Public Policy**, which assumes that human decision-making in health contexts is governed by **System 1 (fast, automatic, emotional)** and **System 2 (slow, deliberate, logical)** thinking, as proposed in **Dual Process Theory**. The goal is to design *nudges*, *boosts*, and *choice architectures* that align with human cognitive architecture to improve outcomes in domains like vaccination, diet, exercise, smoking cessation, medication adherence, and preventive screening. ### Core Terminology Grid | Term | Definition | Health Application | |------|------------|-------------------| | **Nudge** | A predictable aspect of choice architecture that alters behaviour without forbidding options | Placing fruit at eye level in cafeterias | | **Boost** | Enhancement of competence or decision-making skill | Numeracy training for reading food labels | | **Libertarian Paternalism** | Policy stance preserving freedom while steering choices | Organ donation default rules | | **Choice Architecture** | The design of the context in which decisions are made | Hospital discharge form layouts | | **Sludge** | Excessive friction in a process that prevents desired action | Complicated insurance claim forms | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — The Staircase vs Escalator Design** > > Imagine a hospital building. The **rational economic model** would assume that if stairs are present, health-conscious individuals will *choose* them. However, behavioural observation reveals that **escalator usage is roughly 95%** even when stairs are available and visibly accessible. The intervention here is **architectural**, not educational — by painting the stairs with music motifs, placing motivational signage, and improving their aesthetic appeal (the "piano staircase" intervention), stair usage rose to **66%**, a **2300% increase** in healthy behaviour. > > This is the essence of a **behavioural health intervention**: instead of changing *what people want*, we change *how the choice is presented* to make the healthier option the path of least resistance. ### The Health Behaviour Change Trinity Every behavioural health intervention can be mapped onto three dimensions: 1. **The Individual Mind** — addressing cognitive biases, time-inconsistent preferences, and bounded rationality 2. **The Choice Environment** — restructuring defaults, framing, and information salience 3. **The Social Context** — leveraging social norms, peer effects, and identity-based motivation > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Board Favourite Concept:** The interplay between **present bias (β–δ model)** and **loss aversion** is the most frequently examined intersection in health intervention design questions. Always link the intervention to the specific cognitive bias it addresses. ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants - **Default Effect Magnitude:** Opt-out organ donation programs achieve participation rates of **90–99%**, while opt-in programs achieve only **8–15%** (Johnson & Goldstein, 2003 meta-pattern). - **Present Bias Discount Factor (β):** Typically estimated in the range **0.7 ≤ β ≤ 0.9** for health behaviours like exercise and savings. - **Loss Aversion Coefficient (λ):** Empirical range **1.5 ≤ λ ≤ 2.5** (Kahneman & Tversky baseline: λ ≈ 2.25). - **The EAST Framework** (Behavioural Insights Team): **E**asy, **A**ttractive, **S**ocial, **T**imely. - **COM-B Model Components:** **C**apability, **O**pportunity, **M**otivation → **B**ehaviour. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Theoretical Architecture of Health Behavioural Interventions Health behavioural interventions rest on **three theoretical pillars**. Understanding each is mandatory for KTU ESE questions. ### Pillar 1: Bounded Rationality in Health Decisions Herbert Simon's **Satisficing Principle** states that individuals do not optimize health outcomes; instead, they select the *first option that exceeds an aspiration level*. In health contexts, this manifests as: - Accepting the first treatment option offered - Defaulting to the doctor's recommendation without researching alternatives - Choosing branded medications due to recognition rather than efficacy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Satisficing Utility Function (Simon, 1957):** > Decision-maker selects alternative $a^*$ such that $U(a^*) \geq A$, where $A$ is the **aspiration level**. Search stops at the first $a$ satisfying this condition. ### Pillar 2: Dual Process Theory (Kahneman, 2003) | System | Characteristics | Health Behaviour Implication | |--------|-----------------|------------------------------| | **System 1** | Fast, automatic, intuitive, emotional | Reaching for sugary snacks; skipping sunscreen | | **System 2** | Slow, effortful, logical, deliberate | Reading nutritional labels; calculating BMI | **Interventions must engage System 1** to be effective at scale, because System 2 activation requires cognitive resources that are depleted in busy daily contexts. ### Pillar 3: The EAST Framework (Behavioural Insights Team, UK) This is the **highest-yield framework** for KTU questions on health interventions. 1. **EASY** — Reduce friction. Default options, simplified forms, automatic enrolment. 2. **ATTRACTIVE** — Draw attention. Salience, personalization, gamification. 3. **SOCIAL** — Show what others do. Descriptive norms, peer comparison. 4. **TIMELY** — Prompt at the right moment. Just-in-time interventions, immediate feedback. ## 2.2 The COM-B Model (Michie et al., 2011) The **COM-B Model** is a comprehensive behaviour change framework mapping the three necessary conditions for any behaviour: $$\text{Capability} + \text{Opportunity} + \text{Motivation} = \text{Behaviour}$$ | Component | Sub-types | Health Intervention Example | |-----------|-----------|------------------------------| | **Capability** | Physical, Psychological | Medication reminders (physical); health literacy training (psychological) | | **Opportunity** | Physical, Social | Walking paths in parks (physical); fitness groups (social) | | **Motivation** | Reflective, Automatic | Goal-setting apps (reflective); habit-formation cues (automatic) | ## 2.3 Major Categories of Health Behavioural Interventions ### Category 1: Default Option Restructuring (The Opt-In / Opt-Out Lever) - **Mechanism:** Exploits **status quo bias** and **omission bias**. - **Health Applications:** Organ donation, vaccination enrolment, generic drug dispensing, healthy food defaults in cafeterias, retirement health savings. ### Category 2: Framing and Information Design - **Loss Frame:** "Not screening means losing 5 years of healthy life." - **Gain Frame:** "Screening adds 5 healthy years to your life." - **Empirical Finding (Rothman & Salovey, 1997):** Loss framing is more effective for **detection behaviours** (screening), while gain framing works better for **prevention behaviours** (sunscreen, diet). ### Category 3: Social Norms and Peer Comparison - **Descriptive Norms:** "70% of patients in this clinic take their medication as prescribed." - **Injunctive Norms:** "Your doctor strongly recommends this vaccination." - **Utility Function Inclusion:** $U_i = U_i(x_i, x_{-i})$ — utility depends on others' choices. ### Category 4: Commitment Devices - **Mechanism:** Addressing **present bias** (β–δ model) by binding future behaviour to a present commitment. - **Examples:** Gym membership with upfront cost, smoking cessation deposit contracts, medication lockboxes. ### Category 5: Incentive Design (Standard and Behavioural) - **Standard incentives:** Direct payments for healthy behaviour. - **Loss-framed incentives:** Deposits forfeited upon non-compliance. - **Empirical result:** Loss-framed incentives produce **2–3× greater behavioural change** than equivalent gain-framed incentives in health domains. ### Category 6: Reminders and Implementation Intentions - **Gollwitzer's Implementation Intentions:** "If-Then" planning. - **Format:** "If situation X arises, then I will perform behaviour Y." - **Health Applications:** Vaccination reminders, medication adherence prompts, exercise scheduling. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Application Domain | |---|-------------------|----------------|--------------------| | 1 | $\beta\text{-}\delta \text{ model: } U_t = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \beta^t \delta^t u(c_t)$ | $\beta$ = present bias, $\delta$ = exponential discount | Health savings, exercise adherence | | 2 | Loss Aversion: $V(x) = \lambda \cdot V(-x)$ for losses | $\lambda \approx 2.25$ | Incentive framing | | 3 | Aspiration-based choice: $U(a^*) \geq A$ | $A$ = aspiration level | Doctor-patient choice | | 4 | Probability weighting: $w(p) = \frac{p^\gamma}{(p^\gamma + (1-p)^\gamma)^{1/\gamma}}$ | $\gamma \approx 0.61$ | Health risk communication | | 5 | COM-B: $C + O + M \rightarrow B$ | Capability, Opportunity, Motivation | Intervention design | | 6 | Reference-dependent utility: $v(x \vert r) = (x-r)^{\alpha}$ for gains | $r$ = reference point | Framing health outcomes | | 7 | Default effect: $P_{\text{opt-out}} \approx 0.90$; $P_{\text{opt-in}} \approx 0.15$ | Status quo bias coefficient | Organ donation, vaccination | | 8 | Implementation intention: $P(B \vert \text{If-Then}) = k \cdot P(B \vert \text{no plan})$ | $k \approx 1.5 - 3$ | Adherence interventions | | 9 | EAST heuristic: $\{E, A, S, T\}$ | Easy, Attractive, Social, Timely | Public health design | | 10 | Social norm utility: $U_i = \alpha u(x_i) + \gamma u(x_i - \bar{x})$ | Conformity motive | Healthy eating campaigns | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility Note:** While this course belongs to social science, the **mathematical modelling of health behaviour** intersects with public health engineering, healthcare systems design, insurance risk modelling, and telemedicine interface design — all relevant to KTU multidisciplinary projects. ## 2.5 The β–δ Model and Present Bias (Detailed Treatment) **Laibson (1997)** proposed the **quasi-hyperbolic discounting model** to capture empirically observed present-biased preferences in health: $$U_t = u(c_t) + \beta \sum_{\tau=1}^{T-t} \delta^{\tau} u(c_{t+\tau})$$ Where: - $u(c_t)$ = instantaneous utility at time $t$ - $\delta \in (0,1)$ = standard exponential discount factor (long-run patience) - $\beta \in (0,1]$ = present bias factor (short-run impatience) - $\beta < 1$ implies the individual places **disproportionately less weight on the present relative to the future** **Health Implication:** A smoker may intellectually value future health ($\delta$ moderate) but consistently overweights present pleasure ($\beta$ low), creating a policy target for commitment devices and defaults. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Default Effect Magnification (Status Quo Bias) The default effect occurs because the **cognitive cost of active choice** exceeds the **disutility of the default option** for most individuals. We can formalize this as follows. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Let $C_a$ = cognitive cost of active opt-out, $C_i$ = cognitive cost of active opt-in, and $D$ = disutility of the default option. For an **opt-out** regime, the individual accepts the default if: $$D < C_a + \epsilon_a$$ For an **opt-in** regime, the individual must actively choose to participate: $$U_{\text{participate}} - D_{\text{participate}} > C_i + \epsilon_i$$ **Step 1:** Define the participation probability under opt-out: $$P_{\text{opt-out}} = P(D < C_a + \epsilon_a)$$ Assuming $D$ and $C_a$ are independent and $C_a \gg 0$ (high friction), this probability is high. **Step 2:** Define the participation probability under opt-in: $$P_{\text{opt-in}} = P(U_{\text{participate}} - D_{\text{participate}} > C_i + \epsilon_i)$$ Since $C_i$ is the same friction cost and $D_{\text{participate}}$ is non-zero (e.g., perceived risk of organ donation), this probability is lower. **Step 3:** Compute the ratio (default effect ratio): $$R_{\text{default}} = \frac{P_{\text{opt-out}}}{P_{\text{opt-in}}}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting empirical estimates from organ donation studies: $$P_{\text{opt-out}} \approx 0.90, \quad P_{\text{opt-in}} \approx 0.15$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{default}} &= \frac{0.90}{0.15} \\ &= 6.0 \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** Switching from opt-in to opt-out produces a **6-fold increase** in participation for the same underlying population preferences. This is the *default effect leverage* in health policy. > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Tip:** Always derive the *ratio* rather than absolute probability. KTU examiners reward **comparative magnitude analysis** with full marks. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Loss Aversion in Health Incentive Design Kahneman and Tversky's **Prospect Theory** forms the theoretical basis for loss-framed health incentives. ### Step-by-Step Derivation The value function in PT is: $$v(x) = \begin{cases} x^{\alpha} & \text{if } x \geq 0 \text{ (gains)} \\ -\lambda(-x)^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < 0 \text{ (losses)} \end{cases}$$ Where $\alpha \approx 0.88$ and $\lambda \approx 2.25$. **Step 1:** Consider a $100 incentive for completing a health screening. Gain-frame value: $$v_{\text{gain}} = (100)^{0.88} \approx 100^{0.88} \approx 53.7$$ **Step 2:** Consider a $100 deposit that is **forfeited** if the health screening is not completed. Loss-frame value: $$v_{\text{loss}} = -2.25 \times (-100)^{0.88} \approx -2.25 \times 53.7 \approx -120.8$$ **Step 3:** The psychological magnitude is: $$|v_{\text{loss}}| / v_{\text{gain}} = 120.8 / 53.7 \approx 2.25$$ **Step 4:** The behavioural response ratio is approximately: $$R_{\text{loss/gain}} \approx \lambda = 2.25$$ **Step 5:** Conclusion — A $100 loss-framed deposit produces **roughly 2.25× the behavioural impact** of a $100 gain-framed reward in health contexts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Empirical Caveat:** Loss-framed incentives may **backfire** for low-income populations due to genuine financial constraints. Behavioural interventions must be paired with **equity analysis**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Health Behaviours to Interventions | Health Behaviour | Dominant Bias | Recommended Intervention | Expected Effect Size | |------------------|---------------|--------------------------|----------------------| | **Vaccination hesitancy** | Omission bias, present bias | Opt-out default + social norm messaging | High (5–10× uptake) | | **Medication non-adherence** | Present bias, complexity aversion | Reminders + simplification + blister packs | Moderate (1.3–1.5× adherence) | | **Unhealthy diet** | Salience bias, present bias | Cafeteria architecture + traffic light labels | Moderate (10–20% selection shift) | | **Physical inactivity** | Status quo bias, present bias | Piano staircases + commitment devices | Moderate (2–3× usage) | | **Smoking** | Projection bias, present bias | Deposit contracts + loss-framed warnings | High (1.5–2.5× cessation) | | **Organ donation** | Status quo bias, omission bias | Opt-out default | Very high (6× registration) | | **Screening avoidance** | Omission bias, optimism bias | Loss-framed letters + default appointments | High (1.5–2× attendance) | | **Hand hygiene** | Inattention, salience | UV-marked germs + visual cues | Moderate (1.4× compliance) | | **Sugar consumption** | Salience, present bias | Smaller plates + placement + labelling | Moderate (10–25% reduction) | | **Alcohol overconsumption** | Hot-cold empathy gap | Pre-commitment + unit labels | Moderate (1.2–1.5× reduction) | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Implementation Intentions for Vaccination **Scenario:** A district health officer wants to design an intervention to increase second-dose COVID-19 booster uptake. The current compliance rate is 35%. After deploying a behavioural intervention combining **implementation intentions** + **reminders** + **social norm messaging**, the rate rises to 52.5%. **Step 1:** Calculate the absolute change: $$\Delta = 52.5\% - 35\% = 17.5 \text{ percentage points}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the relative change: $$R_{\text{relative}} = \frac{17.5}{35} \times 100\% = 50\%$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the multiplier: $$M = \frac{52.5}{35} = 1.5$$ **Step 4:** Map to COM-B: - **Capability** boosted by: easy appointment booking - **Opportunity** boosted by: SMS reminders, mobile clinics - **Motivation** boosted by: descriptive norm ("70% of your neighbours are vaccinated") **Step 5:** Cost-effectiveness ratio (assuming intervention cost ₹50 per person): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Cost per additional vaccination} &= \frac{50}{0.175} \\ &= ₹285.71 \text{ per additional dose} \end{aligned}$$ This is far lower than the **₹15,000 average hospitalization cost** for severe COVID, yielding a **benefit-cost ratio of 52.5:1**. > [!NOTE] > **Board Exam Tip:** When answering numerical questions on health interventions, always close with a **policy recommendation** grounded in **equity** and **cost-effectiveness** — KTU examiners award bonus marks for this. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Causal Chain of a Behavioural Health Intervention ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cognitive Bias Present

to Capability and Motivation] FB --> C FB --> M ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Health Intervention Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Health Problem
Identified] --> Q1{Is behaviour
deliberate or
automatic?} Q1 -->|Automatic| A1[Engage System 1
via Defaults and Cues] Q1 -->|Deliberate| A2[Engage System 2
via Information and Boosting] A1 --> EAST[Apply EAST
Framework] A2 --> EAST EAST --> Q2{Population
vulnerable?} Q2 -->|Yes| EQ[Add Equity
Safeguards] Q2 -->|No| PILOT[Run Pilot
Trial] EQ --> PILOT PILOT --> Q3{Effect size
significant?} Q3 -->|Yes| SCALE[Scale Up
and Monitor] Q3 -->|No| ITER[Iterate Design
using COM-B] ITER --> EAST SCALE --> EVAL[Continuous
Evaluation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Note:** The fourth diagram (decision tree) is the **highest-yield schematic** for ESE Part B answers on health intervention design. Memorize its structure and you can adapt it to virtually any scenario question. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **Q: Define the EAST framework in behavioural health interventions. Provide one example of each component in a public health context.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **EAST framework** is a four-principle heuristic developed by the UK Behavioural Insights Team for designing effective behaviourally informed interventions. | Component | Meaning | Public Health Example | |-----------|---------|----------------------| | **Easy** | Reduce friction and effort | Pre-filled vaccination consent forms | | **Attractive** | Make the behaviour visually salient | Bright fruit displays at school canteen eye level | | **Social** | Highlight what others do | "78% of parents in your district vaccinate their children" | | **Timely** | Prompt when the cost is lowest | SMS reminder sent 7 a.m. on appointment day | **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 per correct example × 4 = 2 marks, with 1 mark for structural presentation.)** ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **Q: Explain the term "loss aversion" with reference to health incentive design.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Loss aversion** is the cognitive tendency where the psychological pain of losing a given amount is approximately **2.25 times greater** than the pleasure of gaining the same amount, formalized in Prospect Theory as the coefficient $\lambda \approx 2.25$. In **health incentive design**, this asymmetry implies that a **₹500 deposit forfeited** upon missing a health screening produces a stronger behavioural pull than a **₹500 reward** granted for completing it. Empirically, loss-framed incentives generate **2 to 2.5 times** higher compliance rates in vaccination, smoking cessation, and medication adherence programs. However, designers must consider **equity** because loss-framed deposits impose genuine financial risk on low-income populations. **(3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition + 1 mark for health application + 0.5 marks for equity caveat.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q (a)** Discuss the **COM-B model** of behaviour change. How can it be applied to design a behavioural intervention for improving medication adherence among Type-2 Diabetes patients in Kerala? **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** Critically evaluate the **ethical implications** of using **default options** in public health policy. Use the example of organ donation default rules. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` --- #### Model Solution for Q A (a) — 7 Marks The **COM-B Model** (Michie, van Stralen & West, 2011) posits that any behaviour (B) requires three interacting conditions: 1. **Capability (C):** Physical and psychological ability to enact the behaviour. 2. **Opportunity (O):** External physical and social factors enabling the behaviour. 3. **Motivation (M):** Reflective beliefs and automatic emotional drives. **Application to Type-2 Diabetes Medication Adherence in Kerala:** | COM-B Component | Kerala-Specific Challenge | Behavioural Intervention | |-----------------|---------------------------|--------------------------| | **Physical Capability** | Forgetfulness with complex regimens | Weekly pill organizers; blister packs labelled in Malayalam | | **Psychological Capability** | Low health literacy | Visual pictogram instructions; ASHA worker-led training | | **Physical Opportunity** | Long travel to pharmacy | Mobile pharmacy vans in rural Kasaragod/Wayanad | | **Social Opportunity** | Family-based decision making | Engage eldest family member in adherence pact | | **Reflective Motivation** | Belief that insulin causes addiction | Doctor-led myth-busting video consultations | | **Automatic Motivation** | Habit disruption | Pair medication with chai/coffee time ritual | **Concluding Statement (1 mark):** The COM-B model forces intervention designers to move beyond awareness campaigns and address **structural frictions** and **automatic motivational cues**, which are particularly salient in Kerala's multi-generational household context. --- #### Model Solution for Q A (b) — 7 Marks **Ethical Implications of Default Options in Public Health:** **Argument For (Libertarian Paternalism):** - Sunstein and Thaler argue that defaults are **not neutral** — they inevitably favour one option, so designers should choose the welfare-maximizing default. - Organ donation opt-out systems (Spain, Belgium) achieve >90% registration while preserving freedom to opt out. - Reduces **decision fatigue** and respects bounded rationality. **Argument Against:** - **Autonomy Concern:** Defaults exploit status quo bias, potentially coercing uninformed citizens. - **Manipulation Worry:** Are *nudges* ethically distinct from *manipulation*? Bovens (2009) argues some nudges cross the line. - **Equity Issue:** Defaults that work for the median citizen may harm vulnerable subgroups (e.g., religious minorities, low literacy). - **Transparency Deficit:** Citizens may be unaware of the default, undermining informed consent. **Synthesis:** Ethical default-setting requires **(i) welfare justification, (ii) transparency, (iii) easy opt-out, and (iv) periodic democratic review**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating "nudge" with "mandate." A nudge must preserve freedom of choice. Students who describe mandates lose **2 marks**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Ignoring the **equity dimension** in default design. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for equity analysis. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to cite the **Sunstein–Thaler** libertarian paternalism framework, which is the **canonical** KTU reference for this topic. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q (a)** Using the **β–δ (quasi-hyperbolic) discounting model**, explain why individuals make **time-inconsistent health decisions**. How do **commitment devices** address this? Illustrate with the example of smoking cessation. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** Explain the role of **social norms** in promoting healthy behaviour. Discuss the risks of **norm misperception** with reference to alcohol consumption among college students. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` --- #### Model Solution for Q B (a) — 7 Marks **The β–δ Model (Laibson, 1997):** $$U_t = u(c_t) + \beta \sum_{\tau=1}^{T-t} \delta^{\tau} u(c_{t+\tau})$$ Where $\delta$ is the long-run exponential discount factor and $\beta \in (0,1]$ is the **present bias factor**. **Time-Inconsistency Derivation:** The discount factor between period $t$ and $t+1$ is: $$D_{t, t+1} = \frac{\beta \delta^{1}}{1} = \beta \delta$$ The discount factor between period $t+1$ and $t+2$ is: $$D_{t+1, t+2} = \delta$$ If $\beta < 1$, then $D_{t, t+1} < D_{t+1, t+2}$, implying the individual **discounts the immediate future more heavily than the further future**. This is **dynamic inconsistency** — preferences reverse over time. **Application to Smoking:** A smoker at time $t$ (cold state) plans to quit: - Quitting yields long-term health: $V_{\text{quit}} = 50$ (utility units) - Continuing yields immediate pleasure: $V_{\text{continue}} = 30$ In the cold state, $\beta$ is approximately 1, so: $$U_{\text{quit}} - U_{\text{continue}} = 50\delta - 30 \approx 50(0.95) - 30 = 17.5 > 0 \quad \text{(quits)}$$ In the hot state (craving), $\beta$ drops to ~0.6: $$U_{\text{quit}} - U_{\text{continue}} = 50(0.6)(0.95) - 30 = 28.5 - 30 = -1.5 < 0 \quad \text{(continues)}$$ **Commitment Devices as Solution:** Commitment devices **bind the cold-state preference**, eliminating the hot-state reversal. Examples: - **Deposit contracts:** Smoker deposits ₹10,000; forfeited if smoking detected at 6 months. - **Pre-commitment apps:** Lock-screen blocks cigarette purchase websites. - **Social commitment:** Public declaration of quit date increases follow-through by 40%. > [!TIP] > **Board Tip:** Always pair the **mathematical demonstration** of present bias with a **real-world mechanism** that mitigates it. KTU examiners expect integration of theory and application. --- #### Model Solution for Q B (b) — 7 Marks **Social Norms and Healthy Behaviour:** Social norms influence health behaviour through two channels: 1. **Informational channel:** "If 80% of my peers exercise, perhaps I am missing important information about its benefits." 2. **Preferencial channel:** Utility depends on relative standing: $U_i = \alpha u(x_i) + \gamma u(x_i - \bar{x})$. **Effective Social Norm Interventions:** - Hotel towel reuse programs: "75% of guests in this hotel reuse towels" → 26% increase in reuse. - Energy bill comparisons: "You use 20% more electricity than your neighbours" → 2% reduction in consumption. - Vaccination letters: "92% of children in your area are vaccinated" → significant uptake increase. **Risks of Norm Misperception (Alcohol Example):** **The Bochner (1963) / Perkins (2002) Finding:** College students systematically **overestimate peer alcohol consumption** by 30–50%. This "false social norm" creates a self-reinforcing cycle: $$\text{Perceived Norm} \uparrow \rightarrow \text{Desire to Conform} \uparrow \rightarrow \text{Actual Consumption} \uparrow \rightarrow \text{Perceived Norm} \uparrow$$ **Behavioural Intervention — The Social Norms Marketing Approach:** Provide **accurate** descriptive norms: - "Most KTU students (68%) consume 2 or fewer drinks per week." - This corrects misperception and **reduces** alcohol consumption by 10–20% in randomized trials (Moreira et al., 2009). **Critical Pitfall:** Misuse of social norms can backfire — for example, an accurate norm stating "30% of students binge drink" can inadvertently *normalize* the behaviour. Designers must report **positive majority norms** whenever they exist. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Part B:** > - **Mathematical Rigor:** When β–δ appears in the question, you **must** derive the discount factor ratio. Students who skip the algebra lose 2–3 marks. > - **Counter-arguments:** In the social norms question, presenting only the upside without the **misperception risk** will cap your score at 5/7. > - **Currency & Examples:** Use **Indian or Kerala-specific** examples where possible. Examiners reward contextual application. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Behavioural Interventions for Healthy Behaviours** ### Core Frameworks (Must Memorize) - ☐ **EAST Framework** — Easy, Attractive, Social, Timely (4 components + example each) - ☐ **COM-B Model** — Capability, Opportunity, Motivation → Behaviour - ☐ **Dual Process Theory** — System 1 (fast) vs System 2 (slow) - ☐ **Libertarian Paternalism** — Thaler & Sunstein ### Key Cognitive Biases (Must Know & Apply) - ☐ **Status Quo Bias** → default interventions - ☐ **Present Bias (β–δ)** → commitment devices - ☐ **Loss Aversion (λ ≈ 2.25)** → loss-framed incentives - ☐ **Omission Bias** → screening and vaccination - ☐ **Salience Bias** → cafeteria architecture - ☐ **Optimism Bias** → risk communication ### Numerical Constants (Frequently Tested) - ☐ Default effect: opt-out ≈ 90%, opt-in ≈ 15% - ☐ Loss aversion coefficient: λ ≈ 2.25 - ☐ Probability weighting: γ ≈ 0.61 - ☐ Present bias: β ≈ 0.7–0.9 - ☐ Value function curvature: α ≈ 0.88 ### High-Yield Examples (Keep Ready for ESE) - ☐ Organ donation opt-out (Spain/India comparison) - ☐ Piano staircase for exercise - ☐ Deposit contracts for smoking cessation - ☐ Hotel towel reuse social norm - ☐ Vaccination reminder + social norm combo - ☐ Smaller plates and cafeteria placement for diet - ☐ Hand hygiene UV-marked germs - ☐ Kerala-specific: ASHA workers, mobile pharmacy vans, family-based adherence ### Ethical & Critical-Thinking Hooks - ☐ Equity and vulnerable populations (always mention) - ☐ Nudge vs Mandate distinction - ☐ Transparency and democratic review of defaults - ☐ Misperception risk in social norm campaigns - ☐ Loss-framed incentive equity caveat ### Question-Answering Strategy for KTU - ☐ For 3-mark Part A: Definition + 1 example + 1 application/caveat - ☐ For 7-mark sub-part: Theory + numerical/derivation + Indian application + caveat - ☐ For 14-mark Part B: Always include a **table** for COM-B or EAST and a **Mermaid-style flowchart description** in words - ☐ Cite **Thaler, Sunstein, Kahneman, Laibson, Michie** by name where relevant ### Common Pitfall Map - ☐ Don't confuse *nudges* with *mandates* - ☐ Don't present defaults as neutral - ☐ Don't apply social norms without checking for misperception - ☐ Don't use loss-framed incentives without equity analysis - ☐ Don't use the β–δ model without deriving the discount factor ratio

topics.title: Health and Happiness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Health and Happiness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Health and Happiness** in Behavioural Economics is the interdisciplinary study of how psychological, cognitive, and emotional factors influence individual decision-making related to health behaviours, medical consumption, and subjective well-being. It systematically integrates neoclassical health utility models with empirical findings from behavioural science to explain deviations from rational choice in health domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT866 / Module 4):**
> *Health and Happiness* examines how individuals make decisions about their own well-being, why these decisions often violate the predictions of the standard rational-actor model, and how policy "nudges" (à la Thaler & Sunstein) can be designed to improve health outcomes and life satisfaction without removing choice.

The two sub-domains are:

- **Health Behavioural Economics** — analyses choices about smoking, diet, exercise, medical insurance, vaccination, medication adherence, and preventive screening using concepts like **present bias**, **loss aversion**, **optimism bias**, and **hyperbolic discounting**.
- **Happiness Economics (Economics of Well-Being)** — studies **Gross National Happiness (GNH)**, the **Easterlin Paradox**, **hedonic adaptation**, and **experienced utility** as alternatives to GDP for measuring social progress.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cake Today vs. Health Tomorrow" Analogy**
> Imagine a student who knows that eating a slice of cake every day raises the 10-year risk of diabetes by **15%**, yet still chooses the cake. A standard economist would say this is irrational if utility from health > utility from cake. A behavioural economist says: *"The brain is not a calculator — it is a storyteller."* The student **overweights the immediate pleasure** (present bias), **underweights the probabilistic future pain** (probability neglect), and **believes the risk applies to others more than to themselves** (optimism bias). Health economics corrects the **architectural environment** (default options, plate size, labeling) so the healthy choice becomes the **default, easy, attractive, social, and timely** choice — the **EAST** framework.

In short: **People consistently make systematic, predictable errors when choosing for their future health — and these errors are not random; they follow patterns we can model, measure, and policy-correct.**

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **GNH — Gross National Happiness** (Bhutan, conceptualised by Karma Ura, 1972)
- **QALY — Quality-Adjusted Life Year** (Weinstein & Stason, 1977)
- **DALY — Disability-Adjusted Life Year** (Murray & Lopez, 1994; WHO standard)
- **SWB — Subjective Well-Being** (Kahneman, Diener, Lucas & Schimmack)
- **β (Beta) and δ (Delta) — Hyperbolic discounting parameters** (Laibson, 1997)
- **Easterlin Paradox finding (1974):** Beyond a threshold of ~**$15,000–20,000** (real 1986 USD), higher national income does **not** yield higher average self-reported happiness.

## 1.4 The Bridge Between Health and Happiness

Health is one of the strongest **determinants of subjective well-being** documented in happiness research. Studies (e.g., Graham, 2008; Powdthavee, 2008) consistently find that:

$$ \text{Mental Health} \rightarrow \text{Life Satisfaction} \quad (\text{correlation } r \approx 0.55\text{–}0.70) $$

But the relationship is **bidirectional**: poverty and stress cause illness; illness causes poverty and unhappiness. Behavioural economics provides the *micro-foundations* explaining the **upstream cognitive causes** of both ends of this loop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Health Utility Function — A Behavioural Revision

The **standard Grossman (1972) health capital model** treats health as a durable good that depreciates with age and can be "invested in" via medical care and healthy behaviour. The individual maximises:

$$ U = U(H_t, Z_t) \quad \text{subject to budget and time constraints} $$

Where $H_t$ = health stock at time $t$, $Z_t$ = consumption of other goods. The behavioural revision adds three distortions:

1. **Time-inconsistent preferences** (β–δ model).
2. **Probability misperception** (rank-dependent / Prospect Theory weighting).
3. **Affective forecasting errors** (projection bias, hot-cold empathy gap).

### 2.1.1 The Hot-Cold Empathy Gap (Loewenstein, 1996)

A person in a "cold" (unemotional) state **underestimates** the cravings of their "hot" (emotional/hungry/stressed) state. This explains relapse in addiction, smoking after dinner, and impulsive eating.

$$ U_{\text{cold}}(x) < U_{\text{hot}}(x) \quad \text{for tempting goods } x $$

### 2.1.2 Hyperbolic Discounting (Laibson, 1997)

Standard exponential discounting assumes a **constant** discount rate. Behavioural evidence shows agents discount the near future *much* more steeply than the distant future:

$$ D(t) = \frac{1}{1 + \beta t} \quad \text{(hyperbolic)} $$

versus

$$ D(t) = e^{-\delta t} \quad \text{(exponential)} $$

This generates **present bias** — quitting smoking today, exercising today, or starting medication today is constantly postponed.

## 2.2 Probability Weighting in Health Decisions

Kahneman & Tversky (1979) **Prospect Theory** probability weighting function:

$$ w(p) = \frac{p^\gamma}{(p^\gamma + (1-p)^\gamma)^{1/\gamma}}, \quad \gamma \approx 0.61 $$

Applied to health:

- People **overweight small probabilities** (lottery tickets, rare side effects of vaccines).
- People **underweight large probabilities** (high chance of heart attack from poor diet).
- This explains **simultaneous smoking and over-insurance**.

## 2.3 The Easterlin Paradox (1974)

> [!NOTE]
> **Easterlin's Finding:** Within a country, richer individuals are happier than poorer ones; *across* countries and *over time* within a country, average income growth has not produced sustained increases in average self-reported happiness beyond a moderate income threshold.

Three modern behavioural explanations:

| # | Mechanism | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Hedonic Adaptation** | Individuals return to a baseline level of well-being after positive/negative life changes. |
| 2 | **Social Comparison** (Festinger, 1954) | Utility depends on **relative** income, not absolute income. |
| 3 | **Aspiration Treadmill** | Material aspirations rise in step with income, neutralising the satisfaction gain. |

## 2.4 GNH — Gross National Happiness

Bhutan's **GNH Index** operationalised by the Centre for Bhutan Studies uses **9 domains, 33 indicators, 124 variables**:

1. Psychological Well-being
2. Health
3. Time Use
4. Living Standards
5. Education
6. Cultural Diversity & Resilience
7. Ecological Resilience & Diversity
8. Good Governance
9. Community Vitality

GNH is computed as:

$$ \text{GNH} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} w_i \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{ \text{Indicator } j \text{ is sufficient} \} $$

where sufficiency is measured against an **objective threshold**, not against others.

## 2.5 The EAST Framework for Health Nudges (Behavioural Insights Team, UK)

| Letter | Principle | Health Example |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| **E** | **Easy** | Place fruit at eye-level in cafeteria; default to generic statins. |
| **A** | **Attractive** | Loss-framed SMS: *"You will lose your 7-day streak if you miss today's pill."* |
| **S** | **Social** | Show peers' vaccination rates: *"73% of your neighbours got the flu shot."* |
| **T** | **Timely** | Send appointment reminders at the moment of intended action. |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Cheat Sheet for KTU University Exam — Module 4**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units | KTU Use |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | Exponential Discounting | $D(t) = e^{-\delta t}$ | $\delta$ = per-period discount rate, $t$ = years | Standard rational benchmark |
| 2 | Hyperbolic Discounting | $D(t) = \frac{1}{1+\beta t}$ | $\beta$ = present-bias parameter | Explains procrastination, addiction |
| 3 | Quasi-Hyperbolic (β–δ) | $D(t) = \beta^t \cdot e^{-\delta t}$ | $\beta \in (0,1]$ | Laibson (1997) hybrid model |
| 4 | Prospect Theory Weighting | $w(p) = \frac{p^\gamma}{(p^\gamma+(1-p)^\gamma)^{1/\gamma}}$ | $\gamma \approx 0.61$ | Probability distortions |
| 5 | QALY | $\text{QALY} = \sum_{t} q_t \cdot \Delta t$ | $q_t \in [0,1]$ = quality weight, $\Delta t$ = years | Cost-utility analysis |
| 6 | DALY | $\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$ | YLL = Years of Life Lost; YLD = Years Lived with Disability | WHO burden-of-disease |
| 7 | ICER | $\text{ICER} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta E}$ | $\Delta C$ = incremental cost, $\Delta E$ = incremental QALY | Threshold for funding |
| 8 | Hedonic Adaptation Rate | $A(t) = S \cdot (1 - e^{-\alpha t})$ | $S$ = life-event utility, $\alpha$ = adaptation speed | Explains Easterlin |
| 9 | Willingness-to-Pay for QALY | $\text{WTP} = \frac{\Delta \text{Income}}{\text{Mortality risk reduction}}$ | Standard gamble / time-trade-off elicitation | Health valuation |
| 10 | VSL (Value of a Statistical Life) | $\text{VSL} = \text{WTP} \cdot \text{Inverse risk}$ | Average ~ **$10 million** (US EPA, 2020) | Public health policy |
| 11 | Kahneman's U-Index | $U = \frac{\sum t_i \cdot (u_i < 0)}{T}$ | $T$ = total wakeful time | National happiness index |
| 12 | GNH Sufficiency | $\text{GNH} \ge \theta_k$ | $\theta_k$ = sufficiency threshold for indicator $k$ | Bhutan policy screening |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all subsequent derivations, **absolute value** of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$, never with vertical pipes, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Public Policy

While the topic is economic, its implications are deeply **engineering-relevant** in:

- **Public Health Engineering** — designing hospital defaults and electronic health record nudges.
- **Ergonomics & Human Factors** — engineering control panels, alarms, and feedback to overcome operator *automation bias*.
- **Smart City Design** — defaulting cities to cycle lanes, stair-only access, and visible calorie labels.
- **AI in Healthcare** — designing chatbot-driven adherence tools that exploit the **EAST** principles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Exponential to Hyperbolic Discounting (Laibson's Quasi-Hyperbolic Model)

**Step 1 — Start from rational exponential discounting.**

An agent maximises:

$$ \sum_{t=0}^{T} U(c_t) \cdot e^{-\delta t} $$

Where $U(\cdot)$ is instantaneous utility, $c_t$ consumption, $\delta > 0$ the constant discount rate.

**Step 2 — Introduce present bias parameter $\beta$.**

The behavioural agent applies an *additional* discount on **all future** periods, but **not on the present** ($t = 0$):

$$ D(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t = 0 \\ \beta \cdot e^{-\delta t} & \text{if } t \ge 1 \end{cases} $$

**Step 3 — Combine into the full discounted utility objective.**

$$ V = U(c_0) + \sum_{t=1}^{T} U(c_t) \cdot \beta \cdot e^{-\delta t} $$

**Step 4 — Show why this generates time-inconsistent preferences.**

Suppose at $t = 0$ the agent chooses $c_1^*$. Now shift perspective to $t = 1$:

$$ V_{t=1} = U(c_1) + \sum_{t=2}^{T} U(c_t) \cdot \beta \cdot e^{-\delta (t-1)} $$

The marginal utility of $c_1$ is now $U'(c_1) \cdot \beta$, **not** $U'(c_1)$. The agent no longer endorses the original plan — **time inconsistency**.

**Step 5 — Take the continuous-time limit to obtain pure hyperbolic form.**

If $\beta = e^{-\delta \cdot \tau}$ for some "delay parameter" $\tau$, then:

$$ D(t) = e^{-\delta(t+\tau)} \approx \frac{1}{1+\delta\tau} \cdot e^{-\delta t} \approx \frac{1}{1+\beta t} $$

giving the **hyperbolic discount function** first documented by Mazur (1984) in pigeons and later in humans by Thaler (1981).

**Step 6 — Economic implication for health.**

For a smoking cessation reward paid after $t = 12$ months versus immediate gain from a cigarette, the *ratio* of discounted utilities is:

$$ \frac{D_{\text{hyper}}(12)}{D_{\text{hyper}}(0)} = \frac{1/(1+12\beta)}{1} = \frac{1}{1+12\beta} $$

With $\beta = 0.10$ (empirical estimate for high-stakes health), this equals $\approx 0.45$, meaning the future reward must be **2.2× larger** than the present temptation just to break even — a clear bias.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: QALY Computation in Practice

**Step 1 — Define health-state utility weights.**

Each health state $s$ is assigned a utility $q(s) \in [0,1]$:

- Dead = 0
- Perfect health = 1
- Blindness ≈ 0.65
- Chronic kidney disease on dialysis ≈ 0.55

**Step 2 — Multiply each weight by time spent in that state.**

$$ \text{QALY} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q(s_i) \cdot \Delta t_i $$

**Step 3 — Worked example (KTU-style):**

A patient lives **5 years in perfect health** ($q = 1$), then **10 years with mild arthritis** ($q = 0.8$), then **2 years bedridden** ($q = 0.3$), then dies.

$$ \text{QALY} = (1 \times 5) + (0.8 \times 10) + (0.3 \times 2) + (0 \times 0) $$

$$ = 5 + 8 + 0.6 = 13.6 \text{ QALYs} $$

**Step 4 — Use in cost-utility analysis.**

A new drug costs **₹5,00,000** and adds **1.5 QALYs** versus standard care.

$$ \text{ICER} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta E} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.5} = \text{₹3,33,333 per QALY} $$

If the WTP threshold in India is **₹3,00,000 per QALY** (rough benchmark), the drug is **marginally cost-effective**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Prospect-Theory Probability Weighting & Vaccine Hesitancy

**Step 1 — Define the weighting function.**

$$ w(p) = \frac{p^{\gamma}}{(p^{\gamma} + (1-p)^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma}}, \quad \gamma = 0.61 $$

**Step 2 — Compute for a vaccine with 0.001% chance of side effect.**

$$ w(0.00001) = \frac{(0.00001)^{0.61}}{((0.00001)^{0.61} + (0.99999)^{0.61})^{1/0.61}} $$

$$ (0.00001)^{0.61} \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-4}, \quad (0.99999)^{0.61} \approx 0.99994 $$

$$ w \approx \frac{1.30 \times 10^{-4}}{(1.30 \times 10^{-4} + 0.99994)^{1.64}} \approx \frac{1.30 \times 10^{-4}}{0.99997} \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-4} $$

The **objective** weight is $0.00001$ — already tiny.

**Step 3 — For a 30% chance of contracting the disease without vaccine:**

$$ w(0.30) = \frac{0.30^{0.61}}{(0.30^{0.61} + 0.70^{0.61})^{1/0.61}} \approx \frac{0.475}{(0.475 + 0.797)^{1.64}} \approx \frac{0.475}{1.272^{1.64}} \approx \frac{0.475}{1.471} \approx 0.323 $$

This is **slightly higher** than objective $0.30$.

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The agent **overweights the rare side effect** and **barely overweights the common disease risk**, so the perceived risk-benefit reverses — explaining vaccine hesitancy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Health vs. Happiness Models

| Model | Author (Year) | Domain | Key Behavioural Insight | Equation |
|-------|---------------|--------|--------------------------|----------|
| Health Capital | Grossman (1972) | Health | Health as durable investment good | $\dot H = I_t - \delta H_t$ |
| Hyperbolic Discounting | Laibson (1997) | Health, Addiction | Time-inconsistent health choices | $D(t) = (1+\beta t)^{-1}$ |
| Prospect Theory | Kahneman & Tversky (1979) | Health Risk | Distorted probability weighting | $w(p) = f(p;\gamma)$ |
| EAST Nudge | BIT (2012) | Policy | Four pillars of effective nudge | EAST criteria |
| GNH | Ura, Alkire, Zangmo (2012) | Happiness | 9-domain sufficiency model | $\text{GNH} = \sum w_i d_i$ |
| Easterlin Paradox | Easterlin (1974) | Happiness | Income ≠ happiness above threshold | Empirical (no closed form) |
| Hedonic Adaptation | Brickman & Campbell (1971) | Happiness | Adaptation to life events | $A(t) = S(1-e^{-\alpha t})$ |
| Hot-Cold Empathy Gap | Loewenstein (1996) | Health | State-dependent utility | $U_{\text{cold}} \neq U_{\text{hot}}$ |
| QALY | Weinstein & Stason (1977) | Health | Quality-weighted life years | $\sum q_t \Delta t$ |
| Experienced Utility | Kahneman et al. (1997) | Happiness | Moment vs. remembered utility | $U_{\text{exp}} \neq U_{\text{rem}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Causal Loop of Health ↔ Happiness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    incomeA[Absolute Income] --> compare[Social Comparison]
    adapt[Hedonic Adaptation] --> baseline[SWB Baseline]
    compare --> baseline
    baseline --> happinessA[Reported Happiness]
    incomeA --> healthA[Health Capital Stock]
    healthA --> baseline
    healthA --> cost[Medical Cost Burden]
    cost --> incomeA
    policiesA[Nudge Policies EAST] --> healthA
    policiesA --> baseline
    baseline --> productivity[Productivity]
    productivity --> incomeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the loop:** Health raises baseline happiness, which raises productivity, which raises income, which funds better health. **Nudges** act on *both* health and baseline directly, breaking the vicious sub-loop in low-income settings.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — The EAST Nudge Architecture for Public Health

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Easy]
        A1[Default to generic statins]
        A2[Place fruit at eye level]
        A3[Pre-filled pill boxes]
    end
    subgraph B[Attractive]
        B1[Loss-framed SMS reminders]
        B2[Personalised risk avatars]
        B3[Visual calorie labels]
    end
    subgraph C[Social]
        C1[Peer vaccination-rate SMS]
        C2[Workplace step challenges]
        C3[Family commitment devices]
    end
    subgraph D[Timely]
        D1[Reminders at mealtime]
        D2[Quit-date SMS on day 1]
        D3[Post-discharge check-in at 72 h]
    end
    A --> outcome[Healthier Behaviour]
    B --> outcome
    C --> outcome
    D --> outcome
    outcome --> swb[Happiness Outcome]
    swb --> policy[Policy Feedback Loop]
    policy --> A
    policy --> B
    policy --> C
    policy --> D
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Prospect-Theory Probability Distortion in Health Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    objective[Objective Probability p] --> weightFn[Weighting Function w p gamma]
    weightFn --> perceived[Decision Weight perceived p]
    perceived --> highProb[High p Overweighted 0 to 30 range]
    perceived --> midProb[Mid p Underweighted 30 to 80 range]
    perceived --> lowProb[Low p Overweighted 80 to 100 range]
    highProb --> result1[Patient overuses preventive screening]
    midProb --> result2[Patient ignores moderate cardiovascular risk]
    lowProb --> result3[Patient fears rare vaccine side effects]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Decision Architecture: Rational vs. Behavioural Health Choice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R[Rational Model]
        R1[Know all risks] --> R2[Apply exponential discounting]
        R2 --> R3[Choose Max Expected Utility]
    end
    subgraph Be[Behavioural Reality]
        B1[Optimism Bias] --> B2[Hyperbolic Discounting]
        B2 --> B3[Hot Cold Empathy Gap]
        B3 --> B4[Probability Distortion]
        B4 --> B5[Choice Inconsistent with Max EU]
    end
    R3 --> gap[Behavioural Gap]
    B5 --> gap
    gap --> nudge[Nudge Intervention]
    nudge --> R3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the "Easterlin Paradox" and explain its three main behavioural-economic interpretations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Easterlin Paradox (1974) is the empirical finding that within a country richer individuals are happier than poorer ones, but across countries and over time, sustained income growth does not raise average self-reported happiness beyond a threshold of approximately **$15,000–20,000** real income. The three behavioural explanations are:

1. **Hedonic Adaptation (1 mark):** Individuals return to a baseline level of subjective well-being after positive or negative life changes, neutralising the income-driven utility gain.
2. **Social Comparison (1 mark):** Utility from income is *relative* — it depends on one's rank in the reference group (Festinger, 1954). As everyone becomes richer, no one becomes happier.
3. **Aspiration Treadmill (1 mark):** Material aspirations adapt upwards with rising income, so the gap between actual and aspired consumption does not shrink.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State and briefly explain the EAST framework of behavioural public-policy nudges with one health example for each letter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **EAST** framework (Behavioural Insights Team, UK) is a four-principle design checklist for effective nudges:

- **E — Easy (1 mark):** Reduce the friction of the desired behaviour. *Example:* defaulting all statin prescriptions to generic equivalents.
- **A — Attractive (1 mark):** Make the choice visually salient. *Example:* loss-framed SMS — *"You will lose your 7-day streak if you miss today's pill."*
- **S — Social (1 mark):** Show what peers are doing. *Example:* SMS stating *"73% of your neighbourhood is vaccinated."*
- **T — Timely (1 mark):** Prompt at the moment of intended action. *Example:* appointment reminder pinged at the moment the patient sits down to eat breakfast.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Typical Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Grossman (1972) health capital model** and discuss how **behavioural economics revises** it using the **β–δ quasi-hyperbolic discounting model** of Laibson (1997). Derive the discount function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Easterlin Paradox**. Using a labelled diagram, illustrate the income–happiness relationship and discuss the three behavioural mechanisms that generate the paradox. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

The **Grossman (1972) model** treats health as a **durable capital good** that depreciates over time and can be increased through investment (medical care, exercise, nutrition, sleep). The individual maximises a lifetime utility function (1 mark):

$$ U = \sum_{t=0}^{T} U(H_t, Z_t) \cdot e^{-\delta t} $$

subject to a budget constraint and the **law of motion of health capital** (1 mark):

$$ H_{t+1} = (1 - \delta_H) H_t + I_t $$

where $I_t$ is gross investment, $\delta_H$ the depreciation rate, and $H_t$ the stock of health.

The rational-actor prediction is that individuals invest optimally until the marginal cost of a healthy-day equals the marginal benefit.

**Behavioural revision (2 marks):** Empirical evidence (Thaler 1981, Laibson 1997) shows agents do **not** use a constant exponential rate. They use a **quasi-hyperbolic** discount function:

$$ D(t) = \beta^t \cdot e^{-\delta t} $$

**Derivation of the hyperbolic limit (2 marks):** Setting $\beta = e^{-\delta \tau}$:

$$ D(t) = e^{-\delta(t+\tau)} $$

Re-expressing for small $\delta\tau$:

$$ D(t) \approx \frac{1}{1+\delta\tau} \cdot e^{-\delta t} = \frac{1}{1+\beta t} $$

**Implication (1 mark):** The agent **excessively discounts the near future** (skipping the gym today, eating junk tonight) and **under-discounts the distant future** (planning to "get healthy at 60"). This produces **time-inconsistent health plans** — and explains why commitments devices (gym contracts, nicotine patches, savings accounts) raise welfare.

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Easterlin Paradox (1974) is the finding that **within** a country, happiness rises with income, but **across** countries and **over time** within a country, sustained income growth does not increase average self-reported happiness beyond a moderate threshold (~$15,000–20,000 in 1986 USD).

**Diagram (3 marks) — replicate this in your exam:**

```
Happiness
   ^
   |              /------  Plateau (Easterlin)
   |            /
   |          /   Within-country (richer = happier)
   |        /
   |      /
   |    /
   |  /
   |/__________________>  Real Income (log scale)
        Threshold
```

**Three behavioural mechanisms (3 marks):**

1. **Hedonic Adaptation** — life satisfaction returns to baseline after any life event.
2. **Social Comparison** — utility depends on rank, not absolute income.
3. **Treadmill Effect** — aspirations rise with income, neutralising the satisfaction gain.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **β–δ (quasi-hyperbolic) discounting model** of Laibson. Show mathematically why a present-biased agent exhibits **time-inconsistent** preferences for health investments, and discuss its implications for **addictive behaviour and preventive care**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **QALY** and **DALY**. With a numerical example, compute the **Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ICER)** of a new therapy and interpret the result against a hypothetical WTP threshold. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

The **β–δ model** modifies standard exponential discounting with a **present-bias parameter** $\beta \in (0,1]$:

$$ D(t) = \beta^t \cdot e^{-\delta t} $$

If $\beta = 1$, the model collapses to standard exponential discounting (rational benchmark). If $\beta < 1$, all *future* periods receive an additional discount of factor $\beta$ (1 mark).

**Proof of time-inconsistency (3 marks):** Let the agent at $t=0$ choose plan $\{c_0^*, c_1^*, ..., c_T^*\}$ maximising:

$$ V_0 = U(c_0^*) + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \beta^t e^{-\delta t} U(c_t^*) $$

Now re-evaluate at $t=1$:

$$ V_1 = U(c_1) + \sum_{t=2}^{T} \beta^{t-1} e^{-\delta (t-1)} U(c_t) $$

The marginal utility of $c_1$ at $t=1$ is **$U'(c_1) \cdot \beta$**, whereas at $t=0$ it was treated as **$U'(c_1)$** (no extra discount on the present). Hence the optimal $c_1$ chosen at $t=1$ **differs from** $c_1^*$ — preferences are **time-inconsistent** (1 mark).

**Implications for health (2 marks):**
- **Addiction:** Cigarettes/alcohol yield immediate utility, so the agent over-consumes despite intending to quit "tomorrow". Naïve agents ($\beta$ ≈ 0.5) suffer more than sophisticated ones ($\beta$ ≈ 0.9, fully aware of bias).
- **Preventive care:** Vaccination, screening, exercise are discounted steeply. **Commitment devices** (lockboxes, gym contracts, default enrolment in organ-donation) raise welfare.

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year):** $\text{QALY} = \sum q(s_i) \cdot \Delta t_i$ where $q \in [0,1]$ is the utility weight of health state $s$.
- **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year):** $\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$, where YLL = Years of Life Lost due to premature mortality, YLD = Years Lived with Disability.

**Numerical example (4 marks):**

A patient on **standard care** lives 8 years with average utility weight 0.6 → **QALY = 4.8**.

A patient on **new therapy** lives 10 years with average utility weight 0.7 → **QALY = 7.0**.

Incremental QALY = 7.0 − 4.8 = **2.2 QALYs**.

Incremental cost = **₹6,00,000**.

$$ \text{ICER} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{2.2} = \text{₹2,72,727 per QALY} $$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** If the WTP threshold in India is **₹3,00,000 per QALY** (a common benchmark for lower-middle-income settings), the therapy is **cost-effective**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse DALY and QALY:** QALY = good, want to *maximise*; DALY = bad, want to *minimise*. Many students swap the two — automatic 1-mark penalty per question.
> 2. **Always state the WTP threshold** when computing ICER — the number alone is meaningless.
> 3. **Do not write `|x|`** in ICER tables; use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX — markdown pipes break the row.
> 4. **For Easterlin Paradox questions**, you must cite **all three** mechanisms (adaptation, comparison, treadmill) — 1 mark each. Forgetting one = lose 1 mark.
> 5. **For β–δ questions**, the proof of time-inconsistency is the *core* of the answer — omitting the marginal-utility comparison loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Mnemonic trap:** *GNBH* is the index, *GDP* is the rival — never interchange.
> 7. **Hyperbolic ≠ quasi-hyperbolic:** Pure hyperbolic is $\frac{1}{1+\beta t}$; quasi-hyperbolic (Laibson) is $\beta^t e^{-\delta t}$. Examiners *will* check.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4 / Health and Happiness**

- ✅ **Easterlin Paradox (1974):** Income ↑ within a country → happiness ↑; income ↑ across time → no sustained happiness ↑.
- ✅ **Three mechanisms:** Hedonic adaptation, social comparison, aspiration treadmill.
- ✅ **Hyperbolic Discounting:** $D(t) = (1+\beta t)^{-1}$ — overweights present vs. future.
- ✅ **Quasi-Hyperbolic (Laibson 1997):** $D(t) = \beta^t e^{-\delta t}$ — generates **time inconsistency**.
- ✅ **Prospect Theory Weighting:** $\gamma \approx 0.61$; small $p$ overweighted, mid-$p$ underweighted.
- ✅ **QALY:** $\sum q_i \Delta t_i$ — *maximise*. DALY: YLL + YLD — *minimise*. Do not swap.
- ✅ **ICER:** $\Delta C / \Delta E$ — interpret against a WTP threshold (e.g., ₹3 lakh/QALY in India).
- ✅ **VSL:** ~**$10 million** (US EPA 2020). Used in cost-benefit of safety regulation.
- ✅ **GNH — 9 domains:** Psychological well-being, health, time use, living standards, education, culture, ecology, governance, community vitality. Uses **sufficiency thresholds**, not relative comparisons.
- ✅ **EAST Nudges:** Easy, Attractive, Social, Timely — apply these to design any health nudge.
- ✅ **Hot-Cold Empathy Gap (Loewenstein 1996):** Cold-state utility underestimates hot-state craving — explains addiction relapse.
- ✅ **Optimism Bias (Weinstein 1980):** People believe they are *below average* in health risk — undermines preventive behaviour.
- ✅ **Loss Aversion (Kahneman & Tversky 1979):** Health losses loom larger than equivalent gains — explains vaccine hesitancy (loss of "natural immunity" narrative).
- ✅ **Kahneman's U-Index:** $U = \frac{\sum t_i \cdot \mathbb{1}\{u_i<0\}}{T}$ — fraction of wakeful time spent in negative affect; complements GDP.
- ✅ **Default Effect in Health:** Auto-enrolment in organ-donation, generic-drug defaults, opt-out screening — all raise participation dramatically.
- ✅ **Memory vs. Experience Utility:** Peak-End rule (Kahneman 1993) — *evaluated* health is dominated by peak pain and end pain, not duration. A short painful procedure can be preferred to a long mildly painful one.
- ✅ **Engineering crossover:** Defaults in EHR systems, food-canteen design, smart-city cycling lanes — all leverage behavioural principles.

**End of Module 4 / Health and Happiness Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme / PECBT866**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring the Value of Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measuring the Value of Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Behavioural Economics and Health Economics, **Measuring the Value of Treatment** is the systematic process of quantifying the economic, clinical, and welfare-related worth of a medical intervention, drug, therapy, or public health programme. It integrates **clinical effectiveness** (does it work?), **cost-effectiveness** (is it worth the money?), **utility/disutility** (how does it feel to live with it?), and **behavioural preferences** (what do people actually choose, reveal, and state they want?).

It transcends traditional biomedical "cure" metrics by incorporating four cardinal dimensions:

1. **Quantity of Life** — additional years of life gained.
2. **Quality of Life** — the desirability of those years (utility weights 0 to 1).
3. **Cost** — direct, indirect, and intangible resource consumption.
4. **Behavioural Response** — how patients, doctors, and society actually behave, often deviating from rational-agent predictions (loss aversion, status quo bias, present bias, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 connects classical welfare economics with behavioural insights, emphasizing that *real* treatment value cannot be measured by clinical endpoints alone — patient preferences, framing effects, and choice architecture all matter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Car Wash That Almost Killed Me"**
>
> Imagine two car washes. Wash A cleans your car perfectly for ₹200 in 10 minutes. Wash B cleans it *better* for ₹250 in 12 minutes. **Naively**, B is "more valuable."
> **Behaviourally**, however, you might choose A because:
> - ₹50 saved = ₹50 in your pocket (loss aversion against the ₹250 outlay).
> - 2 extra minutes is a *certainty loss* vs. a *probability* of better shine.
> - You already have a loyalty card for A (status quo bias).
>
> **Measuring the Value of Treatment** works the same way. A treatment may be objectively superior (Wash B) but behaviourally undervalued (people still pick Wash A) because of cognitive biases. The economist's job is to *measure* this gap, correct for biases, and reveal the true welfare gain.

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Full Form | What it Measures | Range / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **QALY** | Quality-Adjusted Life Year | Health value combining length + quality of life | dimensionless, years |
| **DALY** | Disability-Adjusted Life Year | Disease burden (years of healthy life lost) | dimensionless, years |
| **ICER** | Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio | Extra cost per extra unit of effect | currency / QALY |
| **WTP** | Willingness To Pay | Max amount a person would pay to avoid/improve outcome | currency |
| **WTA** | Willingness To Accept | Min compensation to accept a worse outcome | currency |
| **VSL** | Value of a Statistical Life | Societal value of reducing mortality risk | currency |
| **VSLY** | Value of a Statistical Life Year | Per-year mortality risk valuation | currency / year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard **WTA > WTP** in behavioural experiments — this is the **endowment effect** and one of the most tested findings in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Treatment Valuation

#### Pillar 1 — Cost-Effectiveness Analysis (CEA)
CEA compares the *cost per unit of health outcome* between two or more interventions. It answers: **"Are we getting the most health bang for our buck?"**

- **Numerator:** Incremental cost $\Delta C$
- **Denominator:** Incremental effect $\Delta E$ (lives saved, cases prevented, etc.)

#### Pillar 2 — Cost-Utility Analysis (CUA)
CUA is a special form of CEA where the denominator is **QALYs** (or sometimes DALYs averted). It captures *both* length and quality of life in a single metric. CUA is the gold standard recommended by **NICE (UK)**, **ICER (US)**, and **WHO-CHOICE** for health technology assessments.

#### Pillar 3 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)
CBA monetises *all* outcomes (including health) using **WTP** or **VSL**. If the net present value of benefits exceeds costs, the intervention passes the **Kaldor-Hicks efficiency** test. CBA is rarer in healthcare because putting a rupee tag on life is politically and ethically fraught.

### 2.2 The QALY Model — Step Logic

A QALY is constructed by multiplying the **time spent** in a health state by the **utility weight** of that state.

**Step 1:** Define discrete health states (e.g., perfect health, mild migraine, bedridden chronic pain, death).
**Step 2:** Assign utility weights $u_i \in [0, 1]$, where 1 = perfect health, 0 = death.
**Step 3:** Multiply each state's duration (in years) by its weight.
**Step 4:** Sum across the time horizon:

$$\text{Total QALYs} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} u_t \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5:** Compare intervention vs. control to get incremental QALYs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "QALY" and not "Life Year"?**
> Living 1 year in perfect health (u = 1) = **1 QALY**.
> Living 2 years at u = 0.5 = **1 QALY** (same welfare, longer duration, lower quality).
> This compresses the dual dimension of *length* and *quality* into one number — the cornerstone of modern health-economic evaluation.

### 2.3 The DALY Counterpart

**DALYs measure the *burden* of disease**, the mirror image of QALYs:

$$\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$$

where:
- **YLL (Years of Life Lost)** = (Standard life expectancy) − (Age at death)
- **YLD (Years Lived with Disability)** = (Disability weight) × (Duration of condition)
