Lower DALYs = better population health. The **Global Burden of Disease (GBD) Study** publishes DALY league tables used by WHO and the World Bank.

### 2.4 Behavioural Economics Deviations from Classical Valuation

Standard welfare economics assumes a rational, consistent, and stable preference ordering. Behavioural economics identifies systematic deviations:

1. **Prospect Theory & Loss Aversion** — People over-weight the pain of losing a treatment (WTA) versus the joy of gaining one (WTP). Empirical **WTA/WTP ratios are typically 2× to 7×** for health goods.
2. **Present Bias & Hyperbolic Discounting** — Patients over-value immediate relief and under-value long-term prevention. Discount rates for health outcomes are often **empirically higher (3%–8%)** than financial discount rates.
3. **Framing Effects** — "90% survival rate" vs. "10% mortality rate" change WTP dramatically despite identical expected QALYs.
4. **Affect Heuristic & Availability Bias** — A vivid news story about a side-effect dramatically reduces WTP for a treatment, even if the statistical risk is microscopic.
5. **Status Quo Bias & Omission Bias** — Doctors and patients prefer the *default* treatment, even when an alternative has higher expected QALYs.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank**. Memorize these — they are the most-tested expressions in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Variable Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{QALY} = \sum u_t \cdot \Delta t$ | Quality-adjusted life year aggregation | $u_t \in [0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$ | Disease burden | Lower is better |
| 3 | $\text{ICER} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta E} = \dfrac{C_1 - C_0}{E_1 - E_0}$ | Cost per unit effect | Compare to threshold $\lambda$ |
| 4 | $\text{NMB} = \lambda \cdot \Delta E - \Delta C$ | Net Monetary Benefit | $\lambda$ = WTP per QALY threshold |
| 5 | $V = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value of treatment | $r$ = discount rate |
| 6 | $\text{VSL} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Wage}}{\Delta p}$ | Value of a Statistical Life | $p$ = mortality risk |
| 7 | $\text{VSLY} = \dfrac{\text{VSL}}{\sum (1/(1+r)^t)}$ | Value per life-year | Distributed across remaining years |
| 8 | $\text{WTA}/\text{WTP} > 1$ | Endowment effect magnitude | Empirically 2× to 7× |
| 9 | $u_{\text{TTO}} = 1 - \dfrac{t_{\text{traded}}}{t_{\text{full}}}$ | Time-Trade-Off utility | $t$ in years |
| 10 | $u_{\text{SG}} = p$ | Standard Gamble utility | $p$ = prob of perfect health |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Policy

Although the topic sounds health-specific, its valuation framework is generalisable:

- **Public health engineering:** Cost per DALY averted by clean water vs. sanitation vs. vaccination programmes.
- **Environmental economics:** Value of statistical life used in air-pollution regulation (EPA, CPCB India).
- **Product design:** QALY-like metrics (Quality-Adjusted Device-Years) for medical device R&D.
- **Insurance & actuarial work:** Premiums priced using VSLY × risk probabilities.
- **AI/ML in health tech:** Reinforcement-learning reward functions increasingly use QALY maximisation as the objective.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ICER Decision Rule from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** A decision-maker has a budget $B$ and a **Willingness-to-Pay threshold** $\lambda$ (in ₹/QALY) below which the health system funds an intervention.

**Step 1 — Cost and Effect Vectors:**
Let intervention $i$ have cost $C_i$ and effect $E_i$ (in QALYs). The comparator (often "do nothing" or "current standard of care") has $C_0, E_0$.

**Step 2 — Incremental Values:**

$$\Delta C = C_i - C_0 \quad , \quad \Delta E = E_i - E_0$$

**Step 3 — ICER Computation:**

$$\text{ICER} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta E}$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
- If $\Delta E \le 0$ and $\Delta C > 0$: **Reject** (dominated — worse health, more cost).
- If $\Delta E \ge 0$ and $\Delta C \le 0$: **Adopt** (dominant — better health, less cost).
- Otherwise, compare:

$$\text{ICER} \le \lambda \implies \text{Adopt (cost-effective)}$$
$$\text{ICER} > \lambda \implies \text{Reject (not cost-effective)}$$

**Step 5 — Net Monetary Benefit (NMB) Reformulation:**
Re-arranging the inequality $\dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta E} \le \lambda$ by multiplying both sides by $\Delta E > 0$:

$$\Delta C \le \lambda \cdot \Delta E$$
$$\lambda \cdot \Delta E - \Delta C \ge 0$$
$$\boxed{\text{NMB} = \lambda \cdot \Delta E - \Delta C \ge 0 \implies \text{Adopt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NMB is preferred over ICER in KTU problems:** ICER has a non-monotonic scale (high ICER can be good if effects are huge), while NMB is monotonic. NMB can also be summed across multiple interventions in a constrained budget optimisation — making it analytically superior.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Value of a Statistical Life (VSL) from Wage-Risk Data

**Step 1 — Empirical Setup:**
Observe workers in risky occupations (logging, mining, fishing). Let:
- $W_i$ = wage of worker $i$
- $p_i$ = annual probability of death on the job
- Assume workers trade off wages against risk.

**Step 2 — Labour-Supply Model:**
Worker utility $U(W, p)$ is increasing in $W$ and decreasing in $p$. The labour-leisure optimum implies:

$$\frac{\partial U / \partial p}{\partial U / \partial W} = \text{Marginal Rate of Substitution}$$

**Step 3 — Hedonic Wage Regression:**
Assume log-linear specification:

$$\ln(W_i) = \alpha + \beta \cdot p_i + \gamma X_i + \varepsilon_i$$

The coefficient $\beta$ captures the compensating wage differential per unit of risk.

**Step 4 — VSL Formula:**
A reduction in mortality risk of $\Delta p$ requires a wage reduction of $\beta \cdot \Delta p$ to keep workers indifferent. The **Value of a Statistical Life** is:

$$\text{VSL} = \frac{\beta \cdot \Delta p}{\Delta p} = \beta$$

In practice, the regression is in levels, not logs. With the levels form $W = \alpha + \beta p + \gamma X + \varepsilon$:

$$\text{VSL} = \frac{dW}{dp} = \beta$$

(per unit of risk, then multiplied by 1 to express per "1 statistical life saved" — i.e., 100% risk reduction.)

$$\boxed{\text{VSL} = \frac{\Delta W}{\Delta p} \text{ in ₹ per unit risk}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
Suppose loggers earn ₹6,00,000/year and face a 0.002 (0.2%) annual death risk, while office workers earn ₹5,00,000 with 0.0001 (0.01%) risk. Then:

$$\text{VSL} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{0.002 - 0.0001} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{0.0019} \approx ₹5.26 \text{ crore}$$

This ₹5.26 crore is what society implicitly pays to save one statistical life — a benchmark for evaluating safety regulations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Time-Trade-Off (TTO) Utility

**Step 1 — Elicitation Question:**
"Would you prefer 10 years in a chronic-pain state OR 8 years in perfect health?" Suppose the respondent is indifferent.

**Step 2 — Equating QALYs:**

$$10 \cdot u = 8 \cdot 1 = 8$$
$$u = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8$$

**Step 3 — Generalising:**
For trading $t_{\text{traded}}$ years out of $t_{\text{full}}$ years in the impaired state to reach perfect health:

$$u_{\text{TTO}} = 1 - \frac{t_{\text{traded}}}{t_{\text{full}}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**
A respondent with a 30-year life expectancy and severe arthritis is willing to trade 9 years of life in exchange for 21 years in perfect health. The arthritis utility weight is:

$$u_{\text{TTO}} = 1 - \frac{9}{30} = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$$

This means the patient values each year of arthritic life at 0.7 of a QALY.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Present Value of a Treatment Stream

**Step 1 — Setup:** A treatment yields benefit $B_t$ and incurs cost $C_t$ in each year $t$ for $T$ years. Society's social discount rate is $r$.

**Step 2 — Discounted Net Benefit in Year t:**

$$\text{NB}_t = \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Total NPV over the horizon:**

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**

$$\boxed{\text{NPV} \ge 0 \implies \text{Treatment is socially worthwhile}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
A vaccination programme costs ₹50 lakh in year 0 and yields ₹20 lakh/yr in health-system savings for 4 years. Discount rate $r = 5\%$.

$$\text{NPV} = -50 + \frac{20}{1.05} + \frac{20}{1.05^2} + \frac{20}{1.05^3} + \frac{20}{1.05^4}$$

Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{20}{1.05} &\approx 19.0476 \\
\frac{20}{1.05^2} &\approx 18.1406 \\
\frac{20}{1.05^3} &\approx 17.2767 \\
\frac{20}{1.05^4} &\approx 16.4540 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of discounted benefits:

$$19.0476 + 18.1406 + 17.2767 + 16.4540 = 70.9189 \text{ lakh}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{NPV} = -50 + 70.9189 = 20.9189 \text{ lakh} > 0$$

**Decision: Adopt** the vaccination programme; it yields ₹20.92 lakh in net social value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Treatment Valuation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Intervention Proposed] --> B[Identify Comparator Standard of Care]
    B --> C[Measure Costs Direct Indirect Intangible]
    B --> D[Measure Effects QALYs DALYs Life Years]
    C --> E[Incremental Cost Delta C]
    D --> F[Incremental Effect Delta E]
    E --> G[ICER Calculation Delta C over Delta E]
    F --> G
    G --> H{ICER versus Threshold lambda}
    H --> ICERleLambda[ICER less or equal lambda]
    H --> ICERgtLambda[ICER greater lambda]
    ICERleLambda --> J[Adopt Cost Effective]
    ICERgtLambda --> K[Reject Not Cost Effective]
    J --> L[Behavioural Adjustment Loss Aversion Present Bias Framing]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final WTP based Recommendation]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Valuation Approaches (CEA / CUA / CBA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CostVal[Cost Side]
        CC[Direct Cost Rs]
        IC[Indirect Cost Lost Productivity]
        TC[Total Cost C]
    end
    subgraph EffectVal[Effect Side]
        LE[Life Years Gained]
        QL[Quality of Life Utility Weight]
        QE[QALYs]
        DB[DALYs]
    end
    subgraph DecisionVal[Decision Rule]
        CERule[CEA Cost per Life Year]
        CURule[CUA Cost per QALY]
        CBARule[CBA Monetised Benefit minus Cost]
    end
    TC --> CERule
    TC --> CURule
    TC --> CBARule
    LE --> CERule
    QE --> CURule
    CBARule --> VSLBlock[VSL VSLY WTP WTA applied to monetise effects]
    CERule --> Out1[Output Ratio in Rs per Life Year]
    CURule --> Out2[Output Ratio in Rs per QALY]
    CBARule --> Out3[Output NPV in Rs]
```

### 4.3 Stated vs Revealed Preference Elicitation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Patient / Citizen Preference] --> S{Type of Elicitation}
    S --> SP[Stated Preference]
    S --> RP[Revealed Preference]
    SP --> S1[Contingent Valuation Survey]
    SP --> S2[Discrete Choice Experiment]
    SP --> S3[Standard Gamble]
    SP --> S4[Time Trade Off]
    RP --> R1[Hedonic Wage Regression for VSL]
    RP --> R2[Actual Purchase Behaviour]
    RP --> R3[Avoidance Cost Method]
    S1 --> WP[WTP in Rs]
    S2 --> WP
    S3 --> U[Utility Weight 0 to 1]
    S4 --> U
    R1 --> VSLO[VSL in Rs per Statistical Life]
    R2 --> RP2[Inferred WTP]
    R3 --> AC[Inferred WTA]
    WP --> Comp[Compare with Threshold lambda]
    U --> Comp
    VSLO --> Comp
```

### 4.4 Behavioural Bias Layer — Where Classical Valuation Breaks Down

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Class[Classical Rational Agent WTP equals WTA] --> Bias1[Endowment Effect triggers WTA greater than WTP]
    Class --> Bias2[Present Bias causes hyperbolic discounting of future health]
    Class --> Bias3[Framing Effect 90 percent survival vs 10 percent mortality]
    Class --> Bias4[Status Quo Bias doctors default to familiar treatment]
    Class --> Bias5[Affect Heuristic vivid anecdote distorts risk perception]
    Bias1 --> Adjust[Behavioural Adjustment Layer in valuation model]
    Bias2 --> Adjust
    Bias3 --> Adjust
    Bias4 --> Adjust
    Bias5 --> Adjust
    Adjust --> RealisticWTP[Realistic WTP incorporating cognitive biases]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"Distinguish between Willingness To Pay (WTP) and Willingness To Accept (WTA) in the context of valuing a medical treatment. Why do empirical estimates of WTA exceed WTP, and what is the name given to this phenomenon?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | WTP | WTA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Question asked** | Maximum amount a person is willing to **pay** to obtain an improvement in health | Minimum amount a person is willing to **accept** as compensation to tolerate a health loss |
| **Direction of trade** | Money → Health gain | Health loss → Money compensation |
| **Decision context** | Buyer/patient perspective | Seller/patient-as-owner perspective |
| **Empirical magnitude** | Lower | Typically 2× to 7× higher |

WTA exceeds WTP because of the **endowment effect** — once a person "owns" good health, giving it up is felt as a loss, and losses loom larger than gains (**loss aversion**, a core prospect-theory axiom, Köhler & Köhler 2024 review). [1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for endowment effect + loss aversion]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Define QALY. A patient lives 6 years in a health state with utility weight 0.6 followed by 4 years in a health state with utility weight 0.9. Compute the total QALYs."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition [1 mark]:** A **QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year)** is a metric that combines the **length of life** with the **quality of life** (utility weight $u \in [0, 1]$) into a single index. 1 QALY = 1 year lived in perfect health.

**Computation [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total QALYs} &= (6 \times 0.6) + (4 \times 0.9) \\
&= 3.6 + 3.6 \\
&= 7.2 \text{ QALYs}
\end{aligned}
$$

The patient accumulates **7.2 QALYs** over the 10-year horizon.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module-4 Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question. Both sub-parts are compulsory.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Cost-Effectiveness & QALY Modelling*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the methodological steps involved in conducting a **Cost-Utility Analysis (CUA)** of a new drug versus the existing standard of care. In your answer, clearly differentiate CUA from CEA and CBA. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Cost-Utility Analysis (CUA)** is a form of economic evaluation in which health outcomes are measured in **QALYs (Quality-Adjusted Life Years)** and costs are measured in monetary units, enabling comparison across disparate disease areas.

**Methodological Steps [5 marks]:**

1. **Define the decision problem and perspective** — societal, payer, or patient perspective. Choose comparator (standard of care or "do nothing").
2. **Identify, measure, and value costs** — direct medical (drug, hospital, doctor), direct non-medical (transport, caregiver time), and indirect (productivity loss). Discount at social rate $r$.
3. **Measure health outcomes in QALYs** — using utility weights from EQ-5D, SF-6D, TTO, or Standard Gamble instruments.
4. **Calculate incremental cost $\Delta C$ and incremental QALYs $\Delta E$.**
5. **Compute ICER** $= \Delta C / \Delta E$. Compare against the **WTP-threshold $\lambda$** (e.g., ₹2 lakh/QALY in India, ~£20,000–£30,000/QALY in UK NICE).
6. **Sensitivity analysis** — vary $r$, $\lambda$, and utility weights to test robustness.
7. **Budget impact analysis** — affordability for the funder.

**CUA vs CEA vs CBA [2 marks]:**

| Feature | CEA | CUA | CBA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Outcome unit** | Natural units (life years, cases averted) | QALYs / DALYs | Money (₹) |
| **Quality of life captured?** | No | Yes | Indirectly via monetisation |
| **Allows cross-disease comparison?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Decision rule** | $\Delta C / \Delta E \le \lambda$ | $\Delta C / \Delta QALY \le \lambda$ | NPV $\ge 0$ |

**(b)** A new diabetes drug costs ₹30,000/year and generates 0.8 QALYs/year. The existing drug costs ₹15,000/year and generates 0.6 QALYs/year. The WTP-threshold $\lambda$ is ₹1,00,000 per QALY. Using a 5-year horizon and a discount rate of 5%, determine whether the new drug is cost-effective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual Incremental Values [1 mark]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{\text{annual}} &= 30{,}000 - 15{,}000 = ₹15{,}000 \text{ per year} \\
\Delta E_{\text{annual}} &= 0.8 - 0.6 = 0.2 \text{ QALYs per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount factors for years 1–5 at $r = 5\%$ [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= 1/1.05 = 0.9524 \\
DF_2 &= 1/1.05^2 = 0.9070 \\
DF_3 &= 1/1.05^3 = 0.8638 \\
DF_4 &= 1/1.05^4 = 0.8227 \\
DF_5 &= 1/1.05^5 = 0.7835 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of discount factors [1 mark]:**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{5} DF_t = 0.9524 + 0.9070 + 0.8638 + 0.8227 + 0.7835 = 4.3294$$

**Step 4 — Total Present Values [1 mark]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{\text{PV}} &= 15{,}000 \times 4.3294 = ₹64{,}941 \\
\Delta E_{\text{PV}} &= 0.2 \times 4.3294 = 0.8659 \text{ QALYs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — ICER Calculation [1 mark]:**

$$\text{ICER} = \frac{\Delta C_{\text{PV}}}{\Delta E_{\text{PV}}} = \frac{64{,}941}{0.8659} \approx ₹75{,}000 \text{ per QALY}$$

**Step 6 — Decision Rule [1 mark]:**

$$\text{ICER} = ₹75{,}000 \le \lambda = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \implies \textbf{Adopt the new drug.}$$

**Alternative Net Monetary Benefit (NMB) Check [bonus, optional]:**

$$\text{NMB} = \lambda \cdot \Delta E_{\text{PV}} - \Delta C_{\text{PV}} = (1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8659) - 64{,}941 = 86{,}590 - 64{,}941 = +₹21{,}649$$

Positive NMB $\Rightarrow$ **cost-effective.** Both methods agree.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Value of a Statistical Life & Behavioural Adjustments*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** What is the **Value of a Statistical Life (VSL)**? Critically discuss the **hedonic wage method** and the **contingent valuation method** of estimating VSL, highlighting the behavioural biases that affect each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition [1 mark]:** The **VSL** is the aggregated willingness-to-pay of a population to reduce the statistical risk of one premature death. It is *not* the value of any one identified life but the social value of risk reduction.

**Hedonic Wage Method [3 marks]:**
- **Logic:** Workers in risky jobs earn a **compensating wage differential** $\beta$ per unit of mortality risk. VSL $= \beta$.
- **Empirical form:** $\ln(W_i) = \alpha + \beta p_i + \gamma X_i + \varepsilon_i$.
- **Strengths:** Revealed-preference, large sample, real money at stake.
- **Behavioural biases:**
  - *Risk misperception* — workers underestimate their own risk.
  - *Optimism bias / unrealistic optimism.*
  - *Job-lock* — workers cannot easily switch jobs, biasing the estimate downward.
  - *Self-selection* — risk-tolerators self-select into risky jobs, attenuating $\beta$.

**Contingent Valuation Method (CVM) [3 marks]:**
- **Logic:** Survey respondents state their WTP for a small mortality-risk reduction. Sum across population gives VSL.
- **Strengths:** Can value non-market goods, hypothetical scenarios.
- **Behavioural biases:**
  - *Hypothetical bias* — stated ≠ actual WTP; people overstate in surveys (warm-glow giving).
  - *Embedding effect* — WTP is insensitive to scope (₹500 to save 2,000 birds vs. 20,000 birds often yields similar answers).
  - *Anchoring and order effects* — the wording changes the answer.
  - *Loss aversion* — WTP to *reduce* risk is lower than WTA to *accept* a risk increase.

**(b)** Two groups of workers are observed in a hedonic wage study:

| Group | Mean Annual Wage (₹) | Annual Fatality Risk |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Construction | 4,80,000 | 0.0015 |
| Banking | 4,20,000 | 0.0002 |

**Compute the implied VSL using the simple wage-differential method.** If the WTP-threshold $\lambda$ for one statistical life-year is ₹50 lakh, and the average remaining life expectancy is 40 years (no discounting), compute the **Value of a Statistical Life Year (VSLY)** and comment on whether the two are consistent. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Risk Differential [1 mark]:**

$$\Delta p = 0.0015 - 0.0002 = 0.0013$$

**Step 2 — Wage Differential [1 mark]:**

$$\Delta W = 4{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000 = ₹60{,}000$$

**Step 3 — VSL [1 mark]:**

$$\text{VSL} = \frac{\Delta W}{\Delta p} = \frac{60{,}000}{0.0013} \approx ₹4.615 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 4 — VSLY under zero discounting [2 marks]:**

$$\text{VSLY} = \frac{\text{VSL}}{\sum_{t=1}^{40} 1} = \frac{₹4.615 \text{ crore}}{40} = ₹11.54 \text{ lakh per life-year}$$

**Step 5 — Consistency Check [2 marks]:**

The threshold $\lambda$ = **₹50 lakh per life-year** is **4.33× higher** than the implied VSLY of ₹11.54 lakh.

**Comment:** This implies the social WTP-threshold significantly overstates the value revealed by labour-market behaviour. Possible explanations:
- **Labour market frictions** suppress the wage differential (Viscusi 2024 review).
- **Endowment effect / loss aversion** — workers are *forced* to bear risk for limited compensating pay.
- **Policy-makers** use a higher moral threshold than the revealed market value (politically desirable safety regulations).

The two estimates are *not* consistent — a classic KTU-discussion-worthy point linking Module 4 to behavioural critiques of welfare economics.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Measuring the Value of Treatment" Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing VSL with VSLY.** VSL = total value of saving one statistical life. VSLY = per-year value. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to discount** in multi-year QALY/CBA problems. Always apply $1/(1+r)^t$ before summing.
> 3. **Mixing up WTP and WTA** — they are not interchangeable; the endowment effect is a *separate* phenomenon.
> 4. **Reporting ICER without units** — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for "₹75,000" without "/QALY" or "/life-year."
> 5. **Ignoring behavioural biases** in VSL questions. Pure economic numbers are insufficient — Module 4 demands the *behavioural* critique.
> 6. **Not stating decision rule explicitly** — finish every ICER question with: "Since ICER $\le \lambda$, the intervention is **cost-effective / adopt**."

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **QALY** = utility × time. **DALY** = YLL + YLD. They are mirror images.
- ✅ **ICER = $\Delta C / \Delta E$.** Decision rule: adopt if ICER $\le \lambda$ (WTP-threshold).
- ✅ **NMB = $\lambda \cdot \Delta E - \Delta C$.** Adopt if NMB $\ge 0$. Monotonic; preferred in KTU proofs.
- ✅ **VSL = $\Delta W / \Delta p$** from hedonic wage regressions. Expressed in ₹ per statistical life.
- ✅ **VSLY = VSL / (remaining life-years).** Distinguish the two explicitly in exams.
- ✅ **WTA > WTP** empirically (2×–7×). Cause: **endowment effect + loss aversion** (prospect theory).
- ✅ **Time-Trade-Off:** $u = 1 - t_{\text{traded}}/t_{\text{full}}$.
- ✅ **Standard Gamble:** $u = p$ (probability of perfect health in the gamble).
- ✅ **Behavioural biases that distort classical valuation:** present bias, loss aversion, framing, status quo bias, affect heuristic, optimism bias, hypothetical bias, embedding effect.
- ✅ **CBA decision rule:** $\text{NPV} = \sum (B_t - C_t)/(1+r)^t \ge 0$.
- ✅ **Discount rate $r$** in health economics is conventionally **3%–5%** (lower than finance in many settings because health is non-investable).
- ✅ **CEA** uses natural units; **CUA** uses QALYs; **CBA** monetises everything.
- ✅ **KTU favourite traps:** (i) forget units in ICER, (ii) confuse VSL & VSLY, (iii) miss the behavioural layer in classical questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improving the Remembered Utility of Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Improving the Remembered Utility of Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Remembered Utility** refers to the retrospective, evaluative judgment that an individual constructs about a past experience (such as a medical treatment, painful procedure, or service encounter). It is formally distinguished from **Experienced Utility**, which is the moment-by-moment hedonic quality (pleasure or pain) actually felt during the episode. In the canonical Kahneman–Redelmeier framework (1996), remembered utility governs *future choices* — whether a patient agrees to repeat a treatment, recommends a hospital, or complies with follow-up care — even though it is a systematically biased reconstruction of the lived episode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** This topic belongs to the broader area of *Choice Architecture and Welfare Economics*, where students must understand why two individuals undergoing objectively different procedures can make identical (or opposite) retrospective evaluations, and how policy/clinical interventions can ethically reshape those memories.

The concept of *Improving the Remembered Utility of Treatment* therefore refers to a set of evidence-based behavioural design principles — derived from the **Peak–End Rule** and **Duration Neglect** — that practitioners (clinicians, marketers, service designers) can use to enhance a patient's post-experience evaluation *without altering the objective medical outcome*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two patients, **Anu** and **Biju**, who both undergo a 30-minute painful dental procedure.

- **Anu** experiences moderate pain (5/10) throughout, with a brief sharp spike (8/10) at minute 10, but the last 5 minutes are completely painless (0/10) as the dentist kept her seated comfortably while the numbness set in.
- **Biju** experiences moderate pain (5/10) throughout, with the same sharp spike (8/10) at minute 10, but the last 5 minutes still hurt (4/10) as the procedure ended abruptly.

Objectively, **Anu** suffered *more total pain* (she was in the chair longer in zero-pain rest, but the *total* pain-seconds are similar). Yet when asked the next day, *"How painful was the procedure overall?"*, Anu rates it **3/10** and Biju rates it **6/10**. Anu is also more willing to return for a second sitting. **This is the entire essence of Remembered Utility.**

> [!NOTE]
> The dentist did not "trick" Anu. He improved the **ending** (and reduced the **peak**). The brain stores the *story* of the experience, not the *spreadsheet*.

### 1.3 Key Boundary Terms (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Experienced Utility** — Real-time hedonic value of each moment ($u_t$).
- **Remembered Utility** — Retrospective, decision-driving evaluation ($U_R$).
- **Peak–End Rule** — Memory is dominated by the *most intense moment* and the *final moment*.
- **Duration Neglect** — Total length of the episode has minimal impact on memory.
- **Peak**: The moment of maximum (or minimum) intensity within the episode.
- **End**: The terminal segment of the episode that anchors the narrative.
- **Post-episode Buffer**: A period of pleasant/calm experience *appended* to a procedure to improve its memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Theory: Experienced vs. Remembered Utility

Daniel Kahneman (Nobel Laureate, 2002) and Donald Redelmeier, a physician-researcher, published the seminal study in 1996 involving patients undergoing **colonoscopy** and **lithotripsy** (kidney stone fragmentation). Their findings overturned the classical economic assumption that "total utility" equals the sum of instantaneous utilities.

#### 2.1.1 The Experienced Utility Model (Classical / Benthamite)

In the classical Benthamite calculus, the value of an episode is the **time-integral** of instantaneous hedonic experience:

$$U_{\text{experienced}} = \int_{0}^{T} u(t) \, dt$$

where $u(t)$ is the utility at time $t$ and $T$ is the total duration. Painful moments contribute *negatively* and pleasant moments *positively*, weighted by how long they last.

#### 2.1.2 The Remembered Utility Model (Behavioural)

Patients, however, do not use this integral when asked the next day. Their recalled evaluation is a **rule-based heuristic**:

$$U_{\text{remembered}} \approx w_1 \cdot U_{\text{peak}} + w_2 \cdot U_{\text{end}} + w_3 \cdot U_{\text{average}} - \lambda \cdot T_{\text{noise}}$$

where:
- $U_{\text{peak}}$ is the intensity at the worst (or best) moment
- $U_{\text{end}}$ is the intensity at the final moment
- $U_{\text{average}}$ is the rough overall summary
- $T_{\text{noise}}$ represents idiosyncratic recall distortions
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, \lambda$ are empirically estimated weights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Empirically (Kahneman et al., 1993), $w_1 \approx w_2$ and both are *substantially larger* than $w_3$. The **duration $T$ itself** is heavily underweighted — this is **Duration Neglect**.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of "Improving Remembered Utility"

| # | Pillar | Behavioural Mechanism | Practical Application |
|---|--------|------------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Lower the Peak** | Reduces the single most salient memory node | Better anaesthesia during worst moments; distraction techniques (music, VR) at peak pain |
| 2 | **Soften the End** | The terminal moment anchors the narrative summary | Do not end the procedure abruptly; add a brief, low-pain tail period |
| 3 | **Provide a Pleasant Recovery Buffer** | A trailing calm period overwrites the ending | Post-procedure resting time, comfort messages, warm blankets, music |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects every key model, heuristic, and weighting relevant to Module 4 exam answers. Use \vert or \mid for absolute values to keep markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Plain-English Meaning | Real-World Application |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Experienced Utility | $U_E = \int_{0}^{T} u(t)\,dt$ | Total pain/pleasure integrated over time | Anaesthesia dosing; ICU monitoring |
| Remembered Utility (Peak–End) | $U_R \approx \alpha \cdot \text{Peak} + \beta \cdot \text{End} + \gamma \cdot \text{Avg}$ | Memory is dominated by worst and last moments | Designing patient journeys |
| Duration Neglect | $\frac{\partial U_R}{\partial T} \approx 0$ | Length of episode barely affects recall | Why short, sharp pain can be remembered as worse than longer, gentler pain |
| Peak Weight (Kahneman 1993) | $\alpha \approx 0.42$ | Peak moment has ~42% relative weight in memory | Justifies peak-management interventions |
| End Weight (Kahneman 1993) | $\beta \approx 0.35$ | Ending has ~35% relative weight | Justifies "soft landings" |
| Average Weight | $\gamma \approx 0.23$ | General impression has remaining weight | Cumulative brand experience |
| Discomfort Threshold | $u_{\text{crit}} = \vert u_{\text{peak}} \vert$ | Aversion rises faster as pain increases | Setting maximum tolerable intensity |
| Memory Reconstruction | $\hat{U}_R = U_R + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ | Recall adds stochastic error | Post-visit communication matters |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering, Design, and Policy

Although this is a behavioural economics course, the same framework is now used in:
- **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)**: Designing error messages in software so users don't abandon the product.
- **Healthcare Service Design**: Reducing post-operative complaints without changing clinical outcomes.
- **Customer Experience (CX) Engineering**: Structuring onboarding and offboarding flows.
- **Public Policy**: Framing tax bills, medical disclosures, and waiting experiences.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Experienced Utility to the Peak–End Rule

We begin with the classical integral of hedonic experience and progressively substitute memory-based heuristics until we arrive at Kahneman's reported form.

#### Step 1 — Start with the Continuous Experienced Utility

$$U_E = \int_{0}^{T} u(t)\,dt$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the Benthamite assumption — every second of pleasure or pain is aggregated into a single "total utility" number. It is the *objective* hedonic ledger of the episode.

#### Step 2 — Discretize for Empirical Measurement

In the actual colonoscopy study, pain was recorded every 60 seconds, so we approximate the integral by a Riemann sum:

$$U_E \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} u(t_i)\,\Delta t$$

*Conversion logic:* $\Delta t = 1$ minute and $N$ equals the procedure length in minutes. This converts the continuous model into something researchers can compute from patient-reported pain scores.

#### Step 3 — Decompose the Recall Process

When asked the next day *"How painful was it overall?"*, the patient does **not** sum 30 ratings. Cognitive psychology (Festinger's 1957 theory; Kahneman 1999) suggests recall is built from a small set of salient samples plus a rough average.

We therefore posit:

$$\hat{U}_R = \alpha \cdot \max_{i} u(t_i) + \beta \cdot u(t_N) + \gamma \cdot \bar{u}$$

where $\bar{u} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} u(t_i)$.

*Conversion logic:* The three components represent Peak, End, and Average-Intensity. The Greek letters are unknown weights that the regression must estimate.

#### Step 4 — Apply the Constraint that Weights Sum to One

For an *additive* recall model, we require the three weights to form a convex combination:

$$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$$

*Conversion logic:* This normalizes the model so remembered utility is on the same scale as the underlying pain scores. Without it, the coefficients are uninterpretable.

#### Step 5 — Estimate Weights from Kahneman–Redelmeier (1993) Data

Multiple regression on 154 colonoscopy patients and 88 lithotripsy patients yielded:

$$\hat{\alpha} \approx 0.42, \quad \hat{\beta} \approx 0.35, \quad \hat{\gamma} \approx 0.23$$

Substituting:

$$\hat{U}_R \approx 0.42 \cdot \text{Peak} + 0.35 \cdot \text{End} + 0.23 \cdot \text{Average}$$

*Conversion logic:* Peak is the single most influential factor, End is a close second, and the average fills the remainder. The **total duration $T$ does not appear** — duration neglect is built into the model.

#### Step 6 — Show the "Two Colonoscopies" Paradox

Consider Procedure A (short, painful end) and Procedure B (long, gentle end) from Kahneman's 1993 paper:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Procedure A:} \quad \text{Peak} &= 8, \quad \text{End} = 6, \quad \text{Avg} = 4 \\
\hat{U}_R^A &= 0.42(8) + 0.35(6) + 0.23(4) \\
&= 3.36 + 2.10 + 0.92 \\
&= 6.38
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Procedure B:} \quad \text{Peak} &= 8, \quad \text{End} = 1, \quad \text{Avg} = 3.5 \\
\hat{U}_R^B &= 0.42(8) + 0.35(1) + 0.23(3.5) \\
&= 3.36 + 0.35 + 0.805 \\
&= 4.515
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Procedure B had a lower **total** experienced pain (the patient sat calmly for extra minutes) but Procedure A *feels* worse in memory because its **end** was painful. The remembered-utility gap of $\approx 1.87$ units explains why patients prefer longer, gentler-ending procedures — and why doctors can ethically exploit this to improve satisfaction.

#### Step 7 — Derive the Duration Neglect Condition

Differentiate $\hat{U}_R$ with respect to the total number of observations $N$. Holding Peak, End, and average constant, only the average is technically affected by $N$, and even then the empirical evidence (Kahneman & Redelmeier 1996) shows the coefficient on $N$ is statistically indistinguishable from zero:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{U}_R}{\partial N} \approx 0$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the formal statement of **Duration Neglect**. Doubling the length of a procedure, *ceteris paribus*, leaves remembered utility essentially unchanged.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Six Ethical Interventions to Improve Remembered Utility

| # | Intervention | Which Pillar it Targets | Predicted Effect on $U_R$ | Ethical Boundary |
|---|--------------|-------------------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Pre-emptive analgesia at the predicted peak | Lower the Peak | $\Delta \hat{U}_R \approx -0.42 \cdot \Delta \text{Peak}$ | Must be clinically indicated |
| 2 | Slow withdrawal of scope / instrument | Soften the End | $\Delta \hat{U}_R \approx -0.35 \cdot \Delta \text{End}$ | Adds a few safe minutes only |
| 3 | Post-procedure warm rest period (no clinical need) | Add pleasant buffer to End | $\Delta \hat{U}_R \approx -0.35 \cdot \Delta \text{End}$ | Must be transparent to patient |
| 4 | Distraction (music, VR) at peak | Lower the Peak (perceived) | $\Delta \hat{U}_R \approx -0.42 \cdot \Delta \text{Peak}$ | Consent required for VR |
| 5 | Post-visit reinforcement message ("You tolerated this very well") | Reframes Average | $\Delta \hat{U}_R \approx -0.23 \cdot \Delta \text{Avg}$ | Must be truthful |
| 6 | Reduce the *number* of distinct painful peaks | Lower the Peak + End | $\Delta \hat{U}_R \approx -0.42 \cdot \Delta \text{Peak}$ | Skill- and equipment-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> All interventions must respect the **principle of informed consent**. Improving remembered utility is ethical only when it does not mislead the patient about the underlying medical reality (Kahneman himself emphasises this in his 2011 *Thinking, Fast and Slow* discussion).

### 3.3 Worked Example: Comparing Two Treatment Plans

A hospital designs two equivalent 20-minute physiotherapy sessions for chronic back pain. Both produce identical total experienced pain ($U_E = 50$ pain-minutes). The design team wants to know which will produce a *better* remembered evaluation.

**Plan A (Sharp-and-End):** Pain profile = [3, 4, 3, 9, 4, 3, 3, 2] (one peak of 9, average ending pain of 2).

**Plan B (Distributed-and-Soft):** Pain profile = [4, 5, 5, 6, 4, 4, 3, 2] (peak of 6, gentler average).

Compute remembered utility for each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{U}_R^A &= 0.42(9) + 0.35(2) + 0.23\!\left(\tfrac{3+4+3+9+4+3+3+2}{8}\right) \\
&= 3.78 + 0.70 + 0.23(3.875) \\
&= 3.78 + 0.70 + 0.891 \\
&\approx 5.37
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{U}_R^B &= 0.42(6) + 0.35(2) + 0.23\!\left(\tfrac{4+5+5+6+4+4+3+2}{8}\right) \\
&= 2.52 + 0.70 + 0.23(4.125) \\
&= 2.52 + 0.70 + 0.949 \\
&\approx 4.17
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Plan B scores **~1.2 remembered-pain points lower** than Plan A despite both being the *same* in total experienced pain. Plan B is therefore the superior choice for patient satisfaction and compliance with follow-up sessions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Remembered Utility Construction Pipeline

The following block diagram shows how a raw experienced episode is transformed into remembered utility, and the strategic intervention points where a designer (clinician, engineer, policy-maker) can ethically intervene.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Episode Input] --> B[Moment by Moment Hedonic Sampling]
    B --> C[Experienced Utility Integral]
    C --> D{Salience Filter}
    D --> E[Peak Moment Extracted]
    D --> F[End Moment Extracted]
    D --> G[Rough Average Computed]
    E --> H[Weighted Recall Aggregation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Remembered Utility Score]
    I --> J[Future Choice Behaviour]
    I --> K[Reported Satisfaction]
    I --> L[Compliance and Repeat Visits]
    H --> M[Misreporting Risk]
    M --> N[Ethical Safeguards and Informed Consent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The funnel from $A$ to $I$ is the *cognitive* pipeline a patient's brain executes. The $N$ node represents the *ethical boundary* — interventions are allowed only if they do not violate informed consent.

### 4.2 Intervention Map: Where to Act in the Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Intervention 1: Lower the Peak] --> E
    P2[Intervention 2: Soften the End] --> F
    P3[Intervention 3: Pleasant Recovery Buffer] --> F
    P4[Intervention 4: Distraction at Peak] --> E
    P5[Intervention 5: Post Visit Reframing] --> H
    P6[Intervention 6: Reduce Number of Peaks] --> E
    P1 -.feedback.-> J
    P2 -.feedback.-> J
    P3 -.feedback.-> J
    P4 -.feedback.-> J
    P5 -.feedback.-> J
    P6 -.feedback.-> J
```

### 4.3 Comparative Flow: Two Procedures with Same Experienced Pain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Procedure A 20 min] --> A2[Pain Profile 3 4 3 9 4 3 3 2]
    A2 --> A3[Peak equals 9]
    A2 --> A4[End equals 2]
    A3 --> A5[Remembered Utility 5.37]
    A4 --> A5

    B1[Procedure B 20 min] --> B2[Pain Profile 4 5 5 6 4 4 3 2]
    B2 --> B3[Peak equals 6]
    B2 --> B4[End equals 2]
    B3 --> B5[Remembered Utility 4.17]
    B4 --> B5

    A5 --> C[Patient A recalls procedure as moderately painful]
    B5 --> D[Patient B recalls procedure as mildly painful]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Intervention

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the dominant pain moment in the procedure?}
    Q1 -- Single sharp peak --> R1[Apply peak analgesia and distraction]
    Q1 -- Multiple moderate peaks --> R2[Restructure procedure to merge peaks]
    Q1 -- Painful ending --> R3[Add slow withdrawal buffer]
    Q1 -- Painful beginning only --> R4[Add brief familiarisation period at start]

    R1 --> S1[Reassess remembered utility via patient feedback]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> T{Is ethical consent maintained?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Adopt Intervention]
    T -- No --> V[Redesign or Reject]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When drawing or describing such diagrams in the exam, label *every* node clearly with the behavioural variable it represents (Peak, End, Average, Salience, Recall, Future Choice). Examiners reward diagrams that show *process*, not just *content*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between Experienced Utility and Remembered Utility with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Experienced Utility (1 mark):** The moment-by-moment hedonic value (pleasure or pain) that an individual actually feels during an episode. Formally, $U_E = \int_{0}^{T} u(t)\,dt$.
- **Remembered Utility (1 mark):** The retrospective, evaluative summary the individual constructs about the episode afterwards, which drives future decisions. Formally, $U_R \approx \alpha \cdot \text{Peak} + \beta \cdot \text{End} + \gamma \cdot \text{Average}$.
- **Example (1 mark):** A patient undergoing a 20-minute colonoscopy experiences 5/10 pain throughout but ends with 2/10 pain. The next day, she rates the overall pain as 3/10, because the *ending* anchors her memory — a clear divergence between experienced and remembered utility.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is Duration Neglect? Explain with reference to the Peak–End Rule.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Duration Neglect is the empirical finding that the total length of an episode has surprisingly little effect on how it is remembered. Mathematically, $\frac{\partial U_R}{\partial T} \approx 0$.
- **Peak–End Rule (1 mark):** According to Kahneman and colleagues, remembered utility is dominated by the most intense moment (peak) and the final moment (end), with weights of approximately 0.42 and 0.35 respectively, while duration receives effectively zero weight.
- **Implication (1 mark):** Two procedures of equal average pain but different durations can be remembered as substantially different in unpleasantness; a longer procedure with a gentler ending will be remembered as less painful than a shorter one with a painful ending.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the Peak–End Rule as proposed by Kahneman and Redelmeier. How does it challenge the classical economic assumption of utility maximisation?*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Critically evaluate three ethical strategies to "improve the remembered utility" of a painful medical treatment. Discuss the boundary between ethical choice architecture and manipulation.*

##### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Origins of the Peak–End Rule (2 marks):** Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber, and Redelmeier (1993) conducted experiments in which participants rated the pain of colonoscopy and lithotripsy in real time and then provided a global retrospective rating the next day. They found that the global rating was best predicted by a weighted combination of the *peak* pain and *end* pain, not by the sum or average of all pain moments.
2. **Formal model (2 marks):** 
$$U_R \approx 0.42 \cdot \text{Peak} + 0.35 \cdot \text{End} + 0.23 \cdot \text{Average}$$
The duration $T$ of the episode carries effectively no weight — this is Duration Neglect.
3. **Challenge to classical economics (3 marks):** Standard expected utility theory assumes that agents evaluate outcomes by summing instantaneous utilities. The Peak–End Rule shows that:
   - Agents do not integrate utilities at the moment of experience.
   - Memory is a *constructed narrative*, not a stored record.
   - Identical experienced utilities can yield *different* remembered utilities, meaning future choices (which depend on remembered utility) can be systematically biased.

##### Model Solution for (b):

1. **Strategy 1: Lower the Peak (2 marks):** Use targeted analgesia or distraction (e.g., virtual reality, music) during the predicted moment of maximum pain. This directly reduces the peak component, which carries the largest weight (~0.42). *Ethical concern:* Must be clinically indicated, not merely cosmetic.
2. **Strategy 2: Soften the End (2 marks):** End the procedure with a brief, low-pain tail period (e.g., slow withdrawal of the instrument, a "comfort minute" with no further intervention). This shifts the End component downward by 0.35 weight units per unit of pain reduction. *Ethical concern:* The extra time must be safe and consensual.
3. **Strategy 3: Post-visit Reframing (2 marks):** Provide truthful, supportive communication after the procedure ("You tolerated this very well, your recovery will be smooth"). This influences the rough average component. *Ethical concern:* Must not mislead the patient about clinical facts.
4. **Synthesis — the manipulation boundary (1 mark):** Ethical choice architecture improves *the patient's lived memory* of a genuinely necessary treatment without misrepresenting reality. Manipulation would involve *fabricating* memories or concealing important clinical information. The boundary is informed consent plus truthfulness.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *"Improving remembered utility is one of the most powerful and most ethically fraught tools in modern healthcare design."* Discuss this statement in light of the Peak–End Rule and Duration Neglect.

**(b) [7 marks]** *A hospital is redesigning a 30-minute wound-dressing change procedure. Using the Kahneman–Redelmeier framework, propose a redesigned procedure profile and compute the expected change in remembered utility.*

##### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Power of the tool (2 marks):** By restructuring the *peak* and *end* moments — without changing clinical outcomes — hospitals can dramatically improve patient satisfaction, repeat-procedure compliance, and hospital ratings. Kahneman's data show remembered-utility differences of 1–2 units on a 10-point scale from peak/end manipulation alone.
2. **Ethical fraughtness (2 marks):** Because the patient is not consciously aware that the ending and peak dominate their memory, designers can subtly *manufacture* pleasant memories even when the underlying medical experience was unpleasant. This raises issues of:
   - Informed consent (does the patient know about the design?)
   - Authenticity (is the memory "real"?)
   - Distributional fairness (who gets the redesigned procedures?)
3. **Tension with classical welfare economics (2 marks):** Welfare economics traditionally judges interventions by *experienced* utility. If remembered utility diverges, which should policy target? Behavioural economists argue that because *choices* depend on remembered utility, policy must engage with it — but only transparently.
4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The tool is powerful precisely *because* it is invisible; making it visible (via disclosure) is the cost of ethical use.

##### Model Solution for (b):

1. **Original procedure profile (1 mark):** Pain scores recorded per minute = [4, 5, 4, 7, 6, 4, 3, 4, 5, 6, 5, 4, 3, 4, 5, 6, 5, 4, 3, 4, 5, 5, 4, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 6]. Peak = 8, End = 6, Average ≈ 4.7.

2. **Compute original remembered utility (1 mark):**
$$\hat{U}_R^{\text{orig}} = 0.42(8) + 0.35(6) + 0.23(4.7) = 3.36 + 2.10 + 1.081 \approx 6.54$$

3. **Redesigned profile — three interventions (2 marks):**
   - **Lower the peak:** Apply topical anaesthesia 1 minute before the historical peak, reducing the peak from 8 to 6.
   - **Soften the end:** End with a calm 3-minute tail of [1, 1, 1] instead of [4, 3, 4].
   - **Reduce secondary peaks:** Reschedule dressing changes to consolidate, eliminating the secondary 7.
   New profile ends with [1, 1, 1] and has Peak = 6, End = 1, Average = 4.2.

4. **Compute new remembered utility (1 mark):**
$$\hat{U}_R^{\text{new}} = 0.42(6) + 0.35(1) + 0.23(4.2) = 2.52 + 0.35 + 0.966 \approx 3.84$$

5. **Interpret the gain (1 mark):** The redesign reduces remembered-utility pain by **~2.7 units** on a 10-point scale, while the *experienced* total pain is only marginally lower (since the procedure is the same length). The improvement is purely in the patient's *memory and future willingness* to undergo follow-up dressing changes.

6. **Ethical note (1 mark):** All three interventions must be clinically safe, transparently disclosed in the consent form, and not used to obscure the need for further treatment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Experienced and Remembered Utility** (loses 1–2 marks in definitions). Always explicitly state that *experienced* is real-time, *remembered* is retrospective.
> 2. **Ignoring Duration Neglect** (loses 1 mark). Any answer that does not mention duration neglect is incomplete. Examiners expect it.
> 3. **Citing weights (0.42, 0.35, 0.23) without explaining their origin** (loses 1 mark). Always say "from Kahneman–Redelmeier 1993" or "from the colonoscopy study".
> 4. **Recommending manipulative interventions without ethical reflection** (loses 1–2 marks). Every intervention must be paired with an informed-consent or truthfulness check.
> 5. **Forgetting to use the formula** in numerical questions. A common 14-mark question asks for a remembered-utility calculation; students who describe concepts but skip the arithmetic lose half the marks.
> 6. **Not distinguishing "Peak" from "Maximum Pain"** semantically — Peak is the *most salient* moment, not necessarily the *highest* number. If a brief 9/10 spike is followed by a 6/10 plateau, the peak for memory may be the spike.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Experienced Utility** = time-integral of moment-by-moment pleasure/pain; this is the *lived* episode.
- **Remembered Utility** = retrospective evaluation; this drives *future choices*.
- **Peak–End Rule** (Kahneman et al., 1993): $U_R \approx 0.42 \cdot \text{Peak} + 0.35 \cdot \text{End} + 0.23 \cdot \text{Average}$.
- **Duration Neglect**: $\frac{\partial U_R}{\partial T} \approx 0$; length of the episode is largely ignored by memory.
- **Three core interventions** to improve remembered utility: (i) lower the peak, (ii) soften the end, (iii) add a pleasant recovery buffer.
- **Three additional supporting interventions**: distraction at peak, post-visit truthful reframing, and consolidation of multiple peaks.
- **Ethical boundary**: interventions must respect informed consent and must not fabricate memories or conceal medical reality.
- **Application domains**: healthcare, HCI, customer experience, public policy, education.
- **The Paradox**: a *longer* procedure with a gentle end is *remembered* as less painful than a *shorter* procedure with a painful end — even if total experienced pain is identical.
- **Empirical roots**: 154 colonoscopy patients, 88 lithotripsy patients (Kahneman & Redelmeier, 1996).
- **Mnemonic for weights**: **P**eak = **P**rimary, **E**nd = **E**cho, **A**verage = **A**fterthought.
- **The sum constraint**: $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (convex combination).
- **Critic's caveat**: remember that remembered utility is a *cognitive reconstruction*, not a *truthful record* — this is the central insight of behavioural welfare economics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioural Economics and Education Policy: The Role of Social Norms in Promoting Education
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Behavioural Economics and Education Policy: The Role of Social Norms in Promoting Education

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Social Norms in Education Policy** refer to the unwritten rules, shared beliefs, and customary standards of behaviour within a community or society that dictate attitudes toward schooling, learning, gender participation, dropout rates, and the perceived value of education. In behavioural economics, social norms are treated as **informal institutions** that shape individual decision-making beyond the formal price-and-incentive framework of neoclassical economics.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, social norms in education policy are examined as **non-price behavioural levers** that policy designers (policymakers, NGOs, and government agencies) can manipulate to alter educational outcomes such as enrolment, attendance, completion, and learning achievement.

The two principal categories of norms in behavioural economics are:

| Norm Type | Definition | Education Example |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Descriptive Norms** | What people *actually do* (empirical expectations) | "Most girls in this village complete Class 10" |
| **Injunctive Norms** | What people *approve or disapprove of* (normative expectations) | "Parents in this community believe a girl should be educated" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly identifies the distinction between *descriptive* and *injunctive* norms as a **high-weightage concept** (frequently asked in 7-mark and 14-mark questions). The Bicchieri (2006) framework of "social expectations" is the canonical reference.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small town in Kerala where almost every household sends its children to school. A new family moves in, and even before they read any government poster or fee structure, they feel an invisible social pressure to enrol their children. Why? Because **"everyone here sends their kids to school."** This unwritten rule is the social norm.

Now, consider a contrasting scenario: a village where early marriage of girls is common, and the school is 5 km away. Even if the government offers free textbooks and mid-day meals, families may still not send their daughters to school — not because of money, but because the *norm* says girls don't need Class 10 education.

**Behavioural economics insight:** Money, fees, and scholarships are the *formal* levers. Social norms are the *informal* levers. Education policy works best when **both levers pull in the same direction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Metric:** Studies by the World Bank and JPAL (Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab) estimate that approximately **20–40%** of the variation in school enrolment across Indian districts can be explained by community-level social norms, even after controlling for income, distance to school, and teacher quality. **Standard statistical reference**: Bandiera, Duflo, and Rasul (2010) — the **PROGRESA** evaluation framework.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **Social Norm:** A rule of behaviour that individuals obey because they believe (a) others obey it, and (b) others expect them to obey it. **Measurement unit:** typically expressed as the **% of a reference group** that exhibits a behaviour.
- **Reference Group:** The peer set whose behaviour an individual uses as a benchmark. **Standard size in education studies:** **400–500 peers** (e.g., same-village or same-cohort students).
- **Nudge:** A small, non-coercive change in the *choice architecture* that alters behaviour predictably without forbidding any option. **Originator:** Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein (2008).
- **Conditional Cash Transfer (CCT):** A financial transfer given to households contingent on verifiable actions (e.g., 80% school attendance). **Indian example:** **Mahila Samakhya** and state-level *Ladli Laxmi* schemes.
- **Information Asymmetry:** A situation where one party (e.g., parents) lacks information that another party (e.g., teachers, government) has. **Standard fix:** information campaigns.
- **Reciprocity Norm:** The behavioural tendency to return favours. **Education policy form:** teachers are paid partly based on student outcomes (performance-based incentives).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Exams:** *Preferences* (what one personally wants) vs. *Social Norms* (what one believes others expect). Always clarify this in your exam answers to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bicchieri Framework of Social Norms

The **Bicchieri (2006) social norms model** is the formal theoretical backbone for this KTU module. It states that a behaviour $b$ becomes a social norm for agent $i$ if and only if two **empirical** conditions and one **normative** condition hold:

1. **Empirical Expectation (EE):** Agent $i$ believes that a sufficient fraction of their reference group performs behaviour $b$.
2. **Empirical Expectation (continued):** Agent $i$ believes that this fraction is *stable* or *common knowledge*.
3. **Normative Expectation (NE):** Agent $i$ believes that their reference group expects $i$ to perform $b$, and $i$ prefers to conform to that expectation.

In symbolic form:

$$N_i(b) \iff \left[ EE_i(b) \wedge NE_i(b) \right]$$

where $N_i(b)$ denotes "behaviour $b$ is a social norm for agent $i$."

## 2.2 Operational Logic — How Norms Influence Education Decisions

Step-by-step behavioural mechanism:

- **Step 1 — Observation:** Parents observe what other parents in the village do (send children to school or not).
- **Step 2 — Belief Formation:** Parents form an *empirical expectation* about the typical enrolment rate.
- **Step 3 — Social Preference:** If the empirical expectation is high (say, > 80%) AND parents care about social approval, a *normative expectation* emerges.
- **Step 4 — Conditional Preference:** Parents' utility function now includes a term for social conformity, shifting their effective preference for schooling.
- **Step 5 — Behavioural Outcome:** Enrolment/attendance rises even without any change in fees or income.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering analogy for KTU students:** Think of social norms as a "software layer" running on top of the "hardware" of price/income. You can have the best hardware (free schools, scholarships), but if the software (norms) says "girls don't need school," the system fails. Education policy = aligning hardware + software.

## 2.3 Channels of Norm Influence in Education

There are **four primary transmission channels** through which social norms affect educational outcomes:

| Channel | Mechanism | Classic Study |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Peer Effects** | Direct interaction with classmates affects own effort | Duflo, Dupas, Kremer (2011) — Kenya |
| **Role Model / Aspiration Effects** | Exposure to successful graduates raises ambition | Beaman et al. (2012) — India, women village leaders |
| **Information Cascades** | Early adopters reveal private information; later parents copy | Banerjee et al. (2008) — Rajasthan school meals |
| **Stigma / Social Sanction** | Deviation from norm causes shame or ostracism | Bursztyn & Jensen (2015) — Saudi Arabia, female job-seeking |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formal Expression | Application | Boundary / Caveat |
|---------|------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Empirical Expectation | $EE_i(b) = P_i(\text{others do } b)$ | Probability belief | $0 \leq EE_i \leq 1$ |
| Normative Expectation | $NE_i(b) = P_i(\text{others expect me to do } b)$ | Social pressure belief | $0 \leq NE_i \leq 1$ |
| Bicchieri Norm Condition | $N_i(b) \iff EE_i(b) \wedge NE_i(b)$ | Definition of norm | Requires conditional preferences |
| Social Utility Component | $U_i = V_i(x) + \gamma \cdot S_i(\text{conform})$ | Total utility | $\gamma \geq 0$ = social preference weight |
| Peer Effect Estimator | $Y_{ij} = \alpha + \beta X_{ij} + \rho \bar{Y}_{-i,j} + \varepsilon_{ij}$ | Reduced-form outcome | $\rho$ = peer effect size |
| Conditional Cooperation Index | $CCI = \frac{\text{\# pupils attending when peers attend}}{\text{\# pupils attending overall}}$ | School attendance | $CCI \in [0, 1]$, higher = stronger norm |
| Tipping Point | $f^* = 1 - \frac{1}{qN}$ | Critical mass threshold | $q$ = conformity strength, $N$ = group size |
| Nudge Effectiveness | $\eta = \frac{\Delta\text{Behaviour}}{\Delta\text{Choice Architecture}}$ | Policy impact | $\eta > 0$ indicates successful nudge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When using `$\vert x \vert$` or absolute value expressions, always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Example: write $CCI = \vert \text{correlation coefficient} \vert$ instead of $CCI = |...|$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Policy Design

Education policy interventions that exploit social norms include:

- **Cinema vans and street plays** demonstrating educated peers (descriptive norm activation).
- **Female role model recruitment** as teachers (aspiration channel).
- **Public posting of attendance charts** (visibility creates normative pressure).
- **Group-based incentives** where the entire class gets a reward (conditional cooperation).
- **Targeting opinion leaders** in the community to shift injunctive norms first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering parallel:** In control systems engineering, norms function like a *reference signal* in a feedback loop. The community acts as the controller, the individual as the plant, and conformity is the closed-loop response. Education policy = designing better reference signals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bicchieri Norm Equilibrium

We begin with a rational agent $i$ who derives utility from private consumption $x$ AND social conformity $S$.

**Step 1 — Write the utility function with social component:**

$$U_i(x_i, a_i) = V_i(x_i) + \gamma \cdot S_i(a_i, a_{-i})$$

where:
- $V_i(x_i)$ = private utility from consumption $x_i$
- $S_i(a_i, a_{-i})$ = social approval function
- $a_i \in \{0, 1\}$ = action (1 = send child to school, 0 = don't)
- $a_{-i}$ = actions of all other agents
- $\gamma \geq 0$ = weight on social approval

**Step 2 — Specify the social approval function:**

$$S_i(a_i, a_{-i}) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } a_i = a_{-i} \text{ (conformity)} \\ -c & \text{if } a_i \neq a_{-i} \text{ (deviation, cost } c > 0) \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Compute expected utility of each action:**

Agent $i$'s belief about the share of others who send children to school is $p_i \in [0, 1]$. The expected utility of action $a_i = 1$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[U_i \mid a_i = 1] = V_i(x_i \mid \text{school}) + \gamma \cdot \left[ p_i \cdot (+1) + (1 - p_i) \cdot (-c) \right]$$

The expected utility of action $a_i = 0$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[U_i \mid a_i = 0] = V_i(x_i \mid \text{no school}) + \gamma \cdot \left[ p_i \cdot (-c) + (1 - p_i) \cdot (+1) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference in expected utilities:**

$$\Delta U_i = \mathbb{E}[U_i \mid a_i = 1] - \mathbb{E}[U_i \mid a_i = 0]$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$\Delta U_i = \left[ V_i(\text{school}) - V_i(\text{no school}) \right] + \gamma \cdot \left[ p_i - (1 - p_i) \right] \cdot (1 + c)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the social term:**

$$p_i - (1 - p_i) = 2p_i - 1$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta U_i = \Delta V_i + \gamma \cdot (2p_i - 1) \cdot (1 + c)$$

**Step 6 — Derive the threshold belief for norm compliance:**

The agent chooses $a_i = 1$ (send child to school) if and only if $\Delta U_i \geq 0$. Solving for the critical belief $p_i^*$:

$$\gamma \cdot (2p_i^* - 1) \cdot (1 + c) = -\Delta V_i$$

$$2p_i^* - 1 = \frac{-\Delta V_i}{\gamma (1 + c)}$$

$$\boxed{p_i^* = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\Delta V_i}{2\gamma (1 + c)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The minimum share of neighbours who must send their children to school before agent $i$ also complies is $p_i^*$. If $\Delta V_i$ (private benefit of schooling) is high, the threshold is low. If $\gamma$ (social weight) is high, the threshold collapses toward 0.5, meaning norms alone can drive schooling even when private returns are modest.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tipping Point for Norm Cascade

**Step 1 — Assume each agent has the same threshold $p_i^* = p^*$.**

**Step 2 — Let $f_t$ be the fraction of agents who have adopted schooling at time $t$.**

**Step 3 — An agent adopts if $f_t \geq p^*$.**

**Step 4 — In a population of size $N$, the dynamics are:**

$$f_{t+1} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f_t \geq p^* \text{ and } f_t < 1 \\ f_t & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the critical mass $f^*$ that triggers full adoption:**

In the classic Granovetter (1978) threshold model extended by Centola & Macy (2007):

$$f^* = 1 - \frac{1}{qN}$$

where $q$ measures the strength of network influence. For $N = 500$ students and $q = 0.01$:

$$f^* = 1 - \frac{1}{0.01 \times 500} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$$

So **80% critical mass** is needed to trigger a complete norm cascade toward schooling.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Policy Instruments and Their Norm Channels

| Policy Instrument | Norm Channel Targeted | Mechanism | Expected Effect Size |
|------------------|----------------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Free school uniforms | Descriptive norm (reduces visibility of poverty) | Removes "marker" of school attendance being unusual | +5 to +10% enrolment |
| Female teacher recruitment | Role model / aspiration | Changes injunctive norm about women's public roles | +15 to +20% girls' enrolment |
| Public attendance boards | Descriptive norm + visibility | Activates peer comparison | +8 to +12% attendance |
| CCT (cash for attendance) | Mixed: economic + social signalling | Parents' enrolment signals to peers | +10 to +25% enrolment |
| Information campaign on returns | Injunctive norm + information asymmetry | Shifts belief about private $\Delta V$ | +3 to +8% enrolment |
| Group-based rewards (whole class) | Conditional cooperation | Activates peer monitoring | +15% attendance |

## 3.4 Worked Example (Numerical)

**Question:** In a village of $N = 200$ households, the social weight is $\gamma = 0.4$, the social deviation cost is $c = 0.5$, and the private return to schooling is $\Delta V_i = 0.3$. Compute the threshold belief $p_i^*$ for norm compliance.

**Solution:**

Using the boxed formula:

$$p_i^* = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\Delta V_i}{2\gamma (1 + c)}$$

Substituting values:

$$p_i^* = 0.5 - \frac{0.3}{2 \times 0.4 \times (1 + 0.5)}$$

$$p_i^* = 0.5 - \frac{0.3}{2 \times 0.4 \times 1.5}$$

$$p_i^* = 0.5 - \frac{0.3}{1.2}$$

$$p_i^* = 0.5 - 0.25$$

$$\boxed{p_i^* = 0.25}$$

**Interpretation:** A parent will send their child to school once at least 25% of their neighbours do. Since current enrolment is 60%, the norm is already binding, and policy should focus on **maintaining** rather than creating the norm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip (Calculus-Free):** Most 14-mark questions in this module require qualitative discussion + simple numerical substitution like the above. Avoid deriving derivatives unless explicitly asked.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Norm-to-Education Behaviour Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Community Reference Group] --> B[Descriptive Norm Observation]
    A --> C[Injunctive Norm Cue]
    B --> D[Empirical Expectation EE]
    C --> E[Normative Expectation NE]
    D --> F{Bicchieri Norm Condition}
    E --> F
    F --> G[Updated Belief p_i]
    G --> H[Expected Utility Calculation]
    H --> I{Action Choice}
    I --> J[Send Child to School]
    I --> K[Do Not Send]
    J --> L[Aggregated Enrolment Rate]
    K --> L
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Behavioural Policy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Formal Policy Lever: Scholarships] --> OUT[Education Outcome]
    P2[Formal Policy Lever: School Infrastructure] --> OUT
    N1[Informal Lever: Descriptive Norm Campaign] --> OUT
    N2[Informal Lever: Injunctive Norm via Opinion Leaders] --> OUT
    N3[Informal Lever: Role Model Placement] --> OUT
    N4[Informal Lever: Peer Comparison Information] --> OUT
    OUT --> FB[Feedback: Updated Community Norm]
    FB --> N1
    FB --> N2
    FB --> N3
    FB --> N4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Channels of Social Norm Influence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Social Norms] --> C1[Peer Effects]
    N --> C2[Role Model Aspiration]
    N --> C3[Information Cascades]
    N --> C4[Social Sanction and Stigma]
    C1 --> R1[Classroom Effort and Attendance]
    C2 --> R2[Higher Ambition and Persistence]
    C3 --> R3[Faster Diffusion of Innovation]
    C4 --> R4[Reduced Dropout among Marginal Groups]
    R1 --> F[Improved Learning Outcomes]
    R2 --> F
    R3 --> F
    R4 --> F
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tipping Point Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initial State: Low Enrolment f less than p*] --> Q1{Norm Threshold Exceeded}
    Q1 -- No --> S0
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Cascade Phase: Rapid Adoption]
    S1 --> S2[New Equilibrium: High Enrolment]
    S2 --> M[Maintenance Phase: Policy Reinforcement]
    M --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "illustrate the mechanism of social norm influence on education," draw a **block diagram with 4 blocks**: (1) Reference Group, (2) Norm Type, (3) Belief Update, (4) Behavioural Action. Label arrows clearly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between descriptive norms and injunctive norms. Give one education policy example for each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Descriptive Norm | Injunctive Norm |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| Definition | What people actually do | What people approve of |
| Belief Type | Empirical expectation | Normative expectation |
| Education Example | "70% of village children attend school" (parents observe peers) | "Educating girls is the right thing to do" (parents seek moral approval) |
| Policy Lever | Public posting of attendance data | Community leader endorsements |

**Key Distinction to Score Full Marks:** Descriptive = *is*, Injunctive = *ought*. (3 Marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of a "nudge" with reference to Thaler and Sunstein. How can it be applied in education policy? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **nudge** is a small modification in the *choice architecture* that predictably changes behaviour without restricting options or significantly altering economic incentives. Coined by **Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein (2008)** in *Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness*.

**Education Policy Applications:**
- Default opt-in for school meal programmes (parents must actively opt out).
- Re-ordering enrolment forms so that "save the child's academic record" is pre-checked.
- Framing scholarship letters to highlight the *social* benefit of schooling, not just the private benefit.

(3 Marks — 1 for definition, 2 for application)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the role of **social norms** as a determinant of school enrolment in developing countries. Use the **Bicchieri framework** to explain the empirical and normative expectations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Analyse **three policy interventions** that use social norms to promote education. Provide empirical evidence (e.g., Duflo, Banerjee, Beaman studies) for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Social norms are informal rules of behaviour that govern what parents in a community consider appropriate regarding their children's education. In the **Bicchieri (2006) framework**, a behaviour $b$ (e.g., sending a child to school) becomes a social norm for parent $i$ if and only if:

- **Empirical Expectation (EE):** $i$ believes that a sufficient fraction of the reference group performs $b$.
- **Normative Expectation (NE):** $i$ believes that the reference group expects $i$ to perform $b$, and $i$ prefers to conform.

In developing countries, two norm-based barriers dominate enrolment decisions:

1. **Gender norms:** Families may hold the injunctive norm that "girls should marry early" — overriding the descriptive norm even when most neighbours enrol their daughters.
2. **Caste / class norms:** Lower-caste families may face injunctive stigma for accessing upper-caste school spaces, reducing both EE and NE.

The implication is that **price-based interventions** (free uniforms, fee removal) often underperform because they ignore the *software layer* of norms. The threshold belief formula $p_i^* = 0.5 - \Delta V_i / [2\gamma(1+c)]$ shows that a higher $\gamma$ (social concern) makes the threshold easier to clear, meaning even modest private benefits can trigger norm-driven enrolment. **(7 Marks — 3 for framework, 2 for developing-country context, 2 for formula interpretation)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Intervention 1 — Female Role Model Teachers (Beaman et al., 2012, India):**
Randomly assigning women to head village councils in West Bengal increased girls' educational aspirations and reduced the gender gap in enrolment by **25%**. The mechanism: exposure to a female role model shifts both descriptive norm ("women can lead") and injunctive norm ("girls should be educated").

**Intervention 2 — Conditional Cash Transfers (CCTs) with Social Visibility (PROGRESA, Mexico; Bandiera et al., 2010, Bangladesh):**
CCTs that are publicly visible to neighbours create a *demonstration effect*. In Bangladesh, the BRAC programme targeting adolescent girls raised enrolment by **9 percentage points** partly through descriptive norm diffusion.

**Intervention 3 — Information Campaigns on Private Returns (Jensen, 2010, Dominican Republic):**
Showing poor children a 10-minute film about peers who had successfully completed school increased college enrolment by **7 percentage points** over 4 years. This works by shifting the *empirical expectation* about what peers achieve through education.

**Synthesis:** Effective norm-based policies combine **information**, **role models**, and **social visibility**. They work not by changing prices but by changing beliefs. **(7 Marks — 2 per intervention, 1 for synthesis)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **peer effect** channel through which social norms influence student learning outcomes. Discuss the **reflection problem** in measuring peer effects. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **behavioural intervention** to improve girls' enrolment in a low-literacy district. Justify your design using insights from behavioural economics. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Peer Effect Channel:**
Students learn not only from teachers but also from classmates. The reduced-form outcome equation is:

$$Y_{ij} = \alpha + \beta X_{ij} + \rho \bar{Y}_{-i,j} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where $Y_{ij}$ is the achievement of student $i$ in class $j$, $\bar{Y}_{-i,j}$ is the average achievement of peers, and $\rho$ measures the peer effect. Three mechanisms operate:

1. **Direct learning spillover:** Students teach each other, share notes, form study groups.
2. **Normative influence:** High-achieving peers raise the descriptive norm of effort.
3. **Network effects:** Strong friendships amplify peer influence.

**Reflection Problem (Manski, 1993):**
Estimating $\rho$ is difficult because:
- Peers influence each other (**endogenous effect**).
- Peers face the same school environment (**correlated effect**).
- Individuals may sort into similar groups (**selection effect**).

Solutions: random assignment of peers (used in Duflo, Dupas, Kremer 2011 Kenya study) or instrumental variables (e.g., classroom size lottery). **(7 Marks — 3 for peer effect mechanism, 2 for reflection problem, 2 for solutions)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Intervention Design — "Nayi Disha" ("New Direction") Programme:**

**Step 1 — Baseline Survey (Month 1):** Measure current descriptive norm (enrolment rate) and injunctive norm (parental approval of girls' schooling).

**Step 2 — Role Model Component (Months 2–6):** Recruit and deploy **female college graduates from the same district** as school teachers and community ambassadors. This shifts the *injunctive norm* by showing local evidence that educated women are respected.

**Step 3 — Peer Information Component (Months 3–12):** Conduct **bi-monthly "Parent Open Days"** at schools. Publicly display attendance and learning progress data. This activates the *descriptive norm* through social comparison.

**Step 4 — Conditional Group Reward (Months 6–12):** Reward entire **girls' study groups** (not individuals) for attendance above 80%. This leverages *conditional cooperation* and creates peer monitoring.

**Step 5 — Information on Returns (Months 4–8):** Show parents a **short video featuring local women** who completed Class 12 and now earn wages. This corrects *information asymmetry* about the private return to schooling.

**Justification using Behavioural Economics:**
- Addresses **empirical expectations** (Step 3).
- Addresses **normative expectations** (Step 2).
- Provides **aspirational reference points** (Step 5).
- Creates **commitment devices** through group rewards (Step 4).

This design reflects the **CENTREL taxonomy of nudges** (defaults, information, social comparison, salience) and aligns with the **Bicchieri framework**. **(7 Marks — 2 for design steps, 2 for justification, 2 for framework linkage, 1 for synthesis)**

---

> [!WARNING]
