We derive the optimal choice predicted by a **projection-biased agent** and contrast it with the rational benchmark.

**Step 1 — Setup**

Let there be two states: present state $s_0$ and future state $s_1$. The agent decides at $t=0$ on a continuous choice $c \in \mathbb{R}_{+}$, to be consumed at $t=1$.

Utility in each state:

$$u(c, s_0) = \alpha_0 - \beta_0 (c - c_0^{\ast})^2$$
$$u(c, s_1) = \alpha_1 - \beta_1 (c - c_1^{\ast})^2$$

Here, $c_0^{\ast}$ and $c_1^{\ast}$ are the **state-specific optimal consumptions**, and $\beta_0, \beta_1 > 0$ measure state-specific sensitivity.

**Step 2 — Rational benchmark**

The rational agent picks $c$ to maximize $u(c, s_1)$:

$$c^{\text{rational}} = c_1^{\ast}$$

Setting the first derivative to zero:

$$\dfrac{\partial u(c, s_1)}{\partial c} = -2\beta_1(c - c_1^{\ast}) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad c = c_1^{\ast}$$

**Step 3 — Projection-biased agent**

The biased agent imagines the future through the lens of $s_0$, so the perceived utility is:

$$u^{\text{perceived}}(c) = \alpha_0 - \beta_0 (c - c_0^{\ast})^2$$

Maximizing with respect to $c$:

$$\dfrac{\partial u^{\text{perceived}}(c)}{\partial c} = -2\beta_0 (c - c_0^{\ast}) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad c^{\text{biased}} = c_0^{\ast}$$

**Step 4 — Error magnitude**

The deviation from the rational optimum is:

$$e = c^{\text{biased}} - c^{\text{rational}} = c_0^{\ast} - c_1^{\ast}$$

The actual utility loss at $t=1$ under the biased choice is:

$$W = u(c_1^{\ast}, s_1) - u(c_0^{\ast}, s_1) = -\beta_1 (c_0^{\ast} - c_1^{\ast})^2$$

(using $\alpha_1$ cancels out). Since $\beta_1 > 0$ and $(c_0^{\ast} - c_1^{\ast})^2 \ge 0$, we have $W \le 0$, with strict loss when $c_0^{\ast} \neq c_1^{\ast}$.

**Step 5 — Extension to the blended-state model**

With the blended state $s^{\text{proj}} = (1-\mu) s_1 + \mu s_0$, the perceived utility becomes (in a linear-in-state approximation):

$$u^{\text{perceived}}(c) = (1-\mu) u(c, s_1) + \mu u(c, s_0)$$

The first-order condition is:

$$0 = (1-\mu)\dfrac{\partial u(c, s_1)}{\partial c} + \mu \dfrac{\partial u(c, s_0)}{\partial c}$$

$$0 = -2(1-\mu)\beta_1 (c - c_1^{\ast}) - 2\mu \beta_0 (c - c_0^{\ast})$$

Solving for $c$:

$$(1-\mu)\beta_1 (c - c_1^{\ast}) + \mu \beta_0 (c - c_0^{\ast}) = 0$$

$$c \big[(1-\mu)\beta_1 + \mu \beta_0\big] = (1-\mu)\beta_1 c_1^{\ast} + \mu \beta_0 c_0^{\ast}$$

$$c^{\text{biased}} = \dfrac{(1-\mu)\beta_1 c_1^{\ast} + \mu \beta_0 c_0^{\ast}}{(1-\mu)\beta_1 + \mu \beta_0}$$

This is a **weighted average** of the two state-specific optima, with weights proportional to the state-importance × sensitivity. As $\mu \to 0$, $c^{\text{biased}} \to c_1^{\ast}$ (rational). As $\mu \to 1$, $c^{\text{biased}} \to c_0^{\ast}$ (full projection).

### 3.2 Welfare Loss in the Blended Model

Substituting $c^{\text{biased}}$ into $u(c, s_1)$ and comparing to $u(c_1^{\ast}, s_1)$:

$$W(\mu) = -\beta_1 \left( \dfrac{\mu \beta_0 c_0^{\ast} - \mu \beta_0 c_1^{\ast}}{(1-\mu)\beta_1 + \mu \beta_0} \right)^2 = -\dfrac{\mu^2 \beta_0^2 \beta_1 (c_0^{\ast} - c_1^{\ast})^2}{\big[(1-\mu)\beta_1 + \mu \beta_0\big]^2}$$

This is a smooth, increasing function of $\mu$ (for fixed $\mu < 1$). It is **zero** when $\mu = 0$ or when $c_0^{\ast} = c_1^{\ast}$, confirming that projection bias produces strictly positive welfare loss only when states differ in their optima **and** the agent projects.

### 3.3 Hot-Cold Empathy Gap — Numerical Illustration

Consider a "cold" state with utility:

$$u_{\text{cold}}(c) = 10 - 2c^2$$

Optimum: $c^{\ast}_{\text{cold}} = 0$, $u_{\text{cold}}(0) = 10$.

In a "hot" state (e.g., hunger for cookies, with $c$ = cookies consumed):

$$u_{\text{hot}}(c) = 8 - (c-5)^2$$

Optimum: $c^{\ast}_{\text{hot}} = 5$, $u_{\text{hot}}(5) = 8$.

A cold agent (planning future cookie consumption) projects and chooses $c = 0$, expecting utility $8 - 25 = -17$ (huge negative). Realizing only after the fact, the agent experiences welfare loss:

$$W = u_{\text{hot}}(0) - u_{\text{hot}}(5) = -17 - 8 = -25$$

This is a stark illustration: a **cold planner** who would never buy cookies is correct only if cookies are never consumed; once the hot state arrives, the optimal is to consume 5, not 0.

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for KTU answers**: In exam questions involving numerical examples, students are expected to compute the *biased optimum*, the *rational optimum*, and the *welfare loss* explicitly. Marks are awarded for showing all three.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Rational vs. Projection-Biased Agent

| Dimension | Rational Agent | Projection-Biased Agent |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Belief about $s_{t+k}$** | $s_{t+k}$ (correct) | $(1-\mu) s_{t+k} + \mu s_t$ |
| **Perceived utility** | $u(c, s_{t+k})$ | $u(c, (1-\mu)s_{t+k} + \mu s_t)$ |
| **Choice of $c$** | $c_1^{\ast}$ | Weighted average |
| **Welfare** | Maximal in $s_{t+k}$ | Reduced by $W(\mu)$ |
| **Anticipation accuracy** | Perfect in expectation | Biased toward $s_t$ |
| **Counterfactual simulation** | Full | Anchored on present |
| **Policy implication** | No "cooling-off" needed | Cool-off, pre-commitment useful |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Projection Bias

The following diagram maps the cognitive pipeline through which projection bias distorts a decision, contrasting the rational pathway with the biased pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agent at time t in state s_t] --> B{Decision Required about future action at t plus k}
    B --> C1[Rational Pathway]
    B --> C2[Projection Biased Pathway]
    C1 --> D1[Simulate future state s_t_plus_k accurately]
    D1 --> E1[Compute expected utility E_t u c_t_plus_k s_t_plus_k]
    E1 --> F1[Choose c_rational]
    C2 --> D2[Project present state s_t into the future]
    D2 --> E2[Compute u c_t_plus_k blended s_t s_t_plus_k]
    E2 --> F2[Choose c_biased]
    F1 --> G1[Actual utility u c_rational s_t_plus_k is maximised]
    F2 --> G2[Actual utility u c_biased s_t_plus_k is lower]
    G2 --> H[Welfare Loss W equals u c_rational s minus u c_biased s]
    G1 --> I[No welfare loss]
```

### 4.2 Mechanism of the Hot-Cold Empathy Gap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Hot State Agent] -->|underestimates gap| B1[Plans for cold future]
    A2[Cold State Agent] -->|underestimates gap| B2[Plans for hot future]
    B1 --> C1[Choice anchored to hot preferences]
    B2 --> C2[Choice anchored to cold preferences]
    C1 --> D1[Regret or excess when state changes]
    C2 --> D2[Regret or restraint when state changes]
    D1 --> E1[Hot-Cold Empathy Gap in action]
    D2 --> E1
```

### 4.3 Policy Interventions Against Projection Bias

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Identify Domain with Projection Bias] --> Q1[Cool Off Periods]
    P --> Q2[Pre Commitment Devices]
    P --> Q3[Default Architecture]
    P --> Q4[Information Provision at Cold State]
    Q1 --> R[Reduce state mis projection]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    R --> S[Improved welfare and reduced bias driven mistakes]
```

### 4.4 Projection Parameter Effect on Biased Choice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[mu equals 0] --> O1[c_biased equals c_rational]
    M05[mu between 0 and 1] --> O2[c_biased is weighted average]
    M1[mu equals 1] --> O3[c_biased equals c_t]
    O1 --> W1[W equals 0 no loss]
    O2 --> W2[W greater than 0 partial loss]
    O3 --> W3[W maximal loss]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: In the Mermaid diagrams above, node IDs have been chosen to be alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`. Labels are written in plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting symbols, in compliance with KTU-Premier-Engine V10 rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define Projection Bias. How is it different from Present Bias?** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Projection bias is the tendency of individuals to overestimate the extent to which their *future preferences, emotions, and behaviors* will mirror their *current* preferences, emotions, and behaviors, leading them to project their present state onto the future when making predictions or choices.

It differs from **present bias** in two key ways:

- **Present bias** is about *temporal discounting* — overweighting the immediate present relative to the future, even if tastes are stable.
- **Projection bias** is about *taste/state mis-prediction* — assuming that future tastes will match current tastes, even when the time delay would change the underlying state (hunger, mood, sobriety, etc.).

In short: present bias mistakes the *weight* of time; projection bias mistakes the *content* of future preferences. **Mark distribution: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for distinction.**

---

**Q2. Explain the term "Hot-Cold Empathy Gap" with an example.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hot-Cold Empathy Gap**, introduced by Loewenstein (1996), refers to the difficulty individuals have in predicting their own behavior across visceral states. When in a *hot* (aroused, hungry, angry) state, people underestimate how much their preferences will shift once they are in a *cold* (calm, satiated, sober) state, and vice versa.

**Example**: A person who is angry (hot state) may draft a harshly worded email, believing it is appropriate. Hours later, in a calm state, they regret the email and wish they had waited. The hot self projected only the present anger; the cold self, when it arrives, sees things differently.

This is a manifestation of projection bias, specifically tied to **visceral / emotional state mis-prediction**. **Mark distribution: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for example + proper example explanation.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Examination Style — Module 4 / Behavioural Economics]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the formal model of **Projection Bias** as given by Loewenstein, O'Donoghue, and Rabin (2003). Define the projection parameter and derive the biased choice under a two-state blended model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using a real-world example (e.g., intertemporal food consumption, retail shopping, or health decisions), illustrate how projection bias leads to systematic welfare loss. Compute the welfare loss for a numerical example of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup of the model**

Loewenstein, O'Donoghue, and Rabin (2003) model the agent's perceived utility of a future action $a$ as:

$$u^{\text{perceived}}(a) = u(c(a), s^{\text{proj}}_{t+k})$$

where $s^{\text{proj}}_{t+k} = (1 - \mu) s_{t+k} + \mu s_t$ is the **blended (projected) state**. The parameter $\mu \in [0, 1]$ captures the degree of projection:

- $\mu = 0$: rational agent, uses true future state.
- $\mu = 1$: complete projection, uses only present state.
- $0 < \mu < 1$: partial projection, weighted blend.

**Step 2 — First-order condition**

The agent chooses $c$ to maximize $u^{\text{perceived}}(c)$. Setting the derivative to zero:

$$\dfrac{\partial u(c, s^{\text{proj}}_{t+k})}{\partial c} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Quadratic utility example**

Take $u(c, s) = \alpha_s - \beta_s (c - c_s^{\ast})^2$ with state-specific parameters $(\alpha_s, \beta_s, c_s^{\ast})$. The biased choice is:

$$c^{\text{biased}} = \dfrac{(1-\mu)\beta_{t+k}\, c^{\ast}_{t+k} + \mu \beta_t\, c^{\ast}_t}{(1-\mu)\beta_{t+k} + \mu \beta_t}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

- It is a **weighted average** of the two state-optima.
- As $\mu$ rises, the choice is pulled toward $c^{\ast}_t$.
- The rational choice $c^{\ast}_{t+k}$ is recovered as $\mu \to 0$.

**Mark split: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for projection parameter, 2 marks for FOC, 1 mark for explicit biased-choice formula.**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Numerical example (Grocery shopping while hungry):**

Let $c$ denote the number of snack items bought. State $s_0$ = hungry; State $s_1$ = full (next day).

- $u(c, s_0) = 5 - 0.5(c - 4)^2 \Rightarrow c^{\ast}_0 = 4, \beta_0 = 0.5$
- $u(c, s_1) = 6 - 1.0(c - 1)^2 \Rightarrow c^{\ast}_1 = 1, \beta_1 = 1.0$

Rational agent buys $c^{\ast}_1 = 1$ item.

Projection-biased agent with $\mu = 0.6$:

$$c^{\text{biased}} = \dfrac{(0.4)(1.0)(1) + (0.6)(0.5)(4)}{(0.4)(1.0) + (0.6)(0.5)} = \dfrac{0.4 + 1.2}{0.4 + 0.3} = \dfrac{1.6}{0.7} \approx 2.29$$

Welfare loss at $t = 1$:

$$W = u(c^{\ast}_1, s_1) - u(c^{\text{biased}}, s_1)$$

$$u(c^{\ast}_1, s_1) = 6 - 1.0(1-1)^2 = 6$$

$$u(c^{\text{biased}}, s_1) = 6 - 1.0(2.29 - 1)^2 = 6 - 1.0(1.29)^2 = 6 - 1.6641 = 4.3359$$

$$W = 6 - 4.3359 = 1.6641 \text{ utility units}$$

**Interpretation:** The projection-biased shopper buys 2.29 items (anchored on hunger) instead of the rational 1 item, suffering a welfare loss of ~1.66 utility units. The next day, with a full stomach, they regret the excess.

**Mark split: 2 marks for example setup, 2 marks for parameter calculation, 2 marks for explicit welfare-loss formula, 1 mark for interpretation.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Distinguish between **Projection Bias** and **Hyperbolic Discounting** as two sources of intertemporal inconsistency. Discuss whether projection bias can occur *even if* the discount function is exponential. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **three real-world policy interventions** designed to counter projection bias. For each, explain the underlying mechanism and provide a concrete example (e.g., cooling-off laws, default options, pre-commitment devices). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — Definitions**

- **Hyperbolic Discounting**: The discount factor is $\beta \cdot \delta^t$, with $\beta < 1$, causing a sharp drop in patience between $t=0$ and $t=1$. Future utilities are discounted steeply, but the *relative* ranking of two future rewards is stable.

- **Projection Bias**: The agent mis-predicts the *content* of future utility by projecting the present state. The discount function itself can be perfectly exponential.

**Step 2 — Independence claim**

Yes, projection bias can occur with an exponential discount function. Consider an exponential discounter $D(t) = \delta^t$ with $\delta$ constant. The agent at $t=0$ evaluates action $a$ as:

$$U_0(a) = \delta^k \cdot u(c(a), s_0) \quad \text{(biased, present-state projected)}$$

$$U_0^{\text{rational}}(a) = \delta^k \cdot u(c(a), s_k) \quad \text{(rational)}$$

The bias arises purely from $u(c(a), s_0) \neq u(c(a), s_k)$, **independent of the discount rate**. Thus projection bias is a **taste/state mis-prediction bias**, not a discounting bias.

**Step 3 — Joint action**

When both biases are present, they compound. Hyperbolic discounting already causes $t=0$ to dominate; projection bias additionally makes the *content* of that dominant evaluation state-anchored, amplifying the welfare loss.

**Mark split: 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for formal independence argument, 2 marks for joint-effect discussion, 1 mark for conclusion.**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

| # | Intervention | Mechanism | Example |
|---|--------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Cooling-Off Periods** | Insert a time gap during which the agent transitions to a colder state, reducing projection. | 14-day return policy for large purchases; mandatory waiting period for tattoos. |
| 2 | **Default Architecture** | Set the default option to the rational one, so the agent must actively deviate (in a cold moment). | Auto-enrolment in retirement savings; opt-out organ donation. |
| 3 | **Pre-Commitment Devices** | Lock in a future choice while the agent is still in a cold state. | Ulysses contracts; commitment savings accounts; "no-buy" lists. |
| 4 | **State-Matched Information** | Provide information to agents in the *cold* state about the *hot* state they will enter. | Public-health anti-drunk-driving ads at bars; calorie labels visible at point of purchase. |
| 5 | **Friction / "Speed Bumps"** | Slow down the decision to allow state transitions. | Confirmation dialogs for irreversible actions; second-click warnings. |

**Detailed Mechanism — Example 1: Cooling-Off Periods**

In a 14-day return policy for electronics, the law acknowledges that the purchase is often made in a *hot* state (excitement, salesperson influence) and that 14 days of ownership allow the consumer to transition to a *cold* evaluative state, at which point the rational preference (keep or return) becomes clear. The intervention works by **decoupling the purchase decision from the consumption decision** in time, breaking the projection link.

**Detailed Mechanism — Example 2: Pre-Commitment Devices**

A commitment savings account (e.g., locking money for 5 years) is set up when the saver is in a *cold, rational* state. Future hot-state temptations to withdraw are blocked by the device. This works by **anticipating the future state in the present state** and using the present state's authority to constrain the future state's choices.

**Mark split: 2 marks per intervention, with explicit mechanism and example, totaling 6 marks, plus 1 mark for a synthesis/comparison remark.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Projection-Bias Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating projection bias with present bias.** They are *related but distinct*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Marks are deducted when students use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the projection parameter $\mu$.** Any formal answer must include the parameter and state the boundary conditions $\mu = 0$ (rational) and $\mu = 1$ (full projection). Omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Failing to compute welfare loss.** Many students describe the bias qualitatively but do not quantify $W = u(c^{\text{rational}}, s) - u(c^{\text{biased}}, s)$. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using present-state utility to evaluate future welfare.** This is the *definition* of the bias. Do **not** use $u(c^{\text{biased}}, s_t)$ when computing welfare; the true welfare is evaluated in $s_{t+k}$.
> 5. **Vague policy examples.** "Government should educate people" is not a specific intervention. Use concrete tools: cooling-off laws, default rules, pre-commitment devices.
> 6. **Mixing up hot-cold empathy gap with general projection bias.** The empathy gap is a *visceral-state* specialization of projection bias. Be precise.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Projection bias = overestimation of how much *future* preferences match *present* preferences.
- **Formal model**: Blended state $s^{\text{proj}}_{t+k} = (1-\mu) s_{t+k} + \mu s_t$.
- **Projection parameter $\mu$**: 0 = rational, 1 = full projection.
- **Biased choice**: Weighted average of state-specific optima.
- **Welfare loss**: $W = -\beta_1 (c_0^{\ast} - c_1^{\ast})^2$ in the simple quadratic case; zero only if $\mu = 0$ or $c_0^{\ast} = c_1^{\ast}$.
- **Hot-Cold Empathy Gap** (Loewenstein 1996): A *visceral* form of projection bias where hot/cold states cause under-prediction of preference shifts.
- **Distinct from present bias**: Present bias is *temporal weighting*; projection bias is *taste mis-prediction*.
- **Distinct from hyperbolic discounting**: Discounting can be exponential; projection bias is about state, not time.
- **Real-world examples**: Hungry grocery shopping, angry emails, impulsive purchases, addiction cravings, energy use, retirement saving.
- **Policy tools**: Cooling-off periods, defaults, pre-commitment, friction, state-matched information.
- **Key reference**: Loewenstein, G., O'Donoghue, T., & Rabin, M. (2003). *Projection Bias in Predicting Future Utility*. **Quarterly Journal of Economics**, 118(4), 1209–1248.
- **Standard exam cue words**: "future self," "state mis-prediction," "projection parameter," "welfare loss," "visceral state," "hot-cold empathy gap."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Present Bias # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Present Bias (Time Inconsistency Bias):** A cognitive bias wherein individuals systematically place a disproportionately high subjective value on immediate rewards relative to delayed rewards of equal objective magnitude, leading to dynamic inconsistency in intertemporal choice. Formally modelled as **Quasi-Hyperbolic (β-δ) Discounting** by Laibson (1997), where the discount factor applied between the present (t=0) and the very next period (t=1) is structurally different from the discount factor applied between any two future periods. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PECBT866 – Behavioural Economics**, Present Bias is positioned as a foundational departure from the neoclassical **Exponential Discounting Model**, which assumes agents are dynamically consistent (i.e., their preferences remain stable across time). Present Bias violates the **Stationarity Axiom** of discounted utility theory and is a primary generator of behaviours such as procrastination, undersaving, addiction, and inconsistent planning. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Present Bias is examined under Module 4 alongside related concepts like *Dynamic Inconsistency*, *Hyperbolic Discounting*, and *Naïve vs Sophisticated Agents*. Expect 14-mark derivation or application questions in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ## 1.2 The Three Building Blocks of the Bias | Component | Meaning | Symbolic Representation | |---|---|---| | **Present** | Reward available at $t = 0$ | $V_0$ | | **Future** | Reward available at $t > 0$ | $V_t$ | | **Immediacy Premium** | Extra weight the brain gives to "now" | Captured by $\beta \in (0, 1)$ | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Magical Mangoes" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine your friend offers you two options: > **(A) One mango right now**, or > **(B) Two mangoes delivered to you in one week.** > > A *rational exponential discounter* with any reasonable patience would pick (B). A *present-biased agent* however, often picks (A) — not because they dislike mangoes, but because the word *"right now"* carries a magnetic pull on the decision-making brain. The trick is: if your friend asks the same agent **one week from now** (so option A becomes "one mango today" and B becomes "two mangoes next week"), the same person may suddenly switch to (B). The preference has *flipped* simply because the timing shifted — this flip is the very definition of **Dynamic Inconsistency**, and it is the fingerprint of Present Bias. ## 1.4 Where Present Bias Sits in the Behavioural Economics Map Present Bias is one of the **"Big Three" anomalies** (alongside Loss Aversion and Reference Dependence) that distinguish behavioural economics from classical economics. It lives at the intersection of: - **Cognitive Psychology** (dual-system theory: System 1 hot, System 2 cold) - **Neuroeconomics** (activity in the limbic system vs prefrontal cortex) - **Intertemporal Choice Theory** (Samuelson's discounted utility framework) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** The bias is quantified using a single parameter — $\beta$ (beta) — with $\beta = 1$ representing full rationality (no bias) and $\beta < 1$ representing varying degrees of present bias. Empirically estimated values for $\beta$ in laboratory and field studies typically fall in the range $\mathbf{0.70 \leq \beta \leq 0.95}$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Discounting Models — A Comparative Breakdown ### Model A: Exponential Discounting (Neoclassical Benchmark) The classical Samuelson (1937) formulation. The agent discounts future utility by a constant factor per period. $$V = \sum_{t=0}^{T} u(c_t) \cdot \delta^{t}$$ - **Why:** Stationary preferences ensure that the decision made today is the same one the agent will want to act on tomorrow. - **How:** The discount factor $\delta \in (0,1)$ is applied multiplicatively, so the rate of decline is constant in proportional terms. - **Limitation:** Cannot generate preference reversals; predicts consistent behaviour over time. ### Model B: Hyperbolic Discounting (Mazur, 1984) A descriptive model that emerged from animal psychology experiments (pigeons choosing between small-soon and large-later rewards). $$V(t) = \frac{V_{\text{reward}}}{1 + k \cdot t}$$ - **Why:** Captures the empirical fact that delay aversion is steepest in the immediate future and flattens out for longer horizons. - **How:** The discount factor between $t=0$ and $t=1$ is much smaller than between $t=10$ and $t=11$, even though the objective time gap is identical. - **Limitation:** Continuous-time form is hard to work with in dynamic programming; gives point predictions but is mathematically unwieldy for infinite-horizon models. ### Model C: Quasi-Hyperbolic / β-δ Discounting (Laibson, 1997) The model preferred in modern behavioural economics because it preserves the tractability of exponential discounting while injecting a present-bias term. $$V(t) = \begin{cases} u(c_t) & \text{if } t = 0 \\ \beta \cdot \delta^{t} \cdot u(c_t) & \text{if } t \geq 1 \end{cases}$$ - **Why:** Splits the "immediacy premium" (β) from the standard patience parameter (δ). This decoupling is what allows for tractable analysis of dynamic inconsistency. - **How:** A multiplicative penalty $\beta$ is applied to **all** future utility, but **not** to present utility, creating an asymmetric weighting of now vs. later. - **Limitation:** The discrete form restricts it to period-by-period analysis; not as smooth as hyperbolic discounting in continuous time. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Why Does the Bias Exist? - **Dual-System Mechanism:** Kahneman's System 1 (fast, emotional, impulsive) overrides System 2 (slow, deliberative, patient) when rewards are immediate. Delayed rewards engage System 2; immediate rewards engage System 1. - **Salience & Availability Heuristic:** "Now" is psychologically vivid; "later" is abstract and easily deprioritised. - **Evolutionary Heuristics:** Ancestral environments rewarded immediate action (a present berry vs. a future hunt); the cognitive machinery was tuned for short horizons. - **Cortex-Limbic Imbalance:** fMRI studies (McClure et al., 2004) show the limbic system activates strongly for immediate rewards, while the prefrontal cortex activates for both — the asymmetry in activation produces the bias. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Interpretation | Typical Value / Range | |---|---|---|---| | Exponential discount factor | $\delta^{t}$ | Standard patience parameter | $\delta \in (0,1)$ | | Present-bias parameter | $\beta$ | "Now" premium penalty on future | $\beta \in (0,1)$ | | Quasi-hyperbolic weight | $\beta \cdot \delta^{t}$ for $t \geq 1$ | Effective weight of period-$t$ utility | $\beta \cdot \delta < \delta$ | | Hyperbolic discount value | $V(t) = V \cdot (1 + k \cdot t)^{-1}$ | Continuous-time form | $k > 0$ | | Long-run discount rate | $\approx \delta$ for $t \gg 1$ | Distant-future rate | Equal to exponential | | Short-run discount rate | $\approx 1 - \beta\delta$ near $t=0$ | Near-term rate | Strictly higher | | Dynamic consistency test | $D(t, s) = \frac{V(t)}{V(s)}$ | Ratio check at two horizons | Constant → consistent | | Implied annualised rate | $r_{impl} \approx \beta\delta$ | Effective yearly discount | $\beta\delta < \delta$ | | Naïve agent belief | $\beta^{naive} = 1$ | Believes they are rational | $\beta^{naive} = 1$ | | Sophisticated agent belief | $\beta^{soph} = \beta < 1$ | Knows their bias | $\beta^{soph} < 1$ | | Commitment device value | $\Delta = V^{commit} - V^{naive}$ | Welfare gain from commitment | $\Delta > 0$ if biased | | Self-control cost | $C_{SC}$ = utility loss of pre-commitment | Cost of locking future choices | Model-specific | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Substitution Note:** In all formula cells, mathematical vertical bars (e.g., absolute value or norm notation) are rendered using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve the markdown table boundary integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility Across Engineering & Policy Domains - **Personal Finance:** Explains why people fail to save enough for retirement despite intending to. - **Public Policy (Nudge Units):** Justifies "Save More Tomorrow" programs (Thaler & Benartzi) that lock in future raises as retirement contributions. - **Health Engineering:** Informs the design of medication adherence apps that deliver immediate micro-rewards for long-term compliance. - **Software / UX Engineering:** Underpins the design of "default opt-ins" — a present-biased user is far more likely to accept a pre-checked box than actively opt in later. - **Energy & Sustainability:** Explains undersubscription to long-term solar panel contracts in favour of immediate consumption. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Exponential to Quasi-Hyperbolic Discounting We start from the standard discounted utility framework. An agent at time $0$ evaluates a stream of consumptions $\{c_0, c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_T\}$ using: $$V_0 = \sum_{t=0}^{T} u(c_t) \cdot D(t)$$ where $D(t)$ is the discount function. Under exponential discounting, $D(t) = \delta^{t}$. Under present bias, the discount function is **piecewise**: $$D(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t = 0 \\ \beta \cdot \delta^{t} & \text{if } t \geq 1 \end{cases}$$ ### Step 1 — Substitute into the utility stream We replace the exponential weight with the piecewise form: $$V_0 = u(c_0) \cdot 1 + \sum_{t=1}^{T} u(c_t) \cdot \beta \cdot \delta^{t}$$ ### Step 2 — Factor out the bias parameter Because $\beta$ is constant across all $t \geq 1$, we can factor it out: $$V_0 = u(c_0) + \beta \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{T} u(c_t) \cdot \delta^{t}$$ ### Step 3 — Reinterpret in two-system language The expression can be read as: *"Place full weight on today's consumption, and apply a discounted exponential with an extra $\beta$-penalty to all future periods."* This is the **operational meaning** of present bias. ### Step 4 — Verify dynamic inconsistency Suppose the agent at time $0$ prefers $c_1$ over $c_2$ (i.e., $V(c_1) > V(c_2)$). At time $0$: $$V_0(c_1) = \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_1)$$ $$V_0(c_2) = \beta \cdot \delta^{2} \cdot u(c_2)$$ If $V_0(c_1) > V_0(c_2)$, then dividing by $\beta\delta$: $$\delta \cdot u(c_1) > \delta^{2} \cdot u(c_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad u(c_1) > \delta \cdot u(c_2)$$ ### Step 5 — Re-evaluate at time 1 When the agent reaches time $t=1$, the choice $c_1$ becomes "now" and $c_2$ becomes "later": $$V_1(c_1) = 1 \cdot u(c_1)$$ $$V_1(c_2) = \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_2)$$ ### Step 6 — Compare the new preference For the agent to prefer $c_2$ at time 1 despite preferring $c_1$ at time 0, we need: $$\beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_2) > u(c_1)$$ Combined with Step 4's result $u(c_1) > \delta \cdot u(c_2)$, we can construct a chain: $$u(c_1) > \delta \cdot u(c_2) \geq \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_2)$$ The two inequalities together generate a **preference reversal** — the agent's plan at $t=0$ is overturned at $t=1$. **This is the formal demonstration of dynamic inconsistency driven by Present Bias.** ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Models | Property | Exponential | Hyperbolic | Quasi-Hyperbolic (β-δ) | |---|---|---|---| | Discount function | $\delta^{t}$ | $(1 + kt)^{-1}$ | $1$ if $t=0$; $\beta\delta^{t}$ if $t \geq 1$ | | Parameters | $\delta$ | $k$ | $\beta, \delta$ | | Time consistency | Yes | No | No | | Continuity | Smooth | Smooth | Piecewise (kink at $t=0$) | | Tractability | High | Low | High | | Empirical fit (lab) | Poor | Good | Good | | Empirical fit (field) | Poor | Moderate | Strong | | Default in KTU exam answers | Reference baseline | Mention as precursor | **Primary model** | | Predicts preference reversals | No | Yes | Yes | | Captures "now vs. later" asymmetry | No | Implicitly | **Explicitly via $\beta$** | ## 3.3 Derivation — The Laibson (1997) Consumption-Savings Result Laibson showed that a $\beta-\delta$ consumer with $\beta < 1$ behaves as if facing a "**present-bias wedge**" in their Euler equation. The standard Euler equation for exponential discounters is: $$u'(c_t) = \delta \cdot u'(c_{t+1})$$ For the quasi-hyperbolic agent, the intertemporal marginal rate of substitution becomes: $$\frac{u'(c_t)}{u'(c_{t+1})} = \frac{\delta}{\beta}$$ ### Step 1 — Set up the period-t maximisation The agent at $t$ chooses $c_t$ (and implicitly $c_{t+1}$) to maximise: $$V_t = u(c_t) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_{t+1}) + \beta \cdot \delta^{2} \cdot u(c_{t+2}) + \cdots$$ subject to a budget constraint $c_t + s_t = y_t$ and $c_{t+1} = (1+r) s_t$ (no income in future period, for simplicity). ### Step 2 — Take the first-order condition Differentiate with respect to $s_t$ (savings): $$-u'(c_t) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot (1+r) \cdot u'(c_{t+1}) = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the marginal rate of substitution $$u'(c_t) = \beta \cdot \delta \cdot (1+r) \cdot u'(c_{t+1})$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for the implied consumption growth rate Taking logs and linearising around the steady state gives: $$\Delta \ln c_{t+1} \approx \frac{1}{\sigma} \left[ r - \frac{1-\beta}{\beta} \right]$$ where $\sigma$ is the coefficient of relative risk aversion (intertemporal elasticity inverse). When $\beta < 1$, the term $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta} > 0$ acts like a **virtual interest rate surcharge**, making the agent save less than the exponential benchmark. ### Step 5 — Interpret The present-bias term $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta}$ behaves like an extra cost of saving. With $\beta = 0.7$ (a common empirical estimate), this virtual rate is roughly $42.9\%$, which is enormous — explaining why present-biased agents tend to over-consume in the present and undersave for the future. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** This Euler-equation wedge is the most common derivation asked in 14-mark ESE questions. Be ready to reproduce Steps 1–4 cleanly and explain the $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta}$ wedge in words. ## 3.4 Worked Example — Choosing Between ₹100 Now and ₹150 Next Year **Setup:** Agent has utility $u(c) = \ln c$, $\delta = 0.95$, $\beta = 0.80$. Two options: - **Option A:** $c_0 = 100$ now, $c_1 = 0$ next period. - **Option B:** $c_0 = 0$ now, $c_1 = 150$ next period. ### Step 1 — Compute utility of Option A $$V_0(A) = u(100) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(0)$$ Since $u(0) = -\infty$ for log utility, strictly Option A is chosen. To make the problem non-degenerate, we modify: - **Option A:** $c_0 = 100$, $c_1 = 1$. - **Option B:** $c_0 = 1$, $c_1 = 150$. ### Step 2 — Utility of Option A $$V_0(A) = \ln(100) + (0.80)(0.95)\ln(1) = \ln(100) + 0 = 4.605$$ ### Step 3 — Utility of Option B $$V_0(B) = \ln(1) + (0.80)(0.95)\ln(150) = 0 + 0.76 \cdot 5.011 = 3.808$$ ### Step 4 — Decision at $t=0$ Since $V_0(A) = 4.605 > V_0(B) = 3.808$, the agent **chooses Option A** at time $0$. ### Step 5 — Re-evaluate at $t=1$ (hypothetical) If the agent could re-choose at $t=1$ between (consuming the 100 now-and-foregoing the 1) and (keeping the 1 now and 150 later), the calculation would be: $$V_1(A') = \ln(100) + (0.80)(0.95)\ln(1) = 4.605$$ $$V_1(B') = \ln(1) + (0.80)(0.95)\ln(150) = 3.808$$ If however the agent now compares *the same* $c_1 = 1$ (consumed now) vs. $c_1 = 150$ (consumed later), the weight on the future option becomes $0.76$ (no extra layer of $\beta$ since the $t=0$ perspective is gone). The 100-now-vs-150-later reversal happens precisely because the **asymmetric application of $\beta$ to "now"** is the source of inconsistency. ### Step 6 — What does a rational exponential agent do? For $\beta = 1$, the choice is between $4.605$ (Option A) and $5.011$ (Option B) — the rational agent chooses **B**. The present-biased agent chooses **A** because of the $\beta$ penalty on the future. This is the *measurable* welfare loss of present bias. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Taxonomy of Discounting Models ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Intertemporal Choice Theory] --> B[Classical Model] A --> C[Behavioural Models] B --> B1[Exponential Discounting
Samuelson 1937
V equals sum u of c sub t times delta to the t] C --> C1[Hyperbolic Discounting
Mazur 1984
V of t equals V over 1 plus k times t] C --> C2[Quasi-Hyperbolic or Beta-Delta
Laibson 1997
Beta times delta to the t for t greater than or equal to 1] C2 --> D1[Naive Agent
Believes beta equals 1
No commitment needed] C2 --> D2[Sophisticated Agent
Knows beta less than 1
Seeks commitment device] C2 --> D3[Partially Sophisticated
beta-naive greater than beta-soph] C1 --> E1[Animal Lab Evidence
Pigeon and rat studies] C2 --> E2[Field Evidence
Savings, exercise, addiction] D2 --> F1[Commitment Devices
Ulysses contracts, Save More Tomorrow] D1 --> F2[No commitment
Repeated failure to follow plans] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Flow of a Present-Biased Agent ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Reward offered at time t equals 0 NOW] --> node2{Is reward immediate} node2 -->|Yes| node3[Limbic system activates
System 1 hot response] node2 -->|No| node4[Reward at time t greater than 0 LATER] node3 --> node5[High subjective value
Immediacy premium applies] node4 --> node6[Prefrontal cortex engages
System 2 cold response] node5 --> node7[Choose NOW option
Beta penalty NOT applied to self] node6 --> node8[Apply Beta times delta to the t
Discounted future value] node7 --> node9[Action taken] node8 --> node9[Action taken] node9 --> node10{Inconsistency check at next period} node10 -->|Yes reversal| node11[Dynamic Inconsistency
Preference flip detected] node10 -->|No reversal| node12[Consistent choice
No present bias present] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — The Euler Equation Wedge Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD baseA[Standard Exponential Euler Equation] --> cond1[u-prime of c sub t equals delta times u-prime of c sub t plus 1] baseB[Present-Biased Euler Equation] --> cond2[u-prime of c sub t equals beta times delta times 1 plus r times u-prime of c sub t plus 1] cond1 --> cmp1[Implied saving rate is S-star classical] cond2 --> cmp2[Implied saving rate is S-star biased where S-star biased is less than S-star classical] cond2 --> wedge[The term fraction 1 minus beta over beta acts as a virtual interest rate surcharge] wedge --> out1[Agent undersaves relative to classical benchmark] wedge --> out2[Agent overconsumes in present period] out1 --> policy[Policy Implication: Nudges, defaults, commitment devices] out2 --> policy policy --> example1[Save More Tomorrow Program Thaler Benartzi] policy --> example2[Default opt-in to pension schemes] policy --> example3[Cooling-off periods for large purchases] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (node1, baseA, etc.). All node labels are free of markdown bold/italic tags, ensuring clean rendering in the Mermaid v10+ engine. ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Present Bias in a Decision Pipeline | Stage | Component | Function | Present-Bias Manifestation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Stimulus Input** | Reward option (now vs. later) presented | Now option is more salient | | 2 | **Encoding Module** | Brain encodes value and timing | Immediacy premium applied | | 3 | **Valuation Module** | Computes $V(0)$ and $V(t)$ | $\beta$ wedge distorts ratio | | 4 | **Comparison Module** | Selects higher $V$ | May favour now even if $\text{E}[V_{later}] > V_{now}$ | | 5 | **Action Output** | Behaviour executed | Immediate consumption / under-saving | | 6 | **Re-evaluation Loop** | At $t=1$, re-encodes options | New $\beta$-application reveals inconsistency | | 7 | **Feedback to Planner** | Agent updates plan or ignores it | Sophistication level determines response | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Define Present Bias. How does it differ from Exponential Discounting?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > **Present Bias** is a cognitive bias in which individuals assign disproportionately greater subjective value to rewards available immediately compared to identical rewards available in the future (1 mark). It is formally captured by the **Quasi-Hyperbolic (β-δ) Discounting** model of Laibson (1997), where future utility at any period $t \geq 1$ is multiplied by a present-bias factor $\beta \in (0,1)$ in addition to the standard exponential discount $\delta^t$ (1 mark). > > **Difference from Exponential Discounting:** Under exponential discounting, a single constant rate $\delta$ is applied symmetrically across all time periods, ensuring **dynamic consistency** (preferences do not reverse). Under present bias, the discount applied between $t=0$ and $t=1$ is *structurally steeper* than between $t$ and $t+1$ for $t \geq 1$, generating **dynamic inconsistency** (preferences can reverse) (1 mark). ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Explain the concept of Dynamic Inconsistency with a suitable example.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > **Dynamic Inconsistency** is a violation of the stationarity axiom, where a decision-maker's optimal plan made at time $t$ is not the plan they would still prefer to execute at time $t+1$ (1 mark). It arises naturally from Present Bias because the $\beta$-weight applied to "future" utility changes once that future becomes "present" (1 mark). > > **Example:** A student on 1st January plans to study 4 hours daily from 5th January onwards. By 4th January, the same student decides to start "tomorrow" instead. The plan was rational when made; the action is biased when executed (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **A) (7 Marks)** Derive the Quasi-Hyperbolic Discounting model from first principles. Explain the role of the parameters $\beta$ and $\delta$ and show how the model generates dynamic inconsistency. **B) (7 Marks)** Compare and contrast Naïve, Sophisticated, and Partially-Sophisticated Present-Biased agents. Discuss the welfare implications for each type, with a real-world example for each. #### Model Solution for Part A (7 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Start with Samuelson's Discounted Utility Framework (1 mark):** > The standard discounted utility of a consumption stream $\{c_t\}_{t=0}^{T}$ is: > $$V_0 = \sum_{t=0}^{T} u(c_t) \cdot D(t)$$ > where $D(t)$ is the discount function and $u(\cdot)$ is the instantaneous utility function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 2 — Exponential Baseline (1 mark):** > Under exponential discounting, $D(t) = \delta^t$ for all $t \geq 0$, where $\delta \in (0,1)$. This is the classical Samuelson (1937) form and implies time consistency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 3 — Inject the Present-Bias Term (2 marks):** > Define a piecewise discount function: > $$D(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t = 0 \\ \beta \cdot \delta^{t} & \text{if } t \geq 1 \end{cases}$$ > The term $\beta \in (0,1)$ is the **present-bias parameter** that captures the immediacy premium. Multiplying by $\beta$ shrinks the value of all future utility relative to present utility. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 4 — Roles of Parameters (1 mark):** > - $\beta$ = "now" premium; $\beta = 1$ implies no bias. > - $\delta$ = standard exponential patience; same role as in classical theory. > - The composite term $\beta\delta^t$ is the **effective** weight on period-$t$ utility for $t \geq 1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 5 — Generate Dynamic Inconsistency (2 marks):** > At $t=0$, the agent compares $c_1$ vs. $c_2$. They choose $c_1$ if: > $$\beta\delta \cdot u(c_1) > \beta\delta^2 \cdot u(c_2)$$ > Simplifying: $u(c_1) > \delta \cdot u(c_2)$. > > At $t=1$, the same choice re-evaluated. The agent now chooses $c_2$ if: > $$\beta\delta \cdot u(c_2) > u(c_1)$$ > > Combining: the agent can simultaneously satisfy $u(c_1) > \delta u(c_2)$ and $\beta\delta u(c_2) > u(c_1)$ whenever $\beta < 1$. This contradiction implies the agent's plan at $t=0$ can be overturned at $t=1$ — i.e., **dynamic inconsistency**. #### Model Solution for Part B (7 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Classification Framework (1 mark):** > Present-biased agents are classified by their *belief* about their own $\beta$: > - **Naïve:** believes $\beta^{belief} = 1$ (no bias). > - **Sophisticated:** knows $\beta^{belief} = \beta < 1$. > - **Partially-Sophisticated:** believes $\beta^{belief} \in (\beta, 1)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 2 — Naïve Agent (2 marks):** > Believes they will not behave impulsively tomorrow, so they make ambitious plans (e.g., "I'll save 30% of my income from next month"). When the future arrives, the bias kicks in and the plan is abandoned. The result is **repeated self-disappointment and failed plans**. > **Example:** A dieter who plans a strict calorie limit for next week and abandons it by Wednesday. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 3 — Sophisticated Agent (2 marks):** > Knows they will be tempted, so they actively restrict their future choice set. They use **commitment devices** (Ulysses contracts) to lock in the plan. > **Example:** A person who sets up automatic transfers of 20% of salary to a retirement account on payday — the money is gone before the temptation arrives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 4 — Partially-Sophisticated Agent (1 mark):** > Aware of *some* bias but underestimates its magnitude. They make partially-committed plans, leading to **moderate inconsistency** — better than naïve, worse than sophisticated. > **Example:** A smoker who buys only one pack at a time, thinking this will control consumption, but ends up buying multiple packs per day. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 5 — Welfare Comparison (1 mark):** > Sophisticated > Partially-Sophisticated > Naïve in terms of welfare, assuming commitment devices are available at low cost. The welfare gap arises because naïve agents over-consume and suffer the future consequences, while sophisticated agents pre-empt the trap. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` — **Alternative Choice** **A) (7 Marks)** "Present Bias leads to systematic under-saving." Derive the Euler equation for a present-biased agent and explain how the term $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta}$ acts as a 'virtual interest rate surcharge', causing under-saving. **B) (7 Marks)** Discuss three real-world behavioural consequences of Present Bias (with examples) and explain how the concept justifies the use of "nudges" and default options in public policy design. #### Model Solution for Part A (7 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Set up the agent's problem at time $t$ (1 mark):** > The agent maximises: > $$V_t = u(c_t) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(c_{t+1}) + \beta \cdot \delta^2 \cdot u(c_{t+2}) + \cdots$$ > subject to $c_t + s_t = y_t$ and $c_{t+1} = (1+r)s_t$ (no future income, for simplicity). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 2 — First-order condition with respect to savings (2 marks):** > $$\frac{\partial V_t}{\partial s_t} = -u'(c_t) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot (1+r) \cdot u'(c_{t+1}) = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 3 — Rearrange to get the biased Euler equation (1 mark):** > $$u'(c_t) = \beta \cdot \delta \cdot (1+r) \cdot u'(c_{t+1})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 4 — Identify the wedge (2 marks):** > Divide both sides by the exponential benchmark Euler equation $u'(c_t) = \delta(1+r) u'(c_{t+1})$: > $$\frac{\text{Biased LHS}}{\text{Benchmark LHS}} = \beta$$ > The factor $\beta < 1$ on the right makes the agent save less than the rational benchmark. Equivalently, the marginal rate of substitution is "tilted" by $\beta$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 5 — Linearise around the steady state (1 mark):** > For $u(c) = \frac{c^{1-\sigma}}{1-\sigma}$ and $\sigma > 0$: > $$\Delta \ln c_{t+1} \approx \frac{1}{\sigma}\left[r - \frac{1-\beta}{\beta}\right]$$ > The term $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta} > 0$ enters as if it were a tax on savings. With $\beta = 0.7$, this virtual surcharge is $\approx 42.9\%$, which dwarfs any real-world interest rate and explains dramatic under-saving. #### Model Solution for Part B (7 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Define nudges and defaults (1 mark):** > A **nudge** (Thaler & Sunstein, 2008) is a non-coercive change in the choice architecture that alters behaviour predictably without restricting the choice set. A **default** is a pre-set option that applies if the agent does nothing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 2 — Behavioural Consequence 1: Procrastination (2 marks):** > Present-biased agents put off unpleasant tasks with delayed costs (studying, exercise, paperwork) in favour of immediately pleasant activities. The cost of the task is salient now; the benefit is distant. > **Example:** Delaying enrolment in a pension scheme until "next year", repeated for 20 years. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 3 — Behavioural Consequence 2: Under-saving & Over-consumption (2 marks):** > The immediacy premium makes present consumption feel more valuable than future consumption, so the agent consumes too much today and saves too little. > **Example:** A young professional spending their entire salary each month despite intending to save "eventually". > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 4 — Behavioural Consequence 3: Inconsistent Health & Addiction Choices (1 mark):** > Addictive substances exploit the very mechanism of present bias: the immediate pleasure is weighted heavily, the long-term health cost is discounted away. > **Example:** A smoker continuing to smoke despite knowing the cancer risk — the immediate "relief" is $\beta$-weighted heavily. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 5 — Link to Public Policy (1 mark):** > Because present-biased agents *want* to follow through on long-term plans but *fail* at the moment of decision, the policy solution is to **lock in good choices in advance** via defaults, automatic enrolment, and pre-commitment. This is the "**choice architecture**" approach of behavioural public policy, exemplified by India's National Pension System (NPS) auto-enrolment, opt-out organ donation registries, and default green-energy tariffs in many countries. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Confusing $\beta$ and $\delta$:** Students frequently mix up the roles. Remember: $\delta$ controls *patience*; $\beta$ controls the *immediacy premium*. Saying "lower $\delta$ means more present bias" is wrong — lower $\beta$ is more present bias. > 2. **Forgetting the piecewise form:** Writing $V(t) = \beta \delta^t$ for *all* $t$ (including $t=0$) loses the dynamic-inconsistency property. Always state the piecewise form. > 3. **Skipping the steady-state linearisation:** The 14-mark question often expects you to derive the implied $\Delta \ln c_{t+1}$ equation. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Writing "people are irrational":** KTU examiners reward precise language — say "the model departs from exponential discounting" or "the agent exhibits dynamic inconsistency," not "they're irrational." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Present Bias** - **Core Idea:** Disproportionate weight on immediate rewards → dynamic inconsistency. - **Formal Model:** $\beta$-$\delta$ (Laibson 1997); piecewise: $D(0)=1$, $D(t)=\beta\delta^t$ for $t \geq 1$. - **Parameter Range:** $\beta \in (0,1)$, $\delta \in (0,1)$. Empirical $\beta \approx 0.7$–$0.95$. - **Predicted Behaviour:** Procrastination, under-saving, addiction, preference reversals. - **Dynamic Inconsistency Test:** Show that the agent's $t=0$ preferred plan is overturned at $t=1$ when $\beta<1$. - **Three Agent Types:** Naïve ($\beta^{belief}=1$), Sophisticated ($\beta^{belief}=\beta$), Partially-Sophisticated. - **Euler Equation Wedge:** $u'(c_t) = \beta\delta(1+r)u'(c_{t+1})$; implied growth rate carries the term $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta}$. - **Virtual Interest Rate Surcharge:** $\frac{1-\beta}{\beta}$ behaves like a tax on savings, explaining under-saving. - **Hyperbolic vs. β-δ:** Hyperbolic = $V(t)=V/(1+kt)$, smooth but intractable. β-δ = piecewise but tractable; preferred in policy work. - **Policy Lever:** Defaults, automatic enrolment, opt-out structures, commitment devices. - **Real-World Examples:** NPS auto-enrolment, Save More Tomorrow, Ulysses contracts, default organ donation, gym-membership sign-ups. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not claim exponential discounting *itself* is wrong; rather, it is **incomplete** for describing empirically observed preference reversals. - **Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question typically combines (a) a derivation (~7 marks) and (b) a discussion / application (~7 marks). Practice both halves. - **One-Line Memory Aid:** *β penalises the future; δ discounts the future; the kink at t=0 is where the bias lives.*

topics.title: Pre-Commitment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT866 - BEHAVIOURAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-Commitment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Commitment** is a strategic decision-making device in which a rational agent deliberately restricts their own *future* set of feasible choices *today*, in order to overcome the predictable self-control problems and present-biased preferences that will arise *tomorrow*. It is the formal behavioural-economics mechanism through which the **"sophisticated" decision-maker** (an agent who is aware of their own future irrationality) binds themselves to a course of action that their future, myopic self would otherwise avoid.

In the canonical KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (mapped to **Thaler & Shefrin, 1981**; **Schelling, 1978**; and **Laibson's β-δ Present-Biased Model**), pre-commitment is treated as the *first-best solution* to the **intra-personal principal-agent problem** that arises when an individual's inter-temporal preferences are dynamically inconsistent.

The standard classification recognises two principal categories:

| Type | Description | Behavioural Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Commitment** | Externally enforced, irreversible, costly to break (e.g., gym 1-year contract, Ulysses pact) | Strongest present-bias correction |
| **Soft Commitment** | Self-imposed, easily reversible, social or psychological pressure (e.g., public pledges, New Year's resolutions) | Weakest present-bias correction; relies on willpower |

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — Ulysses and the Sirens

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ulysses Contract (Self-Binding Strategy)**

In Homer's *Odyssey*, the hero **Ulysses** knows that he and his crew will soon sail past the island of the **Sirens**, whose enchanting song will irresistibly lure any listener to steer the ship onto the rocks. Ulysses cannot trust his *future self* to resist — he is sophisticated enough to know he will be tempted. So *today*, while rational, he orders his crew to **tie him tightly to the mast** and to **plug their own ears with wax**, pledging that they must ignore any of his future pleas to be released.

This is the purest, most cited example of pre-commitment:
1. **Time 0 (Ulysses rational):** Knows future self will be tempted.
2. **Time 0 Action:** Restricts future options (ties hands of crew, restricts his own freedom).
3. **Time 1 (Ulysses tempted):** Wants to break the commitment, but **cannot**, because the device was made external and binding.

The Sirens represent any hyperbolic, immediately-rewarding temptation (junk food, online shopping, procrastination, addictive substances, impulsive spending). The mast represents the **commitment device**.

## 1.3 Modern Real-World Examples

- **Salad Subscription Services** — Pre-paying for 6 months of healthy meals to avoid cooking fast food.
- **Forfeit Contracts (stickK.com)** — A user deposits money that is **forfeited** if they fail a goal (smoking, gym attendance).
- **Christmas Savings Clubs** — Depositing small weekly amounts that can only be withdrawn in December, preventing mid-year impulse spending.
- **Public Goal Announcements** — Telling friends you will run a marathon (social-commitment device).
- **Odysseus / Ulysses Pacts in medicine** — Psychiatrists pre-authorising involuntary treatment during a future psychotic episode.
- **Automatic Pension Escalation** — Default opt-in to increase retirement savings each year.

## 1.4 The Three Pre-Conditions (KTU Framework)

For a pre-commitment device to be effective, three conditions must hold (O'Donoghue & Rabin, 1999):

$$\text{Pre-Commitment works} \iff \{\text{Sophistication},\ \text{Credibility},\ \text{Future Temptation}\}$$

1. **Sophistication (Awareness):** The agent must *know* that their future self will be tempted.
2. **Credibility:** The device must be *costly or impossible* to break.
3. **Future Temptation:** There must genuinely exist a present-biased temptation that requires binding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pre-commitment only matters when β < 1 (present bias exists). For an exponential discounter (β = 1), pre-commitment yields no additional benefit because their preferences are already *time-consistent*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Pre-Commitment Works

The behavioural economist's chain of reasoning is built on **five sequential logic steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Present Bias Exists:** Empirical evidence (Frederick, Loewenstein & O'Donoghue, 2002) shows most agents have β ∈ [0.5, 1.0] in the Laibson model, meaning immediate rewards are overweighted relative to delayed rewards.
2. **Step 2 — Dynamic Inconsistency:** Because the discount function is *hyperbolic*, the optimal plan decided at t = 0 is **not** the optimal plan when re-evaluated at t = 1. The agent will knowingly deviate tomorrow from the plan made today.
3. **Step 3 — The Sophistication Gap:** A *sophisticated* agent anticipates this deviation. This creates a *meta-preference* (a preference over one's own preferences) — the agent prefers the t = 0 plan to the t = 1 plan, *but cannot enforce it* without help.
4. **Step 4 — The Commitment Device as Solution:** A pre-commitment device **externalises** the enforcement, removing the future self's option to deviate. The agent's choice set at t = 1 is contracted, eliminating the inferior equilibrium.
5. **Step 5 — Welfare Improvement:** The agent's *lifetime utility* (weighted by their own *sophisticated* preferences) is strictly higher with the binding device than without it.

## 2.2 The Hyperbolic Discounting Foundation

The mathematical engine of pre-commitment theory is the **Quasi-Hyperbolic (β-δ) Discount Function** proposed by **Laibson (1997)**:

$$D(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t = 0 \\ \beta \cdot \delta^{t} & \text{if } t > 0 \end{cases}$$

where:
- $t$ = time delay (periods from now)
- $\delta \in (0, 1)$ = standard exponential discount factor (long-run patience)
- $\beta \in (0, 1]$ = **present-bias parameter** (short-run impatience)

The agent's discounted utility from a stream of consumption $\{c_0, c_1, c_2, ...\}$ is:

$$U = u(c_0) + \beta \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \delta^{t} u(c_{t})$$

The critical feature is that **all** future periods are scaled by the same factor β, while period 0 is not. This single discontinuity creates time-inconsistency.

## 2.3 The Time-Inconsistency Condition

Consider a choice between reward $X$ at $t = 0$ and reward $Y$ at $t = 1$ (with $Y > X$). The agent at $t = 0$ chooses $Y$ iff:

$$u(X) \leq \beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(Y)$$

But at $t = 1$ (when the delayed reward has become immediate), the same agent chooses $X$ over $Y$ iff:

$$\beta \cdot \delta \cdot u(Y) < u(X) \cdot \frac{1}{\beta}$$

This contradiction is the **time-inconsistency trap** that pre-commitment devices are designed to dissolve.

## 2.4 The Welfare Gain from Pre-Commitment

A *sophisticated* present-biased agent derives the following value of committing to a consumption path $\{c_t^*\}$:

$$V^{commit} = u(c_0) + \beta \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \delta^{t} u(c_{t}^{*})$$

Whereas without commitment, the realised path $\{c_t^{nc}\}$ satisfies only:

$$V^{no\,commit} = u(c_0) + \beta \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \delta^{t} u(c_{t}^{nc}) \quad \text{with } c_t^{nc} \text{ chosen by the myopic t=0 self at each period}$$

Sophistication implies:

$$V^{commit} \geq V^{no\,commit}$$

with strict inequality whenever the present bias is binding (β < 1) and temptation is non-trivial.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ | Present-bias parameter | $0 < \beta \leq 1$ (dimensionless) | Quasi-hyperbolic discounting |
| $\delta$ | Long-run discount factor | $0 < \delta < 1$ (dimensionless, per period) | Standard exponential discount |
| $D(t)$ | Discount function at delay $t$ | $[0, 1]$ | Total present value of future reward |
| $U$ | Inter-temporal utility | Utils (subjective) | Objective function |
| $u(c_t)$ | Instantaneous utility of consumption | Concave, increasing | Within-period utility |
| $V^{commit}$ | Lifetime utility with commitment | Utils | Welfare under pre-commitment |
| $V^{no\,commit}$ | Lifetime utility without commitment | Utils | Welfare benchmark |
| $k$ | Cost of breaking the commitment | Currency units | Strength of the device |
| $r$ | Market interest rate | % per annum | Real-world benchmarking of $\delta$ |
| Sophistication | Awareness of one's own future bias | $\{0, 1\}$ or $\hat{\beta} \in [0, 1]$ | O'Donoghue & Rabin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Rule for Exams:** $\delta = \frac{1}{1+r}$ where $r$ is the per-period interest rate. If $r = 5\%$ annually, then $\delta \approx 0.9524$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

Although rooted in economics, pre-commitment theory has engineering and design analogues that KTU examiners value:

- **Embedded Systems Design** — Firmware that locks out the user from changing critical safety parameters (e.g., medical device dose limits).
- **Cybersecurity** — Mandatory password rotation intervals that the system *enforces* rather than recommends (forcing function).
- **Industrial Engineering** — TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) schedules that lock out machines for routine servicing, preventing operators from postponing maintenance.
- **Behavioural Public Policy** — "Save More Tomorrow" (Thaler & Benartzi, 2004) — employees pre-commit to allocating future *raises* to retirement savings, exploiting the fact that future income is not yet felt as a loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Optimal Commitment Plan for a Two-Period Saver

Consider a *sophisticated* present-biased agent with quasi-hyperbolic preferences facing two periods ($t = 0$ and $t = 1$), with income $Y$ in each period and no borrowing/lending market. The agent must decide how much to save in period 0.

**Step 1 — Define the variables:**
- $s$ = amount saved in period 0
- Consumption in period 0: $c_0 = Y - s$
- Consumption in period 1: $c_1 = Y + s$ (assuming zero interest for simplicity, $r = 0$)
- Instantaneous utility: $u(c) = \ln(c)$ (standard concave form for the exam)

**Step 2 — Write the lifetime utility with pre-commitment:**

Under commitment, the agent fixes $s^*$ at $t = 0$ and cannot renegotiate. Sophisticated preferences imply:

$$
V^{commit}(s) = \ln(Y - s) + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot \ln(Y + s)
$$

**Step 3 — First-Order Condition (FOC) for the optimal commitment level:**

Maximise $V^{commit}(s)$ with respect to $s$. Differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial V^{commit}}{\partial s} = -\frac{1}{Y - s} + \frac{\beta \delta}{Y + s} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the FOC algebraically:**

$$
\frac{\beta \delta}{Y + s} = \frac{1}{Y - s}
$$

Cross-multiply:

$$
\beta \delta (Y - s) = (Y + s)
$$

Expand:

$$
\beta \delta Y - \beta \delta s = Y + s
$$

Group $s$ terms on one side and constants on the other:

$$
\beta \delta Y - Y = s + \beta \delta s
$$

Factor:

$$
Y(\beta \delta - 1) = s(1 + \beta \delta)
$$

Therefore the *optimal commitment savings* is:

$$
s^{*} = Y \cdot \frac{\beta \delta - 1}{1 + \beta \delta}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since $0 < \beta \leq 1$ and $0 < \delta < 1$, the numerator $(\beta\delta - 1)$ is **always negative**, and the denominator $(1 + \beta\delta)$ is always **positive**. Therefore $s^{*} < 0$, which means the agent would optimally commit to *dissaving* (consuming more now). This is the classical **"sophistication reversal"**: a fully sophisticated present-biased agent knows they will over-consume tomorrow, so they pre-commit to *under-consuming* today, building a buffer.

**Step 6 — Compare with the no-commitment (naïve) outcome:**

Without commitment, the agent at $t = 0$ solves:

$$
\max_{s} \;\; \ln(Y - s) + \beta \delta \ln(Y + s)
$$

which yields exactly the same $s^{*}$. **However**, at $t = 1$ the agent, now facing immediate consumption, wants to undo the savings. The myopic FOC at $t = 1$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial c_1} = \frac{\beta \delta}{c_1} \neq 0 \;\; \text{(at the committed } s^{*})
$$

revealing that the committed plan is *suboptimal* for the t = 1 self. The commitment device prevents this re-optimisation.

**Step 7 — Numerical example for the exam:**

Let $Y = 100$, $\beta = 0.7$, $\delta = 0.95$. Then:

$$
s^{*} = 100 \cdot \frac{(0.7)(0.95) - 1}{1 + (0.7)(0.95)} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.665 - 1}{1 + 0.665} = 100 \cdot \frac{-0.335}{1.665} \approx -20.12
$$

Interpretation: The sophisticated agent optimally commits to **dissaving 20.12 units** — i.e., to *not* save at all, because they know their future self would have squandered any savings through present-bias.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Welfare Loss from Naïveté (Demand for Commitment)

**Step 1 — Define the welfare gain from commitment:**

$$
\Delta W = V^{commit}(s^{*}) - V^{no\,commit}(s^{nc})
$$

**Step 2 — Show that $\Delta W$ is increasing in the *sophistication gap*:**

The sophistication gap is the difference between the agent's *perceived* β (which they use for planning) and the *true* β (which drives behaviour). Let the agent *believe* they are $\hat{\beta}$ but actually act as $\beta$, with $\hat{\beta} < \beta$ (a naïve agent under-estimates their bias).

**Step 3 — Total commitment utility under naïve perception but true behaviour:**

The agent thinks they will save $s^{plan}$ chosen with $\hat{\beta}$:

$$
V^{plan} = \ln(Y - s^{plan}) + \hat{\beta} \delta \ln(Y + s^{plan})
$$

But they actually save $s^{actual}$ chosen with $\beta$ at $t = 1$:

$$
V^{realised} = \ln(Y - s^{actual}) + \hat{\beta} \delta \ln(Y + s^{actual})
$$

The "demand for commitment" increases as:

$$
V^{plan} - V^{realised} \to \text{larger as } \hat{\beta} - \beta \text{ becomes more negative (more naïve)}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

A *fully naïve* agent ($\hat{\beta} = 1$, believing themselves to be exponential) will *not* seek commitment, even though they would benefit most from it. A *fully sophisticated* agent ($\hat{\beta} = \beta$) will seek the exact optimal commitment level. This paradox — that those who need commitment most seek it least — is known as the **Sophistication Gap** and is a frequent KTU Part B exam question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Pre-Commitment Choice

| Agent Type | $\hat{\beta}$ | $\beta$ | Seeks Commitment? | Optimal Welfare Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Naïve (over-confident)** | 1.0 | 0.6 | **No** — believes they have no bias | Worst — chronic under-saving |
| **Sophisticated** | 0.6 | 0.6 | **Yes** — knows future bias | Best — optimal pre-commitment |
| **Partially Naïve** | 0.85 | 0.6 | **Mildly** — under-estimates own bias | Sub-optimal — partial commitment |
| **Time-Consistent** | 1.0 | 1.0 | **No** — no bias exists | Standard exponential optimum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Pre-Commitment Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agent at Time 0 - Rational Self] --> B{Identify Future Temptation?}
    B -- No --> C[Standard Exponential Choice - No Commitment Needed]
    B -- Yes --> D[Is Agent Sophisticated? - Aware of Future Bias?]
    D -- No, Naive --> E[No Pre-Commitment Sought - Chronic Welfare Loss]
    D -- Yes, Sophisticated --> F[Choose a Commitment Device]
    F --> G[Hard Device - Ulysses Contract - Externally Enforced]
    F --> H[Soft Device - Social Pledge - Self-Enforced]
    G --> I[Time 1 Arrives - Temptation Activates]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is Device Credible and Binding?}
    J -- No --> K[Agent Breaks Device - Myopic Self Wins]
    J -- Yes --> L[Future Self Cannot Deviate]
    L --> M[Original Plan Executed - Higher Lifetime Utility Achieved]
    M --> N[Final Outcome - Welfare Improved vs No Commitment]
    K --> O[Final Outcome - Welfare Same as No Commitment]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — β-δ Present Bias and the Commitment Gap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Period 0 Decision - Rational Self] --> P2[Form Intended Plan using beta-hat]
    P2 --> P3[Period 1 Arrives - Myopic Self Takes Over]
    P3 --> P4{Use True beta to Re-Optimise?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Deviation from Original Plan]
    P4 -- No, Plan Locked --> P6[Original Plan Executes]
    P5 --> P7[Welfare Loss Equals Commitment Value]
    P6 --> P8[Optimal Welfare Achieved]
    P1 -.Pre-Commitment Device.-> P6
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Sophistication Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Agent Self-Model] --> S2{Perceived beta-hat}
    S1 --> S3{True beta}
    S2 -- beta-hat equals beta --> S4[Sophisticated Agent]
    S2 -- beta-hat greater than beta --> S5[Naive Agent - Over-Confident]
    S2 -- beta-hat less than beta --> S6[Hyper-Sophisticated - Rare]
    S4 --> SC1[Seeks Optimal Commitment]
    S5 --> SC2[Does Not Seek Commitment Despite Needing It]
    S6 --> SC3[Over-Binds, May Over-Commit]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ulysses Contract in Software/Policy Terms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Layer [INPUT LAYER - Time 0 Preferences]
        I1[User Specifies Goal: e.g., Save 5000 per Month]
        I2[User Specifies Penalty: e.g., Forfeit 2000 if Goal Missed]
        I3[User Sets Lockout Date: e.g., 12 Months]
    end

    subgraph Processing_Layer [PROCESSING LAYER - Commitment Engine]
        C1[Encrypt Goal and Penalty in Smart Contract]
        C2[Deploy to Trusted Third Party - Bank or App]
        C3[Block Modification API Calls Until Lockout Date]
    end

    subgraph Output_Layer [OUTPUT LAYER - Time 1 Behaviour]
        O1[Period 1 Myopic Self Wants to Cancel]
        O2[Commitment Engine Checks API Request]
        O3{Is Request Allowed?}
        O3 -- No --> O4[Penalty Triggers - Funds Forfeited]
        O3 -- Yes --> O5[Goal Fails - Welfare Loss Realised]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> O2
    O1 --> O2
    O3 --> O4
    O3 --> O5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for KTU:** The Ulysses Contract maps directly onto **smart contract architecture** in blockchain (e.g., Ethereum escrow), **Pomodoro timer locks** in productivity software, and **role-based access controls** in industrial control systems. The behavioural principle is identical: externalise the enforcement to a system that the future self cannot override.
